actions.thought: 73o is one of the worst possible starting hands, particularly from UTG in a 6-handed game where I will be out of position post-flop against multiple players. No reads on opponents in Hand 1, deep stacks (100BB), so folding preserves stack and avoids difficult spots with a hand that has poor playability and showdown value.
J2o is a very weak hand, not in any reasonable opening range from middle position. Easy fold.

I am in the Cutoff with 83 offsuit. This is a very weak hand with no high-card value or connectivity. Even though the two players before me folded, 83o is not part of a standard opening range from this position. Folding is the correct mathematical and strategic play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Folded to me on the button with antes absent and blinds in the pot, so raising is higher EV than limping/folding: we can win 150 uncontested often and we have position postflop. J3s is weak but playable as a steal; if either blind 3-bets, we can comfortably fold.

I hold 6♦4♣ in the small blind facing a 2.5x open to 250 (I have posted 50; calling costs 200 more). This is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and a high chance of being dominated by the button’s opening range. Calling would put me out of position in a bloated pot with marginal equity (200 to win ~600 → need ~25% equity) and bad reverse implied odds; a reraise is a thin, risky bluff. Early in the tournament I should conserve chips and choose better spots, so folding is the best option.

I am in the Big Blind with K6o facing a 2.5x raise from the Button. The pot odds are approximately 27% (calling 150 to win a total pot of 550). While K6o has reasonable raw equity against a wide button range, it suffers from poor playability out of position, especially at 100bb stack depth where reverse implied odds (being dominated by better Kings) are significant. Defending ranges typically include K8o+ or K7o+ against a 2.5x raise; K6o is just below the standard defense threshold. Therefore, I will fold to avoid a marginal spot early in the tournament.
73o in UTG is a clear fold. This is one of the weakest hands in poker - offsuit, not connected, no high cards. Playing this from early position would be a significant -EV mistake.

I am in middle position with Ace-Four offsuit. While I have an Ace, the kicker is very weak and the hand has poor playability and realization of equity post-flop, especially when out of position or against stronger ranges. In a 6-max game, A4o is generally a fold from this position as it is easily dominated by hands like AJ, AQ, and AK which opponents behind me are likely to hold or raise with. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

Unopened pot in the cutoff with 100bb stacks. K7o is not a premium, but it’s within a reasonable CO opening range in 6-max: we have position, a king blocker to reduce strong defending/3-bet combos, and good immediate fold equity versus the blinds. I’ll use a consistent, pressure-oriented open size to attack the blinds.

I’m on the button with Qs Th facing a 250 cutoff open (pot 400). QTo is a playable, positionally strong hand versus a cutoff open — it has good flop equity and post‑flop playability. Calling 250 (about 2.5% of my ~99BB stack) keeps the pot manageable, preserves position, and lets me see a flop to either realize equity or apply pressure. I’ll re-evaluate on the flop: c‑bet or check depending on texture and opponents’ actions; fold to strong resistance if I completely miss. I’ll only 3‑bet here against a very loose opener or with a clear plan to shove/continue; otherwise call and play post‑flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Three offsuit (T3o), which is a very weak hand. Facing a raise from the Cutoff and a call from the Button, I am out of position against two opponents with a hand that has very poor playability and equity. Although the pot odds are roughly 3.25 to 1, the reverse implied odds and the likelihood of being dominated make this an easy fold.

6c2h is an extremely weak hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. Facing a cutoff raise to 250 and a button call, the pot is multiway with strong ranges from both opponents. Pot odds of calling 150 into 650 (~23% equity needed) are insufficient given my hand's poor equity against their ranges (~10-15% vs raise/call). Early in the tournament (hand 2/150), preserving stack is key; no value in continuing with trash.

We’re out of position versus the Button on a very dynamic 9h-6s-7h flop. With K7o we have a marginal made hand (second pair, weak kicker) that doesn’t benefit much from building a big pot, and our fold equity with a c-bet is limited because Villain’s flatting range hits this board heavily (pairs, straight/flush draws). Checking keeps the pot controlled, protects our checking range (we also check strong hands here), and lets us comfortably continue versus many reasonable stabs while avoiding getting blown off equity by a raise.

I’m on the button with Q♠ T♥ on 9♥ 6♠ 7♥. The preflop raiser (FiveTwo) checked the flop, so I can use position and fold equity to take the pot now or get value from worse draws. I have two overcards plus a gutshot to an 8 (4 outs) and 3 outs each to Q or T — about 10 clean outs (≈21% to improve on the turn, ≈38% by the river). The board is fairly connected and two‑hearted, so I’ll keep the sizing moderate to target missed high cards/broadways and small pairs while retaining pot control if called. A half‑pot c‑bet (325 into 650) needs only ~33% fold frequency to be break‑even, which is realistic versus a cutoff opener who checked. Plan: bet 325 to win the pot now or realize equity; if called I’ll reassess on the turn and proceed cautiously; if raised I’ll give credit to a made hand and fold unless the raise is small enough to justify a call for the draws.

Minni’s 325 stab is ~1/3 pot into a very connected, draw-heavy flop that the Button can bet with a wide range (overcards with hearts, straight draws like T8/85, plus pairs like 9x/7x/6x). With K7o we have second pair with a decent kicker and only need ~25% equity to continue (325 to win 975). Raising would mostly isolate ourselves versus stronger made hands and strong draws while folding out a lot of the bluffs we want to keep in. Calling keeps the pot controlled, protects our checking range, and lets us re-evaluate on the turn versus sizing and runout.

The Tc is a very dynamic overcard that generally favors the Button’s continuing range (Tx, T9, 98, and especially T8 now getting there). Our 7x has dropped to third pair with weak equity versus a lot of their value region, and leading risks getting raised off our hand or bloating the pot when we’re often behind. Checking keeps our range protected (we can still have strong hands/2p/sets), allows Villain to continue bluffing missed overs/hearts/straight draws, and lets us decide based on their sizing (likely continue vs small bets, fold more versus big pressure).

I have Q♠T♥ on 9♥-6♠-7♥-T♣ — I’ve turned top pair with a very strong queen kicker. Villain (FiveTwo) is the preflop raiser who check‑called my flop raise, then checked the turn: his range includes medium pairs (9x/7x/6x), some broadways, and draws (8x straight draw, heart draws). Checking behind gives free cards to the straight/heart draws and lets him realize equity cheaply. I want to both extract value from worse tens/second pairs and deny correct pot odds to single‑card draws, so I’ll bet for value and protection. Pot is 1300; I’ll size up to ~2/3 pot (800) — big enough to charge draws and get calls from worse pairs/Tx, but small enough to fold to a significant check‑raise (which would represent a made two‑pair/straight/set). Plan: bet 800; if called, re‑evaluate on the river (value‑bet/bluff‑catch accordingly); fold to a large raise on the turn.

Turn Tc is a very bad card for our flop check-call range with K7o: our 7x drops to third pair, and the turn completes a straight for any hand containing an 8 (a large portion of Button’s flop stabbing range: 98/87/86/85/T8/J8/Q8/A8 suited, etc.). It also adds a lot of natural value barrels (Tx, T9, 97/76, sets) and reduces the proportion of pure bluffs. Minni’s 800 into 1300 sizing is strong and sets up heavy river pressure; calling would often put us in a spot where we pay now and still have to fold to many river barrels, while we have no meaningful redraws (no flush, and pairing doesn’t beat straights). Without relevant blockers (we don’t block 8x) and with poor realization OOP, the best EV is to let it go.

I am UTG (Under the Gun) in a 6-max game with Ace-Eight offsuit. In professional play, A8o is generally considered too weak to open from the earliest position, as it is easily dominated by better Aces (AK-AT) and doesn't play well post-flop when out of position. While it has some blocker value, folding is the standard GTO (Game Theory Optimal) play here to maintain a strong range and avoid difficult spots against the Five players behind me.

Unopened pot and I’m first in from middle position. 9♣8♦ offsuit is a marginal open here: it plays poorly multiway, is frequently dominated when called, and can get squeezed by late position given I’ve opened the last two hands (more action-prone image). With deep stacks and plenty of better opens available, I’d rather pass and preserve a tighter MP range.

Cutoff with Q♠4♦ and ~107bb effective, no one has opened yet. Q4o is a marginal holding but from the cutoff I want to take the initiative and try to steal the blinds rather than limp and play a mediocre hand multiway out of position. I’ll open-raise to a standard 2.5x (250) to apply pressure, isolate the blinds, and keep the pot manageable. Plan post-flop: use position to c-bet favorable textures, fold to heavy resistance or a sizable 3-bet unless I pick up good equity; otherwise concede when facing strong aggression.

I hold 9d 5d on the Button. The Cutoff (Minni) has opened to 250 (2.5bb). While I have position, 95s is a weak suited gapper. The gap makes straight potential lower than a connector, and the high card value is low. My raw equity against a typical Cutoff opening range is likely around 34%, which is slightly below the direct pot odds required to call (38%), and there are still two players left to act in the blinds who could squeeze. Folding is the standard disciplined play here.

AQo is a premium hand that dominates much of Minni's cutoff opening range (which includes many Ax, KQ, broadways, suited connectors). Facing a 2.5x open after multiple folds, this is a standard spot to 3-bet for value, fold equity against her continuation, and initiative OOP. Previous hands show Minni is aggressive post-flop, so building a pot OOP with a strong hand is better than calling. Sizing to 8bb (800 total) is standard vs her raise size, leaving room for post-flop flexibility with deep stacks (~100BB). BB may squeeze or call, but AQo handles that well.

Facing a 3-bet from Elon in the SB after Minni opened from CO. I have A3o which is a weak ace - often dominated by 3-bet ranges which typically include strong aces (AK, AQ, AJ). Getting about 1.6:1 pot odds but A3o plays poorly post-flop against a 3-bet range. Clear fold.

I opened to 250 from the cutoff with Q♠4♦ intending to steal, but Elon (SB) has 3-bet to 800. Q4o is a weak, often-dominated holding vs a typical 3-bet range and performs poorly out of position. Calling 550 into the 1,150 pot only gets ~32% pot odds (550/(1150+550)) and would commit me OOP with marginal equity; a 4-bet bluff is unattractive because I lack blockers and would face bigger action. Fold and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

6-max UTG with ~96bb, KQo is strong enough to open for value and initiative. Limping invites multiway pots from position; folding is too tight. I’ll use the table’s established open size (2.5bb) to pressure blinds and define ranges while keeping the SPR reasonable if called.

I have T8s (Ten-Eight hearts) in middle facing an UTG raise to 250. T8s is a playable, suited connector that wants to see a flop — it’s not strong enough to 3-bet out of position against an UTG opener, and a small re-raise (to 400) would just bloat the pot and invite 3-bets from players still to act. Pot (400) vs call (250) and my deep stack give me good implied odds to try to flop a pair/flush/straight. Plan: flat-call 250, see the flop, and proceed cautiously — continue aggressively only with real equity (top pair, strong draws); fold to heavy action with no improvement.

I am holding Jc 7d in the Cutoff. The action started with an UTG raise to 250 and a call from Middle Position. J7 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability and equity, especially against an UTG opening range. With two players already involved and players left to act behind me, there is no justification for entering the pot. The correct play is a standard fold.

K6o is a marginal hand at best, offsuit with a weak kicker. Facing an UTG raise from an aggressive player (FiveTwo has raised UTG multiple times recently) and a flat call from Minni, the range is strong. Pot odds are about 2.6:1 (650:250), requiring ~28% equity multiway, but K6o has poor equity and playability OOP postflop if called. Position helps, but not enough to justify calling or squeezing with such a weak hand this early in the tournament. Standard fold to preserve stack for better opportunities.

KQo in the small blind with a raise and call in front. Getting 3.25:1 pot odds to call 200. KQo is a solid hand - can 3-bet but being OOP makes calling preferable. Will see flop and reassess based on board texture.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s 9s. FiveTwo raised to 250 from UTG, and both Minni and Claude have called. The pot is currently 850, and it costs me only 150 more to call. This gives me pot odds of approximately 5.6 to 1. 96s is a playable suited gapper that performs reasonably well in multi-way pots where I have high implied odds if I hit a strong draw or two pair. Given the price, folding is incorrect, and raising into three opponents with this hand is unnecessarily risky. I will call to see the flop.

Flopped top pair Queens with King kicker on a Q33 board. This is a strong hand - effectively two pair. With 4 players in the pot, I should bet for value and protection. Betting around 60% pot to extract value from worse Queens and pocket pairs while charging draws.

On a flop of Qc 3h 3s, I have no equity with my 9s 6s. Claude has led for 600 into a pot of 1000, which is a significant bet into three opponents. Given that I have no pair and no draw, and there are still two players (FiveTwo and Minni) left to act behind me, continuing in this hand would be a waste of chips. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

We have KQ on Qc-3h-3s: top pair with a strong kicker on a very static, paired board. Claude’s lead from the SB into multiple players can be Qx for value/protection, some pocket pairs, and occasionally 3x; there aren’t many draws we need to “charge,” so raising mostly just isolates us against better (trips) and folds out worse/bluffs. Calling keeps Claude’s weaker Qx and bluffs in, keeps the pot controlled multiway with Minni still to act, and lets us re-evaluate if Minni raises or if Claude barrels big on later streets.

Flop is Qc 3h 3s; I hold Th8h and have missed — no pair, only a two-card backdoor heart flush and three tens as clean outs. Odds to pair a Ten by the river ≈ 12.5%; two-card backdoor flush to hit by river ≈ 4.3%; combined equity roughly 16–17%. The pot is 2200 and it costs 600 to call, so I need ≈21.4% equity to make a pure call profitable (600/(2200+600)). This is a multiway spot (preflop raiser still in and Claude betting) which reduces implied odds — even if I hit a Ten I’ll often be behind Qx or crushed by sets. Raising as a bluff into two callers is thin and unnecessary this early in the tournament. My preflop plan was to fold to heavy action without improvement, so I’ll fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

I have top pair top kicker on a Q334 board. FiveTwo called my flop bet, likely holding a worse Queen, a pocket pair, or possibly trips/full house. The 4s brings a backdoor flush draw. My hand is still very strong heads-up. I should bet for value around 60% pot to extract from worse Queens and pocket pairs while charging any draws.

We have KQ on Qc-3h-3s-4s: top pair, strong kicker on a paired, low-draw board. Claude’s flop lead and turn barrel are certainly strong, but his value range is not only boats/3x—he can very plausibly be betting QJ/QT/Q9, some pocket pairs (55–JJ), and occasional bluffs/turn barrels (e.g., spade backdoors that picked up equity or A5-type gutshots). Raising is high-risk/low-reward because worse Qx and bluffs mostly fold while better (AQ, trips/full houses) continue. Given the price (1400 to win 5000) and our hand strength, the best play is to call and re-evaluate river sizing/runout.

River completed the spade flush (4 spades on board). I have top pair top kicker but no spade. FiveTwo called flop and turn - could have Qx, pocket pairs, or a spade draw that just got there. Betting risks getting raised by flushes. Checking allows me to control pot size, induce bluffs from missed draws, and call a value bet from worse Queens while avoiding a tough spot if raised.

Claude’s flop lead and turn barrel represent a lot of Qx/value, but the river spade is a bad card for his one-pair hands and he now checks, which skews him toward pot-control (QJ/QT/Q9, TT–JJ) and some give-ups more than nutted hands. With QsKc we have two pair (QQ33K), beat most Qx that would take this line, and we also block some Qx-spade combos. I don’t want to bomb it and fold out worse, or bloat the pot versus flushes/boats, so I’ll go for a thin, small value bet and comfortably fold if he check-raises big.

Facing a 1500 bet into 6500 pot (23% pot sizing). I need to call 1500 to win 8000, giving me 5.33:1 odds - I only need to win ~19% of the time. FiveTwo's small bet sizing is suspicious - a flush would likely bet bigger for value. This small bet could be thin value from worse Queens (QJ, QT) or even a bluff. While the 4-flush is scary, the bet sizing doesn't scream strength. With top pair top kicker and excellent pot odds, I should call.

UTG with 7♥4♣ — a very weak, unsuited, low-connectivity hand. Facing the full table out of position and with ~101BB effective stacks, limp/raise here is unjustified: limp invites multiway pots where this hand performs poorly and a raise with junk risks unnecessary confrontation. Better to preserve chips and wait for a stronger, positionally-appropriate spot. I fold.

I hold 9h 7s (97 offsuit) in Middle Position. This is a very weak hand with low equity and poor playability, especially with four players left to act behind me. Standard opening ranges for MP do not include unsuited one-gappers this low. Given the early stage of the tournament (100BB deep stacks), there is no incentive to play marginal trash hands. The correct play is to fold.

Folded to me in the cutoff with KcJs, a strong offsuit broadway hand playable from late position. Deep stacks and good position warrant an opening raise. Standard size of 2.5x BB (250 total) to build pot and take initiative. Continuation betting postflop will be profitable in position against the blinds.

32 suited is too weak to defend even on the button. While I have position, this hand has very poor playability and will rarely make strong hands. Folding is the clear play against a standard cutoff raise.

I am holding Ace-Queen offsuit in the Small Blind. This is a premium starting hand in 6-max play. A cutoff raise from Elon usually indicates a wide range of hands, many of which AQo dominates. To take control of the hand, define my range, and avoid playing a multi-way pot out of position against the Big Blind, I will 3-bet. A standard 3-bet size from the small blind is roughly 3.5x the initial raise. I will raise to 850 to put pressure on Elon and try to take the pot down now or lead on the flop.

We’re in the BB with 8♣2♠ facing a cutoff open and a small blind 3-bet to 850, and we’re out of position with a hand that has terrible playability and reverse implied odds. Calling 750 more doesn’t realize equity well, and Elon can still continue/4-bet behind us, making things even worse. No credible squeeze option with this holding. Easy fold to preserve stack.

Facing a 3-bet to 850 (3.4x) from the SB after my CO open-raise with KcJs, an offsuit Broadway hand with good playability. SB 3-bet ranges are often polarized, including value like QQ+, AK and bluffs like suited Ax or connectors, especially vs a CO open after button fold. KJo has solid equity (~35-45% vs polarized range) and excellent postflop playability in position (we act last heads-up). Pot odds are favorable: calling 600 more into 1200 pot requires ~33% equity. Deep stacks (100BB+) allow flexible postflop play; plan to c-bet often if check, fold to 4-bet pre. Folding is too tight early in tournament; 4-betting risks too much with marginal blocker hand. Call to realize equity IP.

I am the pre-flop aggressor after 3-betting from the Small Blind. The flop [8d 2h Ts] is relatively dry and favors my perceived range of high pocket pairs and strong broadway cards. While the Ten might have hit some of Elon's calling range (like JTs or T9s), I should maintain initiative with a continuation bet. A bet of about 1/3 the pot size (600) is effective on this texture to put pressure on his underpairs and overcards like KJ or KQ, while keeping the pot manageable if I am currently behind a Ten.

Facing a 600 bet into 2400 pot on a dry 8d-2h-Ts flop as the IP caller to SB's 3-bet with KcJs. Board is uncoordinated and low, favoring our two live overcards (K,J) with backdoor potential. Vs SB's polarized 3-bet range (QQ+/AK value, suited Ax/conn bluffs), we have ~35-40% equity overall and excellent playability IP. Pot odds require only ~20% (600/3000), easily met. Folding is too tight early (hand 5/150, deep stacks 90+BB); raising risks bloating pot OOP vs value or folding out bluffs we dominate. Calling realizes equity, keeps pot manageable, allows turn control (c-bet freq if checked to), and sets up fold to 2nd barrel if drawy/scary or take away if checked. Standard float IP vs aggressive SB.

The 3c on the turn is a complete brick that doesn't change the board state significantly. My Ace-high currently loses to any pair in Elon's range (Tx, 8x, or pocket pairs like 99). After Elon called my continuation bet on the flop, his range is narrowed to hands that likely have a piece of the board or a decent draw. Following up with a second barrel on this blank turn is unlikely to fold out a Ten or an Eight. By checking, I can see if Elon checks back with his own overcards (like KJ or KQ) or if he bets, at which point I can decide whether to fold based on his sizing. Checking is the best way to control the pot and potentially see a free river to hit one of my six outs (Ace or Queen).

Checked to on turn with KcJs in position heads-up vs aggressive SB (Flash) who 3-bet preflop and c-bet flop on dry 8d-2h-Ts-3c board. Board remains uncoordinated, adding minimal draws (gutshot possibilities like J9 or 45). Our hand has two live overcards (K,J blockers to strong Ax hands), backdoor potential, and blocks parts of villain's value range (AK, AJs/T9s). Villain's check after leading flop likely indicates weakness: missed value hands (QQ+/AK), failed draws/bluffs from polarized 3-bet range, or trapping (less likely OOP). Pot odds irrelevant since acting; betting ~50% pot (1500 into 3000) applies pressure to fold worse (Axo/high cards, missed draws), realizes equity IP, denies equity to any turn-improved hands/draws, and builds pot OOP should villain call light. Checking risks free river card to villain's equity or multiway villain range. Deep stacks (90+BB), early tournament (hand 5/150) favor aggressive play IP to maximize fold equity and thin value. Plan: bet 1500, c/r villain's raises if polarized light, proceed cautiously on scary rivers or check back strong rivers.

I am holding Ace-high (AQo) on a board of 8-2-T-3. After 3-betting pre-flop and continuation betting on the flop, I checked the turn to control the pot size since I only have overcards and no made hand. Elon has responded with a 1,500 bet into a 3,000 pot. My current hand loses to any pair in his range (including Tx, 8x, or small pocket pairs). While he could be bluffing with lower overcards like KJ or KQ, the pot odds to call are roughly 25% (1,500 to win 6,000), whereas my chance of hitting an Ace or Queen on the river is only about 13%. Since I am out of position and have no guaranteed way to win the pot without improving, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am in UTG position with Queen-Five offsuit (Q5o), which is a very weak hand. UTG is the tightest position at the table, requiring a strong range to open profitably. Q5o falls well outside opening ranges even for loose strategies. With 144 hands remaining, preserving chips and waiting for better spots or position is the correct play. There is no strategic merit to limping or raising here.

Preflop in middle position with Q3s after UTG fold. Q3 suited has some potential for flush draws or straight draws but is dominated by better queens and has poor postflop playability. With deep stacks and 6 players, calling risks multiway pots with a weak hand. Standard play is to fold to preserve stack for better spots early in the tournament.
K9o in cutoff with two folds. Decent hand in position - should raise to steal blinds or isolate. Standard 2.5x raise to 250.

I am on the button with AJo, a strong starting hand in 6-max play. Claude raised from the cutoff, which typically represents a wide range of hands. By 3-betting to 750, I am putting pressure on the cutoff, potentially winning the pot immediately or isolating Claude while having the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. If I just call, I allow the small and big blinds to enter the pot with a wide range of hands at a discount. A 3-bet here is a standard aggressive play to maximize value and control the hand.

We’re in the small blind facing an open (250) and a 3-bet to 750 from the Button, with the original raiser still behind us. J4s is too weak to defend out of position in a multiway 3-bet pot, and it plays poorly postflop (dominated when we make top pair, limited strong draws). A 4-bet bluff from the SB with this hand is unnecessarily high variance given stack depths and positions. Best decision is to fold and preserve stack.

Claude opened to 250 from the cutoff and Flash 3-bet to 750 from the button. I’m in the big blind with Td9c (off-suit T9) and have to call 650 more (total 750). T9o is a playable, connected hand with good multi-street playability and the cost to continue is modest (650 into a 1150 pot → calling gives me ~36% pot odds). Folding would be too tight with a live blind already invested; 4-betting/raising to 1250 with this hand out of position is over-committal and unnecessary. I’ll call to see a flop, plan to proceed cautiously OOP — fold to heavy action if I miss and don’t pick up equity, and play aggressively if I hit a strong draw or pair.

K9o facing a 3-bet from Button and a cold call from BB is a clear fold. K9o is a marginal hand that plays poorly multiway and out of position. The cold call from Minni is especially concerning as it often indicates strength. Even with ~3.6:1 pot odds, this hand doesn't play well enough post-flop to justify calling.

Check. I missed the flop — Td9c has no pair and essentially no real draw on 2c‑4h‑8s (no flush possible with only one club in hand, only backdoor straight paths and two overcards). I’m out of position to a preflop 3‑better, so pot control is important. Checking lets Flash c‑bet bluffs or small bets that I can call to realize equity; I’ll call modest continuation bets (~≤600–700, ~30–40% pot) but fold to heavy sizing (≈≥900, >~50% pot). If he checks back I get a free turn to either improve or take another stab only with real equity.

As the pre-flop aggressor who 3-bet from the button, I have the range advantage on this dry 8-4-2 rainbow board. This board texture is unlikely to have improved Minni's big blind cold-calling range, which typically consists of medium pocket pairs or high broadway cards. I am placing a continuation bet of 600 (one-third of the pot) to exert pressure on her non-paired hands and to represent an overpair. If she calls, I will re-evaluate based on the turn card.

I missed the flop with T9o on a dry 2c-4h-8s and I’m out of position to a preflop 3-bettor who c-bet small (600 into 2400). That sizing gives me good pot odds: 600 to win 3000 (≈20% equity required). My hand has ~8 outs to a pair (~31% by river) plus some turn/river playability, so a call is +EV to realize equity and see a cheap turn. Folding would be too nitty given the price and my prior investment; raising OOP as a bluff is unattractive versus a wide but sticky 3-bet range and risks committing more without a made hand. Plan: call the 600, see the turn, and only proceed aggressively if I pair or pick up a real draw; otherwise fold to heavy turn aggression.

Check. I have T9o on 2c‑4h‑8s‑2s: no pair, no real draw (only 6 clean outs to a pair on the river, ~13%). I'm out of position versus a player who 3‑betted preflop and c‑bet a small size on the flop, so bluffing OOP here is thin — I don't have the fold equity to justify risking more chips with such poor equity. Checking keeps the pot controlled, lets me see how Flash reacts to the board pairing, and preserves stack to play the river if I hit. Plan: check and fold to a large turn bet; if Flash checks back I get a free river to either realize equity or attempt a river bluff/value if I pair.

The turn 2s is essentially a brick that doesn't change the board texture significantly. My A-high still has decent showdown value against Minni's potential floating range (like KQ or suited connectors that missed). Since the board is paired and dry, betting again might not fold out better hands like 8x or pocket pairs (77-TT), and it risks a check-raise. By checking back, I control the pot size and give myself a chance to hit an Ace or Jack on the river or simply win at showdown if she has a weaker hand.

I missed the river — T9o has no pair or legitimate showdown value on 2c‑4h‑8s‑2s‑4d. The board is double‑paired so any opponent holding a 2 or a 4 now has trips/full house (pocket 2s/4s would be quads). Flash was the preflop 3‑bettor who made a small flop c‑bet then checked the turn, a line that can indicate a wide/marginal range or pot control with a medium/strong hand; either way I have very little fold equity OOP. A river bluff here is thin and likely to be called by single‑pair/medium hands that beat me, so the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV line is to check and concede the pot if he fires. Action: check and fold to a bet.

The board has double-paired (2-2-4-4) with an 8. My best five-card hand is two pair (4s and 2s) with an Ace kicker. Minni has checked the river after the action went check-check on the turn. Her range for cold-calling a 3-bet from the Big Blind and calling a flop continuation bet often includes pocket pairs (55-TT), suited connectors that hit the 8 (like 98s or 87s), or high broadway cards (KQ, KJ). My Ace-high (Ace-Jack) has significant showdown value against her missed broadway hands but is behind her pocket pairs and any 8. Since she has checked twice, I am unlikely to fold out a better hand (like 77 or 8x) with a bet, and I gain nothing by betting if she folds her worse hands (like KQ). Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value and potentially win the pot without risking more chips.

UTG in a 6-handed game with K6s suited. This hand is not strong enough for an open raise from early position; it's outside the standard UTG range (typically 77+, ATs+, KJs+, AJo+, KQo+). Limping is weak and invites multiway pots where K6s performs poorly postflop due to domination risks and low playability. Folding preserves stack and tight image early in tournament with deep stacks (118BB). Plenty of better spots ahead.
3h7d is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability. In middle position with 4 players yet to act, this is a clear fold. No reason to invest chips with such a marginal holding.

I am in the Cutoff with Pocket Jacks (Js Jc). The action has folded to me. Pocket Jacks is a premium hand, especially in a 6-max game. I will open for a standard 3x raise to 300 chips to build the pot and take control of the hand. This sizing is consistent with standard opening play and invites action from worse hands while protecting against overcards.

Flash is opening from the cutoff to 3bb and has shown willingness to apply pressure preflop, so his range is fairly wide. J9o is too weak to 3-bet for value and doesn’t love getting 4-bet or playing a bloated pot when called, but it has reasonable playability in position against a wide CO range. Calling keeps the pot manageable, realizes our equity on the button, and avoids turning a marginal hand into an expensive bluff spot this early/deep.

Facing a cutoff raise to 300 with the button calling and me in the small blind, Th6c is a weak, off-suit hand that will be dominated by much of the raiser’s range. Pot is 750 and it costs 250 to call (calling would need ~25% equity), but I’ll be out of position against at least two opponents postflop and have poor playability and few backdoor possibilities. A light 3-bet/squeeze from the small blind here is risky and likely to get called or re-raised; I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am in the Big Blind with JTo. The Cutoff raised to 300 and the Button called. The Small Blind folded. The pot is currently 750. I need to call 200 more to see the flop. This gives me pot odds of 200 to win 950, which is roughly 4.75 to 1 or about 21% required equity. JTo is a decent hand that connects well with broadway cards and performs adequately in multi-way pots, typically having around 25-28% equity against reasonable opening and flatting ranges in these positions. Although playing out of position against two opponents is a disadvantage, the price is too good to fold. A squeeze raise is risky with this hand as I would hate a 4-bet and playing a bloated pot OOP with JTo is not ideal. Therefore, calling and evaluating the flop is the most prudent play.

I am out of position in a multi-way pot on an Ah 3c Kd board with Jh Tc. I have a gutshot straight draw to the Broadway straight (need a Queen). This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser (Cutoff) and the caller (Button), who are much more likely to have Ax and Kx hands than my Big Blind defense range. Leading out (donk betting) would be ill-advised as I have no showdown value and range disadvantage. Checking is the standard line to see what the opponents do and hopefully realize my equity for free.

The flop comes Ace-high and King-high (Ah 3c Kd), which are both overcards to my pocket Jacks. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and both the Button and Big Blind called, it is highly likely that at least one of them has connected with this board. Betting here into two opponents on such a coordinated board for their calling ranges (which often include Ax and Kx hands) would likely result in being called by better hands and folding out worse ones. Checking back allows me to control the pot size and see a turn card for free, keeping the pot small while I'm likely behind.

Both opponents checked on an A‑K‑x flop in a 3‑way pot, which often caps their ranges (a lot of medium pairs, weak Ax, floats, or give-ups). J9o has essentially no showdown value here, so checking back doesn’t realize much. A small stab leverages our positional advantage and can fold out Pro’s air/small pairs and sometimes even get Flash to release hands like QJ/QT/low pairs that didn’t want to c-bet multiway. I’ll use a small sizing to risk little while still generating folds; if called, I’ll mostly slow down unless the turn improves equity (T/Q/8/diamond) or the runout favors a second barrel.

I am facing a bet of 300 into a pot of 950 on an Ah 3c Kd board, giving me pot odds of roughly 19%. I hold Jh Tc, giving me a gutshot straight draw (4 outs to a Queen). My direct equity to hit the straight on the turn is only about 8.5%. My potential pair outs (J or T) are likely dead against the Ace and King on the board. Additionally, I am out of position, and the pre-flop raiser (Flash) is still to act behind me, creating a risk of a squeeze or check-raise that would force me off the hand. Given the poor direct odds and the positional disadvantage, chasing this draw is not profitable.

The flop of Ah-3c-Kd is very unfavorable for my pocket Jacks, as there are two overcards present. I checked to control the pot, and FiveTwo made a small bet of 300. While the odds to call are relatively good, my hand is likely behind any Ace or King, and I am out of position for the rest of the hand. With only two outs to significantly improve my hand, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack rather than chasing a marginal hand on a dangerous board.
Q4o from UTG is a clear fold. This is a weak offsuit hand and UTG requires a tight range since I have to act first with 5 players behind me. Not worth opening.

I am in middle position with Jd 6d. This hand is too weak to open under normal circumstances, even with the suited value. The standard opening range for this position usually includes J9s+, and J6s is well below that threshold. Considering the tournament depth and my position, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound play.

Unopened pot in the cutoff (pot is just blinds). J2o is too weak to profitably open at 100bb: dominated when we make a jack, poor playability, and limping invites multiway spots with bad reverse implied odds. With no dead money beyond blinds and solid stacks behind, best is to pass and preserve stack for higher-EV opens.

I’m on the button with J♥7♦ and everyone folded to me. This is a late-position open-raise spot — J7o is marginal but plays well in position and has good fold equity. The pot is 150 and the to-call is 100; I’ll make the minimum raise to 200 (2x BB) to steal the blinds and keep the pot manageable. If either blind 3-bets big (standard larger 3-bets we’ve seen from Elon), I’ll fold—this hand doesn’t have the playability to continue out of position against significant aggression. If called, I’ll use my positional advantage to c-bet small or proceed cautiously on connected/oversized boards.

I am in the Small Blind with Th 5h facing a Button min-raise from Minni. Although I am getting decent pot odds (calling 150 to win a pot of 500 if the BB folds), T5s is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Being out of position makes it difficult to realize my raw equity, and the hand is easily dominated. Defending here would likely result in a negative expected value situation due to the positional disadvantage and hand strength. Therefore, folding is the disciplined and correct play.

T7o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability out of position. Minni's button raise after multiple folds is a standard wide-range steal attempt, likely 35-50% of hands. Defending BB requires good pot odds and equity, but calling 100 into 350 gives ~3.5:1 (22% equity needed), and T7o has poor equity (~30-35% vs. button range) but terrible postflop playability OOP with no straight/flush potential together and gappy connectors. No blockers to strong hands. 3-betting as bluff is suboptimal due to poor blockers and reverse implied odds if called. Folding preserves stack for better spots in early tournament phase; EV positive long-term.

I am in the UTG (Under The Gun) position with J5 offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand that is well below the range of hands I should be playing from early position. Standard poker strategy suggests a fold here to avoid playing a weak hand out of position against potentially stronger ranges from the remaining five players.

Td4o is far too weak to enter from middle position 6-handed. It’s dominated when we make top pair, plays poorly postflop out of position vs blinds/late position, and doesn’t benefit from building a pot at ~100bb stacks—especially with aggressive opponents behind who can isolate. Best EV is to just release it.

Cutoff, 8♠6♥ (about 89BB effective). This is a marginal, one-gapper offsuit, but in 6-max cutoff I should be opening relatively wide to seize position and steal the blinds. I prefer to raise rather than limp to gain fold equity and avoid multiway pots; however I won’t be committing a large portion of my stack with this hand. I’ll open-raise to 250 (≈2.5x BB). Plan: fold to a substantial 3-bet preflop (hand is too weak to continue deep OOP), and if called I’ll c-bet selectively on dry flops and proceed cautiously vs heavy resistance or multiple callers.

I hold 9c 3d on the Button. This is a statistically very weak hand (trash). Minni has opened to 2.5bb from the Cutoff. Despite having the positional advantage, my hand has insufficient equity and playability to call or 3-bet bluff profitably against a standard Cutoff opening range. The pot odds do not justify playing a hand this weak. The standard and correct play is to fold.

QhJh is a strong suited broadway hand from the SB facing a CO raise to 250 (2.5BB). It has excellent playability, equity vs calling range, and blocking value. Deep stacks (~100BB+). Standard play is to 3-bet for value/isolation rather than flat call, as calling risks multiway pot with BB behind and plays awkwardly OOP. Minni (87BB) is aggressive based on history (wins pots postflop). 3-bet sizing to ~3.2x the raise (800 total) builds pot while denying equity to worse hands. Folds worse, gets called by worse, BB folds often.

Facing a 3-bet from Elon with A7o in the BB. This is a marginal hand that plays poorly against 3-bet ranges, especially out of position. Elon showed strength in Hand #3 with a 3-bet as well. A7o doesn't have great equity against a reasonable 3-betting range and will be difficult to play postflop OOP. Easy fold.

I opened the cutoff to 250 with 8♠6♥ and Elon (SB) 3‑bet to 800. The pot is ~1,150 and I must call 550 to continue (total commit = 800), so the call requires roughly 550/(1150+550) ≈ 32% equity. 8‑6 offsuit is a marginal hand with poor multi‑street playability against a 3‑bet range that likely contains pairs and broadways; my realized equity is well below the break‑even point. 4‑bet bluffing here is unattractive (I don’t have the right blockers and Elon has shown willingness to put in big 3‑bets), and calling to play a small pot with a weak hand is a low‑EV line early in the tournament. I’ll fold and preserve ~86BB for better spots.

62o is well below an UTG opening range in 6-max. Limping invites multiway pots out of position with a hand that realizes equity poorly and is dominated when it pairs. Given the table’s tendency to isolate/3-bet, folding preserves stack and avoids tough postflop spots.

Fold. J♣8♦ offsuit is a weak, poorly-connected hand with no suit and limited postflop playability, especially multiway. There are several players to act behind (cutoff, button, SB, BB), so calling 100 would likely create a multiway pot where my equity is low and I’d be playing out of position. Open-raising to 200 to steal risks a 3-bet or a caller with better hands; with ~86 BB effective I can wait for a much clearer spot. Preserve the stack and look for a stronger hand or better position.

I hold King-Seven offsuit (K7o) in the Cutoff. While this is sometimes an opening hand in late position, I have observed that Elon on the Button has been very aggressive, 3-betting opens in recent hands (Hand #3 and Hand #9). Opening a marginal hand like K7o with an aggressive player on my direct left is risky, as I would likely have to fold to a 3-bet or play a bloated pot out of position if he calls. Therefore, the disciplined play is to fold.

Preflop on the button with Qs8c after three folds (UTG, MP, CO). Excellent position to steal blinds. Q8o is within a standard button stealing range (queen-high, decent playability postflop). Pot is 150, blinds posted 50/100. Standard raise size around 2.5x BB to 250, which pressures blinds who may fold wide OOP. My stack deep at 120BB, low risk for high reward if they fold. Limping or folding too passive; calling (limping) bad from button. Raise to build pot in position or take it down now.

A4o in the SB facing a button raise. This is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. I'll be OOP against the raiser postflop, and A4o has reverse implied odds when I hit an ace (could be dominated). Not strong enough to 3-bet for value, and calling OOP with this hand leads to tough spots. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am in the Big Blind holding 10 of Hearts and 9 of Diamonds. Elon (Button) raised to 250. With the Small Blind folding, the pot is 400 and it costs me 150 to call. T9o is a solid hand to defend the Big Blind against a late position open, as I am getting attractive pot odds (approx 2.6:1). While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the hand has decent playability on many boards. I will call to see a flop.

The board is 6-6-2 with two diamonds. I have T9o, which is essentially air right now. As the defender in the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading into the button on this board doesn't accomplish much, as I lack a strong hand or a significant draw. I will check and see how Elon reacts.

Heads-up on the flop in position after my button raise to 250, which Flash defended from the BB for a pot of 550. The board is 6h6d2d: paired sixes, low cards, with a weak diamond flush draw possible (two diamonds). My Qs8c is queen-high with no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw—pure air, but a solid bluff candidate in a spot where my continuation betting range includes strong hands like 66, 65s, 76s, A6s, sets, two-pair combos, and overpairs that check back less often. Flash's check suggests weakness; BB defense vs button raise is wide (suited connectors, Ax, broadway, pairs), but many miss this board, and he might fold better-than-queen hands fearing my range hits or to realize equity denial. Position allows me to c-bet wide for fold equity (~50-60% expected), taking down the pot immediately often, while charging draws. Deep stacks (118BB), so low risk. Standard sizing: ~50% pot (275) balances value and bluffs, keeps pot manageable if called. Checking folds best hands in range and lets Flash realize equity cheaply on turn; betting maximizes EV by denying equity and winning dead money. Prioritize folding out better hands now over pot control with showdown value.

On a board of 6h-6d-2d, I have Ten-high with a backdoor flush draw. Elon, the pre-flop aggressor from the button, has made a standard half-pot continuation bet. While I have two overcards, they are not strong enough to warrant a float out of position, especially since my Ten or Nine might not even be the best hand if they hit. Elon's range includes many overpairs and stronger high cards. Floating here would likely lead to a difficult decision on the turn without much equity. Therefore, I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

UTG in 6-max with T♣7♦ — an unimpressive, unsuited, semi-gapper with a weak kicker. In early position I’d be playing out of position against five opponents, including several who've shown aggression. Calling 100 into a 150 pot here is a marginal investment and opening or raising with this holding is unnecessarily loose. With a healthy stack I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot or a stronger hand in position. Fold.

I am in Middle Position (effectively Hijack in a 6-handed game) with QTs. The UTG player has folded. QTs is a strong suited broadway hand that is well within the standard opening range from this position in a 6-max setting. The stacks are deep (approx 95 BB), allowing for maneuverability post-flop. While there are aggressive players behind (Elon in CO has been 3-betting), playing too passively by folding this hand is suboptimal. I will raise to 2.5bb (250) to take initiative and attempt to steal the blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds.

Pro is a tight player based on history, rarely entering pots aggressively, making this their first raise. From cutoff with KJo offsuit, a strong hand with good position, I have excellent blockers and domination potential vs their likely strong but not premium range (e.g., QQ+, AK, AQs). Deep stacks allow postflop play. 3-betting takes initiative, isolates, and applies pressure; calling is fine but less optimal. Sizing to 800 (roughly 3.2x their raise) is standard for value/protection.
5h9d is a very weak hand facing a raise and 3-bet. This is a clear fold - no reason to invest 800 chips with such poor equity against this aggression.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Six offsuit. Middle Position (Pro) raised to 250, and the Cutoff (Elon) 3-bet to 800. A6o is far too weak to cold-call or 4-bet against two potentially strong ranges while out of position. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

We’re in the BB facing a raise and a 3-bet (Pro to 250, Elon to 800). With 2♦4♥ offsuit we have extremely poor playability, dominated equity, and will be out of position in a bloated pot versus a strong range. Calling 700 more is a clear -EV defend, and 4-bet bluffing with the very bottom of the deck is unnecessary and high-variance this deep. Best decision is to release and preserve stack.

I opened with QTs from Middle Position and faced a 3-bet to 800 from Elon in the Cutoff. While QTs is a strong suited broadway hand, playing it out of position against a 3-bet is difficult. Elon has been very active, 3-betting 3 times in the first 11 hands, suggesting a wide range. However, being out of position significantly reduces my ability to realize equity, and QTs is often dominated by hands in a 3-bet range (AQ, KQ, TT+). The pot odds are reasonable (requiring ~31% equity), but the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage make calling a marginally negative expectation play. Preserving my stack of ~92 BB is preferable to playing a bloated pot OOP with a speculative hand.

I am in UTG position with Jack-Eight offsuit (Jd 8s). This is a very weak hand to open from early position, as it is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop out of position against 5 other players. Given the stack depths and position, the standard play is to fold immediately.

Preflop in middle position with 6h8d offsuit, a weak hand with poor playability, low equity, and no strong postflop potential. Standard fold against a full table of 6 players. No reads from history suggest loosening up; opponents have been aggressive but this hand isn't playable. Preserve stack for better spots to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.

K3 suited in the cutoff with UTG and MP already folded. This is a decent spot to open-raise and try to steal the blinds. K-high suited has some playability with flush potential. Standard 2.5x raise to 250.

I am on the button with Ten-Nine offsuit. The Cutoff (Claude) has opened for 2.5x the big blind. While I have position, T9o is a marginal hand that does not play well against a standard opening range from the Cutoff, which typically includes stronger tens, nines, and high cards. Calling often invites a squeeze from the blinds or leaves me in a difficult spot post-flop with a dominated hand. Given the tournament stage and my stack size, I will opt for a disciplined fold and wait for a more profitable opportunity.

Claude is opening from the cutoff, which is typically a fairly wide range. Calling from the small blind with J9o is awkward (out of position, dominated often, and it invites the big blind to come along with great odds). I prefer a 3-bet here to leverage fold equity, isolate Claude, and take initiative; if Claude 4-bets, this is an easy fold.

Claude opened to 250 and FiveTwo 3‑bet to 900. I have K♦7♥ offsuit — a marginal, easily dominated hand with poor post‑flop playability out of the big blind. Calling 800 into a 1,250 pot requires ~39% equity to break even, which K7o rarely has vs a typical SB 3‑bet range (pockets, broadways, Axs, some suited broadways). 4‑betting would be reckless with this holding. With deep but finite stacks and better spots to apply pressure, I fold and conserve chips for a stronger situation.

K3s facing a 3-bet from SB. While I have some playability with flush potential, K3s is too weak to call a 3-bet. I'll be out of position postflop, often dominated when I hit my king, and the implied odds aren't good enough to chase the flush. FiveTwo's 3-bet indicates strength. Easy fold.

UTG with 6 active players in a deep-stack tournament (effective stacks ~100BB). Jd2c is one of the worst possible hands: offsuit, poor high card value, no connectivity for straights/flushes, zero blockers to strong hands. From early position, facing 5 players behind plus blinds, this hand has no business entering the pot. Limping or raising would be a massive mistake, inviting multiway pots where it has no equity. Standard tight UTG range folds this 100% of the time to preserve stack for premium spots. Opponents like FiveTwo show aggression, but irrelevant here—fold to maximize EV long-term.
KQo is a premium hand in middle position. With Elon already folded, I should open raise for value. Standard raise to 250 (2.5x BB) to build the pot while maintaining fold equity against weaker hands.

I am in the Cutoff with A4o. Claude, in Middle Position, has raised to 2.5bb. While I previously successfully 3-bet Claude with AJs in Hand #6, A4o is much weaker and does not perform well against an MP opening range. Calling would likely put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and 3-betting as a bluff is risky given there are still three players behind me. I will play it safe and fold this marginal hand.

We’re on the Button facing a 2.5x open from Claude. 83o has very poor equity and playability (dominated by most of his continuing range), and even though position is good, calling tends to bleed chips postflop. A 3-bet bluff would rely heavily on fold equity and becomes awkward when called due to our hand’s lack of connectivity/suit. Best EV is to simply release and wait for a better spot.

Facing a 2.5bb open from middle position with 7♦5♦ in the small blind. This is a weak, low suited connector and I'll be out of position versus the opener (and still have the big blind to act). Calling 200 into a 400 pot gives ~33% pot odds, but 7-5s has well under that equity vs a standard MP opening range, and the hand plays poorly multiway or OOP. A min 3-bet to 400 has little fold equity and a larger 3-bet is too committal with this holding. With a healthy ~85bb stack I'm better off preserving chips and waiting for a better spot. Fold.

I am in the Big Blind with QTo facing a standard 2.5x open (250) from Claude in Middle Position. I have already posted 100, so I need to call 150 to see a flop in a pot that will be 550 total. This gives me pot odds of roughly 27%. Although QTo is an off-suit broadway hand and I will be out of position, its raw equity against a standard MP opening range is typically 35-40%, well above the required price. Claude has shown activity (raising in Hands 6 and 12) but has folded to 3-bets, suggesting a reasonable opening range rather than a nut-peddling one. Therefore, defending the Big Blind by calling is the standard positive expected value play.

I have flopped Top Pair (Tens) with a strong kicker (Queen) on a relatively connected rainbow board (6c 7h Td). As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop raiser. This allows Claude to continue with his c-betting range, including bluffs and worse value hands. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out his air and isolate me against a stronger range. I plan to check-call if he bets, evaluating the turn.

I raised preflop with KQo and got called by Pro in the BB. The flop of 6-7-T missed me completely, giving me just two overcards. However, this is a standard c-bet spot - I have the preflop aggression and this board doesn't strongly favor the BB's range. A small c-bet around 1/3 pot should take this down often enough to be profitable, and I still have 6 outs to top pair if called.

I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (Queen) on a 6-7-T rainbow board. Facing a small continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 550 (approx. 36% pot) from the pre-flop raiser in Middle Position, I have an easy call. Folding is out of the question given my hand strength relative to his likely wide opening range. Raising would likely fold out his bluffs (like AK, AQ, KJ) which I want to keep in the pot, and isolate me against better hands like overpairs or sets. The small bet size often indicates a range bet or a desire to keep the pot controllable. By calling, I protect my checking range and give the opponent a chance to barrel again on the turn or check back, allowing me to realize my equity and potentially catch bluffs.

The turn brings the 3s, making the board 6c 7h Td 3s. This card is largely a blank, though it completes a straight for the unlikely holding of 4-5. I still hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (Queen). Being out of position with a medium-strong hand, leading out (donk betting) is suboptimal as it likely folds out the opponent's bluffs and isolates me against a stronger range (overpairs, sets, or strong Tx). My goal is to get to showdown cheaply or induce bluffs from missed overcards or draws like 8-9. Checking is the standard play to keep the opponent's range wide and give him the opportunity to fire a second barrel or check back for pot control.

Pro check-called my flop c-bet and checked again on the turn 3s, which is a complete blank. I still have just KQ high with no pair. Pro's line suggests a medium-strength hand like a ten, middle pair, or a draw like 89 for the straight draw. Firing a second barrel is risky - if Pro has any piece of this board they're likely calling again, and I'm just lighting chips on fire with no pair. The 3s doesn't help my bluffing range either. Better to check behind, maintain pot control, and see a free river that could give me top pair (K or Q).

I hold Top Pair (Tens) with a Queen kicker. The river brings a 5s, creating a board of 6c 7h Td 3s 5s. This places four cards to a straight on the board (3-5-6-7), meaning any 4 makes a straight. The opponent checked back the turn, which usually indicates a medium-strength hand (like a weak Ten, 99, 88) or a give-up with overcards. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands, as they will fold given the scary board texture, and better hands (straights, sets, better kickers) will call or raise. Checking is the standard line to get to showdown cheaply or induce a bluff, though bluffs are less likely on this 4-liner board. I intend to check and see a showdown.

River 5s completes some straights (34 makes 3-4-5-6-7). I have K-high with no pair. Pro has check-called the flop and checked turn/river. Their range likely includes weak tens, small pairs, or busted draws like 89. Betting here is pointless - worse hands fold, better hands call. I should check behind and show down my K-high, which will beat busted draws but lose to any pair.
Q6s in UTG is too weak to open. While suited, the hand lacks the strength needed for the earliest position. Standard UTG ranges don't include Q6s. Folding is the disciplined play here.

Holding Ten-Six offsuit in middle position is a very weak starting hand. With several players yet to act and no prior investment in the pot, there is no strategic reason to play this hand. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

Unopened pot in the cutoff, but 7♦5♥ offsuit is too weak to open-raise profitably against competent blinds and plays very poorly postflop when called. Limping is even worse (invites squeezes and creates tough multiway spots). Preserve stack and wait for a better steal candidate.

I have 9♦2♦ on the button — very weak but suited and in the best position at the table. With everyone folded to me and deep effective stacks (≈85–125 BB), stealing the blinds is profitable. I’ll open-raise to apply pressure and generate fold equity rather than limp-call and let the blinds see a cheap flop. I choose a standard 3x-ish sizing (300 total) to discourage light defenses and keep the pot manageable if called. Plan: if called I’ll play aggressively in position (c-bet favorable flops, check/float or proceed cautiously on highly connected/monster boards). If I face a substantial 3-bet I’ll fold this holding — it has poor equity and I don’t want to get tangled out of position against strong ranges.

I am in the Small Blind holding 8d 3s, which is a very weak, unsuited, and unconnected hand. The player on the Button (Minni) has raised to 300 (3bb). Despite the wide range a Button opener might have, 83o has very poor equity and poor playability, especially from out of position. Calling would be a losing play in the long run due to reverse implied odds and lack of equity realization. There is no strategic merit in 3-betting as a bluff with this specific holding given the low frequency of folds required to make it profitable and the high likelihood of being called or 4-bet. The standard and optimal play is to fold.

Js3d is a trash hand with no high card strength, connectivity, or suitedness. Facing a button raise to 300 (3x BB), the standard play from BB is to fold such a weak offsuit hand. Pot odds of calling 200 into 450 (~31%) require too much equity against a wide button range, and postflop playability is poor with reverse position. Better to preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the UTG position with 3d 9s, which is a very weak hand. In a 6-max game, opening with such a wide and weak range from early position is not profitable. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

We’re first in from middle position with T5 offsuit and no dead money beyond the blinds. This hand plays poorly postflop (dominated top pairs, weak kickers, limited straight/flush potential), and opening it will create too many tough spots versus the blinds and players behind. Limping is also -EV and invites isolation. Best decision is to conserve chips and fold.

Cutoff with K♦7♥ after two early folds — this is a marginal hand but a good stealing spot. I want to take the initiative, avoid limping into multiway pots, and apply pressure to the blinds and button. A standard open to 250 (≈2.5x BB) is the right sizing: it folds many weaker blind hands, isolates the button or gets heads-up in position, and doesn't give opponents great price to chase. If I face a sizable 3-bet (especially from Elon in the SB who has shown big 3-bets), I'll fold and preserve my deep stack; if called, I'll proceed cautiously postflop, c-betting favorable textures and folding to heavy resistance when I'm not connected. Thus: raise to 250.

I am on the Button with 8s5d, facing a raise to 250 from Minni in the Cutoff. 85 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity against an opening range, even from late position. Calling would be a losing play in the long run, and 3-betting as a bluff with this specific hand is unnecessary and low frequency. The standard and correct play is to fold.

T8h is a strong suited connector from the SB facing a CO open to 250 (2.5BB). Pot odds are excellent at 200 to call into 400 (pot will be 600 after call), requiring only ~25% equity, and T8s realizes well vs typical late-position opening ranges. Deep stacks allow for implied odds if we hit straights, flushes, or two-pair. Defending OOP with speculative hands is standard to deny equity and play post-flop. BB still to act, but folding such a playable hand is too tight early in the tournament with 150 hands to go. Calling keeps the pot multiway potentially and positions us to play aggressively if we improve.

32 suited is at the very bottom of playable hands. While I'm getting 4:1 odds (150 to win 600), this hand is extremely weak and will be very difficult to play profitably post-flop out of position against two opponents. Even with the suited aspect, the flush potential is minimal and I'll rarely make strong hands. Disciplined fold here.

Preflop call with T8hh was standard speculative defense from SB vs CO open, with good implied odds deepstacked. Flop 4c6cKs completely misses: no pair, no made flush draw (hearts are backdoor only), no strong straight draw (gutshot possibilities at best with 5/7/9, but unlikely). Board favors villain's CO raising range (Kx, QQ+, suited broadways, Ax) with top pair, overpairs, or flush draws. OOP first to act into dry-ish K-high board with two clubs; betting air is suicidal, check-calling risks too much into strong range without equity. Check-fold preserves stack early in tournament (hand 15/150), avoids difficult spots OOP, and realizes fold equity preflop was already denied by villain's range. Minni aggressive historically but this spot standard fold.

I opened preflop from the cutoff and got one caller in the small blind. Flop: K♠ 4♣ 6♣ — I have top pair with a weak 7 kicker. This is a good spot to continuation-bet for value and protection: I want to charge club flush draws and worse Kx/pairs while keeping the pot manageable against a deep, potentially aggressive small blind. A ~50% pot c-bet (~300 into 600) accomplishes that. Plan: if called, re-evaluate the turn — continue for value on safe cards; if a club or a scary overcard arrives and I face heavy action, proceed cautiously (check/protect or fold to large check-raises), and fold to a large polarized check-raise since my kicker is weak.

Flop completely misses T8hh: no pair, no flush draw (hearts backdoor only), gutshot straight draws at best (needing 5x for open-ender or runner-runner). Facing Minni's 300 bet (1/3 pot) into 900 after her CO raise preflop, her range is strong: Kx, QQ+, suited broadways, club flush draws. Pot odds ~4:1 (25% equity needed), but we have <20% vs that range (mostly overcards or draws for us). OOP check-fold preserves stack early (hand 15/150, deep stacks ~120BB). Minni aggressive historically (e.g., Hand 2 win, Hand 14 raise), but calling risks bloating pot without equity; better to avoid tough spots and wait for better spots to speculate.

A♠K♥ is a premium UTG hand 6-handed. Limping invites multiway pots and lowers our ability to realize equity; raising wins the blinds often and builds a pot when we’re ahead of calling ranges. Using the common sizing at this table (2.5x) keeps our range consistent and gives good leverage postflop with initiative.

Fold. UTG opening to 250 in a 6-handed game represents a relatively strong, tight range. 7♣6♥ offsuit is a marginal, dominated hand with poor playability out of position and low implied odds, and calling 250 into a 400 pot requires ≈38% equity to be break-even — far more than this hand has vs an UTG range. I’ll preserve my ~9k stack and wait for a better spot in position rather than spew chips in a likely multiway, OOP pot.

I hold Ks 4s in the Cutoff. The UTG player (FiveTwo) has opened for a standard raise of 250. An UTG opening range is typically strong and dominates a weak suited King like K4s. While the hand has flush potential, it suffers from reverse implied odds due to kicker issues and the likelihood of being behind stronger Kings or Aces. Additionally, there are three players left to act behind me who could squeeze. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

9s3s is a weak suited hand with poor playability, even from the button. Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo to 250 into a 400 pot, the hand lacks the equity, straight potential, or suited strength to defend profitably. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots. Deep stacks don't change this.

I have pocket tens in the SB facing a UTG open to 250. TT is a premium hand that plays well as a 3-bet. FiveTwo opened UTG which typically indicates strength, but TT is ahead of most opening ranges. 3-betting to around 800 builds the pot when I'm likely ahead and helps define villain's range. If they 4-bet, I can evaluate, but calling here plays the hand too passively.

I am holding AKo in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand. There has been an UTG open from FiveTwo to 250 and a 3-bet from Claude in the Small Blind to 800. This 3-bet from the Small Blind against an UTG opener usually represents a very strong range, but AKo is well ahead of or flipping against much of that range (like JJ, TT, or AQ). By putting in a 'cold 4-bet' from the Big Blind, I am asserting maximum strength and taking control of the pot. This move is likely to force a fold from FiveTwo and put Claude in a difficult position where he must either fold, call out of position, or shove. Given my stack size of over 90 big blinds, a 4-bet to 2100 is a standard sizing that allows me to define the hand immediately and potentially win a sizeable pot without seeing a flop.

We have AKo facing a cold 4-bet after opening UTG. Flat-calling invites a nasty multiway pot out of position with no initiative and makes it hard to realize our equity. The pot already has a lot of dead money (open + 3-bet + 4-bet + blinds), and both opponents’ continuing ranges will be strong, but AKo is still near the top of ours and performs well enough vs QQ+/AK-type ranges, especially with the dead money. Best play is to 5-bet to a size that effectively commits the shorter stacks (puts Flash all-in if he continues) and maximizes fold equity versus Claude’s non-premiums while avoiding awkward SPRs postflop.

Facing a 5-bet shove from UTG after my 3-bet and Flash's 4-bet. This is an extremely polarized spot - FiveTwo's UTG 5-bet shove range is typically AA, KK, QQ, and sometimes AK. Against this range, TT has roughly 35% equity. I need to call 7350 into a pot of ~18,400, requiring ~40% equity. I'm not getting the right price. Additionally, Flash is still behind and could wake up with a hand. While TT is a strong hand, it's a clear fold against this action - UTG 5-bet ranges are almost never light at these stack depths.

I am holding AKo in the Big Blind. I previously made a cold 4-bet to 2100 to assert strength. However, the UTG opener, FiveTwo, has responded with a 5-bet to 8150. This is an incredibly strong line, as FiveTwo is 5-betting into a cold 4-bet from the Big Blind, which represents one of the strongest ranges possible. Mathematically, I need approximately 35% equity to call the remaining 6050. Against a range of {QQ+, AK}, AKo only has about 25% equity. Even if I expand his range to include JJ, my equity only rises to 31%. Given the tight configuration of UTG vs. BB Cold 4-bet, it is highly likely FiveTwo has AA, KK, or is at best flipping with AK. Folding allows me to preserve a solid stack of 72.5 big blinds and wait for a better spot rather than risking my tournament life on a marginal call against an extremely polarized and strong range.

UTG in a 6-handed game with J♠2♣ — a very weak, offsuit hand with poor kicker and almost no playability. I’m first to act and with ~90BB I don’t want to limp-call out of position where I’ll be dominated or open-raise with a marginal holding that’s easy to 3-bet. Several opponents have shown aggression in prior hands, so preserving my stack for better spots is the correct, standard play here. Fold.

I hold Ace-King offsuit (AKo) in Middle Position, which is a premium starting hand. With the UTG player folding, I have the initiative to open the pot. The standard opening size in this game has frequently been 2.5x (250 chips), which is appropriate for the stack depths (approx. 97 BBs effective). Raising allows me to build the pot for value, thin the field, and take control of the hand. Folding or limping would be incorrect with such a strong holding.

9s7c is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, no high card strength, and minimal potential for straights or flushes. Facing a raise to 250 from Pro in middle position, where they have shown aggression in prior hands (e.g., raises in hands 11, etc.), this is an easy fold from the cutoff. Pot odds are poor (calling 250 into ~400 pot), and with deep stacks (124BB effective), no need to speculate with trash early in the tournament. Preserve stack for better spots.
53 offsuit is a junk hand. Even with position on the button, this is way too weak to defend against a middle position raise. Easy fold.

I am holding Queen-Five offsuit in the small blind. Pro has raised to 250 from middle position. Q5o is a very weak hand that is easily dominated by a standard opening range. Being in the small blind, I would have to play the rest of the hand out of position if I called. Given the tournament stage and my current stack, there is no reason to defend with such a marginal hand. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my chips for a more favorable situation.

Pro opens MP to 2.5x and we’re in the BB with Q3o. Even with decent pot odds, this is a dominated, low-playability offsuit queen that realizes equity poorly out of position (often making second-best top pair vs better Qx). It’s not a good candidate for a 3-bet bluff either versus an MP range. Preserve stack and fold.

I am in UTG position with King-Ten offsuit (Kd Th). In a 6-max game, KTo is generally considered too weak to open from early position due to the risk of domination by hands like KQ, KJ, AK, and AT, and the disadvantage of playing out of position post-flop. Furthermore, active players behind me (Flash, Elon) increase the likelihood of facing a 3-bet, which would force a fold. Therefore, the prudent play is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand or better position.

Preflop in middle position with 2c4d, one of the worst possible hands with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card value. No steals viable here with multiple players behind including blinds who defend aggressively based on history (FiveTwo often raises, Minni calls). Pot odds to call 100 into 150 are poor for speculative trash, risking stack unnecessarily in early tournament phase. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots and maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.
QJd in cutoff is a strong hand with two folds in front. Suited broadway cards play well in position. Standard open to 250 (2.5x) to build the pot and isolate.

I am on the button with Jack of Spades and Four of Spades. Claude has raised to 2.5x from the cutoff. While J4s is suited, it is a very weak hand to defend against a raise, even with position. The blinds are still to act, and FiveTwo has shown significant aggression in previous hands. Folding is the most prudent play here to avoid a marginal situation with a dominated hand.

Pocket 55 is too strong to fold getting 2:1, and with Claude ~79bb effective we have good implied odds to set-mine. A small blind 3-bet here would often just build a big pot OOP with a hand that doesn’t realize well when called/4-bet. I’ll defend by calling and play postflop mainly for sets/straight possibilities on favorable textures.

I have 6♦2♦ in the big blind facing a cutoff raise to 250 and a small‑blind caller. This is a very weak hand but it’s suited and I get excellent pot odds to complete: I need to put in 150 more into a 600 pot (pot becomes 750), so I only need ~16.7% equity to break even. With two opponents and deep stacks (I have ~89bb) this is a reasonable set‑mining / speculative call — I can realize implied odds if I hit a pair or flush. I won’t 3‑bet: the hand has poor blockers and low postflop playability multiway, so a raise would be too spewy and I’d be out of position vs the raiser. Plan: call to see the flop; fold to a substantial 3‑bet preflop; postflop, proceed only with real equity (pair/straight/flush draw) and be prepared to fold to heavy pressure when missed.

Multiway flop on Ks-Ts-3d is very favorable for the preflop raiser’s range and for BB’s defending range (Kx/Tx/spade draws). With 55 we have a weak underpair with poor realization OOP and no backdoor equity worth building a pot for. Donk-betting mostly gets called by better or forced to continue vs raises, so the best line is to check and proceed cautiously versus any substantial bet.

I have 6♦2♦ in a 3‑way pot on K♠‑3♦‑T♠. I missed — no pair, only a backdoor diamond draw that needs running diamonds and at best ~24% to pair by the river plus only ~5% to make a backdoor flush (combined equity roughly mid‑20s). This is a multiway pot against the preflop raiser and a caller, so bluff equity is poor and I have no meaningful blockers. Best play is pot control and to preserve stack: check and see the turn. If I face a significant bet from the raiser I’ll fold (I won’t bluff‑raise or barrel without real equity); only continue if I improve or the bet is ridiculously small relative to the pot.

I have QJd on Ks3dTs board - giving me an open-ended straight draw (any A or 9 completes). Both opponents checked to me, showing weakness. This is a prime c-bet spot - I have position, the betting lead, and good equity with my OESD plus backdoor diamond flush draw. Betting around 50% pot to apply pressure and build the pot with my draw.

Claude’s 1/3-pot c-bet into two players is usually strong-ish (Kx/Tx, spade draws), and 55 has very poor immediate equity. However the sizing is small and we’re getting good direct odds, plus strong implied odds versus Kx/Tx when we spike a set (effective vs Claude ~7.1k behind). I’ll continue for one card with a clear plan: fold most turns when we don’t improve, and play fast for value if we hit a 5.

I have 6♦2♦ on K♠-3♦-T♠ — no pair, only a backdoor diamond possibility and about a mid‑20s% chance to pair by the river. Claude’s flop raise is small relative to the pot (375 into 1500) so my break‑even equity to call is only ~20% (375/(1500+375)). With my ~24%+ combined equity, a call is +EV to realize equity multiway. Raising would be a thin, ill‑advised bluff (bad blockers, multiway) and folding would be too tight given the price. Plan: call to see the turn, but fold to meaningful turn pressure if I still haven’t improved; only continue aggressively if I make a pair or pick up a real draw (diamond on the turn, two‑pair, etc.).

Turn A♥ is a very bad card for 55: it improves Claude’s c-betting range (Ax, AK, ATs, AsX) and puts extra top-pair combos into both opponents’ ranges. Multiway and out of position, we have essentially no credible value-bet and a low-equity bluff (we don’t block spades or strong Ax/Kx). Best is to keep the pot small, realize our slim showdown/set-improvement equity, and be ready to fold versus meaningful pressure.

Check. I hold 6♦2♦ on K♠-3♦-T♠-A♥ and missed — the turn A is not a diamond so my backdoor flush is gone and I have no pair. I only have 6 outs (three 6s + three 2s) ≈ 6/46 ≈ 13% to improve on the river. This is a multiway pot versus the preflop raiser who showed flop aggression; bluffing or barreling here is low EV and I have poor blockers. With pot = 1875 and my stack = 8400, the correct line is pot control: check and see the river, then fold to any meaningful bet unless I pair or am offered a very cheap price to call.

The Ace on the turn completed my nut straight (A-K-Q-J-T). I have the absolute nuts here. Both opponents checked to me again, likely holding Kx, Tx, or flush draws. I need to bet for value and build the pot. With the flush draw still out there, I want to charge appropriately. Betting around 65% pot (~1200) extracts value from worse hands and sets up a river shove.
