Turn A♥ heavily favors Claude’s betting range (Ax/AK/AT, plus strong Kx/Tx and spade draws) and crushes our 55. We’re facing 1200 into 3075 multiway, so we’d need ~28% equity to call, but we essentially have only ~4% to spike a 5 on the river and almost no realistic showdown value otherwise. Even with implied odds, Claude’s remaining stack can’t compensate for how rarely we improve (and sometimes a river 5 still isn’t fully clean vs straights/flushes). Best decision is to fold and preserve stack.

Fold. I hold 6♦2♦ on K♠-3♦-T♠-A♥: no pair and no real draw (backdoor diamond gone). I only have six outs (three 6s + three 2s) ≈ 13% to improve on the river. Claude’s line (preflop open, flop bet/raise to 375, and a sizable turn bet to 1200) represents a strong value-heavy range (Kx/Tx/Ax, two-pair, sets) and gives me poor equity. Pot math: the pot is 3075 and I must call 1200 — I’d need roughly 28% equity to break even on that call (1200 / (3075+1200) ≈ 28%), well above my ~13% chance to hit. I’m out of position, have weak blockers, and a raise would be an overly thin bluff. Folding preserves chips and follows my earlier plan to fold to meaningful turn pressure when unimproved.
67o is a complete trash hand from UTG in a 6-handed game with deep stacks. No position, low playability, no showdown value. Folding preserves stack for premium hands. Limp or raise would be spew.
T7o is a weak hand in middle position with 4 players still to act behind me. This hand plays poorly multiway and I'm likely to be dominated or have kicker issues. Standard fold.

I am in the Cutoff position with 8s 4h, which is an extremely weak hand. Two players before me have folded. While I could attempt a bluff, there are still three players to act behind me (Button, SB, BB), making a steal attempt with such poor cards unprofitable. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Button folds to me and I have a premium pocket pair (JJ). I want to build the pot, deny equity to the blinds’ overcards, and still keep sizing efficient at ~2.5x since stacks are deep enough. Raising also isolates the blinds and lets me represent strong ranges on many flops.

I have A♦7♦ in the small blind facing a button open to 250 (2.5BB). This is a wide, steal-heavy raise and A7s has good playability and blocker value (blocks some strong Axs). With ~83BB effective I prefer to defend by calling rather than 3-betting out of position with a marginal suited ace — a small 3-bet here would often get called and put me in difficult OOP spots. Calling 200 more (250 total) gives reasonable pot odds and lets me realize equity, pursue nut-flush/ace improvements, or pot-control on dry boards. Plan: call to see the flop and re-evaluate — continue on favorable A or diamond flops, check/control or fold to large aggression on very hostile textures.

I am in the Big Blind with 84o, facing a button raise to 2.5bb and a call from the Small Blind. The pot odds are attractive (calling 150 into 600, or 20% equity required), but 84o is one of the weakest hands in the deck. Playing this hand multi-way from out of position is difficult; my equity realization will be poor due to the positional disadvantage and lack of playability. It is better to preserve chips than to defend with such a weak holding.

I have A♦7♦ — top pair but a very vulnerable one on a monotone heart flop (A♥ 3♥ 6♥). The button was a wide open-raiser, so his range contains many one- or two-heart combos and also A‑x hands with better kickers. Out of position with a weak kicker and no flush/backdoor prospects, I want pot control and information. I’ll check to see how the opener reacts: call modest continuation bets to deny equity to single‑heart hands and extract value from worse A‑x/pocket pairs, but fold to large polarizing aggression that strongly represents a made flush or a better Ace. If he checks back I’ll reassess the turn and look to value-bet safe runouts or pot-control on dangerous cards.

Monotone A-high flop is very good for the caller’s continuing range (A-x, made flushes, lots of single-heart hands), and our JJ is currently an underpair. While Jh gives us strong equity (flush draw) and decent showdown value, betting tends to fold out worse no-heart hands and get action mainly from better hands, plus it exposes us to an uncomfortable check-raise. In position, the highest-EV line is to take the free card, realize our equity to a heart/J, and keep the pot controlled while also inducing bluffs on later streets.

Preflop I called A♦7♦ to defend against a wide button open and then checked the vulnerable, monotone-heart flop for pot control. The button checked the flop and again on the turn (3♦ pairs the board), which narrows his range toward missed hands, weak A‑x and small pocket pairs and away from many completed heart flushes. I still have top pair but a mediocre kicker and I'm OOP, so I want to extract value from worse A‑x and medium pairs while keeping the pot manageable. I will make a small-to-medium value bet (≈1/2 pot) to get called by worse aces and pairs, charge any one‑heart/partial draws, and retain the freedom to fold to a strong raise that would represent a flush or trips. If he raises I will re-evaluate (likely fold to a large raise).

SB’s 300 lead into 900 on the paired turn looks like a blocker/protection bet with a lot of one-heart hands, some Ax, and occasional traps. With JhJs we have a strong draw (9 heart outs) plus position and good immediate price (300 to win 1200 = 25%), and raising would bloat the pot while drawing to a non-nut flush and risk getting jammed by better made hands/flushes. Calling keeps bluffs and worse hands in, realizes equity, and preserves the option to value-bet a heart river or apply pressure when checked to on bricks.

I have A♦7♦ — top pair with a weak kicker. The button checked back the monotone-heart flop and then called my small turn bet (300), which narrows his range toward weaker A-x, medium pairs and missed draws rather than many completed heart flushes. The J♣ on the river is a relatively safe card for my range (doesn't complete hearts and doesn't give many obvious two-pair combos). Pot is ~1200. Betting ~1/2 pot (600) is the best line to extract value from worse aces and medium pairs, charge any missed draws, and keep the pot controlled; the sizing is small enough to get called by thin hands but large enough to fold out pure air. If he reacts with a large check‑raise I will re-evaluate (likely fold to a polarized, large raise).

River J gives us jacks full of threes (very strong). Minni’s small river lead after the small turn lead looks like a blocker/value bet from a lot of Ax and especially made flushes from the monotone flop; those hands will often pay off a raise, while the few hands that beat us (33/rare slowplayed AA) are a small part of the range. So we should value-raise rather than just call, using a size that still gets calls from worse made hands.

FiveTwo opened wide preflop, checked the monotone-heart flop and then check‑called my small turn bet. I have A♦7♦ — top pair but a weak kicker and out of position. He then check‑raised my 600 river bet to 1,800. That line is polarized, but the raise size is relatively modest. Calling 1,200 more into a 3,600 pot gives me ~25% break‑even equity; my hand beats many thin A‑x hands, medium pairs and bluffs he might turn into a river raise, and my Ad removes some value A‑combos from his range. Raising would be reckless with a poor kicker and little fold equity; folding now would too often relinquish a likely winner for a small price. I will call to see showdown and extract value from worse A‑x and pairs.
Q7o is a weak hand from UTG position. This is an easy fold - not playable from early position with 5 players left to act behind me.

I am holding Ten-Deuce offsuit (T2o) in middle position. This is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em and should almost never be played, especially with several players left to act behind me. Given my current stack size and the position, folding is the only standard and correct play.

Unopened to me in the CO, but 8♠5♦ offsuit is a weak, poorly playable holding that gets dominated by the blinds’ defending ranges and doesn’t realize equity well postflop (especially facing 3-bets or multi-street pressure). With comfortable stack depth, I don’t need to force a marginal steal here—wait for higher-EV opens.

I’m on the button with T6o — a marginal hand but very playable in position and a good candidate to open-steal against just the blinds. Calling 100 gives the blinds a free flop with good pot odds and loses fold equity; folding is too tight. I’ll open-raise to a standard 2.5x BB (250) to apply pressure, isolate the blinds, and keep the pot a manageable size relative to my ~60BB stack. Plan: if called, play aggressively in position and c-bet on favorable/dry flops; if faced with a sizable 3-bet, re-evaluate and mostly fold unless the sizing or opponent reads justify a call/4-bet.

I hold 2c 7d (7-2 offsuit), which is the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise to 250 from the Button (Minni), I am out of position in the Small Blind. The pot odds do not justify calling with such a weak hand, and a 3-bet bluff is ill-advised given the hand quality and stack depths. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 250 after folds. Pot is 400, need to call 150 more (total 250 committed). 6s7c is a suited connector with excellent playability, straight and flush potential. Excellent implied odds given Minni's 5800 stack (58bb) and my deep 123bb stack. Defending wide vs BTN steal range is standard; this hand flops well and has good equity vs her wide opening range. Calling OOP is better than folding (too tight) or 3-betting (not premium enough OOP). Will play postflop cautiously.

Flop of Jh Js Th is a paired board with flush draw potential (two hearts). My 6s7c completely misses: no pair, no straight draw (gutshot or open-ender), no flush draw. Equity is very low against Minni's button opening range, which includes many overpairs (QQ+, AK), Ax suited, broadway, and some suited connectors that connect better. Preflop call was standard for playability and implied odds, but this flop is a complete whiff. OOP first to act, pot 550, deep stacks. Checking is mandatory; will fold to any bet as there's no fold equity, no semi-bluff, and continuing would be spew. Minni can have value (JJ, TT, QT, JTs, etc.) or draws, but we realize no equity.

Flop: J♥ J♠ T♥; my hand T♦6♥ = second pair (pair of tens) with a weak kicker. I was the preflop aggressor on the button and the Big Blind called — his defending range is wide and contains lots of Jx, many Tx, pocket pairs and some two‑pair/trip combos that connect very well with this flop. A standard c-bet here would frequently get called by better hands (Jx, JT, TT) and only fold out pure air; I don’t want to build a pot where I’m often behind. 

Given position and stack depth I want to control the pot and see a turn card. Plan: check and evaluate — if Villain bets a small/standard c-bet I’ll usually call to keep weaker holdings and bluffs in his range and re-evaluate on the turn; if he jams or overbets big I’ll fold since I’ll be behind a large portion of his flatting range. If he checks back, I keep my showdown equity and can extract or bluff later on safe turn cards. Betting here is leverage-poor and unnecessarily risky, so I’ll check to pot-control and gather more information.

Turn 7s pairs my 7c, giving me middle pair with a weak 6 kicker on a paired JJ board with Th and now 7s. Board: Jh Js Th 7s. OOP first to act, pot 550. Preflop Minni BTN open wide, both checked flop (dry paired board that misses much of her range). Her check-back suggests weakness (missed overcards, weak draws, air) or trap (sets/two-pair), but unlikely premium given check OOP after my check. Middle pair has showdown value vs her polarized range but vulnerable to Jx, Tx, better 7x, 89 straights (now made?), overpairs. Betting thin value risks check-raises from better or folding out bluffs; no protection needed really. Checking OOP for pot control is best: induces bluffs from missed draws (hearts/spades now with 7s), lets weak Ax/Tx value-bet light which I can call profitably, cheap river OOP. Deep stacks, no need to bloat pot with marginal made hand. Will call small bets, fold to large.

I have T♦6♥ on J♥ J♠ T♥ 7♠ — second pair with a weak kicker. The BB defended a wide range to my button open, which includes lots of Jx (trips), JT/TT, and other pocket pairs that beat me, plus some weaker tens and bluffs. Betting the turn would bloat a pot where I’m often behind and would mostly get called by better hands or fold only pure air, so it’s poor value and exposes me to raises. With ~58BB effective and being in position, I want to control the pot and realize my equity. I’ll check to keep worse hands and bluffs in his range and force him to act; on the river I’ll re-evaluate — call small/medium bets for the pot odds, consider a small thin-value bet on safe rivers if he checks, and fold to large overbets that polarize strongly to Jx/trips. Action: check.

River Ad is a scary card that improves Ax hands (now top pair good kicker), overcards, and possibly completes some straights or improves broadways, but primarily hits strong value in Minni's button range (AJ, AQo+, etc.). My 7c6s is still just middle pair weak kicker (7s pairs board's 7s), no improvement. Board: Jh Js Th 7s Ad remains paired, draw-heavy pre-turn but now somewhat dry. Minni's double check-back (flop and turn) OOP caps her range significantly: unlikely monsters (JJ, sets) as they'd bet for value/protection; more likely missed overcards (KQ, AK without A), weak pairs (88-99), air, or trapped medium strength that slowed down. OOP first to act on river, pot 550, deep stacks (121bb effective). Betting thin value is disastrous: folds out worse (bluffs/air), gets called/raised by better (Jx, Tx, Ax), no protection needed for pair on blankish river. Fold equity low given her capping. Checking preserves pot control, induces bluffs from her weak range (Ax missed flush/straight draws, overcards), allows cheap showdown vs small value-bets (call profitably OOP if <1/3 pot as she can barrel light), folds to big bets where called equity crushed. Postflop plan intact from turn: pot control with marginal SDV, exploit capped range. Check/call small, check/fold big.

Check. My hand (T♦6♥) makes two pair on the river (Jacks and Tens) — a strong showdown hand but vulnerable to any Jx, pocket tens/full houses and the occasional slow‑played strong hand. Villain defended wide preflop and has been very passive (check–check through the streets), so checking keeps worse hands and missed draws in and avoids bloating the pot where I’m occasionally behind. Betting would fold out a lot of the hands I beat and risks a check‑raise from the narrow range that crushes me, so I’ll pot‑control and take it to showdown. If faced with a small/medium bet I’ll usually call for value; I’ll fold to a large polarizing overbet.

I am in the Under The Gun (UTG) position with 4c 3h, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Playing such a hand from early position is mathematically incorrect and would lead to significant chip loss in the long run. Folding is the only viable strategic move here.

3c6d offsuit is far below an MP opening range in 6-max: it’s dominated when it makes a pair, has poor playability, and doesn’t realize equity well multiway with five players left to act (including the blinds). With no dead money beyond the blinds and no strong positional advantage, the EV of limping or opening is negative. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

J♥6♣ is a weak, offsuit jack with a poor kicker and very limited postflop playability. UTG and Middle have folded, but there are still three players to act behind me (Button, SB, BB). Limping (calling 100) would put me in multiway pots out of position with a hand that is often dominated; open‑raising risks being squeezed or facing calls from better hands. I have a healthy ~63 BB stack, so there is no need to gamble with a marginal hand. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot — fold here.

I am on the Button with Jd 5d. The action has folded to me. My stack is 97 BBs. While the Button is a prime position to steal the blinds, J5s is on the lower end of playability, usually falling just outside standard opening ranges (typically J6s+ or J7s+). Furthermore, the players in the blinds, particularly Elon in the SB, have shown aggression (3-betting and raising frequently), which makes opening marginal hands less profitable as I may face re-raises or floating. With a weak suited hand that can be easily dominated, folding is the disciplined and standard play here.

Preflop in SB with folds around, facing only BB. 67o is a speculative hand with decent playability and straight potential. SB steal raises are standard here to win the 150 pot immediately ~70% of the time. Raising to 250 (2.5x BB) puts pressure on BB while keeping pot small if called, allowing postflop maneuverability with position advantage heads-up. Folding too tight early in tournament; limping invites multiway pots unnecessarily. Minraise avoided to show strength.

JQo in the BB facing a min-raise from SB. Getting 2.3:1 pot odds (150 to win 350). JQo is a solid hand that plays well postflop. Calling is standard here - 3-betting is an option but calling keeps the pot manageable with a hand that has good playability but isn't premium.

Preflop raise from SB with 67o was a standard steal attempt, and BB Claude's call with a wide range includes many Ax, broadway, suited hands that often miss this Qs-Qh-4h flop entirely or have weak pairs/draws. Board is paired top, dampening draws somewhat but creating a scary texture for villain's floats and weak calls. I have position postflop (act first but initiative), backdoor straight/flush potential, and strong range rep as preflop raiser (Qx, overpairs, sets). Continuation betting ~60% pot (300 into 500) folds out his equity-poor hands like KJo+, AJo, low suited connectors while keeping bet affordable relative to stacks (119bb deep). If called, further reads on villain; easy fold to raise. Checking invites free cards or check-raises, less optimal for building stack early tournament.

I flopped trip Queens with a Jack kicker - an excellent hand. Elon bet 300 into 500. With such a strong hand, I want to extract maximum value. Calling here keeps his bluffs and weaker hands in play, disguises my strength, and allows him to continue betting on later streets. The heart draw on board gives him reason to keep firing. Slow-playing makes sense here.

Turn 7h pairs my 7s, giving me two pair Queens and Sevens (QQ with board QsQh, 77 with 7h and my 7s), plus backdoor 6h for additional equity. Strong made hand heads-up. Villain Claude (BB caller preflop, flop caller) has wide range: weak Qx (KJ-QT), busted draws, Ax, suited connectors, hearts like AhX, 5h3h for gutshots/flush draws. Flop call on paired QQ4h suggests not super strong (no sets likely), but turn completes obvious heart flush draws and adds straight possibilities (e.g., 85). I have initiative as preflop raiser/c-bettor, acting first (SB OOP). Checking risks giving free cards to draws or check-raise; value betting extracts from worse pairs (QJ-QT, 7x, 44, AQo+), floats, and weaker draws while charging them off. Pot 1100 deep stacks (~100bb), bet 2/3 pot (700) builds pot, denies equity, sizes well for river shoves/calls if he has flush. Avoids overbetting into possible monsters (unlikely Q7s, rare sets). Maximizes EV by getting value early in tournament.

I have trip Queens with Jack kicker - a monster hand. The turn brought a third heart (7h), creating a potential flush draw on board. Elon bets 700 into 1100. With the flush draw present, I should raise to charge draws and build the pot with my strong hand. If Elon has a queen, he's calling anyway. If he's on a draw, I want to make him pay. Raising to around 1800 gives good value while keeping worse hands in.

Facing a turn raise to 1800 after my value bet of 700 with top two pair (QQ77) plus a heart flush draw (6h with three board hearts). Pot is 3600, calling 1100 into ~4700 offers excellent pot odds (~4:1). Villain Claude defended wide preflop from BB and called flop c-bet on paired QQ4h board, suggesting a broad range including weak Qx, pairs, Ax, suited connectors, and heart draws. Turn 7h completes obvious flushes but also improves my hand significantly. His raise polarizes to made flushes (e.g., AhXh, KhXh), full houses (77 for Qs full, rare Q7, 44 for full unlikely), or value with improved Qx/7x, plus possible bluffs (busted draws turning into semi-bluffs). My hand crushes his weaker value range (QT-QJ, 7x, 44) and has 9+ outs vs pure flush (3 remaining 7s, 2 Qs for boat, 4s, plus runner-runner straights/flushes). Folding top two + FD is too tight early in tournament (hand 21/150); raising risks folding out worse hands or facing all-in from nuts, while stacks remain deep (my 109bb, his ~70bb). Calling keeps pot controlled OOP, allows river control (check/call or bet if bricks), and realizes equity. Maximizes EV by balancing aggression with pot odds in deep-stack play.

River 3s is a brick that doesn't improve my two pair (QQ77 with 6h flush draw) or complete many new draws. Pot 4700, acting first OOP after villain's turn raise to 1800 (which I called 1100). His range for turn raise on heart-completing board is polarized: nuts (flushes with 3 board hearts, Qx trips from flop, sets like 44/77 for fulls/trips, rare Q7/QQ fulls/quads) ~70%, or bluffs/semi-bluffs (busted heart draws, turn floats without improvement). His river check balances: traps with nuts, gives up with air, pot control with marginal. My hand is in the middle: crushes air/bluffs but loses to all nuts (even Qx trips beats two pair), ties some weaker 7x two pairs. Betting (e.g., 2k-3k) gets no value from air (they fold/check behind), while nuts call/raise, turning ~EV-neutral to negative EV (-bet size vs nuts portion). Checking realizes full showdown value: beats air 100%, loses same as betting but without risking extra bet that nuts snap. Deep stacks (98bb/70bb), early tournament (hand 21/150), no need to thin value thin; check maximizes EV by avoiding bloating pot OOP vs polarized range and villain's history of balanced aggression (e.g., Hand 18 turn bet but folds to resistance). Preflop steal worked, postflop initiative taken but villain's raise correctly called thin; check closes sensibly.

I have trip Queens with Jack kicker - a very strong hand. Elon checked the river after calling my turn raise. The board has no flush (3 hearts but river was a spade). I need to value bet here - Elon likely has a Queen with worse kicker, a pocket pair, or possibly a flush that got there. Betting around 2/3 pot (~3000) extracts value from worse hands while not overcommitting if he somehow has me beat with a better kicker or full house.

Facing a river bet of 3000 into 7700 (pot odds ~3.57:1, requiring ~22% equity). With QQ77 two pair (Queens and Sevens), this hand crushes Claude's bluffing range after his turn raise and river bet following my check: missed flush draws (e.g., AhKh, lower hearts without pair), busted straight draws (85, 56), and polarized floats without showdown value. Vulnerable to nuts like flushes (two hearts), Q7 full house, 44 set of Fours (full effectively vs my two pair), or QT trips, but Claude's wide BB defend preflop, flop call on paired QQ4 board, and turn raise suggest a mix rather than pure monsters (sets/QQ unlikely to flat flop cb; he'd raise flop). History shows Claude aggressive but balanced (e.g., Hand 18 value bet turn, Hand 4 big pot won). His 4050 stack commits most (~75%) with the bet, indicating polarization; bluffs ~30-40% of range sufficient for +EV call. Folding too tight early (hand 21/150, stacks deep ~100bb/40bb); raising to 6000 commits ~60% stack, risks folding worse while facing shove from nuts. Calling realizes full equity vs bluffs, controls pot OOP, maximizes stack growth in tournament.

9♥4♦ offsuit is far too weak to open from UTG in a 6-handed lineup. Limping invites multiway action with a dominated, low-connectivity hand and poor implied odds, while raising burns chips without credible board coverage. Best EV is to preserve stack and fold.

I have A♠ K♥ (offsuit) in middle position with ~63.5bb. UTG folded and there is only the big blind (100) to call, so this is a standard open-raise spot. AKo is a top-tier opening hand — I want to build the pot, gain fold equity, and thin the field to play heads-up postflop. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the big blind): big enough to price out many speculative hands but not so large as to overcommit. This leaves me ~6050 behind to navigate postflop. Postflop plan: if called I’ll continuation-bet on ace/king or dry boards for value and play cautiously on coordinated boards; if I face a 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate by villain and sizing (often 4-bet for value/isolation vs call to realize equity), and only fold to shove sizes that make a call clearly incorrect.

I am in the Cutoff with Q9o. The player in Middle Position (Minni) has opened to 300 (3bb). Q9o is a weak offsuit hand that is easily dominated by an MP opening range (which typically includes AQ, KQ, QJ, 99+). Calling would be speculative and likely -EV due to reverse implied odds and the risk of being squeezed by the Button or Blinds. 3-betting is not attractive as Q9o lacks good blockers or post-flop playability. The standard and disciplined play is to fold.

J9o is a strong button hand with good playability and position against a middle position open from Minni. Minni has shown to be an active raiser but folded to previous 3-bets from this position (e.g., Hands 3 and 9). A 3-bet to 900 (3x her raise) applies fold equity, polarizes our range, and builds the pot with initiative while maintaining good SPR post-flop. Calling is viable for implied odds, but 3-betting maximizes value and fold equity given history and position. Folding is too tight with 68BB stacks.

Ac8c is a playable suited ace facing a 3-bet. Elon has been aggressive with 3-bets throughout this session. While I'm out of position, the suited ace gives me good implied odds - I can flop a flush draw, top pair, or backdoor equity. Getting about 1.6:1 on a call, and with stack depth allowing for post-flop play, calling is reasonable here. Folding would be too tight with this hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 8-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Minni opened, Elon 3-bet from the button, and Claude cold-called from the Small Blind. The ranges of my opponents are likely very strong, particularly the 3-better and the cold-caller. Despite getting decent pot odds, my hand has virtually no equity or playability in a multi-way 3-bet pot out of position. I will fold.

I opened to 300 with A♠K♥ and Elon (BTN) 3‑bet to 900; Claude (SB) cold‑called, Flash folded. Pot is 2,200 and it’s 600 more to call (900 total). With a cold caller present a small 4‑bet to 1,500 is unlikely to isolate and just bloats a multiway pot OOP where AKo’s equity is diminished. I prefer to call and see a flop: AKo plays well postflop for pairs and has good showdown equity; I can c‑bet for value on A/K or dry boards and play cautiously on coordinated/flushy boards, folding to heavy action if I miss. Calling keeps pot control and preserves stack depth for better spots.

Missed flop completely - I have Ac8c on a 2d Ks Td board. No pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), and facing two opponents including a 3-bettor. This is a clear check and evaluate spot. I have ace high with minimal equity and no reason to put chips in the pot.

I have top pair (K) with the best kicker (A). This is a very strong hand on K♠ T♦ 2♦, but the board has two diamonds so there are backdoor/flush draws to charge and protect against, and I'm multiway versus a preflop 3‑bettor (Elon) and a cold‑caller (Claude). Checking gives free cards to diamond draws and turn cards that can make two pair/straights; betting extracts value from worse Kx, Tx, pocket pairs and charges draws. I’ll make a standard value/protection bet of ~50% pot — large enough to charge draws and get called by worse, but small enough to keep weaker made hands and draws in the pot so I can continue. If I face a small/medium raise I’ll call and reevaluate on the turn; if I face a large overbet/shove from the 3‑bettor I will be willing to fold (AK one pair is often behind that line).

Flop of K♠10♦2♦ hits my 9♦J♥ perfectly for the nut flush draw (9 outs) plus gutshot straight draw to Q (3 additional clean outs, total ~12 outs or ~48% equity heads-up by river). Strong semi-bluff spot with position on button in 4200 pot facing Minni's 1400 donk bet (after calling my preflop 3-bet OOP). Minni polarized: likely Kx/set or draw/bluff; her 4050 stack vulnerable. Claude's SB check weak, unlikely to continue strongly. Raising polarizes my 3-bet range, applies max fold equity vs Minni, builds pot OOP for nutted hands, denies equity to weaker draws. Call gets too many feet OOP multiway; fold discards too much equity/playability. Size to ~3x (4200 total) pressures Minni to fold marginal made hands or worse draws while leaving SPR low.

I have Ac8c on 2d Ks Td - complete air with no pair and no flush draw (wrong suit). Facing a 1400 bet from Minni and a raise to 4200 from Elon who only has 1700 behind (essentially all-in). I have zero equity here - just ace high against two opponents showing serious strength. Easy fold.

I have top pair (K) with the best kicker (A♠K♥) on K♠ T♦ 2♦. I bet 1,400 to charge draws and get value, and the preflop 3‑bettor (Elon) raised big to 4,200. Facing 2,800 to call into a pot of 8,400 gives me ~25% break‑even equity — AK top pair has better than that against a sensible raise range that will include some Kx, some sets/TT/22, and some bluffs/semi‑bluffs. I don’t want to fold a very strong one‑pair hand without further information, but I also don’t want to overcommit to a polarized raise by shoving now. Calling preserves about 1,250 behind for future decisions, keeps worse Kx and some T‑x hands in the pot, and lets me re‑evaluate on the turn (fold to an over‑the‑top shove on a scary card, value‑bet or check back on safe cards). Therefore I will call the 2,800 to see the turn and reassess.

I have top pair with the best kicker (A♠ K♥) on K♠ T♦ 2♦ 3♦. Elon (preflop 3‑bettor) committed a large raise on the flop (4,200) and still has only ~1,700 behind — his line is polarized toward sets/strong Kx and some bluffs/semi‑bluffs. My remaining stack is only 1,250 vs a pot of 11,200: any bet I make is effectively an all‑in but tiny relative to the pot (<12% pot) and will get called by most hands that beat me while not folding many of the worse hands I want value from. Betting here has almost no fold equity and is a poor use of chips; checking preserves my options and lets Elon reveal more information. Plan: check to control the pot size and see whether he barrels or jams; if he checks back I take a free run at the river for value/protection, if he shoves I will call only when his line/size indicates significant bluff frequency or when pot odds make a call justified — otherwise I fold to a range‑polarized shove (sets/two‑pair/flush). Given the board texture (third diamond on turn) and my shallow stack, the safest +EV line is to check now and reevaluate to any turn bet/shove.

Turn 3♦ completes the diamond flush draw for hands containing a single diamond, making the board very draw-heavy with three diamonds (2♦, 3♦, 10♦). My 9♦ J♥ holds a strong 10-high flush draw (9 flush outs remaining), plus a gutshot straight draw to Q (3-4 outs, some overlap with Q♦). Equity is approximately 45-50% against typical calling ranges (top pair, two-pair, weaker flushes, sets). Minni checks OOP after calling a large flop raise into a big pot, which polarizes her somewhat (strong made hand like Kx♦, KT, set, or flush; or weak/missed draw). With pot 11,200, her stack 1,250, my stack 1,700 (SPR ~0.15), this is a mandatory shove spot on the button. Shoving all-in for 1700 leverages massive fold equity against marginal made hands without a flush (e.g., KQ, KJ no ♦), value from weaker flushes/draws (e.g., low ♦ draws), and denies her free cards/equity realization. Her check may indicate caution with a vulnerable hand. Checking risks a low-equity showdown (J-high loses to most of her range) or allows river bluff/value betting by her; aggression maximizes EV given position, initiative, history of her folding to pressure (e.g., Hands 3,9,15), and tournament goal to build stack. Pot odds for her call are favorable (~8%), but fold equity compensates.

I have top pair (K) with the nut kicker (A♠K♥) on K♠ T♦ 2♦ 3♦. Elon (preflop 3-bettor) has taken a very aggressive, polarized line (3-bet preflop, huge flop raise, now shoves turn) — that range contains strong value (sets, two-pair/strong Kx, and any two-diamond nut-flush combos) but also some bluffs/semi-bluffs. The pot is 12,900 and I must call 1,250, so my break-even equity is only ~8.8% (1,250 / (12,900+1,250)). Although many value hands crush me, I still have meaningful equity versus a polarized shove (I beat worse Kx and all pure bluffs, and I block some premium combos), and the pot odds make a call +EV. With only 1,250 behind this is effectively an all‑in decision — folding forfeits a very large pot for zero equity. I will call the all‑in.

Pocket jacks UTG in a 6-handed game is a clear open-raise for value and to narrow the field. I want a standard sizing that builds the pot but doesn’t overcommit — 3x the big blind is appropriate given stack depths and table tendencies. This size gives fold equity vs weaker hands, keeps multiway spots manageable, and still allows me to comfortably call or 4-bet versus reasonable 3-bets from deep stacks. If Elon (very short) shoves after my raise I’ll be a heavy favorite and can call; if a big 3-bet comes from Claude/Pro I’ll reassess on sizing and opponent. Raising also defines my range and lets me take the initiative postflop. Raise to 300.

I am holding Jd 7c in Middle Position facing a 3BB raise from the UTG player (Minni). J7 offsuit is a statistically weak hand that falls well outside of a defensible calling or 3-betting range against an UTG open, even if the opener is somewhat loose. There are still four players left to act behind me, increasing the risk of a squeeze. Calling would likely lead to a difficult post-flop situation with a dominated hand and poor playability. The correct and standard play is to fold immediately.

Short stack with 450 chips (4.5BB). Ah8h is a strong suited ace hand for shoving from cutoff over an UTG raise. Offers good equity against Minni's calling range (blocker to AA/AK, suited for flush potential), plus significant fold equity given the raise size. Calling leaves me with only 150 chips (1.5BB) postflop, which is unplayable. Folding is too tight with this stack depth and hand strength in late position. Min raise is exactly 450, perfect for an all-in shove. Minni has been active but shoving applies pressure.

Pocket 5s on the button facing a min 3-bet to 450. Getting good pot odds (2:1) to set-mine with a small pair. Position is excellent, stack is deep enough for implied odds. Calling to see a flop and looking to hit a set.

I am holding 7s Th in the Small Blind. The action ahead of me involves an UTG raise from Minni, a small 3-bet from Elon, and a call from Claude on the button. Although the price to call is relatively cheap (400 chips into a 1350 chip pot), 10-7 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly multi-way and out of position. Given the strength shown by the UTG open and the subsequent action, folding is the most disciplined and correct play to preserve my stack.

We’re getting an excellent price: 350 to win 1350 (and we realize some equity even OOP), and the “3-bet” sizing to 450 is so small that ranges are likely wider than a normal 3-bet pot. K8o isn’t a hand to 4-bet/squeeze with here (too dominated when called and we’d be OOP in a bloated pot), but it’s strong enough to defend given the odds and dead money. Plan postflop is to play fit-or-foldish: check range, continue mainly with decent Kx/8x+draws, and avoid stacking off with one pair on coordinated boards versus multiway action.

I opened UTG with JJ (raised to 300), and Elon made a small 3-bet to 450 that Claude and the big blind have already called — this is now a multiway pot and I’ll be out of position. Jacks are strong enough to continue but I don’t want to bloat the pot by 4-betting multiway (I’d either isolate and face callers with a bloated pot OOP or price myself into uncomfortable spots vs bigger pairs/AK). The extra cost to continue is only 150 for good pot odds, so I’ll call the 3-bet, see a flop, and re-evaluate based on texture and opponents’ actions. Total commit on this street will be 450 (300 already, +150 more).

Multiway 3-bet pot with K8o on 9s-6d-3s gives us only K-high, no pair, no spade, and essentially no meaningful backdoor equity. Leading would get called/raised too often by all the 9x/overpairs/spade draws in ranges, and we don’t benefit from building a pot OOP. Standard is to check range and continue only if we pick up real equity or face very favorable sizing/lines.

I have JJ on a 9s-6d-3s flop in a multiway pot (Claude and FiveTwo still in; Elon appears all-in preflop). This is a classic situation to favour pot control. JJ is an overpair to a very dry board — there are only backdoor spade possibilities — but I'm out of position and facing multiple callers who could have sets or two-pair/overcards that will call a c-bet. Betting OTF in a multiway pot gives me little fold equity and would either bloat the pot OOP (forcing uncomfortable decisions on later streets) or just get value‑called by hands that currently beat me. With deep stacks behind and an all-in already committed, the prudent, +EV line is to check and realize equity, then re-evaluate on the turn: call reasonable continuation bets, but fold to clear, strong aggression that polarizes to sets/QQ+. If everyone checks through I can continue controlling the pot and extract value on later streets if the board stays benign.

Missed my set with pocket 5s on a 9-6-3 board with two spades. Three opponents checked to me. My underpair has minimal showdown value and betting into three players risks getting called or raised by better hands or draws. With the flush draw on board and no equity improvement, I'll take the free card and hope to hit my set on the turn.

Multiway pot (4 players) after a tiny 3-bet, everyone checked flop, but the turn 4d adds more connectivity (A5/75/54) and a diamond draw. With Kh8c we still have pure K-high, no spade, no diamond, and no meaningful straight draw, so we have almost no equity when called. Leading here as a bluff into multiple opponents is low-percentage because someone will frequently have at least a pair (9x/6x/33/44) or a draw and continue, forcing us into burning chips. Best is to check and realize whatever small showdown value we have, and only consider bluffing later on very favorable river runouts if it checks through again.

I hold JJ, opened UTG and called the small 3‑bet — currently in a multiway pot (Elon is already all‑in). Flop (9s‑6d‑3s) was very dry so I checked to control the pot; the turn (4d) is another harmless low card. I’m out of position against multiple live opponents with deep stacks, so betting here has very little fold equity and mainly bloats the pot into ranges that often beat me (sets, two‑pair) or get called by draws/med pairs. I’ll keep the pot under control and realize equity by checking. If someone leads out I’ll call small/medium sized bets for value but will respect large raises that polarize to monsters and fold accordingly. For now: check and re‑evaluate on the river.

I have pocket 5s on a 9-6-3-4 board. Importantly, I now have an open-ended straight draw (2 or 7 completes my straight: 2-3-4-5-6 or 3-4-5-6-7). With three opponents checking to me and the action checked around, I could semi-bluff but with multiple players and flush draws possible, I'll take the free card. I have 8 outs to the straight plus 2 outs for a set - roughly 20% equity to improve on the river.

We have pure K-high with no busted draw to represent and we’re still multiway after two streets of checks. The river Ac is a good bluff-card in theory, but it also connects with a lot of the ranges that took this line (Ax floats, A5/A4 type hands, and even some delayed value), and getting three opponents to fold often enough is unlikely. With very low fold equity and no real upside versus just giving up, the best play is to check and preserve chips.

I have pocket Jacks (JsJh) in a multiway pot that I controlled earlier by checking down. The river A♣ is an awkward card — it likely improves parts of my opponents’ calling ranges (A-x) and makes thin value-betting less attractive. Everyone checked the turn and checked the river, showing passivity and giving me little reason to bluff; a river bet here has poor fold equity and mainly gets called by hands that beat me or by worse pairs that I could often get to call smaller bets, but I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position. With a healthy stack relative to the pot, the lowest-variance +EV line is to check and take my hand to showdown. If someone fires into me I’ll call small-to-medium bets for value but will respect larger river aggression.

River brought an Ace on a 9-6-3-4-A board. My pocket 5s missed the straight draw (needed 2 or 7). Three opponents have all checked to me. While the Ace is a scare card I could bluff with, betting into 3 opponents with complete air is too risky - someone likely has at least a pair. My pair of 5s might actually beat busted draws at showdown. Checking is the safe play.

I am in UTG position with 5s 2d, which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given the poor hand strength, early position, and full table of opponents yet to act, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot. Folding is the only correct play to preserve chips.

3s5c is an extremely weak offsuit hand with no high card strength, poor connectivity, and minimal postflop playability. Preflop from middle position, even if facing just the big blind (to call 100 into 150 pot for ~2.5:1), the equity is insufficient against any reasonable villain range. My stack is ~18.5BB, so folding trash preserves chips for better spots in this multiway tournament dynamic. Opponents from history play loose-aggressively, but no need to speculate with garbage.

Q6o is a weak hand, even from the cutoff. While I could potentially open here with 3 players behind, Q6o is below standard cutoff opening ranges. With no suited component and poor playability postflop, folding is the disciplined play here.

I am on the button with K8s, which is a strong hand for an opening raise in a 6-max game after everyone before me has folded. This is a standard spot to attack the blinds and try to win the pot immediately or play a pot in position. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind.

We have the absolute top of range (AA) in the small blind versus a Button open, which is typically wide. Flatting OOP invites the BB in and keeps SPR high; 3-betting builds a pot we’re happy to stack off in, denies equity to suited/connected holdings, and gives us the initiative on most flops. With Flash’s effective stack (~68bb), a 3-bet sizing around 4x is ideal to set up comfortable turn/river commitment lines. If Flash 4-bets (including jam), we continue/stack off.

I'm in the big blind with K♦9♦ facing a button open to 250 and a small‑blind 3‑bet to 950. Pot is ~1300 and I need to put in 850 more (950 total). K9s has good playability — suited with a King blocker — and the call is a small fraction of my stack (~6.5%). Calling keeps the pot controlled, lets me realize equity, and avoids committing myself out of position with a marginal hand by 4‑betting. If the button comes along I'll proceed cautiously out of position; if I miss a flop and face heavy aggression I'll be ready to fold. Therefore I will defend and call to see the flop.

I opened K8s from the button and faced a 3-bet from the small blind and a cold-call from the big blind. While K8s is often dominated in this spot, I am getting approximately 3:1 pot odds (700 to call into a current pot of 2150) and I will have the advantage of position for the remainder of the hand. Given the stack depths and the suited nature of my hand, calling to see a flop is profitable, provided I play cautiously if I hit a King, as I could be against AK or KQ.

We flop absolute nuts (top set) on an extremely dynamic A♠T♣Q♠ board in a 3-bet pot, multiway. Checking risks giving a free card to a huge portion of opponents’ ranges (KJ, J9, spade draws, pair+draws) and misses value from top-pair/two-pair/strong draws that will continue. With Flash short (effective ~6.1k into 2.85k), a ~50% pot c-bet cleanly sets up a turn shove while still getting calls from dominated made hands and draws. If raised, we’re happy to stack off.

Call 1400. Board As Tc Qs with K♦9♦: I have no pair but I have 7 clean outs (3 kings + 4 jacks) to make a pair/straight by the river — roughly ~28% equity to improve by showdown. FiveTwo was the preflop 3-bettor and is continuation-betting here, so his range is strong (lots of Ax, AQ/KQ, QQ/TT), but the required call is 1400 into a pot where I need ~20% equity to justify calling (1400/(5650+1400) ≈ 20%). My estimated equity (~28%) exceeds that, so a call is +EV. Raising OOP as a bluff with a marginal hand against a 3-bettor risks committing chips unnecessarily and will often run into better hands; folding now would concede too much given the pot odds. Plan: see the turn — if I hit a K or J I can proceed aggressively; if I miss and face significant turn aggression I’ll fold.

I have Kc 8c on a board of As Tc Qs. While I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack), the board is extremely dangerous. FiveTwo (the pre-flop 3-bettor) led out into two players, and Minni (the big blind) called. Both actions indicate strong ranges, likely containing Aces, two-pair, or strong spade draws. My King is likely not an out as I am almost certainly behind an Ace or a Queen. Calling 1,400 with only 6,100 behind to chase a 4-outer (which might not even be the winning hand if a spade hits or someone has AJ) is mathematically poor. I will fold and preserve my remaining chips.

Turn pairs the Ten, giving us Aces full (near-nuts). Villain’s continuing range contains many hands that will still pay (KJ made straight, AQ/AK/AT/QT, plus spade draws), and we want to charge draws while setting up a clean river jam with a low SPR. A larger value bet also allows Minni to shove over the top with straights/draws, which we happily call.

Fold. FiveTwo is a preflop 3‑bettor who barreled the flop and now jams a large turn bet (4,000 into the pot, pot = 9,650). Calling 4,000 here requires ~29.3% equity (4,000 / (9,650+4,000)). With K♦9♦ my only realistic clean outs are 3 kings and 4 jacks (7 outs) to improve on the river — roughly 7/46 ≈ 15.2% — well below the price. Worse, the line strongly represents very strong value hands (AT combos that make a full house on the turn, TT quads, AQ/AK, QQ, strong A‑x) that either already beat me or will still beat my made pair/straight. A raise/bluff here has little fold equity vs a 3‑bettor and would commit too many chips. Folding preserves my stack for better spots.

Preflop from UTG in a 6-handed game with K3h suited. This is a speculative hand with poor high-card strength and large gaps, unsuitable for opening or limping early position. My stack is 18.5BB, but the UTG shoving/pushing range requires much stronger hands like 66+, ATs+, AJo+, KQs+. Folding preserves stack for better spots and avoids playing out of position with a weak hand against potentially wide ranges from later positions, including aggressive players like FiveTwo.

Q2o is a weak hand in middle position with 4 players behind. Not suited, poor kicker, and limited playability. Standard fold here - this hand doesn't meet the threshold for opening or calling from this position.

I am in the Cutoff with 5d 6s. This is a very weak starting hand, and even in a 6-max game, it is not strong enough to open from this position. With an aggressive player like FiveTwo on the button and two players still to act in the blinds, there is a high likelihood of being called or re-raised. Given the marginal value of 5-6 offsuit, the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Folds to us on the button with KQo, a clear open in 6-max. We want to leverage position, deny blinds equity, and build pots with a hand that plays well postflop. Both blinds are mid-stacked and likely to defend wide, so a standard 2.5x keeps our risk controlled while still generating fold equity and creating a manageable SPR if called.

I’m in the small blind with 9♣8♣ facing a button raise to 250 (2.5bb). With ~108bb effective this is a strong, playable suited connector with good implied odds and multi-street potential — but I’m out of position vs the raiser and the big blind. I’ll call the additional 200 (250 total) to keep the pot controlled, see a flop, and realize equity; 3-betting OOP with 98s is doable as a mix but unnecessary here. If the big blind 3-bets large I’ll fold or re-evaluate; vs single-callers I’ll proceed postflop looking to hit straights/flushes or apply pressure on suitable textures.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Five offsuit. The Button raised to 250 and the Small Blind called. I need to call 150 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 750 total, giving me excellent 5:1 pot odds. While playing A5o out of position in a 3-way pot can be tricky, the price is too good to fold an Ace. I close the action and will proceed cautiously post-flop.

I have 9♣8♣ on 9♥6♥K♣ — top pair but with a weak 8-kicker and only a backdoor club draw. This is a multiway pot (Button + BB) and I’m out of position to both, while the K on the flop is very much in the opener’s range. In these spots a c-bet OOP often gets called by better Kx or overplayed hands and can see a raise; I want to control the pot and realize equity rather than build a big pot with a marginal hand. I’ll check to keep the pot manageable, call small continuation bets to see a turn, and fold to large, non‑reasonable aggression. If it checks through I get a free turn to improve (club or pair) and can re-evaluate then.

I hold Ace-Five offsuit on a Kh-9h-6c flop. I missed the board completely, having only Ace high with no significant draws. The board texture favors the pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo) who likely has Kings in their range, or the Small Blind who called. Being out of position in a 3-way pot with effectively air, I have no fold equity to justify a bluff and no value to bet. The standard line is to check to the pre-flop aggressor and fold to a bet.

Multiway on K♣9♥6♥, KQ is top pair with a strong kicker but the board is dynamic (two hearts + some straight connectivity). With both blinds checking, we should value-bet and deny equity to heart draws and hands like 9x/6x/gutters that will otherwise realize too cheaply. A medium sizing keeps worse Kx and draws continuing while still charging them; we don’t need an overbet in a 3-way pot where ranges are tighter and raises are more value-heavy. Plan: bet and proceed cautiously versus aggression (especially big check-raises).

I have 9♣8♣ on 9♥6♥K♣: top pair with a weak kicker and only a remote backdoor club possibility. FiveTwo’s bet of 375 into a 1125 pot (~1/3 pot) is a standard small c-bet — it contains a mix of Kx/value hands and missed hands/bluffs. I’m out of position and there’s a live big blind behind me, so raising OOP with a marginal top pair would bloat the pot and risk getting isolated or 3‑bet by worse equity. Pot odds to call are ~25% (375 to win ~1500), which is reasonable given my equity vs a mixed c‑bet range, so I’ll call to control the pot and see a turn. Plan: call now, re-evaluate on the turn — fold to large, non‑credible aggression (big raise/3‑bet), but if checked to me I may take a stab or slow‑play depending on the card; if I improve (two pair/trips/strong draws) I will extract value.

On the flop, the pot is 1500 chips after FiveTwo bets 375 and Minni calls. I am getting 4:1 odds to call. However, I hold Ace-Five offsuit on a Kh-9h-6c board, meaning I have only Ace-high with no flush or straight draws. FiveTwo's c-bet into two opponents indicates strength (likely a King or strong draw), and Minni's check-call suggests they also have a piece of the board or a strong draw. My equity is extremely low (likely drawing dead or to at most 3 outs which might not be clean). Despite the decent pot odds, I am well behind two ranges and out of position. I cannot profitably continue.

I hold 9♣8♣ and the turn is 9♥-6♥-K♣-6♦ — I’ve just improved to two pair (9s and 6s). However the button (FiveTwo) opened preflop and c‑bet the K‑high flop, so his continuing range contains a lot of Kx combos which on this turn are now two pair (K & 6) and therefore beat my 9&6. There are also possible sets/full houses (66, 99) that crush me. Betting OOP here would often get called or raised by hands that dominate me; I don’t want to bloat the pot with a vulnerable two‑pair. I’ll check to control the pot and gather information. Plan: if FiveTwo checks back we go to river cheaply and re‑evaluate; if he bets a small/medium size (roughly ≤50% pot, ~≤750) I’ll call one street to see the river; if he raises or overbets (≈pot or larger) I will fold — too many hands in his range beat me.

Turn pairs the 6, which slightly reduces the number of two-pair combos we lose to and makes some of Minni’s flop continuing range (9x, heart draws, gutters) still quite incentivized to continue. With KQ we’re still ahead of a lot of Kx (KJ/KT), 9x that peeled once, and all heart draws, so we should keep value-betting while charging equity. I’ll use a medium sizing to get called by worse and draws without bloating the pot too much versus the portion of their range that is now trips/boats (6x) and may check-raise.

I’ve improved to two pair (9s and 6s) on 9♥-6♥-K♣-6♦. FiveTwo opened pre, c-bet the K on the flop and now barrels a small turn bet of 650 into a 2150 pot (~30%). Heads-up this line contains a lot of Kx, some bluffs and a smaller number of hands that actually crush me (66, 99, or Kx hands that hold a 9 or a 6). Raising OOP would bloat the pot and get me into trouble vs those dominating combos; folding is too passive given the small size and the hand’s decent showdown value. The call is cheap (650 to win ~2800 — ~23% pot odds) and I’m ahead of a large portion of his continuing range, so I’ll call to control the pot and see the river. Plan: call 650 now, re-evaluate on the river — fold to large/clearly polarized raises (e.g. a shove or pot-sized bet), extract value if checked to or if I improve.

I’m holding 9♣8♣ on 9♥-6♥-K♣-6♦-4♠ — that’s two pair (9s & 6s). FiveTwo opened and barreled flop and turn, then checked the river, which looks like weakness or a capped Kx/missed draw range. My two‑pair is vulnerable to a few combos that beat me (66 quads/full house, 99 full house, K9/K6 combos), but it’s ahead of a lot of his continuing range (single Kx, weaker pairs, missed overcards). With the pot at 2,800 I want to extract thin value from Kx and other worse hands that will call a modest bet while keeping the pot manageable against the few hands that crush me. I’ll make a small value bet of 1,000 (≈36% pot). If he raises pot‑sized or shoves, I’ll fold — that line will usually denote the combos that beat me; if he calls I expect many Kx/bluff‑catchers and I go to showdown.

Minni’s line is mostly check/call twice then a small river lead (1000 into 3800). Our KQ is still a strong bluff-catcher/value hand: we beat all worse Kx (KJ/KT), 9x that floated, and any missed heart draws that may stab as a bluff. The hands that clearly beat us (6x/trips/boats, 99, occasionally 44) exist, but this sizing looks more like a blocker/thin value or missed-draw stab than a polar value bet. Raising would isolate ourselves vs better hands and fold out the bluffs/worse value we want to keep in, so the best play is to take the price and call.
T8o in UTG is too weak to open. This hand plays poorly from early position and will face action from 5 players behind. Standard fold.

I have been dealt Pocket Kings, which is the second-strongest starting hand in Hold'em. My goal is to extract maximum value and protect the hand. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot while remaining consistent with previous opening sizes.

Flash opens 3x from middle position and we’re in the cutoff with 7♦6♣ offsuit. This hand is near the bottom of playable holdings and, being offsuit, realizes equity poorly even with position. With the Button/SB/BB still to act—especially Elon in the BB with a short stack who can jam—calling invites squeeze/jam scenarios and tough postflop spots for a marginal hand. No good 3-bet bluff properties here either. Best is to pass and preserve stack for higher-EV spots.

Fold. On the button with 7♠5♦ facing a 3x open to 300. This is a weak, disconnected offsuit hand with poor equity versus a typical raiser’s range. Calling 300 into a 450 pot requires ~40% equity to break even (300/(450+300)=40%), which 7-5o will not have against an opener — and calling invites action from the SB and a short-stacked BB (Elon, 1750) who can easily shove and put me in a tough spot. A min-raise to 500 is a light bluff that risks a shove or a 3-bet; with a healthy stack I should preserve chips and wait for better spots. Fold.

I hold Q5o in the Small Blind facing a 3bb open raise from Flash in Middle Position. Q5o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially from out of position. I do not have the pot odds or equity to justify a call, and the hand is too weak to consider a bluff 3-bet. The standard play is to fold.

Preflop in BB with AJo, a strong hand with good playability and blockers. Facing an isolated MP raise to 300 from Flash (5800 stack), who has shown aggression but folded to 3-bets and pressure in prior hands (e.g., Hand 5, Hand 16). Pot 450, calling 200 offers great odds (~3:1), but 3-betting takes initiative, applies fold equity, and builds pot OOP with a premium hand. My stack is 1750 (~17.5BB), so sizing to 900 (3x the raise) balances value and semi-bluff potential. Flash likely folds wide or calls light, giving me good equity either way postflop.

I have Pocket Kings, an extremely strong hand. Elon has raised to 900, leaving himself with only 50 chips behind from his starting stack of 950. Since he is effectively all-in, I will raise to put him all-in now. There is no reason to slow play here against such a short stack.

Facing a 4-bet from Flash after my 3-bet with AJo in BB. My remaining stack is 950, making this a shove-or-fold spot. Calling 600 leaves only 350 behind into a ~3050 pot, committing me fully on any flop anyway while giving Flash a free shot to realize equity OOP. Shoving to 1850 applies max pressure on Flash's likely polarized 4-bet range (QQ+/AK, maybe some bluffs given his aggression). AJo has ~35-40% equity vs that range and blocks AK. Flash has folded to pressure before (e.g., Hand 5 turn fold, Hand 16 fold to bigger shove). Pot odds for call are good (~4:1), but fold equity from shove outweighs, protecting my short stack and taking down the 2450 pot often. No check/call nuance preflop.

I have Pocket Kings, an elite premium hand. Elon has moved all-in for 1850 total. Having already committed 1500 to the pot, I only need to call 350 more to contest a pot of over 3700. This is an automatic call regardless of Elon's range, as I am priced in even against his strongest possible hands.

5-handed but still UTG, 8♣3♣ is far below a profitable open: it makes too many dominated one-pair/flush situations and realizes equity poorly when called/3-bet. With opponents reasonably deep and capable of defending/playing back, there’s no need to force marginal steals from first position. Best is to preserve stack and wait for stronger opens.

UTG+1 with K♠6♣ is a marginal, offsuit hand with a weak kicker and poor post‑flop playability. In a five‑handed game UTG+1 is effectively early position — I’ll face action from the button and both blinds — and this table has shown a tendency to open and apply pressure preflop. Limping (calling 100) often sees multi‑way pots where my K6o is dominated; raising to the min is thin and will invite 3‑bets from deeper/aggressive stacks. I have a healthy stack (~86 BB), so there’s no need to spew chips with a weak holding. Fold and wait for a better position and hand.

I am on the Button with Q9o in a 5-handed game. Action has folded to me. This is a standard spot to open-raise for a blind steal and to play the pot in position if called. Q9o falls within the top ~40% of hands, which is a typical Button opening range. My opponents in the blinds (Claude and Flash) have chips to play, but my tight image (having folded frequently in previous hands) might grant my raise more respect. I will raise to 2.5BB (250) to put pressure on the blinds. If I face a 3-bet, I can easily fold, but I expect to take it down pre-flop or have a positional advantage post-flop often enough to make this profitable.
9-5 offsuit is a weak hand. Facing a button raise, this is a clear fold - poor playability, often dominated, and out of position. No reason to defend here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit. Pro has raised to 250 from the Button. While the pot odds are roughly 2.6 to 1 (150 to call into a 400 pot), T3o is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly out of position. I have a healthy stack of 79 big blinds and see no reason to defend such a marginal hand against a likely wide but still superior button range.

UTG with T♥5♠ in a 5-handed table: weak, poorly connected, easily dominated and I’ll be out of position against four players behind (including very aggressive raisers like FiveTwo and Pro). The pot is only 150 and calling/limping from UTG invites raises and multi-way trouble; a raise is too loose and will get punished. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot in position. Fold.

I am holding Queen-Four offsuit (Q4o) in UTG+1 in a 5-handed game. This is a very weak hand with poor playability and dominated potential. Since the action has folded to me, I have no reason to enter the pot with such a weak holding, especially from early position with a comfortable stack of over 95 big blinds. The standard play is to fold and wait for a better hand.

On the button with Q8o after folds to me. This is a standard steal spot - Q8o is plenty good enough to open from the button against just the blinds. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x) to take down the blinds or play in position if called.

I am in the Small Blind with 7s 6c, which is a very weak hand. Claude has raised from the Button to 250. Although I am getting decent pot odds to call, playing 7-high offsuit out of position for the rest of the hand is generally a losing strategy, especially with an active Big Blind yet to act. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

Button open to 2.5bb and we’re closing the action in the BB getting a decent price, but 73o is near the bottom of hands to defend: it’s dominated, makes weak one-pair hands, and has poor playability postflop even with position disadvantage. Against an active opponent, turning this into a 3-bet bluff is also too thin with this holding. Preserve stack and wait for better defend/3-bet candidates.

I am in UTG position with 9d 2d. This is a very weak suited hand (9-high, unconnected). Playing this hand from early position is not profitable as I will be out of position against any callers and the hand has very low equity. The standard and correct play is to fold.
J9o in UTG+1 is a weak hand for this position. With 4 players still to act behind me and no strong equity, this is a clear fold. I should be selective from early position.

I am holding 3d 4h on the button. This is a very weak hand, and while I have the opportunity to attempt a steal, the players in the blinds (FiveTwo and Minni) have been active and aggressive. Given my healthy stack of nearly 80 big blinds, there is no reason to risk chips with one of the worst starting hands in poker. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

Folded to me in the SB heads-up vs the BB with KTo. This is comfortably ahead of my completing range and benefits from taking the initiative OOP. Raising pressures Minni’s BB defend, can win the pot immediately, and when called I can c-bet many flops with good high-card equity and strong blockers (Kx/Tx). I’ll use a standard ~3x SB open size to generate fold equity without bloating the pot too much out of position; if facing a large 3-bet I can comfortably fold given stack depths and tournament dynamics.

I have pocket threes (3s3h) — a small pair — facing a single raise to 300 from the small blind. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (300 total). With ~85bb effective vs a very deep opener, this is a textbook set-mining spot: calling keeps weaker broadway and suited hands in, gives strong implied odds to hit a set, and avoids committing chips unnecessarily by 3-betting a low pair out of position. 3-betting here is thin value and can put me in trouble vs 4-bets or better hands; folding is too tight with this stack depth. I'll call to see a flop and proceed based on texture and action.

This flop (Q♣-9♠-8♠) connects heavily with BB’s defend range (pairs, two-pair/sets, JT, lots of spade/straight draws), so our range advantage as the SB opener is muted and we’re more vulnerable to a check-raise. With K♥T♠ we have a valuable gutshot to the nuts (J makes the straight) plus some overcard equity, so we’re happy to realize our equity without inflating the pot OOP. Checking keeps our range protected (we can have strong hands here too) and lets us proceed as a call vs reasonable sizing or fold vs big pressure depending on runout/action.

Heads-up vs FiveTwo with 3♠3♥ on Q♣8♠9♠. I called preflop as a set-mining call; on this flop I only have a small pair and a very weak backdoor spade draw (needs runner-runner). FiveTwo checked, which often indicates a missed c‑bet or pot-control with a marginal made hand. With ~83bb effective I don’t want to bloat the pot with a vulnerable low pair — best line is pot control and equity realization. I’ll check and plan to call small-to-medium bets to see a turn (folding to heavy aggression), and only get aggressive if I hit a set or the turn gives a clear advantage.

Turn pairs the top card, which is a relatively good card for the SB opener to apply a delayed c-bet: it reduces BB’s two-pair combos, and we credibly represent strong Qx that would now bet for value/protection. With K♥T♠ we have a gutshot to the K-high straight (J river) and also pick up additional equity on any spade river (our T♠ makes a flush if a 4th spade falls). Given the small pot and Minni’s passive line so far, I want to take this down often with a small stab while keeping my risk controlled; if I face a raise I can comfortably fold.

I hold 3♠3♥. After the turn (Qc 8♠ 9♠ Qs) I now have two pair (Queens and Threes). FiveTwo was the preflop opener, checked the flop and now bets a small 225 into ~825 — a sizing that looks like a probe/thin value bet rather than a polarized shove. My hand is strong versus many of his non-Q, non-flush combos but is vulnerable to any Q (which gives him trips) and to any two-spade holecards that now make a flush. With ~83bb effective, the correct line is pot control: call the small bet to keep worse hands and bluffs in and re-evaluate on the river. Raising would bloat the pot and risk committing more against hands that already beat me; folding is too nitty given my two pair and the cheap price. I will call 225, plan to fold to heavy aggression, and decide on river based on the card (be cautious if the river is a spade or a queen, press for value if it’s a blank).

River bricks our gutshot/flush draw and we’re on pure K-high. After checking flop and betting small on the paired Q turn, our range credibly contains plenty of Qx that now wants value/protection, while Minni’s line (check/call) contains many 8x/9x, weaker Qx, and missed spade draws/A-high floats. Given Minni’s likely passivity, checking back doesn’t induce many bluffs and we often just lose to any pair or A-high; taking the initiative gives us fold equity vs missed draws and a meaningful portion of one-pair hands that don’t want to face a strong-looking river barrel on a paired board. I’ll use a solid, non-all-in pressure size (~70% pot) to maximize folds without risking too much of my chip-lead; if raised, this is an easy fold.

I hold 3s3h — that gives me two pair (Queens and Threes) on Qc 8s 9s Qs 7d. The pot was ~1,050 before the river; FiveTwo bets 750 (pot shown 1,800) so calling 750 would commit me to win 2,550 — break‑even equity ≈ 29.4%. FiveTwo’s line (SB open preflop, check on the flop, a tiny 225 turn probe, now a much larger 750 on the river) is exactly the sort of line that contains lots of value: any Q (trips), two‑spade hole cards that made the flush on the turn, JT straights and sets (99/88), all of which beat my two pair. The hands I realistically continue against are single pairs and occasional missed draws/bluffs, which are not numerous enough to give me >29% equity here (I estimate my equity vs his river‑betting range closer to ~20–25%). Raising is thin and will mostly get called by better hands; calling is marginal and would be spewing chips when my read and prior plan were to fold to heavy river aggression. I fold and preserve stack for better, clearer spots.

Q9o is a marginal hand from UTG position. Even at a 5-player table, opening Q9o UTG exposes me to 3-bets from players behind with stronger ranges. With decent stack and 120 hands remaining, no need to get out of line with a mediocre holding. Standard fold.

I am holding Jd 4h in UTG+1. This is a very weak starting hand, and with four players left to act behind me, there is no strategic value in playing it. J4o does not fit into any standard opening range in this position.

Button vs blinds, A5o is a profitable open: we have strong ace-high showdown value, wheel/straight potential, and an ace blocker that reduces the chance we run into premium Ax/AA when the blinds continue aggressively. With fold equity preflop and positional advantage postflop, raising is higher EV than limping. I’ll use a standard small 5-handed BTN open to keep ranges wide and risk modest while still pressuring the blinds’ stacks.

I’m in the small blind with T♥6♥ facing a standard button open to 250. This hand is marginal — while it has a suited heart and a ten blocker, it’s frequently dominated by the button’s raising range (TX, broadways) and plays poorly out of position post‑flop. Calling 200 to defend would put me OOP against the raiser (and still give the BB a chance to come along), often creating multiway pots where my hand has low playability and poor reverse implied odds. A tiny 3‑bet as a bluff is possible but thin given remaining stacks and no strong blockers. Preserve chips, wait for a better spot — fold here.

I am in the Big Blind with 2s 6c, facing a raise to 250 from the Button (FiveTwo). The pot odds are favorable (calling 150 to win 550, roughly 27%), and my raw equity against a wide button opening range is technically around 30-32%. However, 2-6 offsuit is one of the worst hands to play post-flop, especially out of position. It suffers from extremely poor equity realization due to the lack of connectivity and high card strength. Calling here would likely lead to difficult post-flop spots where I bleed more chips. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am holding a strong pocket pair (Ten of Clubs, Ten of Diamonds) in a 5-handed game. Being first to act (UTG), I should open for a standard raise to build a pot and narrow the field. Pocket tens are ahead of most ranges here, though I need to be cautious of aggressive 3-bets from players like FiveTwo, who has shown significant aggression in previous hands. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard and effective play.

Facing a 3x UTG open in a 5-handed game, 7♠5♣ offsuit (here 7h5c) has very poor playability and realization: it’s frequently dominated when it makes a pair, has no suited equity, and calling invites squeezes from BTN/SB/BB while we’re still relatively early in the lineup. A 3-bet bluff is also bad versus an UTG range and a shorter stack profile. Best EV is to conserve chips and wait for better spots.

Facing an UTG open to 300 on the button with A♣6♥ and ~80bb effective. This is a marginal but playable hand in position — I block many strong Ax combos and have excellent post‑flop leverage. Folding is too tight; a small 3‑bet to 500 is thin (UTG's range is likely strong and could 4‑bet or call) and a larger 3‑bet would be overcommitting with a mediocre hand. Pot odds to call are reasonable (300 into a 750 pot after my call → ~40% break‑even), and in position I can fold to heavy aggression or realize equity when I hit an Ace/6 or take the pot away with postflop play. Plan: call preflop, evaluate on the flop — continue with top pair/strong draws or selectively float against weak c‑bets, fold to large, committed 3‑bets.

I am in the Small Blind with K5 offsuit, facing a raise to 300 from UTG (Flash) and a call from the Button (Minni). My hand is weak, both in terms of raw equity and playability, especially out of position against two opponents. UTG's opening range is likely strong, dominating my King, and the multi-way pot further diminishes my equity. I need to call 250 to win a pot of roughly 1000, requiring 25% equity which I likely do not realize effectively given my positional disadvantage. Folding is the disciplined choice.

K8o in the BB facing a 300 raise from UTG with one caller. I'm getting 3.75:1 pot odds (750:200), which is decent. K8o is marginal but closing the action with good pot odds makes this a reasonable call. I can potentially hit top pair or a straight draw.

I have K8o on a 4h-6s-Jh board. I completely missed - no pair, just an overcard (K) and a weak backdoor flush draw (two hearts). I'm first to act in a 3-way pot. With this weak holding, I should check and see what develops. If someone bets, I'll likely fold without improving.

I have an overpair to the 4 and 6 on the board, but the Jack is an overcard. As the pre-flop aggressor, I'll lead out with a continuation bet of approximately half the pot (500 chips). This bet serves to extract value from middle pairs or draws (like the heart flush draw) and provides information on the strength of my opponents' hands. If I encounter significant resistance, I will have to proceed with caution given the Jack on the board.

I hold A♣6♥ — a pair of sixes (middle pair) with an ace kicker on 4♥ 6♠ J♥. UTG (Flash) opened and now c‑bet 500 into a ~1450 pot (a fairly small, non‑polarized size). In position with a vulnerable but decent hand, the correct line is to call and keep the pot controllable: I block some A‑x value combos, have decent showdown value, and can realize equity or float and take the pot away on later streets. Folding is too weak versus a small c‑bet and a raise is unnecessary — it risks getting 3‑bet or committed when I’m often behind to Jx/overpairs. Plan: call 500 (~5 BB), see the turn, fold to heavy turn shove/large raise if I don’t improve, and extract value or apply pressure only if I pick up a stronger holding (A or 6) or the opponent shows weakness.

I have K8o with 8h on a 4h-6s-Jh board. I completely missed - no pair, just an overcard (K) and a weak backdoor flush draw. Facing a 500 bet from Flash and a call from Minni in a 3-way pot. Getting about 3.9:1 odds (1950:500), but I only have ~6 outs to top pair at best, and even if I hit a K, I could be outkicked. With two opponents showing interest, my K-high has very little equity. The backdoor flush draw adds minimal value. This is a clear fold.

I have improved to a set of Tens on the turn, which is currently the near-nuts, only trailing pocket Jacks which is highly unlikely given the pre-flop action. The board contains various draws, including a heart flush draw and straight draws (78, 89, QK). Minni called a continuation bet on the flop, suggesting she likely has a Jack or a draw. I need to bet for value and to charge these draws. A bet of 1300 into the 1950 pot (approximately 66% pot) is a strong sizing that extracts value from top-pair hands like AJ or KJ and prices out the draws.

I hold A♣6♥ — a middle pair with the best possible kicker against an UTG opener. Flash opened UTG, c‑bet a small 500 on the flop and now fires 1,300 on the turn (pot 3,250). Calling 1,300 here requires ~28.6% equity (1,300 / (3,250+1,300)), which is reachable because I beat many bluffs and weaker Ax/low pairs and I block a number of strong A‑x combos. Raising is thin — I can get 3‑bet or shoved on and I’m often behind to Jx/Tx/overpairs — and folding is too passive given my position and showdown value. I’ll call to keep the pot controllable and see the river; if I don’t improve and face a large shove or polarizing river bet I will fold.

On the river, the board has paired with a Six, and I have successfully made a full house (Tens full of Sixes). This is an incredibly strong hand, as I only lose to pocket Jacks (unlikely given the pre-flop action) or the case Six. Minni has called both the flop and turn, which suggests she either has a strong Jack (like AJ or KJ) or was chasing a draw. Since the heart flush draw and most straight draws missed, I need to bet an amount that can still be called by a Jack or any Trip 6s she might have backed into. A bet of 2500 is approximately 55% of the pot, which is a strong value-targeting sizing that aims to maximize profit from her calling range without necessarily forcing a fold from top-pair hands.

River pairs give me trips (6h + board 6s/6d) — a very strong, showdown‑ready hand. Flash (UTG) has been steadily aggressive (300 pre, 500 flop c‑bet, 1300 turn, 2500 river). A 2500 river into ~7050 is a fairly small/medium sizing that will get called by Jx, Tx and many worse one‑pair or two‑pair hands but also can include some bluffs. There are combos that beat me (full houses: pocket JJ/TT/44 or hands like J6/T6), but those are relatively rare versus his overall line. I want to extract maximum value and put him to a decision for his remaining chips — a minimum raise to 5000 total is the best play: it prices in worse hands and likely forces an all‑in or a call for extra chips, while avoiding an unnecessary full shove that only increases variance against the small set of full house combos. Action: raise to 5000 total.

I have a full house (Tens full of Sixes). This is an extremely strong hand that is only beaten by pocket Jacks (JJ) or pocket Sixes (66). Given the pre-flop action (Minni just calling a 3x raise), JJ is unlikely as it would typically 3-bet. Pocket Sixes are possible but represent only one combination. Minni could be raising with trip Sixes, a Jack-high straight that she doesn't realize is beaten, or even a smaller full house like 44. Given the massive pot odds and the strength of my hand, I am never folding here. I will call the raise to see the showdown.
