K3o is too weak to open from UTG 5-handed: it’s dominated by the calling/3-betting ranges behind, plays poorly out of position, and we also have a very short stack (Minni ~9.5bb) directly behind who can jam over an open and force us to fold, burning EV. With a deep stack we can wait for better spots to apply pressure.

Stack = 950 (≈9.5 BB). I’m UTG+1 with 5♦3♣ offsuit — a very weak, unplayable hand out of position. With 3 players to act behind (button, SB, BB) who are capable of calling/raising, this is not a spot to limp or open-shove. At ~10 BB my shove range should be much stronger; calling 100 out of position with this hand is spewy. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot or a positionally advantageous hand.

I am on the Button with a very weak hand (74o). The action has folded to me. Despite the positional advantage, 74o is too weak to profitably open-raise for a steal, especially against the Small Blind and Big Blind who cover me and have deep stacks (131BB and 152BB respectively). My hand has poor playability and equity. The standard play here is to fold.

5-2 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. Even though it's folded to me in the SB, I'm out of position against Flash who has a bigger stack. Trying to steal with this hand or limping to play OOP with garbage isn't profitable. Better to fold and save the 50 chips.

I’m UTG with 6♣7♥ and only 950 chips (~9.5 BB). With four players to act behind (including very deep stacks) this is a classic push-or-fold situation, but 6-7 offsuit is well outside a profitable UTG shove range — it plays poorly if called and will often be dominated. Calling 100 to see a flop leaves me ~8.5 BB out of position with a marginal hand; min-raising to 200 leaves me very shallow (~7.5 BB) and simply invites 3-bets or shoves from big stacks. Fold and preserve the short stack to wait for a better spot to shove or pick up blinds.

I am in UTG+1 with Ten-Three suited (3d Td). With 5 players remaining, this is effectively the cutoff position after the UTG fold, but T3s is a very weak hand with low playability and reverse implied odds. My stack is healthy at 94 BBs, so there is no pressure to make loose moves. Folding is the standard and correct play here.
Q8o on the button with two players already folded. This is a standard steal spot - I have position and a playable hand. I'll raise to 250 to try to take down the blinds or play in position postflop.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Jack of hearts, which is a premium starting hand in a 5-handed game. Claude, on the Button, has raised to 250. Given the strength of my hand and my position, a 3-bet is the standard play here to take initiative and build the pot. I will raise to 900 to either win the pot immediately or narrow the field before the flop, while also putting pressure on the Button's wide opening range.

We’re in the BB with KQo facing a BTN open and an SB 3-bet. Flatting 800 invites Claude to come along and leaves us playing an awkward multiway 3-bet pot out of position with a hand that can be dominated. Flash has shown willingness to apply pressure preflop, so his squeeze range from the SB can be wider than pure value; KQ has decent blocker value against KK/QQ/AK/KQ while still having playable equity when called. A cold 4-bet also leverages our big stack against two mid stacks and will take down a solid pot immediately a high percentage of the time. I’m raising to a size that discourages in-position flats and clearly represents QQ+/AK, while keeping the loss contained if we have to fold to a 5-bet shove.
Facing a 4-bet squeeze with Q8o. Flash 3-bet to 900, then FiveTwo 4-bet to 2800. Q8o is far too weak to continue against this action - I'm crushed by any legitimate 4-betting range. Easy fold.

I started by 3-betting AhJh to 900 from the Small Blind against Claude's button open. However, FiveTwo in the Big Blind has now 4-bet to 2800. This is a very strong cold 4-bet from the Big Blind into two active players. Looking at FiveTwo's history, they have shown extreme aggression in Hand #16 (8150 pre-flop raise) and Hand #24 (4000 turn bet), suggesting they play a very polarized and powerful range when they commit this many chips. While AhJh is a strong hand, calling out of position against a 4-bet usually leads to difficult post-flop spots, and 5-betting is likely overplaying the hand given the stack depths and the strength shown by FiveTwo. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

I am in UTG position with 8h Jd (Jack-Eight offsuit). This is a weak hand that falls well outside standard opening ranges, even in a 5-handed game. My stack is healthy at 94 BBs, but there is no reason to bleed chips with a hand that has poor playability and reverse implied odds out of position. Minni in the Big Blind is very short (8.5 BB) and could easily shove over an open. Folding is the only standard play.

T9o from UTG+1 is a marginal hand that plays poorly from early position. With 4 players still to act behind me including Flash with a big stack and FiveTwo as chip leader, I don't want to open this speculative hand and risk facing a 3-bet. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am on the button with J2o, which is a very weak starting hand. While the button is a good position for stealing blinds, the Big Blind (Minni) is extremely short-stacked with only 8.5 BBs. Short stacks are more likely to shove or defend aggressively against steals. Given the lack of equity J2o has against any reasonable defending range and the risk of being shoved on by a desperate short stack, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

Folds to us in the SB against a very short BB (8.5bb). K2o is too weak to comfortably raise/call off versus a shove, and raising/folding burns chips unnecessarily. Completing keeps variance low, realizes our equity cheaply, and lets us play postflop for a small price while still having plenty of ways to win the pot on later streets if BB checks.

I’m effectively short-stacked (850 ≈ 8.5 BB). With only the SB left and no raise to face, shove-fold is the correct strategy — I need to maximize fold equity and either pick up the blinds or double if called. T♠4♠ is marginal but suited and acceptable to push in this stack depth. Checking is too passive and wastes my best chance to climb the blinds quickly, so I shove all-in.

We limped the SB and now face a very large raise to 850 (8.5bb), which is highly indicative of a short-stack shove/commitment from the BB trying to pick up dead money. We’re getting strong pot odds: calling 750 more to win ~1700 total means we need only ~44% equity. Even though K2o is weak and often dominated by better Kx, the BB’s jam-over-limp range is typically wide (lots of random broadways, suited hands, weaker aces, pairs), and our king blocker helps a bit. With these odds and the chance to win a meaningful pot / potentially knock out a short stack, calling is higher EV than folding here.

Minni appears to have only ~100 chips left behind into a 1700 pot, so this is effectively a shove-or-check spot. On A♠-2♦-6♣ we have a pair (2s) with a decent kicker (Kd) versus a likely wide, short-stack preflop raising range. Betting puts maximum pressure on their air/overcards, denies equity, and still gets called by some worse made hands (6x, smaller pairs) while only risking a trivial amount when they have an ace.

I’m effectively committing my last 100 if I continue — this is an all‑in call. Pot is 1,800 and the bet to me is 100, so I need only ~5.3% equity to break even (100 / (1800+100) ≈ 5.26%). T♠4♠ on A♥-2♦-6♣ is currently air but has ~6 clean outs to pair (three 10s + three 4s) giving roughly a 24% chance to hit by the river, plus a small runner‑runner spade flush possibility. Given my tiny stack, the bet is very cheap relative to the pot and this is exactly the kind of spot a short stack should take to double up. Even versus a range containing Ax or a pocket pair, the required equity is minimal, so calling all‑in is +EV. I call the 100 and put my remaining chips in.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game with 92o, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Given the table dynamics and my position, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot with this hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Four-handed and folded to me on the button, K7o is comfortably within a standard button opening range. We have position and a meaningful stack edge on the SB, so the primary goal is to steal blinds and take initiative with a small-risk sizing. If we face a 3-bet from the BB/SB, we can comfortably fold this combo at these stacks.

I am in the Small Blind with Pocket Fours (4h 4s). The Button (FiveTwo), who is playing deep-stacked and aggressively, opens to 250 (2.5bb). With an effective stack of over 90bb, I have excellent implied odds to set-mine. The price to call is 200 chips to win a pot of roughly 600-700, and if I hit a set, I can likely extract significant value from the aggressive Button. Playing 44 out of position is difficult without a set, but the low cost to see the flop makes calling the most +EV decision compared to folding (too tight vs Button open) or 3-betting (turning a showdown hand into a bluff with poor blockers).

I have AJs in the BB - a premium hand. FiveTwo raised to 250 and Pro called. With a strong suited broadway hand, I should 3-bet for value here. A raise to around 900-1000 will build the pot and potentially isolate or take it down preflop.

We opened K7o on the button for the steal, but BB’s squeeze to 950 over our raise and a SB cold-call is a strong, high-EV spot for him to 3-bet with a tighter/value-heavy range. K7o is heavily dominated, performs poorly multiway if SB continues, and is not a good 4-bet bluff candidate (only a single K blocker, no playability). With plenty of stack and better spots to apply pressure, we simply release.

I originally called the Button open to set-mine cheaply, but the Big Blind has squeezed to 950. To continue, I must call 700 more into a pot of 1450. This requires about 32.5% equity to break even directly, but with pocket 4s, my value comes almost exclusively from implied odds (hitting a set). I am out of position against a strong BB squeezing range (likely TT+, AQ+). My effective implied odds are roughly 12.7:1 (8900 remaining stack / 700 to call), which is below the standard 15-20:1 recommended for set mining, especially out of position where realizing equity is difficult. Without a set on the flop, I will likely be forced to fold to a continuation bet. The price is too high for a speculative hand OOP.

4-handed, UTG plays closer to a cutoff range and A9o is comfortably strong enough to open for value/initiative. My stack covers everyone and especially pressures Pro (91.5bb) on the button, while still keeping the pot manageable if I face a 3-bet from the blinds. I’ll use a standard 2.5x open sizing that’s been common in the match to deny cheap realization and keep my range consistent.

I am on the Button with J9o in a 4-handed game. The player Under the Gun (effectively Cutoff) has raised to 2.5 BB. Although I have position, J9o is a weak offsuit connector that is often dominated by the opener's range (KJ, QJ, AJ, ATs, etc.). Calling creates a spot with reverse implied odds, and 3-betting is not ideal as I block no value hands. Folding is the standard and prudent play to preserve my 91 BB stack for better opportunities.

QTo in the SB facing a UTG open of 250. FiveTwo has been active with opens. QTo is a marginal hand but playable here - getting 2:1 on a call. I'll defend and see a flop with position on the BB. Not strong enough to 3-bet against UTG, but worth seeing a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 2d 7d. The UTG player (FiveTwo) raised to 250 and the Small Blind (Claude) called. I need to call 150 more to see a flop in a pot that would be 750. This gives me pot odds of 5:1. However, 2d 7d is one of the weakest hands in poker and realizes its equity very poorly, especially 3-way. I will be out of position against the initial raiser (FiveTwo). Given the poor quality of the hand and the risk of being dominated or forced to fold on most flops, I choose to fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I completely missed this flop with QTo - no pair, no draw, no spades for a flush draw. The ace-high board likely hits FiveTwo's UTG opening range well. With no equity and poor position, checking is the right play. No reason to bluff into a range that connects well with this board.

Heads-up vs the SB caller on an A-high, somewhat drawy board (two spades), we retain a clear range advantage as the preflop raiser and can profitably c-bet at high frequency. With A9 we have top pair but a middling kicker, so I want value/protection versus worse Ax, 7x/5x, and spade/straight-draws, while keeping the pot controlled and not polarizing into a sizing that invites tough check-raises. A small 1/3-pot bet accomplishes this: it denies equity cheaply, keeps dominated hands in, and lets us fold more cleanly if Claude springs a large check-raise.

I have QTo with no pair and no draw on As7s5h. FiveTwo's small 200 bet into 600 pot is tempting but I have zero equity - no spades for a backdoor flush, no straight draws. I have ~12% to hit a Q or T which isn't enough for the 4:1 odds. The ace-high board favors UTG's range and I'm out of position. Clean fold.

I am in UTG (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play) with Kd 3c (King-Three offsuit). This is a statistically weak hand with no connectivity or suited potential. Even in a shorthanded 4-max table, opening K3o from the first position is generally a losing play, as I have three players left to act who can easily wake up with a better hand or 3-bet me. With a healthy stack of roughly 91 BB, there is no need to steal with such low equity. The standard and prudent play is to fold.

K8o on the button with only blinds to act. This is a standard steal spot - K8o is easily strong enough to open from the button. Raising to 2.5x to put pressure on the blinds and try to take down the pot preflop or play in position postflop.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Four offsuit. The Button (Claude) has opened for a standard 2.5x raise. Jack-Four is a very weak hand to play out of position, even with the pot odds offered. It doesn't flop well and I'll likely find myself in difficult spots on most textures. Given the early stage of the tournament and my healthy stack, there is no reason to defend such a weak range against a button open. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Button opens to 2.5x and we’re getting a great price (150 to win 400), with deep stacks and a suited queen that plays reasonably well postflop (can make top pair, flushes, and some straight draws). Q5s is too weak to 3-bet bluff at a high frequency from OOP given reverse implied odds when we make dominated queens, but it’s an easy defend as a call versus a wide BTN range. Plan: mostly check to the raiser postflop, continue on favorable textures (club-heavy, low/connected boards), and be cautious with thin top-pair holdings.

OOP vs BTN opener on a 7T4 two-heart board, our hand is pure air (no pair, no immediate draw) with only a backdoor club flush and some very thin backdoor straight possibilities. This texture connects reasonably well with our BB defending range (pairs, 7x/Tx, 44), so we don’t need to donk with our weakest holdings. Best is to check our range and decide versus Claude’s c-bet sizing (continue only vs small bets with backdoors / overcard, fold vs larger pressure).

I raised preflop and got called by FiveTwo in the BB. Flop is 4d 7h Th - I missed completely with K-high but this is a decent board to c-bet. I have position and was the aggressor. FiveTwo checked, so a standard continuation bet of ~1/3 pot should take this down often enough to be profitable. The board has a flush draw but otherwise is fairly dry.

Claude’s 200 into 750 is a very small c-bet that will be made with a wide range, and we can’t overfold our BB here. Qc5c is currently air, but we do have an overcard (Q) plus a backdoor club flush and some turn cards (clubs, broadways) that let us continue or apply pressure later. Given the price (200 to win 950) and our ability to realize equity/bluff on favorable turns, calling is higher EV than folding; raising would bloat the pot OOP with too little immediate equity and no heart draw to credibly barrel on many runouts.

Turn pairs the 7, a card that generally strengthens our BB defending range (lots of 7x) and gives us plenty of strong hands to protect a checking range with. With Qc5c specifically we’re essentially pure air now (no flush/straight runouts available with one card to come), so turning it into a donk-bluff is unattractive: we’d be betting with almost no equity and would have to give up versus pressure. Best is to check, keep the pot small, and decide based on Claude’s sizing/frequency (fold to real aggression, consider continuing only versus very small bets where we can still realize occasional Q-high showdown or bluff favorable rivers).

Turn pairs the 7s. I c-bet flop and got called - FiveTwo likely has a ten, a pair, or a heart draw. With just K-high I have no real hand here. The board pairing is slightly good for me (reduces 7x combos) but betting again into a caller with no equity is burning money. Better to check behind, control pot size, and potentially realize showdown value if they're on a busted draw. If they bet river I can fold.

River completes the front-door hearts and also improves some of BTN’s turn-checking floats (e.g., 65/96 making a straight). With Qc5c we have pure showdown marginality (Q-high) and, importantly, no heart blocker, so a river bluff is less attractive: we don’t block his potential flushes and we’re likely to get looked up by any pair/Tx/7x at a decent frequency. Given he’s checked and we realize our (small) showdown equity for free, the best play is to check back rather than fire a thin, high-variance bluff.

River brings 8h, giving me a pair of 8s. The heart flush also completed (7h, Th, 8h on board). My pair of 8s is decent but loses to any ten, any 7, or any flush. FiveTwo checked - if they had a flush they might be trapping. Betting thin for value here is risky since I'm behind many of their calling hands (Tx, 7x, flushes). Check back and show down, hoping to beat busted draws.

J9o from UTG in a 4-handed game is a marginal hand. While 4-handed play allows slightly wider ranges, J9o is still below my opening threshold from early position - it plays poorly postflop and will often be dominated. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in a 4-handed game. With the action folded to me, I will make a standard opening raise to 300 chips to build the pot and take advantage of my positional advantage over the blinds.

4-handed vs a Button open, Flash’s range is wide and we’re the big stack. Calling from the SB would invite the BB in and leaves us playing a marginal suited hand out of position without initiative. 9c6c is a good suited semi-bluff candidate to 3-bet: it has reasonable equity when called, plays well on many boards, and the raise applies pressure to Flash’s wide opens while also discouraging the BB from coming along. If Flash 4-bets, we can comfortably fold and preserve stack.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jd7d. The action started with a raise to 300 from the Button (Flash), followed by a 3-bet to 1000 from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). Facing a raise and a re-raise, my hand is too weak to continue. J7 suited performs poorly against a 3-betting range, and I am out of position. Additionally, the original raiser (Flash) is still to act behind me, creating a risk of a 4-bet or a squeeze. Investing nearly 10% of my stack in this spot is not profitable. The correct play is to fold.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen offsuit, a strong hand. I opened for 300, and FiveTwo, who has shown significant aggression in previous hands, 3-bet to 1000 from the small blind. While AQo is strong, 4-betting might bloat the pot unnecessarily against a player who is capable of having a wide range but also willing to commit many chips with premium hands. Calling allows me to play a medium-sized pot in position, where I can leverage my positional advantage on the flop. The price to call is 700 to win a total pot of 1400, giving me 2:1 odds, which is more than enough to see a flop with a premium holding.

We flopped trips (6s) with 9 kicker on a paired, relatively dry board in a 3-bet pot, heads-up and OOP. This is a strong value hand but not a hand that wants to bloat the pot with a big c-bet because worse hands (A-high, broadways, 77–TT, some 4x) will continue more often versus a small sizing, while bigger bets fold out too much of Flash’s wide preflop calling range. A small c-bet also keeps our range protected (we’d bet small with overpairs/AK here too) and gives Flash room to continue with bluffs. If raised, we can mostly continue (often by calling) to keep bluffs in and avoid isolating ourselves versus better 6x/boats.

I have Ace-high with premium kickers on a dry, paired board. FiveTwo's small continuation bet of 500 into 2100 (approximately 24% of the pot) is a standard play after 3-betting pre-flop, likely representing a wide range of hands including overcards, pocket pairs, or total air. Folding would be too weak given my equity and the excellent pot odds (5.2:1). Raising would turn my hand into a bluff and potentially bloat the pot against a very aggressive opponent. Calling is the most profitable play, allowing me to leverage my positional advantage on the turn while keeping FiveTwo's bluffs in his range.

Turn brings the Td, which is good for value: Flash now has more second-best hands that can continue (Tx, 77–QQ/occasionally JJ+, some 4x, plus sticky A-high/backdoors from the flop). With trips we still want to keep building, but our kicker is weak and we don’t want to polarize into a big sizing that mostly gets action from better 6x/full houses. A small-to-medium turn barrel keeps his range wide, charges his overcards, and sets up a manageable river value bet while still allowing him to bluff-raise sometimes (which we can usually call).

I have Ace-Queen offsuit on a paired board of 6-6-4-T. FiveTwo 3-bet pre-flop and has now fired two small barrels (500 into 2100 on the flop and 900 into 3100 on the turn). The price to call is very attractive, as I am getting better than 4-to-1 odds (900 to win a total pot of 4900). While the Ten is a better card for a 3-betting range (potentially hitting ATs), FiveTwo's aggression across previous hands suggests he is capable of barreling with overcards like AK or air. Given my positional advantage and the pot odds, folding AQ here would be too tight, as I still have equity against his bluffs and may have the best hand if he is overplaying a hand like AK or AJ. I will call to see a river.

River is a relative brick (no flush/straight completes). We still have trips with a decent kicker, and importantly the board contains two 6s while we hold the third—meaning the only way Flash has a 6 is specifically 6d, so “better trips” and boats exist but are quite combo-limited in a typical BTN-call-3bet range. Flash’s double-call line is therefore much more weighted to Tx, 77–QQ, and some stubborn A-high floats than to 6x.

So we should value bet, but with a small/medium sizing to get paid by those one-pair hands and avoid polarizing ourselves into a size that only continues with 6dX/boats. If we face a big raise, we can comfortably fold because it’s heavily value-dense.

Sizing: ~30% pot targets Tx/overpairs efficiently.

FiveTwo has consistently bet small throughout this hand, placing bets of approximately 24%, 29%, and 31% of the pot on the flop, turn, and river respectively. This sizing is often used by aggressive players to either thin-value bet or to keep bluffs cheap. The river 3 of hearts is a total brick that doesn't change the board state. I am getting excellent pot odds to call (1,500 to win 7,900, or about 5.27 to 1), meaning I only need to have the best hand about 16% of the time to break even. Given FiveTwo's history of high aggression and the possibility that he is barreling with hands like AJ, KQ, or air to push me off my Ace-high, I will make the call. While I lose to any pocket pair or a Ten, Ace-Queen is too strong of a bluff-catcher to fold at these odds in a 4-handed game.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game with 2h5h. While suited connectors have some value, 2h5h is too weak to open from the first position, especially with an aggressive chip leader (FiveTwo) on the button. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

Button vs blinds with AA is a premium value open. We want to start building a pot, deny equity to random holdings, and still keep the raise size callable by both blinds (especially with their sub-140bb stacks). A standard ~3x button open accomplishes this and sets up comfortable postflop sizing.

I am in the Small Blind with QJo facing a 3bb open raise from the Button (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader and has been active. While QJo has decent raw equity against a wide button range, playing it out of position is difficult and prone to domination (by AQ, KQ, AJ). Flat calling from the SB is generally suboptimal due to the positional disadvantage and the risk of a squeeze from the Big Blind. A 3-bet is a possibility, but against a 3x open size and a deep-stacked opponent, it risks bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my 90bb stack for better spots.

I have KJo in the BB facing a button open to 300. KJo is a strong hand to defend here. Against a button open (which should be wide), I can 3-bet for value and to take initiative since I'm out of position. A 3-bet to ~900 puts pressure on FiveTwo and allows me to potentially win the pot preflop or play with initiative postflop.

Claude’s 3-bet to 900 versus our 300 button open is a spot where his range can still contain plenty of worse hands (QQ–TT, AK/AQ, some suited broadway bluffs). With AdAh we want to (1) build the pot for a stacks-in line, (2) deny equity to his bluffs, and (3) use a sizing that keeps his worse continues in while still allowing him to jam. A non-all-in 4-bet accomplishes this better than flatting, and also prevents awkward multi-street SPR where he can realize equity cheaply.

I’ll 4-bet to 2500 (about 2.75x his 3-bet), leaving him room to shove and keeping our range uncapped and value-heavy here.

FiveTwo 4-bet to 2500 after my 3-bet. KJo is a marginal hand against a 4-bet range - dominated by AK, crushed by KK+, and flipping at best vs pairs like QQ/JJ. I'm out of position which makes this worse. The 4-bet sizing is small but calling 1600 more OOP with KJo against what's typically a strong range (even if FiveTwo is aggressive) is -EV. Folding and waiting for a better spot.

4-handed UTG plays much wider than full-ring; AJs suited is a clear open for value and to leverage position/initiative against shorter stacks in the blinds. With a big chip lead, I want to apply pressure and avoid going multiway too cheaply, so I’ll use a solid but not bloated sizing that still allows me to respond comfortably to a 3-bet.

I am on the Button with 7s 6d, an offsuit connector. The player in UTG (FiveTwo), who has a large chip lead and has been playing aggressively, has raised to 250. Even though I have position, 67o is too weak to profitably call an UTG raise, especially with the Small Blind and Big Blind still to act behind me. Calling would be speculative and likely -EV. 3-betting is also ill-advised with this hand against an UTG range. Therefore, folding is the disciplined and correct decision to preserve my stack of 90 BBs for better spots.

67o is a weak hand to defend out of position against an UTG raise. FiveTwo has been active but 67 offsuit doesn't play well OOP - lacks the suited component for flush draws. With Flash still to act behind me who could squeeze, this is a clear fold.

I have Ace-King offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in a 4-handed game. FiveTwo, the chip leader, has been opening frequently. I should 3-bet here for value and to take the initiative, especially since I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. A raise to 900 is a standard sizing to put pressure on his opening range while building a pot with a very strong hand.

Flash’s BB 3-bet versus my 4-handed UTG open can be fairly wide, and AdJd plays well against that range with good playability and nut-flush potential. With position postflop (BB acts first), calling keeps in dominated Ax/Jx and weaker broadways, avoids turning a strong-but-non-premium hand into a 4-bet/fold, and gives me excellent pot odds (650 to win 1200). I’ll take the high-EV route and realize equity in position.

On a King-high flop of Ts-5d-Kh, I have top pair with the best possible kicker (AK). This board is relatively dry, although it does offer some straight draws (QJ, J9). Since I was the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have the range advantage here. FiveTwo's range for calling a 3-bet includes hands like KQs, KJs, and pocket pairs. I want to bet for value to get paid by weaker Kings and to prevent free cards for draws like QJ or AJ. A bet of around 40% of the pot (700 chips) is a standard continuation bet that keeps his range wide while building the pot.

Flash’s small flop c-bet (700 into 2550) is a range bet that can include a lot of unpaired broadways/air from his BB 3-bet range. With AdJd on K♠T♠5♦ we have a gutshot to the nuts (Q makes Broadway) plus backdoor nut-flush potential and good positional leverage. Raising would bloat the pot at a low SPR and force us into uncomfortable follow-throughs with mostly ace-high; calling realizes equity cheaply and keeps his weaker hands in while letting us apply pressure on favorable turns (Q/A/J/diamond, or when he checks).

The turn card is the Jack of Spades, which is a very coordinated card. It completes a straight for AQ and improves several hands that FiveTwo might have called a 3-bet with, such as KJ, KT, or potentially QJ/JT. While I still have Top Pair with the best kicker (AcKd), the board texture has become much more dangerous. By checking, I am exercising pot control and avoiding a difficult decision against a raise from the chip leader. I can comfortably check-call most reasonable bet sizes to see the river, or take a free card if he checks behind.

Turn goes check to me on K♥T♠5♦J♠ and I hold A♦J♦, giving me second pair with top kicker plus a gutshot to the nut straight (Q). After Flash’s small flop c-bet, this turn check is often a range give-up/pot-control with hands like AQ/Q9/underpairs/Tx-type holdings, and we benefit from denying equity to his draws while also getting thin value from worse Jx/Tx that will continue. I’ll use a small-to-medium sizing that keeps the pot manageable versus a check-raise (which will be heavily value) while still pressuring his air and charging draws.

On this turn (Js), the board has become significantly more coordinated. While I still have Top Pair with the best kicker (AK), hands like AQ have completed a straight, and KJ, KT, and JT have improved to two pair. However, FiveTwo's bet of 1200 into a 4450 pot (about 27% pot) is quite small, giving me excellent pot odds of nearly 4.7-to-1. Folding TPTK to such a small bet would be overly exploitable, especially against the chip leader who has shown a willingness to be active in many pots. Raising is unnecessary and would likely only get action from hands that beat me. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see a river where I might also improve to a straight with any Queen.

On the river, the board has paired the Ten, resulting in a final board of Ts-5d-Kh-Js-Tc. My hand is Two Pair, Kings and Tens, with an Ace kicker (Kd-Kh-Tc-Ts-Ac). While this is a strong hand, the board is highly coordinated. Any Queen-Ace (AQ) has a straight, and hands like King-Jack (KJ), King-Ten (KT), and Jack-Ten (JT) also beat me, with the latter two having improved to full houses. Given that I checked the turn to control the pot, checking the river is the most consistent and safest play. It allows me to reach showdown cheaply if FiveTwo checks back with a weaker King or a missed draw, and it prevents me from value-betting myself against the range of hands that called my pre-flop 3-bet and flop continuation bet and then bet the turn.

River pairs the T, giving us two pair (Tens and Jacks) with an Ace kicker (best 5: T♠ T♣ J♠ J♦ A♦). This is a strong showdown hand, but it’s in a bad spot to value-bet because Flash’s BB 3-bet → c-bet → check/call turn range contains a lot of hands that now beat us: any Kx (makes two pair K+T), many Tx (now trips), QQ+ (two pair/overpairs improved), plus some slowplayed AQ (straight). The hands we target for thin value (worse Jx, 99/88, some floats) often fold to a river bet, and with his remaining stack being relatively shallow versus the pot, any check-raise/jam puts us in a nasty spot where we can’t comfortably continue. Best play is to take the free showdown and realize our equity.

I am in UTG position with Ten-Three offsuit (T3o), which is a statistically very weak hand (trash). With 90 big blinds, I am not in a push/fold scenario, but I also have no reason to enter the pot from early position with such poor holdings against three active opponents, including a large stack in the Big Blind. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.
42o is one of the worst hands in poker. Even on the button with good position, this hand has virtually no playability or equity. Folding is the clear play here.

In this 4-handed scenario, the action has folded to me in the Small Blind. I am holding Queen-Eight offsuit (Qd 8s). While this hand has some value in a shorthanded game, the Big Blind (FiveTwo) is the chip leader and has been playing very aggressively in recent hands. Playing a marginal hand out of position against an aggressive big stack is often a recipe for losing chips unnecessarily. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
A8o in UTG position, 4-handed. This is a reasonable open-raising hand in a short-handed game. Standard open to 2.5x.

I am on the button holding 7s 3s. Claude has opened with a raise to 250 from UTG. While these are suited cards, they are extremely weak and have poor connectivity. In a 4-handed game, a raise from the first position (UTG/CO) usually represents a solid range. Given the strength of my hand and the fact that calling would put me in a difficult spot against both the original raiser and the aggressive chip leader (FiveTwo) in the Small Blind, folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

Claude’s 2.5bb open from UTG (4-handed) is still a reasonably strong range, and we’ll be out of position the entire hand. 7♢4♣ has very poor playability (dominated when it makes a pair, no suitedness, weak straight potential) and doesn’t realize equity well from the small blind, especially with the BB still to act. A 3-bet bluff is too low-quality here and risks inflating a pot OOP versus a strong continuing range. Best decision is to cut losses and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ks6s facing a 2.5x raise from Claude in the UTG/CO position. The pot odds are favorable: I need to call 150 chips to win a pot that will be 550, offering me roughly 2.66:1 odds. My hand, King-Six suited, has sufficient equity and playability (flush potential, high card strength) to defend against a standard opening range in a 4-handed game, despite being out of position. Folding would be too tight given the price, and 3-betting is not necessary. I will call and see the flop.

I hold Ks6s on a Qs5sTh flop, giving me the second nut flush draw. As the pre-flop caller in the Big Blind playing against the pre-flop raiser (Claude) who is in position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. The board texture connects well with an UTG opening range (sets, strong queens, straights draws), so leading out (donk betting) is unlikely to be effective. I will check with the intention of continuing strongly (check-call or check-raise) if Claude bets.

Missed the flop with A8o - just ace high on Qs 5s Th. Pro checked to me. Standard c-bet spot as the preflop aggressor. I have two overcards and the initiative. Betting around 1/2 pot to continue representing strength and possibly take it down.

I hold the second nut flush draw (Ks6s) on a Qs5sTh board. The opponent has bet 275 into a pot of 550 (half-pot), offering me pot odds of 25%. My flush draw alone gives me approximately 36% equity (9 outs twice), which is significantly higher than the required equity to call. Additionally, I may have implied odds if I hit my flush. Raising is an option but calling is lower variance and keeps the opponent's range wide, including bluffs that I can beat if I improve or show down high card value later. Being out of position, check-calling is the standard and most efficient line to realize my equity.

On the turn, the board pairs with the Ten of Diamonds, making it [Qs 5s Th Td]. This card interacts heavily with the pre-flop raiser's range (AT, KT, QT, JT). I still hold the King-high flush draw. Being out of position, leading out (donk betting) is generally suboptimal as it exposes me to raises and denies me the chance to see a free river if the opponent checks back. Checking is the standard play to realize my equity. If the opponent bets, I will re-evaluate based on the sizing and pot odds, but for now, checking to the aggressor is the correct move.

Turn paired the tens on Qs 5s Th Td. I have ace high with the As. Pro called my flop c-bet and checked again. The paired board is dangerous - if Pro has any ten they now have trips, and if they have a queen they're still ahead. Firing another barrel with just ace high on a paired board is risky. Better to check back, control pot size, and see a free river where I could potentially hit an ace.

I arrived at the river with King-high after my flush draw missed on a board of Qs-5s-Th-Td-2h. The opponent checked back the turn, which caps their range but often implies marginal showdown value like Ace-high, small pocket pairs (66-99), or a weak Queen that is trying to get to showdown cheaply. While I hold the Ks, which blocks some flush draws the opponent might have been bluffing with, I have very little showdown value myself (beating only missed straight draws like J9 or lower missed flush draws). Turning this hand into a bluff is risky because the opponent's capped range includes many hands (like Ax or small pairs) that are strong enough to call a river bet on this texture. Checking gives me a slim chance to win at showdown against a pure bluff that gave up, while avoiding spewing chips into a range that likely has me beat.

River is Qs 5s Th Td 2h. I have ace high with A8o. Pro has checked to me again. With just ace high, I have virtually no showdown value and very little fold equity if I bet - Pro called my flop c-bet so they likely have at least a pair or draw. Bluffing here is -EV as most hands that called flop will call a river bet. Best to check behind and accept the loss rather than throw more chips at this pot.

I am UTG in a 4-max game, which is equivalent to the Cutoff position. Holding Ten-Seven offsuit (T7o), the hand is too weak to open, especially with aggressive players like FiveTwo still to act and a short stack in the Small Blind. Standard pre-flop strategy dictates a fold here.

4-handed and on the button after UTG folds, we should open quite wide. Pocket sixes plays well as a value/semi-value open: often best preflop versus blinds’ defending ranges, can win immediately, and has good postflop playability (set potential and reasonable showdown value on low boards). Limping invites multiway and reduces fold equity; raising also puts pressure on the shorter SB stack. I’ll use a standard 2.5x open to keep ranges balanced and pot sized appropriately in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h 7h facing a Button raise from the chip leader (FiveTwo), who has been opening frequently. While suited connectors have good playability and I have decent depth (84 BBs), playing out of position against an aggressive big stack is difficult. A call would offer decent pot odds (200 to win 400) but suffering from poor equity realization OOP makes this a marginal spot. A 3-bet is an option to attack their wide range, but given FiveTwo's history of massive 4-bet/5-bet aggression (e.g., Hand #16, #39), bluffing is risky. A disciplined fold avoids bleeding chips in a disadvantageous spot.

5s9h is a weak offsuit hand. FiveTwo raised from button - this is a steal position but 95o is at the bottom of my defending range. Getting 2.67:1 pot odds (150 to win 400), but this hand plays poorly postflop and I'll often be dominated or have difficulty realizing equity. FiveTwo has a big stack and has been aggressive. Folding is the disciplined play here.

4-handed UTG ranges do widen, but 96o is still a weak offsuit connector with poor high-card value and reverse-implied-odds issues when called/3-bet (dominated by the broadways and better 9x/6x that will defend). With deep stacks in the blinds and a short-ish Button behind, opening doesn’t generate enough fold equity versus competent defenses to justify investing chips. I’ll pass and preserve my edge for better opens.

Action is on me on the Button with 4 players remaining. The hand is A5o (Ace-Five offsuit). In a 4-handed game, the Button opening range should be quite wide (typically 40-50%+), and A5o falls well within this range as it holds an Ace blocker and plays reasonably well in position if called. While A5o is not a premium hand, folding it on the Button when folded to is generally too tight. The standard opening size at this table has been around 2.5BB (250 chips). I will raise to 250 to attempt to steal the blinds or play the pot in position against the blinds.

K7o in the SB facing a button raise. This is a weak hand to defend out of position. K7o lacks the connectivity and suited quality to play profitably against a button raising range. Folding is the disciplined play here rather than calling 200 with a dominated king or weak kicker situation that will be tough to navigate postflop OOP.

I am in the Big Blind holding 5d 6s. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. I need to call 150 more to see a flop. While the pot odds are roughly 2.66 to 1, 5-6 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against a button open. The playability post-flop is poor, and I'm likely to face further aggression. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game with Pocket Sevens (7c 7s) and a stack of roughly 85 BB. In a short-handed format, pocket sevens are a strong enough hand to open-raise for value and to deny equity to overcards. Folding is too tight, and limping encourages aggressive players behind to isolate. I will raise to 2.5x BB (250), which is a standard opening size at this table, allowing me to represent a strong range while keeping the pot manageable out of position.

I have 8h7h on the button facing a min-raise from UTG. Suited connectors play well in position, and I'm closing the action with only the blinds behind me. Calling is standard here to see a flop with a hand that has good playability and implied odds if I hit.

I'm holding pocket threes in the small blind. Pro raised from UTG and Claude called on the button. This creates a multi-way pot with good implied odds for set-mining. Given the stack depths and the relatively small price to call (200 more into a 650 pot), calling is the most profitable play to see if I can hit a set on the flop.

We’re getting an excellent price in the big blind: 150 more to win a pot of 850 (and it becomes 1000 if we call), so we only need ~15% equity. Even 62o has enough raw equity multiway when we’re closing the action, and our deep stack gives us implied odds when we flop two-pair/trips/strong draws. A 3-bet bluff is poor here (low blockers, out of position vs multiple ranges), so the best EV line is to defend and play cautiously postflop, mostly check/folding on bad textures and continuing only when we connect well.

I have flopped a full house (threes over aces) on a board of Ad-Ah-2d. This is a very strong but also very vulnerable hand if an ace or a higher pair is out there. However, given the pre-flop action where everyone just called or checked the original raise, it is less likely someone has an Ace, although it is certainly possible for anyone to have one. Since I am out of position and the board is paired, leading out might scare away hands I want to extract value from. Checking is the best play here to see how the other three players react. If someone bets, I can evaluate whether to call or raise based on the bet size and the player's tendencies.

Board is Ah Ad 2d in a 4-way pot. With 6s2h we have aces and deuces (two pair), which is strong versus the many hands that whiff and only play the board, but it’s still clearly behind any Ax (trips) and crushed by A2/22. Betting out multiway from the BB mostly folds out worse and gets action from better, while exposing us to raises that put us in a tough spot. Checking keeps the pot controlled, lets Pro/Claude c-bet their range, and allows us to continue as a bluff-catcher/value-catcher depending on sizing and action behind.

I am in a 4-way pot on a Flop of [Ah 2d Ad] holding Pocket Sevens. This board texture hits the calling ranges of the blinds and the button (who often have Ax hands) significantly. While I have the range advantage as the UTG opener (holding AK, AQ, etc.), the probability that at least one of my three opponents holds an Ace is high. Betting here accomplishes little: worse hands (like 55, 66, King-high) will likely fold, and better hands (any Ace, 88+) will call or raise. My hand has showdown value, but it is not strong enough to value bet 4-way. Checking allows me to control the pot size, potentially see a cheap turn, and re-evaluate if action heats up. I will check back.

Board is Ah-2d-Ad with three opponents checking to me. I have 87hh - no pair, just a backdoor heart flush draw. This is a very dry paired board where an ace is likely out there trapping. With three opponents and minimal equity, betting as a bluff is risky. Better to take the free card and see if I can hit running hearts for the flush.

Reviewing the hand, I have pocket threes on a board of Ah-2d-Ad-8s. My current hand is two pair, Aces and Threes. My previous assessment that I flopped a full house was incorrect; to have a full house, there would need to be another Ace or another Three on the board. As it stands, my hand is quite weak. Any Ace has three of a kind, any pocket pair higher than 33 has a superior two pair, and even a random 8 in an opponent's hand makes a better two pair (Aces and Eights). Since the flop checked around, it's possible no one has an Ace, but I am out of position against three opponents. Betting into this board would not be for value, as I likely only get called by hands that beat me. I will check and look to potentially fold if there is significant betting action, or get to a cheap showdown if possible.

4-way pot on Ah Ad 2d 8s with 6s2h gives us AA22 with a 6 kicker: decent showdown value versus pure air, but we’re behind any Ax (trips) and now also behind most pocket pairs (33+ make AAKK/AAQQ/etc) and any 8x (AA88). Since everyone checked flop, ranges are somewhat capped but still contain slowplayed aces and plenty of hands that either beat us or will just fold to a bet. Leading out mainly gets called by better and folds out worse, plus exposes us to a raise in a miserable spot. Best is to keep the pot controlled, take the free card, and be ready to bluff-catch selectively versus small/medium river bets depending on who fires and sizing.

On the Turn, the board is [Ah 2d Ad 8s]. The pot is 4-way, and everyone checked the flop. The 8s likely connects with some defending ranges (89, 87, A8, 88), and any random Ace still beats me. While the flop checks suggest weakness, betting here with 77 achieves very little. Better hands (Ax, 8x, 99+) will call or raise, and worse hands (KQ, low pairs) will fold. My hand serves best as a bluff-catcher or thin showdown value. Checking allows me to control the pot size and hopefully see a free River to realize my equity. I do not want to build a pot with a marginal underpair to the board's trips.

I now have a pair of 8s with the paired aces on board. Everyone has checked twice on this A-A-2-8 board. My 8s could be the best hand if no one has an ace, but betting into 3 opponents risks waking up a slowplayed ace or better pocket pair. With marginal showdown value, I'll check to see a free river and potentially win at showdown or fold to aggression.

On a board of Ah-2d-Ad-8s-Tc, my pocket threes give me two pair, Aces and Threes (A-A-T-8-3). This hand is extremely weak in a four-way pot, especially since the Ten and Eight on the board likely improved someone else's two pair if they weren't already ahead with a higher pocket pair or an Ace. Given that the flop and turn checked around, it's possible no one has a strong hand, but there is no value in betting here as I won't get called by worse hands. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown, though I will likely fold to any significant bet.

River is Ah Ad 2d 8s Tc and we hold 6s2h, giving AA22T. This beats all the pure “board” hands (AAT82), but loses to any Ax (trips), any 8x/Tx (higher two pair), and almost all pocket pairs (AA33+). In a 4-way pot with three streets checked, a lead here is very thin: worse hands mostly fold, while better hands (pairs/8x/Tx/Ax) are the ones that continue or raise. Checking keeps the pot controlled, realizes our showdown value, and avoids getting put in a bad spot versus a raise; we can consider bluff-catching only versus a small stab behind.

On the River, the board reads [Ah 2d Ad 8s Tc]. The pot is 1000 chips, and the action has been checked around on both the Flop and Turn. Flash and FiveTwo (the blinds) have checked to me again. I hold Pocket Sevens. While my hand has some showdown value, the board runout with the 8 and T connects fairly well with the blinds' wide defending ranges (e.g., T9, J8, QT). Any Ace, Ten, or Eight beats me. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands (like 66, 55, or King-high), and will likely only be called by better hands. Checking back gives me a chance to win at showdown against missed flush draws or smaller pocket pairs without risking more chips in a spot where I am frequently beaten.

River completes with Ah-2d-Ad-8s-Tc. I have a pair of 8s (my 8h pairs with the 8s). Everyone has checked through flop, turn, and now river. While the checking indicates likely weakness and no ace, the Ten on the river now beats my 8s if anyone holds a ten. Pocket pairs 99+ also beat me. With marginal showdown value against 3 opponents and the risk of being check-raised if I bet, checking to showdown is optimal. I might win but betting risks only getting called/raised by better.
8-4 offsuit is a very weak hand. From UTG position, I need strong holdings to open. This is an easy fold.

I am on the button with 7d 6h, which is a very weak hand. While the button is an ideal position to steal the blinds, this specific hand doesn't have enough equity to justify a raise against a deep-stacked Small Blind (FiveTwo) and a relatively short Big Blind (Pro). Folding is the most disciplined and safe play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Folds to me in the SB heads-up versus the BB. QJs is well ahead of the BB’s random holding and plays very well with initiative (strong Broadway potential, good playability, can barrel many boards). Completing invites the BB to realize equity too cheaply and makes postflop harder out of position. I’ll open-raise a standard sizing to pressure the BB’s wide defend range while keeping it small enough to avoid bloating pots unnecessarily.

I am in the Big Blind with J3o facing a 3x raise (300) from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). Although I would have position post-flop, J3o is a very weak hand with poor playability and reverse implied odds (weak kicker issues if I hit a Jack). Given the raise size offering me 3:1 odds (calling 200 to win 600), my equity is marginal and likely not realized fully. Against the chip leader who is applying pressure, it is prudent to fold this trash hand and preserve my stack of roughly 82 big blinds for better spots.

I am holding Ace-Five suited (As 5s) in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. This is a strong starting hand with both high-card value and excellent semi-bluffing potential due to its ability to make the nut flush or a wheel straight. Given the small field, I want to open for a standard raise to take control of the pot and potentially take down the blinds or define the range of my opponents. A raise to 3BB (300) is consistent with table dynamics and appropriate for my stack size.

Flash’s UTG open (to 3bb) in 4-handed is still a reasonably strong range, and 9♠5♦ offsuit has very poor playability, is dominated when it makes a pair, and doesn’t realize equity well—especially if the blinds come along. With no suitedness/connectivity and no useful blockers for a profitable 3-bet bluff, the best stack-maximizing decision is to fold and wait for a better spot in position.

I am in the Small Blind facing a 3bb raise from Flash in UTG. My hand, Queen-Six suited (Q6s), is marginal. While suited hands have some playability, Q6 is easily dominated by a UTG opening range (which typically includes AQ, KQ, QJs, and pairs 66+). Being out of position post-flop against an early position raiser significantly reduces my realizable equity. The pot odds (needing ~36% equity) are borderline against a strong range, and the positional disadvantage makes calling a losing play in the long run. 3-betting is too loose here. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

K8o is a marginal hand facing a UTG raise. UTG typically has a stronger range, and K8o plays poorly out of position. Getting 2.25:1 odds isn't enough to compensate for playing this weak hand OOP. Folding is the disciplined play here.

4-handed but still first to act with 7-2 offsuit, the very bottom of range. With no antes, the pot is only 150 and the BTN/SB/BB are all deep enough to defend/3-bet frequently (and have shown aggression), so the fold equity needed to make an open profitable is unlikely. Limping just invites an iso and puts us in poor postflop spots. Preserve chips and keep our opening range strong from UTG.

Playing 4-handed on the Button with Ace-Three offsuit. After the UTG player folds, this is a standard spot to open-raise to steal the blinds. Although A3o is a marginal hand, holding an Ace provides a blocker effect, reducing the likelihood that the remaining two opponents (in the blinds) hold an Ace. Furthermore, in Hand #44, I successfully stole the blinds from the Button with A5o against the same opponents, suggesting the Big Blind (Flash) is capable of folding to steals. I will raise to 250 (2.5bb) to apply pressure. If I face a 3-bet, I can easily fold without committing too many chips.

J5o is a weak offsuit hand in the small blind facing a button raise. This hand plays poorly out of position - it's easily dominated and has poor playability postflop. With Flash still to act behind me in the BB who could squeeze, calling here is -EV. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am in the Big Blind with Td6d, a suited hand with some connectivity. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250, and the Small Blind has folded. It costs me 150 more to call into a pot of 400, giving me almost 2.7-to-1 odds. While T6s is not a premium hand, it plays reasonably well post-flop, and I am closing the action. I have a healthy stack and can afford to see a flop and realize my equity.

The flop is Jc As Kc, which is an extremely heavy and high-card board. As the Big Blind holding Td6d, I have no part of this board. I have ten-high with no draw, as the straight draws (Qx) are not in my hand and I have no clubs for a flush draw. Pro, the pre-flop raiser, is very likely to have connected with this broadway-heavy board. However, since the current action is checked to me, there is no reason to bet into the pre-flop aggressor on a texture that favors their range significantly. I will check and hope to see a free turn card, though I am prepared to fold to any meaningful aggression.

I hold Ac3h on a board of Jc As Kc. I have Top Pair (Aces) with a weak kicker and a backdoor nut flush draw. The board texture is extremely wet and interacts very well with the Big Blind's defending range (which contains many broadway hands like Kx, Jx, QT, KT, KJ, AJ, etc.). While I have top pair, my kicker is poor, meaning I am dominated by almost all other Ax hands. Additionally, betting here is thin for value because worse hands (like middle pair Jx) might fold given the scary board, and better hands (stronger Ax, two pair, straights) will certainly continue or raise. By checking back, I control the pot size, avoid getting check-raised, and can comfortably call a bet on safe turns or get to showdown cheaply. Holding the Ac also blocks the nut flush draw, slightly reducing the number of draw-heavy hands the opponent might continue with.

The board is now Jc-As-Kc-Kd. I have Ten-high and no draw. Pro, the pre-flop aggressor, checked back the flop. While this might indicate weakness, this board is so heavily weighted toward Broadway cards (Aces, Kings, Queens, Jacks) that it is very difficult to represent a hand I don't have. I have no showdown value, but there is no reason to bet as a bluff here when the opponent's range is so likely to have connected or be holding a better high card. I will check and likely fold to any bet.

I hold Ac3h on a board of Jc As Kc Kd. I have two pair, Aces and Kings, with a weak kicker (3). The King on the turn is a very bad card for me. The Big Blind's defending range is heavy on King-X hands (Kxs, Kxo), meaning they likely just made trips. Additionally, any better Ace dominates me, and a straight (QT) is possible. There are very few worse hands that call a bet (maybe a sticky Jx or a flush draw), but many better hands that will continue. Checking back controls the pot size and allows me to try to get to showdown cheaply against missed draws or weaker pairs.

The board is A-K-K-J-4, which is extremely heavy with high cards. I have Ten-high, which has no showdown value. However, the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has checked back both the flop and the turn. This suggests they either have a marginal hand they are trying to take to showdown cheaply (like a small pocket pair or a Jack) or they have completely missed. While I could attempt a bluff to represent a Jack or a slow-played King, this board texture is so connected to a Button raising range that it's unlikely Pro will fold anything that beats me. Given the small pot size and the likelihood that even Pro's 'missed' hands (like Q-high) still beat me, I will check and concede the pot rather than risking chips on a low-frequency bluff.

I hold Ac3h on a final board of Jc As Kc Kd 4d. My best five-card hand is Two Pair (Kings and Aces) with a Jack kicker (K-K-A-J-4). The action has been checked through on the flop and turn. The Big Blind's range for defending and then checking three times likely consists of weak pairs (Jx, underpairs), missed straight/flush draws, or perhaps a weak Ace or King being pot-controlled. 

However, almost any Ace in the opponent's range (A5+) out-kicks me, and any King makes trips. A bet here is unlikely to get called by worse hands (like Jx) often enough to be profitable, while better hands (better Ax, Kx) will never fold and might even check-raise. I have showdown value against missed draws and some lower pairs, but my hand is too thin to value bet. Checking back ensures I realize my equity without risking more chips in a spot where I am often either way ahead or way behind.

I am in UTG position with Jd 4h, which is a very weak off-suit hand. With three players yet to act and no investment in the pot other than the ante (if any, though none specified, blinds are 50/100), this hand has very low equity and poor playability. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand from any position, especially early position. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

6d3d on the button is a weak hand even when suited. While I have position, this is a marginal stealing hand at best. FiveTwo in the BB has a large stack and has shown aggression, making steal attempts risky. The suited aspect gives some playability but 63s is near the bottom of hands I'd want to open. Folding preserves chips for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit. The action has folded to me. While this is a blind-versus-blind situation, T3o is a very weak hand with poor playability post-flop. FiveTwo in the Big Blind is the chip leader and has shown significant aggression throughout the game. Trying to steal or completing the blind with such a weak hand out of position against a loose-aggressive big stack is generally a losing play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

K4s from UTG in a 4-handed game is marginal. While suited Kings have some value, K4s is at the bottom of my opening range from this position. The hand plays poorly postflop, gets dominated by better Kings, and UTG is the tightest position even 4-handed. With a healthy stack of 137BB, I don't need to force marginal spots. Folding is the disciplined play.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit on the button, which is a premium starting hand in a 4-handed game. Since the first player folded, I will put in a standard open-raise to 300 to build the pot and exert positional pressure on the blinds.

Facing a BTN min-open to 300 from Flash, Q7o is a dominated offsuit queen that plays poorly out of position in a single-raised pot and even worse if the BB comes along or squeezes. Flash has shown willingness to continue versus aggression, so turning this into a light 3-bet bluff is low EV. Best is to preserve stack and wait for stronger defend/3-bet candidates (suited hands, better blockers).

I am in the Big Blind with QJo facing a 3x raise from the Button (Flash). My hand is strong enough to defend against a wide Button opening range, given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 450, requiring ~31% equity). QJo has roughly 47% equity against a top 50% range. While being out of position is a disadvantage, the stack depth (83 BB effective) allows for maneuverability. 3-betting is an option but risks isolating myself against a stronger range that dominates me (AQ, AJ, KQ), so calling is the standard and prudent play to keep the opponent's range wide.

I have flopped Top Pair with a strong kicker (QJ on J-4-4). The board texture is very dry and paired, which is generally good for the pre-flop raiser's range, meaning the Button is likely to continuation bet frequently here. As the defender in the Big Blind, my standard line with strong made hands is to check to the aggressor to allow them to bluff or value bet worse hands. Donk betting would likely fold out their air (which has almost 0 equity) or isolate me against better hands (AJ, KK+). I plan to check-call to keep their range wide.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with Ace-Queen offsuit on a dry, paired flop of 4c-4s-Jd. Since my opponent checked to me, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet. I have two overcards to the board and can represent a Jack or a pocket pair easily. A small bet should be enough to fold out many of the Big Blind's air hands while keeping the pot manageable if I am called.

I hold Top Pair with a Queen kicker (TPGK) on a dry, paired board (J-4-4). The opponent (Flash) has bet roughly 1/3 pot (225 into 650), which is a standard c-bet size on this texture often representing a wide range including air, small pocket pairs, and better hands. Folding is incorrect due to my hand strength. Raising (check-raising) would likely fold out his bluffs (which I want to keep in) and isolate me against hands that beat me (AJ, KJ, overpairs, trips). Calling allows me to control the pot size, keep his range wide, and evaluate the turn. My equity is well sufficient to call a small bet.

On the Turn, the Ace of Diamonds is a significant scare card. It interacts heavily with the Button's pre-flop raising and flop c-betting range, as they will have many Ax hands. My hand (QJ) has been downgraded to second pair. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get called by better hands (Ax, better Jx, trips) and fold out bluffs. Checking is the prudent play to control the pot size. I want to see if the opponent checks back (taking a free card or pot controlling with a weak Ace/Jack) or continues aggression. If they check back, I have good showdown value on the river. If they bet, I will re-evaluate based on sizing.

The Ace on the turn is an excellent card for my hand, improving my Ace-Queen to top pair with a strong kicker on a board that is now very likely to be ahead of my opponent's range. Since Pro checked again, they likely have a marginal made hand like a Jack or a smaller pair, or they are drawing. I will bet for value, aiming to get called by Jacks or weaker Aces. A half-pot bet of 550 is a standard sizing here to build the pot while keeping my range balanced.

I hold JsQd on a board of 4c-4s-Jd-Ad. I check-called a c-bet on the flop with top pair. The turn brings an Ace, which is a significant scare card that hits the Button's opening and barreling range (Ax) very hard. The opponent bets 550 into 1100 (half pot). While I have showdown value with second pair, the Ace heavily favors the opponent's range. My hand blocks some value combos (AJ, AQ) but importantly blocks many natural semi-bluff candidates (KQ, KT, QT, and Queen-high flush draws). By blocking the bluffs I beat, the opponent's betting range becomes weighted more heavily towards value hands (Ax, 4x, full houses) that dominate me. Calling puts me in a 'way behind' situation with little improvement potential on the river. Folding preserves my stack in a spot where my range has plenty of better hands to defend (Ax, 4x).

I am UTG in a 4-handed game with Nine-Seven offsuit. This hand is significantly below the standard opening range for the first position to act, even in a short-handed environment. Given that the chip leader (FiveTwo) is on the button and likely to play back at light opens, and the small blind (Pro) is short-stacked and may look for a spot to shove, folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for a better opportunity.

4-handed on the button we should open very wide, and our big stack can apply pressure to two medium/short stacks in the blinds. 4♣3♠ isn’t strong, but it has some playability (wheel/straight/low boards) and plays best in position. Raising also denies the blinds a cheap realization versus a limp, and picks up 150 chips immediately at a good frequency. If faced with a significant 3-bet, this hand is an easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d 3c, facing a Button raise from the chip leader FiveTwo. My hand is extremely weak (6-high, unsuited, unconnected) and performs poorly against even a wide Button opening range. Although the pot odds are decent, playing a trash hand out of position is a losing proposition in the long run. Folding is the only reasonable option.

Q2o is a very weak hand to defend from the BB. Even though button opens wide, Q2o plays poorly post-flop - easily dominated by better queens and has little playability. Folding is the correct play rather than investing 150 more with such a marginal hand.

7♠3♠ is near the very bottom of playable suited hands even 4-handed. From UTG (first to act) we’ll be out of position versus the blinds and can get punished by frequent 3-bets, and the hand realizes equity poorly when called (dominated pairs/top-pairs, weak straight potential). As chip leader, there’s no need to take thin, high-variance opens here; we can pick better steal candidates and spots to pressure shorter stacks.

I am on the Button with 6d 5h (65o), a weak off-suit connector. The UTG player folded. While the Button is a great position to steal the blinds, 65o is at the very bottom of a stealing range and often not included due to poor playability post-flop compared to suited connectors. With active stacks in the blinds (135bb and 131bb), they have plenty of room to defend or 3-bet. Folding is the standard and prudent play here to preserve chips for better spots.

Heads up against the BB with 6s9d. This is a weak offsuit hand, but I'm getting good odds to complete (50 to win 150). Folding loses my 50 already posted. Completing lets me see a flop cheaply and the hand has some straight potential. Raising with this weak hand risks getting 3-bet. I'll complete.

I am in the Big Blind with King of Diamonds and Six of Hearts (K6o). The Small Blind (Claude) has just completed the blind, and the pot is now 200. Since I am already in for the 100 big blind and there is no raise, I have the option to check and see a free flop or raise to try and take the pot down immediately. K6o is a mediocre hand that plays well enough in a limped pot, especially since I will have position on Claude for the rest of the hand. There is no significant reason to bloat the pot with a marginal hand pre-flop. I will check to see the flop for free and leverage my position on later streets.

Flop is AKQ with two hearts - a very coordinated broadway board. My 6s9d has completely missed. I have no pair, no flush draw, and no straight draw. This board heavily favors hands that would call preflop. Betting here as a bluff makes no sense - I have zero equity if called and Flash's range connects well with this texture. Easy check.

I have middle pair (Kings) with a weak kicker on a very coordinated board (Q-K-A). Claude limped from the small blind and checked the flop. While I have a pair, this board hits many parts of a standard limping range, including straight draws (JT), flush draws (hearts), and weak Aces. Betting here likely only gets called by hands that beat me or strong draws that have significant equity against me. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and can evaluate the turn in position.

Turn brought a meaningless 3d. I still have complete air - no pair, no draw, nothing. Flash checked behind on the flop which could indicate weakness, but bluffing here with zero equity makes no sense. If I bet and get called, I'm drawing dead. Check and give up on this pot.

I have middle pair (Kings) with a weak kicker (6) on a very coordinated board (A-K-Q-3). Claude limped from the small blind and has checked both the flop and the turn. While my hand has showdown value, betting into this board texture is risky because Claude's limping range can contain many weak Aces or better Kings that are simply playing passively. By checking back, I control the pot size and ensure I get to the river to see if I can win at showdown or evaluate a potential river bet from Claude in position.

River 8d changes nothing. I have complete air with 6-high. Flash has checked twice showing weakness, but the pot is only 200. Bluffing here for a tiny pot with zero equity if called makes no sense. Even if Flash has weak holdings, they likely have at least ace-high or a pair which beats my 6-high. Just check and concede.

I have middle pair (Kings) on a board with an Ace, King, and Queen. Claude limped from the small blind and has checked every street, including the river. While my hand has showdown value, it is unlikely to get called by worse hands if I bet, as most hands that would call likely contain an Ace or a better King. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value in a small pot and see if my Kings are good without risking more chips.

I am in UTG position with Ten of Spades and Seven of Diamonds (T7o). This is a statistically weak hand that is disconnected and unsuited. Even with only 4 players left, this hand falls well outside a reasonable opening range from the first position. My stack is healthy at 79 BB, so there is no pressure to make loose plays. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips for better spots.

On the button with 7s4c - a very weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity. While I have position, this hand is near the bottom of any reasonable opening range. FiveTwo in the BB has a large stack and has shown aggression. The expected value of attempting a steal here with such a weak holding is likely negative. Better to wait for a stronger spot.

It is folded to me in the small blind. 9-8 offsuit is a strong enough hand to attempt a steal in a blind-vs-blind situation. Although the big blind, FiveTwo, is the chip leader and has shown a tendency for aggression, a standard 3x raise is appropriate here to represent a strong range and take down the pot pre-flop. If called, the hand has decent connectivity for post-flop play, though I will need to be cautious acting out of position.

Flash is opening from the small blind into a big blind defense spot, so his range is wide. J7o is too weak to 3-bet (poor playability when called and dominated by his continuing range), but it’s strong enough to defend versus a 3x SB open given the pot odds (200 to win 600). I’ll take the lower-variance line, realize equity, and use postflop decisions to outplay rather than inflate the pot OOP with a marginal hand.

On a board of 9-J-T, I have hit bottom pair (9s) and an open-ended straight draw (8 outs). This is a highly coordinated board that likely connects well with the Big Blind's defending range (which includes many Jx, Tx, and suited connectors). Being out of position against the chip leader, FiveTwo, I want to exercise some pot control while realizing my equity. Checking allows me to see how the opponent reacts. If they bet, I have sufficient equity with the pair and the straight draw to check-call and see a turn card.

SB opened wide and then checked this very dynamic JT9 board, which often caps him to a lot of A-high/underpairs/marginal one-pair while still containing some traps and strong draws. With top pair (J7) plus a gutshot to the 8, our hand wants value and protection versus his many overcards and straight-draw combos, but we don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a weak kicker. A small stab accomplishes both: it extracts from worse Jx/Tx/9x and denies equity to hands like AQ/KQ/Q8 while keeping our range resilient if he check-raises.

On this highly coordinated board of 9-J-T, I have hit bottom pair and an open-ended straight draw. FiveTwo has led out with a small bet of 250 into a 600 pot. This price is excellent for me to continue, as my pot odds are roughly 3.4:1. I have 8 clean outs for a straight (any Queen or Seven), and my pair of nines provides some additional showdown value or outs to two-pair/trips. Raising is unnecessary and potentially expensive against an aggressive chip leader on a board that hits their range well. Calling is the most profitable and cautious play to realize my equity.

On the turn 2c, which is essentially a blank, I still hold bottom pair and an open-ended straight draw. I am out of position against the chip leader, FiveTwo, who showed aggression on the flop. Checking here is the most prudent move to control the size of the pot and see how the opponent reacts. If FiveTwo bets, the odds offered by the pot, combined with my 8 straight outs and potential to improve my pair, make it a comfortable call. Leading out myself risks a raise that would force me to play for a much larger portion of my stack with a drawing hand.

Flash has checked twice on a very dynamic JT9 board after opening SB, which keeps his range wide and often capped to A-high/underpairs, Tx/9x, and a lot of straight-draws (KQ/Q8/87, etc.), with some traps still possible. Our J7 is top pair but a weak kicker and is very vulnerable to overcards/straight runouts, so checking back gives up too much equity and invites tough river spots. A medium-small turn bet targets calls from worse one-pair hands and draws while charging equity; if he check-raises big we can comfortably fold this kicker.

On the turn 2c, which is a blank card, I still hold bottom pair and an open-ended straight draw (8 outs for the straight, plus 5 outs for trips or two-pair). The pot is 1700 and I need to call 600, giving me pot odds of approximately 3.4:1 (or 26% equity required). My probability of hitting the straight alone is about 17.4% on the river, and including other outs to improve my hand brings the total equity to roughly 28%. Given that I am closing the action on this street and have potential implied odds if I hit a straight against the chip leader's aggressive range, calling is the most mathematically sound and strategically safe play.

The river card is a 6, which is a complete blank and doesn't improve my pair of nines or complete my straight draw. The board remains very coordinated (J-T-9), and my bottom pair has limited showdown value against the Big Blind's range, which includes many Jacks, Tens, and better Nines. Betting into the chip leader here offers little value as I'm unlikely to fold out better hands or get called by worse. Checking is the best option to attempt to reach showdown cheaply or evaluate a potential bluff from the opponent.

River is a pure bluff-catcher spot now. After SB open then check/call flop and turn on a very dynamic JT9 board, Flash’s range is heavy on made hands that can comfortably call (a lot of Jx, some two-pair) plus the key straights (KQ was already made; 87 specifically gets there on the 6). When he checks river, worse hands we beat (missed Q8/K8 type draws, some 9x/Tx) are not guaranteed to call a third barrel, while better Jx and straights either call or can check-raise, making a thin value bet high-risk/low-reward. Taking the showdown with J7 keeps our EV solid and avoids getting punished by the portions of his range that improved or were slowplayed.
4c2c suited from UTG is too weak to open. Even though it's suited, 42s has very poor playability and equity. Folding is the clear standard play here.

I am on the button in a 4-handed game. While Q6o is sometimes an opening candidate in very loose button strategies, it is generally considered a weak hand. Given that FiveTwo (Small Blind) is the chip leader and has shown significant aggression throughout the tournament, and Pro (Big Blind) is playing a solid stack, I expect a high frequency of defense or 3-bets. Q6o does not play well post-flop or against resistance. With 122 BBs and plenty of hands remaining in the tournament, I will opt for a conservative fold and wait for a hand with better equity or playability.

Folds to me in the SB heads-up vs the BB. TJs is a strong, highly playable hand that benefits from taking initiative and building a pot when we can realize equity well on many textures. With Pro at ~78bb and out of position, I want to apply pressure and deny him a cheap look; a standard 3x raise is a good balance between fold equity and keeping his weaker range in.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c8c facing a raise to 300 from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). This is a Blind vs Blind scenario where I have position post-flop. 98s is a very strong defending hand due to its playability and suited connector nature. I am getting 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win 400), and my equity against a wide SB opening range is significantly higher than the required 33%. Calling allows me to realize my equity in position without bloating the pot against the chip leader.
