This 7h-5h-4d flop interacts much more with Pro’s BB defend range (pairs, 76/65/54, 66/88, heart combos) than with my SB raising range, so a high-frequency c-bet isn’t ideal. With TdJh I have two overcards but very little immediate equity and only a backdoor heart draw; if I bet and face a raise, I’m forced to fold and burn equity. Checking keeps the pot controlled, protects my checking range on this texture, and lets me realize my overcard equity while being able to respond to Pro’s sizing if he stabs.

I am in position (BB) against the Small Blind on a [7h 5h 4d] flop. I hold [8c 9c], giving me two overcards and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6 to make a straight). The Small Blind checked to me. This board texture hits the Big Blind's calling range very well, so the check from the SB is expected. Betting here has merit to deny equity, but my hand has good playability and I want to realize my equity for free rather than risking a check-raise on this wet board. I will check back to see a free turn.

Turn improves us to top pair (JTs on 7h-5h-4d-Js). After the flop checks through, Pro’s range is still full of 7x/5x/4x, pocket pairs (66–TT), straight draws (86/63/A3), and heart draws that will call a reasonable bet. We want to start extracting value and charge draws while the pot is still small. A medium sizing also keeps us from overcommitting with a marginal kicker and lets us comfortably fold if Pro shows unexpected aggression with a raise (likely two pair/sets/straights).

I am in the Big Blind with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6) on a board of 7h 5h 4d Js. The pot is 1000 after the Small Blind's turn bet of 400. I need to call 400 to win 1400, requiring approximately 28.5% equity. My gutshot only gives me 4 outs, which is roughly 8.7% equity with one card to come. Even considering potential implied odds or the slight chance my overcards are live, the price is too steep to continue against a turn lead that likely represents a pair of Jacks or better. The disciplined play is to fold.

In a 4-max game, being UTG is equivalent to being in the Cutoff position in a full-ring game. Queen-Jack offsuit is a standard opening hand in this position. I have a healthy stack of 122 Big Blinds and want to apply pressure to the shorter stack in the Small Blind (Pro) and the manageable stack in the Big Blind (Claude). A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to take control of the pot early.

Flash’s 3bb open from UTG (4-handed) is likely fairly strong/standard, and 5♠3♦ offsuit has extremely poor playability and no blockers. Calling needs too much realization (and invites squeezes from the blinds), and turning this into a 3-bet bluff is low quality with no equity/backdoor potential. Preserve stack and wait for better steal/3-bet candidates in position.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Five offsuit (K5o). Flash, the UTG player, has raised to 300 (3bb). K5o is a very weak hand to play against a UTG opening range, as it is often dominated by better Kings (AK, KQ, KJ, KT) and performs poorly against pairs. Additionally, playing out of position for the rest of the hand puts me at a significant strategic disadvantage. The pot odds do not justify a call given these factors. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack.

I have Ad7d in the BB facing a UTG raise to 300. Suited ace plays well postflop with flush potential and ace-high value. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 450). A7s is a solid defend against UTG - good implied odds if I hit the flush or two pair. Calling is standard here rather than 3-betting since A7s is better as a flat given its mediocre kicker.

Completely whiffed this flop with Ad7d on Kc Js 3s. No pair, no diamond draw (board has spades), no straight draw. Against UTG raiser who likely has strong range hitting this board well, I have no reason to lead out. Check and evaluate - will fold to any reasonable bet since I have zero equity here.

I have middle pair (Jacks) with a Queen kicker on a King-high board. In this scenario, my hand has decent showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for three streets of value. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and avoid getting check-raised by a King or a strong draw. This also allows me to keep Claude's weaker range in the hand, which might lead to him bluffing or value-owning himself on later streets. I have position and can comfortably evaluate the turn based on the card and Claude's next move.

Turn brought the 2s, putting three spades on board. I have Ad7d - no pair, no spade for a flush draw, no straight draw. Flash checked back the flop which could indicate weakness or a spade draw that just got there. With essentially zero equity and no reason to bluff into a board that hits Flash's range well (could have any spade for a flush), checking is the only sensible play. I'll fold to any bet.

I have middle pair (Jacks) with a Queen kicker. The turn card (2s) completes the spade flush draw. Claude has checked to me twice. While my hand has some value, it is not strong enough to bet for value against a range that could have easily checked a King or a completed flush to me on this board. By checking back, I continue to control the pot size and keep the pot small, aiming to reach a cheap showdown or catch a bluff on the river. Betting here risks a check-raise that would force me to fold a hand with decent showdown equity.

River brought the Ah giving me top pair with Ad7d. Flash has checked both flop and turn on this spade-heavy board (3 spades). My pair of aces beats missed draws but loses to any flush (common given the spade-heavy board), any Kx, two pair, etc. Flash's passivity could indicate weakness or a made flush trapping. With a weak kicker and 4 to a flush on board, thin value betting is risky - if raised I'm in a tough spot. Checking to showdown is safest, extracting value when ahead while avoiding getting check-raised by flushes or better.

On the river, the board is Kc-Js-3s-2s-Ah. I have a pair of Jacks (second pair on the flop, now third pair behind the King and Ace). The action has been checked down since the flop. Claude's range for calling from the Big Blind and checking three times likely includes middle pairs, missed straight draws (like Q-10 or 10-9), or perhaps a weak Ace that just improved. Since my hand has moderate showdown value against his missed draws and smaller pairs, but is unlikely to be called by anything worse if I bet, checking back is the most prudent play to realize my equity and reach a cheap showdown.

4-handed ranges are wider, but UTG still plays the weakest position postflop. 9♠5♣ offsuit has poor playability (dominated when it makes a pair, no suited equity) and doesn’t realize equity well out of position versus the Button and blinds who can attack limps/opens. With no need to take thin spots given our chip lead, folding preserves EV and avoids tough postflop decisions.

I am on the Button in a 4-handed game with 7s 8d. The UTG player has folded. While the Button allows for a wide opening range to steal the blinds, 87 offsuit is at the lower end of playability. It lacks the flush potential of suited connectors and is easily dominated by higher cards that the blinds might defend with. With the blinds having deep stacks (100BB+), they have leverage to 3-bet or call and play aggressively post-flop. Given my stack is the shortest at 75BB, I prefer to avoid marginal spots where I am likely to be behind or forced to fold to aggression. I will wait for a hand with better playability or equity.

J4 suited in the SB against only the BB. It's a marginal hand but suited with some playability. Completing for 50 is cheap to see a flop with flush potential. Not worth raising, but folding seems too tight heads up.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Eight of hearts, a strong speculative hand in a blind-vs-blind situation. Claude, the Small Blind, has just completed his bet. By raising here, I am exerting pressure on a potentially wide and weak limping range and taking control of the hand with the benefit of position for the rest of the hand. If he calls, the hand remains manageable; if he folds, I take the pot immediately.

J4s is a weak hand that I completed from the SB. Now facing a raise to 300 from the BB, I need to call 200 more into a 400 pot. While I have flush potential, J4s plays poorly out of position against a raise. The pot odds are marginal at best, and being OOP significantly reduces my implied odds. Fold is the disciplined play here.

In a 4-handed game, the UTG position is effectively the Cutoff. A9o is generally strong enough to open from the Cutoff, especially with a 75bb stack. I have been playing somewhat tightly recently, so an open here may get respect. Raising to 2.5x (250) is a standard size to pressure the blinds and Button. If I face significant aggression (3-bet), I can easily fold.

Q3 suited on the button facing an UTG raise of 250. While I have position, Q3s is quite weak against an UTG opening range which is typically strong. The suited nature gives some playability, but with blinds behind who could squeeze, this is a fold. Not worth 250 to see a flop with such a marginal hand.

In a 4-handed game, an UTG raise (equivalent to a Cutoff raise) represents a wide range, but Ace-Five offsuit is a marginal hand to play from the Small Blind. Calling is generally a poor strategy here because it provides the Big Blind (the chip leader, FiveTwo) an excellent opportunity to squeeze or overcall with position. Furthermore, playing a weak Ace out of position for the remainder of the hand is difficult and often leads to chip bleed. While A5o has blocker value, making it a candidate for a 3-bet bluff, I prefer to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation, especially given the stack dynamics and the aggression shown by the Big Blind in previous hands.

With AsTc in the BB versus a 4-handed UTG open, this is too strong to fold and generally profitable to 3-bet. The Ace gives strong blocker effects versus his best continues (AK/AQ), and the Ten plays well versus the wider portion of his opening range in short-handed play. Since there are no players left to act, a small-to-standard OOP 3-bet pressures his marginal opens, denies equity, and lets us realize our edge with initiative while keeping the SPR manageable if he calls.

I opened to 2.5BB from UTG (4-handed) with A9o, which is standard. However, facing a large 3-bet (to 9BB) from the chip leader in the Big Blind puts me in a difficult spot. The pot odds require about 35% equity to call, but A9o suffers from significant reverse implied odds, as I am often dominated by better Aces (AT, AJ, AQ, AK) in the opponent's value 3-bet range. Even against a polarized range, A9o does not play well post-flop, especially in a bloated pot with a low SPR (~3.6). Although I would have position, the prudent play with a marginal off-suit Ace facing a large re-raise is to fold and preserve my stack.
3h7d is a very weak offsuit hand from UTG position. Not playable - easy fold.

I am on the button with 6-2 offsuit. This is a very weak hand with poor playability. While I am in position, the Small Blind (FiveTwo) is the chip leader and has been active, and the Big Blind (Pro) has a relatively short stack which might lead to more aggressive defense. There is no need to risk chips on such a weak hand when I can wait for better spots.

Blinds folded around to a heads-up small blind vs big blind spot. K8 suited is comfortably ahead of a typical BB random holding and plays well with initiative. With a big stack advantage, applying pressure preflop is profitable; raising also denies the BB the ability to realize equity cheaply. I’ll use a standard small-blind open size that keeps their calling range wide but still charges enough.

In a Blind vs Blind scenario, King-Ten offsuit (KTo) is a strong hand, well ahead of the Small Blind's typically wide opening range. Facing a 2.5x raise, I have excellent pot odds to call, needing only about 30% equity. Additionally, I will have position on the chip leader (FiveTwo) post-flop, which provides a significant strategic advantage. A 3-bet could be considered but risks isolating myself against a stronger range or getting blown off the hand by a 4-bet, whereas calling keeps the opponent's range wide and allows me to realize my equity in position. Therefore, calling is the most profitable play.

Flop comes A♣ A♦ A♠, which heavily favors the preflop raiser in range, but my specific hand (K♦8♦) has meaningful showdown value in the common scenario Villain doesn’t have an Ace (kicker war). Leading a c-bet mainly folds out lots of worse hands that I currently beat (Q-high/J-high, small pairs) while getting continued against by Ax and some better Kx, and it doesn’t really “protect” since future board cards often neutralize kickers anyway. Being OOP, I’ll check to keep the pot small, allow Pro to bluff, and be ready to call reasonable bets while folding to big, ace-representing pressure.

The board is A-A-A, an extremely static texture. My hand is King-Ten offsuit. On this board, I hold Trip Aces with a King kicker. Hand rankings dictate that any pocket pair (22+) currently makes a Full House (Aces full of the pair), and any Ace makes Quads. Therefore, I am currently losing to all pocket pairs and Ax hands in the opponent's range. However, I am ahead of all unpaired hands without an Ace (like QJ, 98, etc.) due to my King kicker. 

Betting here achieves little: worse hands (unpaired hands with lower kickers) will likely fold, and better hands (Full Houses, Quads) will call or raise. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity (hitting a K or T would improve me to a Full House, beating lower pocket pairs), and potentially catch bluffs from the opponent on later streets. Given the "way ahead / way behind" dynamic and the lack of value targets, checking is the optimal play.

Turn is AAA4 with two diamonds on board; with K♦8♦ we have a very strong kicker (ahead of the vast majority of BB’s non-A/non-4 holdings) plus a diamond flush draw as backup. Since only one ace remains and BB checked flop, quads are unlikely; meanwhile a small bet can get called by lots of worse kickers/pairs and also charges any diamond peel. I’ll keep sizing small to avoid bloating the pot when BB’s value-raises are heavily weighted to 4x/full house or the last ace (hands we can comfortably fold to).

The board is Ac-Ad-As-4d. I hold King-Ten. My hand rank is Trip Aces with a King kicker (A-A-A-K-4). I am currently losing to any pocket pair (which makes a Full House, Aces full of the pair) and any hand with an Ace (Quads). However, I am ahead of all unpaired hands lower than King-high (e.g., QJ, 98), as my King kicker plays. Facing a small bet of 200 into a pot of 500, I am getting very attractive pot odds (roughly 3.5:1). The Small Blind's pre-flop raising range is wide and likely contains many unpaired hands that might be stabbing at this pot or turning into a bluff. Since I beat all bluffs and only lose to value hands, raising isolates me against better hands, while folding is too tight given the price and my strong kicker. Calling is the correct play to keep his bluffing range in.

River is A♣A♦A♠4♦9♠. With K♦8♦ our best hand is A-A-A-K-9: good showdown value, but not a pure value-bet because (1) Pro’s turn-call range contains a lot of hands that now beat us or were already ahead (9x improves to a full house, 4x is a full house, and some KQ/KJ/KTs beat our K-9 kicker), and (2) worse hands may not pay off much unless we use a tiny sizing. Checking keeps the pot controlled when we’re in a marginal kicker spot, and it also gives Pro a chance to bluff missed diamonds or stab with worse kickers; we can then bluff-catch vs small bets and comfortably fold to big, boat-representing pressure.

On the River, the board is Ac-Ad-As-4d-9s. My hand is A-A-A-K-9 (Trip Aces with a King kicker). My opponent, the Small Blind, raised pre-flop, checked the flop, bet small on the turn, and has now checked the river. I am currently losing to any pocket pair (which makes a Full House) and any Ax hand (which makes Quads). However, I am ahead of all unpaired hands (like QJ, JT, etc.) because my King kicker plays. Betting here has very little value; worse hands (lower kickers) will likely fold, and better hands (Full Houses/Quads) will call or raise. Checking back guarantees I realize my showdown value, winning against his missed high cards and losing the minimum against his monsters.

In a 4-handed game, King-Queen offsuit (KQo) is a very strong starting hand, especially from the Under-The-Gun (UTG) position which functions similarly to the cutoff. It is standard to open for a raise here to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds. I will raise to 300, which is a standard 3x big blind opening.

Facing a 3bb UTG open 4-handed, we’re in position but 8♣2♣ is at the very bottom of playable suited hands. It has poor connectivity, makes weak pairs, and when it makes a flush it’s often dominated (reverse implied odds), especially with deep stacks. A 3-bet bluff here also lacks relevant blockers and will realize equity poorly when called. Best EV is to fold and preserve position/stack for better spots.

I hold 6h Tc (Ten-Six offsuit) in the Small Blind. The UTG player (Flash) has raised to 300. My hand is extremely weak, unsuited, and disconnected. Facing an UTG raise, which typically represents a strong range, and being out of position for the rest of the hand, I have no incentive to enter this pot. The correct play is to fold immediately.

T8o is a weak hand facing a UTG raise. UTG opening ranges are typically strong, and T8o plays poorly against that range. Even with 200 to call into 450, this hand doesn't have good implied odds or playability to justify the call. Folding is the disciplined play here.

4-handed, UTG is effectively a middle/late position spot and we can open quite wide—especially with a big stack that pressures the button and blinds. J8o is near the bottom of an open range but still has reasonable steal EV versus blinds and can realize equity when called. I’ll use a consistent, slightly larger-than-min sizing to pick up the dead money more often; if faced with a 3-bet from these stacks, I can comfortably fold and preserve stack.

I hold 5c 9s (95o) on the button. The UTG player (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader, has raised to 250. My hand is statistically very weak and falls well outside of a profitable calling or 3-betting range against an Early Position open. Even with position, the hand has poor playability and equity. There is no strategic merit in entering this pot. I will fold.

Holding 4s6d in the SB facing a UTG raise. This is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Against an UTG opening range (typically strong), 46o has minimal equity. Flash can still squeeze behind me. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Ten offsuit (Ad Tc). The UTG player (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader, has raised to 2.5x (250). In a 4-handed game, this position is essentially the Cutoff, making the opening range fairly wide. While A-10o is a strong hand, 3-betting out of position against an aggressive chip leader might lead to difficult spots if they 4-bet or call. Calling is a standard and safe play to defend the blind, see a flop, and evaluate the strength of my hand based on the board texture. My stack of 120 BBs allows for post-flop play without being committed too early.

On a board of Queen-Jack-Three rainbow, I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (King) and an overcard (Ace). This board texture strongly favors the pre-flop raiser's range (FiveTwo), as it contains many cards that connect with a standard opening range (AQ, KQ, QJ, JJ, QQ). As the Big Blind, checking to the aggressor is the standard and most prudent play to see how they value the board. I am prepared to call a reasonable bet to see a turn card, given my equity with the gutshot and potential overcard strength.

Heads-up vs BB after our UTG open, this Q-J-3 rainbow flop favors the preflop raiser’s range (strong Qx, overpairs, AQ/KQ/QJ, sets). With Jh8d we have solid but vulnerable showdown value (second pair) and benefit from a small c-bet that (1) denies equity to hands like A-high, KTx, T9/98 type floats, and random overcards, and (2) gets thin value from worse Jx/underpairs that peel once. I’ll keep sizing small to avoid bloating the pot and to make folding to a check-raise easier if Flash shows strength.

On a flop of Qc-Js-3s, I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needing a King) and one overcard (Ace). The pre-flop raiser, FiveTwo, has bet 200 into a pot of 550. This is a small continuation bet of roughly 36% pot. To call 200 to win a total pot of 950, I need approximately 21% equity. While my direct equity with 4 Kings and 3 Aces is around 15% for the turn, the implied odds of hitting a King to make the nut straight are significant, especially against an aggressive chip leader who likely holds many Queens or Jacks in their range. Calling is the most profitable play here, as it allows me to realize my equity and potentially stack the opponent if I hit my draw.

On the turn 2d, the board remains very similar to the flop texture. My hand, Ace-Ten offsuit, still holds a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (King) and one overcard (Ace). Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor and chip leader, FiveTwo, the standard play is to check to him and see how he chooses to proceed. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands or induce a raise, whereas checking allows me to potentially see a free river card or evaluate a bet based on the pot odds offered for my draw.

Turn 2d is essentially a blank that doesn’t improve our Jx much, but it does allow BB to have some slowplays/turn sets (22) and continues to leave a lot of their continuing range as Qx and better Jx that will call again. If we barrel, we’ll often fold out the hands we want to keep (A-high floats, random overcards) while getting called mostly by better, creating a tougher river spot and bloating the pot with a marginal made hand. With position and decent showdown value, checking back keeps the pot controlled, realizes equity, and also induces river bluffs from missed hands while we can still value-catch on many runouts.

The river 8s is largely a blank. My Ace-high (Ad Tc) has limited showdown value, but it is unlikely that a bet would force the chip leader (FiveTwo) to fold a marginal pair like a Jack or a Queen, which constitutes a large part of his checking range on the turn. Since I have missed my gutshot draw, I will check and hope to win a small percentage of the time against his air or missed lower draws, while planning to fold if he puts out a significant bet.

River improves us to two pair (Jacks and Eights) after checking back turn, and Flash’s river check is consistent with lots of Qx/Jx showdown hands that will call a reasonable value bet. We’re only in bad shape versus traps like QJ and sets, and we can comfortably bet/fold to a check-raise. Size up enough to get paid by Qx while still getting crying calls from some Jx/3x: ~2/3 pot.

On the river 8s, the board completes as Qc-3s-Jd-2d-8s. I have missed my gutshot straight draw and am left with Ace-high (Ad Tc). FiveTwo, the chip leader and pre-flop aggressor, has bet 650 into a pot of 950 after checking the turn. This 'delayed' value bet or bluff often represents a marginal hand like a Jack or a weak Queen that was checking for pot control on the turn, or a total air bluff. However, Ace-high is at the very bottom of my calling range here; even some of FiveTwo's missed draws like KT or T9 either beat me with a better kicker or have significant showdown value. Given FiveTwo's stack size and aggressive nature, hero-calling with Ace-high on a board with two overcards to my Ten is a low-probability play. I will fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am in UTG (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play) with 7d 9c, which is an unsuited one-gapper and a very weak hand. With 77 BBs, I have plenty of stack depth and no reason to get involved with trash hands from early position. The standard play is to fold immediately to preserve chips for better opportunities.

I have 7c6c suited connectors on the button with only the blinds left to act. This is a premium spot to raise - suited connectors play well in position, and stealing the blinds with a standard raise is profitable here. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x BB).

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit. The Button (Claude) has raised to 250. While the pot odds are tempting (200 to call into 400), playing Ten-Nine offsuit out of position against both a Button open and an aggressive Big Blind (FiveTwo) is generally a losing play in the long run. I'll wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

Button open is wide 4-handed, and KQo is well ahead of that range. Calling keeps in dominated Kx/Qx and invites multiway less (SB already folded), but being out of position I prefer taking the initiative and building a pot against worse while denying equity to suited/gappy hands. A 3-bet to 900 (about 3.6x) is a solid OOP sizing versus 2.5x opens and should generate folds from the bottom while still getting called by worse broadways and suited hands.

FiveTwo 3-bet me to 900. I have 76s which is a nice opening hand but doesn't play well against 3-bets. Looking at history, FiveTwo has been aggressive with 3-bets (hands 12, 33, 39, 57). While I have position, suited connectors don't have great equity against typical 3-betting ranges. The pot odds are ~1.85:1 (need ~35% equity) which 76s won't have here. Better to fold and preserve chips.
7h9c offsuit from UTG is a weak hand. This is not in a standard opening range from the earliest position where I need to play tight. Easy fold.

I am on the button with 9s 3d, which is a very weak starting hand. While I am in a good position, there is no reason to play this hand when it has been folded to me, especially with a very active chip leader like FiveTwo in the small blind and Pro in the big blind. The optimal play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Folds to me in the SB against the BB. With a big stack advantage and only one player left to get through, I want to open very wide and avoid giving a free flop. T6s has decent playability (can make top pair + draws) and benefits from taking initiative. I’ll use a standard small sizing to keep my range wide and force the BB to defend out of position without bloating the pot unnecessarily.

I am in the Big Blind facing a raise to 250 from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). The pot is 350, and I need to call 150, giving me pot odds of roughly 30%. I will be in position post-flop. However, J3 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability and significant reverse implied odds if I hit a Jack. While I am getting a good price, the equity realization for J3o is low, likely making a call -EV compared to folding. FiveTwo is the chip leader and aggressive, making it harder to navigate post-flop with a trash hand. Conserving chips is better here.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game with 62s. This is a very weak starting hand and does not meet the requirements for an opening range from this position. Even short-handed, I should prioritize playing stronger hands to maintain my stack.

Button vs blinds with K4o is a profitable open in a short-handed game: we have position, strong enough high-card equity, and good immediate fold equity into the blinds. Both blinds have incentive to defend selectively (and SB is shorter), so a small-but-standard raise pressures their ranges while keeping our risk low and allowing easy folds to 3-bets.

I hold AdQc in the Small Blind facing a standard 2.5bb raise from the Button (FiveTwo). FiveTwo is the chip leader and has been opening frequently, suggesting a wide range. AQo is a premium hand that dominates much of a wide Button opening range (Kx, Qx, Ax, suited connectors). I am out of position, so just calling invites the Big Blind to come along and leaves me playing a multi-way pot without initiative. A 3-bet for value is the standard play here to isolate the opener, extract value from worse hands, and deny equity. A sizing of roughly 3.5x-4x the open is appropriate from the blinds. I will raise to 900.

I have TT in the BB facing a 3-bet from Pro (SB) after FiveTwo opened from button. TT is a strong hand that plays well against a typical 3-bet range. Pro has only 6700 behind after putting in 900, so they're somewhat committed. I should 4-bet to around 2500 to put pressure on Pro - if they shove, I'm getting good odds to call with TT against their 3-bet range. This also isolates Pro and gets value from worse hands like AK/AQ while potentially folding out small pairs.

We’re facing a cold 4-bet from the BB after SB 3-bet, and we’re holding K4o. This spot is extremely range-tight: Claude’s 4-bet range is heavily weighted to QQ+/AK (and some JJ/AQ at most), and Pro still has the option to shove over the top with a short stack, making our realization even worse. Calling is dominated and invites a squeeze; 5-bet bluffing is poor because we block only K (not A) and the effective stacks are such that Claude can comfortably continue with his value range. Best EV is to preserve the stack and fold.

I hold AdQc in the Small Blind. After 3-betting the Button's open to 900, the Big Blind (Claude) has cold 4-bet to 2500. This is an extremely strong line, often representing a range of QQ+, AK, and potentially JJ. Against this range, AQo is significantly dominated or at best flipping. I need ~30.5% equity to call, but against a strong cold 4-bet range (JJ+, AK+, AQs), my equity is approximately 25-29%. Furthermore, calling would leave me Out of Position with an SPR < 1, creating a difficult post-flop scenario. Shoving risks my tournament life (58bb remaining) in a spot where I am rarely ahead. Preserving my 5800 stack is the prudent play given the strength shown by the Big Blind.

4-handed UTG still needs a reasonably tight opening range. Q2o is dominated when called, plays poorly postflop, and performs especially badly versus the aggressive/defending tendencies shown by the blinds (and the button can also apply pressure). The small steal EV doesn’t compensate for the frequency we’ll be forced to fold to 3-bets or play multiway out of position with a weak, dominated queen. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am on the Button with Q7 offsuit. The action has folded to me. While the Button is a strong position to steal the blinds, Q7o is a marginal to weak hand, even in a 4-handed game. My stack is 67 BBs, which is healthy, but I am the shortest stack at the table. The Small Blind (Claude) has shown recent aggression (3-betting and 4-betting), making a loose steal attempts risky as I would likely have to fold to a re-raise. Q7o does not play well post-flop if called. Therefore, the prudent play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

K3o in the SB heads up vs BB. This is a marginal hand but with a King I have decent high card value. I can either complete or raise. Given I'll be out of position postflop, I prefer to raise and take initiative, potentially winning the blinds outright. Standard raise to 250.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey offsuit. The Small Blind (Claude) has raised to 250. While I have position post-flop and the price to call is relatively cheap (150 more), K3o is a very weak hand with poor playability. Given Claude has been active and recently showed strength by 4-betting in the previous hand, I will opt for a disciplined fold rather than defending with a marginal King and a weak kicker.

I am in UTG position with King-Deuce offsuit (Kc 2h). This is a very weak hand with poor playability and high reverse implied odds if I hit a King. With 67 big blinds, I have plenty of chips and no need to take unnecessary risks from early position. The standard play in a 4-handed game is to fold this hand pre-flop.

Pocket Aces on the button - the absolute best starting hand. Pro folded, so I'm first to act. I need to raise for value here. Standard sizing of 2.5-3x BB (250-300) is appropriate to build the pot while still getting calls from the blinds with reasonable holdings.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Deuce suited. Claude has raised to 275 from the button. While Q2s has some value in short-handed play, it is a very weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive button raiser and a potentially aggressive big blind (FiveTwo). Claude's opening range on the button is likely wide, but Q2s is dominated by many of those hands and lacks playability post-flop. I prefer to fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my 115bb stack.

Facing a BTN open to 2.75bb, we’re getting a reasonable price in the big blind (175 to win 425) and Claude’s opening range here should be quite wide 4-handed. 54o is near the bottom of a defend, but it has some ability to make disguised straights/two pair and our deep effective stack helps implied odds. I don’t want to 3-bet bluff this offsuit hand and bloat a pot OOP with poor playability, so I’ll take the priced-in defend.

Heads-up OOP on a fairly dynamic 9d-6s-Td flop, 54o has essentially no made hand and no clean immediate draw. This isn’t a good texture to donk-bluff with our exact hand because we can’t comfortably continue versus a raise and we lack backdoors (no diamond, no overcards). Best is to check our range, let Claude c-bet his wide BTN range, and then decide whether to fold (most likely) or very occasionally continue if we pick up strong backdoors/equity on later streets.

I have AA on a 9d-6s-Td flop with two diamonds. This is a somewhat coordinated board with flush and straight draws possible. FiveTwo checked, so I need to bet for value and protection. I'll bet around 60% pot (350-400) to charge draws and extract value from hands like Tx, 9x, and smaller pairs.

Claude’s c-bet is 375 into ~600 (now pot shows 975), giving us about 27–28% required equity to continue. With 5h4c on 9d-6s-Td we have no pair, no flush backdoor, and only very thin runner-runner straight possibilities that don’t realize well OOP versus continued pressure. Check-raising would be a low-quality bluff with essentially no equity and poor blockers. Best play is to preserve stack and fold.
9c6h is a weak unsuited hand from UTG position. This is not playable - no high cards, not suited, poor connectivity. Easy fold.

I am on the button with Qd 5s. While Qx hands can occasionally be used for stealing the blinds in a short-handed game, Q5 offsuit is too weak to merit an open raise here, especially with FiveTwo in the small blind, who has shown significant aggression throughout the tournament. Pro in the big blind is also getting shorter and might be looking for a double-up opportunity. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

With folds to me in the SB it’s effectively heads-up blinds. We have a big stack edge over Pro (66bb) and want to apply steady pressure by raising a wide range and leveraging fold equity. J7o is not a premium, but it’s well within a standard SB open-raise range here, and raising also gives us initiative versus a likely fit-or-fold opponent. I’ll use a consistent, small-but-not-min sizing to avoid giving Pro a great price and to set up profitable c-bets; if Pro 3-bets/shoves, this hand is an easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 4h 3d, facing a raise to 250 from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). The pot odds are decent (calling 150 to win a pot that will be 500, requiring ~30% equity), and the SB's range is likely very wide attempting to steal. However, 43o is one of the worst starting hands with very poor post-flop playability, especially out of position against an aggressive chip leader. Realizing equity will be difficult. It is better to preserve my 66bb stack than to defend with trash out of position.

I am in the UTG position with 3s 6c, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Given the full table and the likelihood of being raised or re-raised by the remaining players, there is no strategic value in entering the pot with this hand. Standard poker strategy dictates a fold here to conserve chips.

4-handed with UTG folded, we’re effectively stealing from the Button versus blinds. Even with a weak holding (8♣3♦), we have a big stack edge, position, and both blinds have shown plenty of straightforward play (Pro especially). Opening keeps pressure on their ranges and wins the pot immediately often enough; using our standard small open size also avoids being transparent. Plan: fold to meaningful 3-bet pressure, play in position if called and c-bet selectively on favorable textures.

I hold 7c2s in the Small Blind, which is the worst starting hand in poker. Facing a raise from the Button (FiveTwo), I have very poor equity and will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Despite the Button's range likely being wide, 72o is too weak to defend with a call or a 3-bet bluff, especially given my stack size of 65.5 BB which allows me to wait for better spots. Folding is the only standard and correct play here.
74o is a very weak hand. Facing a button raise, I'm out of position with a hand that plays poorly postflop. Even with 2.7:1 pot odds, this is an easy fold.

Four-handed UTG is effectively the cutoff, so opening ranges widen and picking up the blinds is valuable. Jh7s is not strong enough to limp/call profitably or to continue versus 3-bets, but it plays fine as a low-investment steal given our chip-lead and positional advantage postflop when called. I’ll use a standard, consistent open size and plan to fold to significant pressure (3-bet) and play straightforwardly if defended.

I hold A7o on the Button facing a 2.5x open from the UTG chip leader (FiveTwo). While FiveTwo has been opening frequently, A7o is a marginal hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially dominated by better Aces in the opener's range. Calling invites squeezes from the blinds and leaves me in a reverse implied odds situation. 3-betting is risky against the deep-stacked chip leader. As the shortest stack (65 BB), preservation is key and I should look for better spots. Folding is the standard and most prudent play.

Q6o is a weak hand to defend from the SB against an UTG raise. FiveTwo has been active but an UTG open still represents reasonable strength. Q6o plays poorly postflop, is easily dominated, and I'll be out of position. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ten-Five offsuit. FiveTwo, the chip leader, has raised to 250 from under-the-gun in this 4-max setup. While the price to call is relatively small (150 more to win a total pot of 400), Ten-Five offsuit is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially when out of position against an aggressive chip leader. Conserving my stack for better opportunities is the more prudent play here.

I am holding Ten-Five offsuit (T5o) from the Under-the-Gun (UTG) position. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em and is certainly not playable from early position. With 65.5 Big Blinds, I have a healthy stack and no reason to enter the pot with trash cards, especially against active opponents with larger stacks behind me. The correct play is a straightforward fold.
5s3d is a very weak hand. Even on the button with fold equity, 53 offsuit is too weak to open. FiveTwo has a large stack and has shown aggression with 3-bets. Not worth trying to steal here.

I am in the Small Blind with 4s 2s. While the cost to call is only 50 chips, this is a very weak hand to play out of position against the chip leader, FiveTwo, who has shown significant aggression throughout the tournament. Folding avoids a potentially difficult post-flop situation where I would likely be dominated or forced to fold to a standard continuation bet.
AJs is a premium hand from UTG. Standard open raise to 250 (2.5x BB) is appropriate here. This hand plays well post-flop with strong high card value and flush potential.

I am on the button with Q4o. Claude has opened the action with a standard raise from UTG. Q4o is a weak hand that is easily dominated by most of Claude's opening range. With two players still to act behind me (one with a very large stack and one with a shorter stack), there is no incentive to call or 3-bet. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

4-handed, Claude’s UTG open is wider than full-ring and we’re getting a very good price (200 to win 400 now, plus implied odds). 7d6d plays well as a defend: it can flop strong draws/hidden made hands and doesn’t suffer as much from domination as weak suited aces/broadways. I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with a light 3-bet versus a range that can continue strongly; calling keeps SPR high and our range protected, and we can comfortably fold if BB squeezes.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 6h facing a raise to 250 from UTG and a call from the Small Blind. The pot odds are compelling: I need to call 150 into a pot of 600, offering 4:1 immediate odds (or 20% equity required). My hand, a suited one-gapper, plays well in multi-way pots and has sufficient equity against a strong UTG range and a capped SB calling range to justify a call. I am closing the action, so there is no risk of a squeeze. While being sandwiched post-flop is not ideal, the price allows me to profitably see a flop and look for two pair, trips, or strong draws.

We’re 3-way OOP on a paired, low board where UTG retains range advantage in overpairs and strong 5x, while our hand has very little immediate equity (only backdoor diamonds and backdoor straight potential). Leading here into two players is low fold-equity and risks getting floated/raised, especially with Pro’s short stack incentivized to continue wider. Best is to check, keep the pot controlled, and look to realize equity cheaply or take a stab on later streets if it checks through and favorable turns arrive (diamond/4/3/7/6).

On a dry, paired board of 2d 5h 5c, I have 8 high with no immediate draws. The board texture interacts reasonably well with the blinds' defending ranges (containing 5x), but I personally missed it completely. As the pre-flop aggressor is still to act behind me, leading out (donk betting) with air into two opponents is ill-advised. My standard line is to check to the raiser and evaluate their action. I will almost certainly fold to any significant bet.

I have AJs on a 2d-5h-5c board. Both opponents checked to me. This is a dry, paired board that doesn't connect well with their calling ranges. My ace high is likely ahead here. A c-bet of ~1/3 pot should take this down often, and if called I can reassess. Betting around 250-300 for value/protection.

Claude’s c-bet is very small relative to the pot, which likely represents a range-bet trying to deny equity to overcards and pick up the pot cheaply. Even though 7d6d has little immediate equity on 2d5h5c, we do have some turn playability: we can improve with a 6/7 (often best versus his overcards), pick up additional equity on diamonds/3/4, and we keep our defending range intact versus these small stabs. Raising here would be a low-equity bluff into a range that contains overpairs and plenty of 5x, and it also risks Pro (still to act) continuing wider given price/stack dynamics. Best is to call once and re-evaluate turns, continuing aggressively only on favorable cards and otherwise playing a controlled check-fold/check-call line depending on sizing.

I hold 8h 6h on a 2d 5h 5c flop. After checking, the UTG raiser bet 275 and the Small Blind called. The Small Blind's call significantly changes the dynamic, as it indicates they likely have a piece of this board (a 5, a pocket pair) or a stubborn Ace-high. With just 8-high, no real draw, and two opponents continuing (one showing strength by calling), I have zero reason to continue. The pot odds of ~17.5% are irrelevant because I have virtually 0% equity to win this hand at showdown and no fold equity to a raise. Folding is the mandatory play.

Turn puts three 5s on board, so our hand is effectively just trips with a very weak 7 kicker. Claude’s range contains a lot of pocket pairs (now full houses) and plenty of higher-kicker unpaired hands that won’t fold to a lead. Betting would be turning a low-equity hand into a bluff with poor fold equity and bad outcomes versus raises. Best is to check, keep pot controlled, and only continue on the river if we get a very favorable runout and/or he shows weakness.

Three 5s on board now - only one 5 remains in deck, so FiveTwo almost certainly doesn't have quads. With A-J, I have strong kickers for the trips. If FiveTwo has unpaired cards, my Ace kicker is likely best. If they have a small pocket pair (66-99), they beat me. A medium-sized bet extracts value from worse high cards while not over-committing. Betting ~50% pot.

On 2d-5h-5c-5s we effectively have trips with a very weak kicker (7). Claude’s turn barrel (650 into a ~2k pot) is highly weighted toward hands that crush us: any pocket pair is now a full house, and any 5x is quads. Even many of his would-be “bluffs” (Ax/Kx/Qx) still beat our 7-kicker at showdown, so we can’t profitably bluff-catch. Our improvement odds are small (and even a 6/7 river boat can still lose to higher pocket-pair full houses/quads). Given the poor equity and future playability OOP versus continued aggression, folding is best.

King-Nine offsuit is a marginal hand that is slightly below the standard opening range from the first position in a 4-handed game. With the chip leader on the button and aggressive players in the blinds, opening this hand out of position is likely a losing play in the long run. I will fold and look for a more advantageous situation.

Button 4-handed with a big stack advantage over both blinds. 5♠3♥ is weak, but this is a profitable spot to apply pressure and steal the 150-chip blind pot with a small risk. I’ll use a minimum open to keep the cost down while still generating fold equity versus two players who can’t defend too wide given stack pressure. Plan: fold to any 3-bet, and if called, play positionally with selective c-betting on favorable textures.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 3h, which is an extremely weak hand (8-3 offsuit). The Button (FiveTwo) has min-raised to 200. Although the price is cheap (150 to call into a pot of 350), playing a trash hand out of position against the chip leader is not a winning strategy. I will fold and preserve my stack.

6h7d in the BB facing a min-raise from the button. Getting 3.5:1 pot odds (100 to win 350). This is a standard defend with a connected hand that can make straights. FiveTwo has been active but a min-raise from the button is often wide. Easy call at this price.

I flopped middle pair (sixes) with 7 kicker on a monotone spade board (6s 2s As). I have no spade, so no flush draw. The ace is scary and any spade completes a flush. My hand has some showdown value but is too weak to bet for value - I'd only get action from better hands (aces, flush draws, made flushes). Standard check here and reevaluate based on FiveTwo's action.

Monotone A♠6♠2♠ heavily favors my preflop raising range (more Ax and higher spades), so this is a good spot to apply pressure with a small c-bet. My hand (5♠3♥) has a wheel gutshot (to a 4) plus a small spade blocker, giving me some equity and making it easier to represent strong made hands. I’ll size down to target folds from Claude’s non-spade / weak-pair holdings while keeping risk low and comfortably folding if he check-raises big on this texture.

I have middle pair (sixes) on a monotone spade board with no spade in my hand. FiveTwo's 150 bet into 400 is small (~37%). Getting 3.7:1 odds (150 to win 550). The small sizing often indicates a weak ace, draw, or probe bet. My pair beats bluffs but loses to aces and any made flush. With decent pot odds and position to fold to aggression on scare cards, calling is reasonable here.

The 2c pairs the board, giving me two pair (sixes and deuces). This is actually a slight improvement since it's harder for opponent to have trips. However, the board still has 3 spades and any spade in their hand makes a flush. An ace also beats me. With the action checked to me (To Call: 0), checking is optimal - betting would only get called by better hands (aces, flushes, spade draws). I want to see a cheap showdown with my marginal two pair.

Claude’s flop check-call on A♠6♠2♠ heavily concentrates him on Ax and/or one-spade holdings that won’t fold often. The turn pairing the 2 reduces my fold equity further (BB has more 2x, and Ax is still strong), and a second barrel risks getting check-raised off a hand with solid equity. With 5♠3♥ I now have a meaningful river draw (any spade gives me a flush; any 4 gives me a wheel straight—12 outs total, noting 4♠ overlaps), so the highest-EV line is to take the free card in position and realize my equity, then decide on the river based on whether I improve and Claude’s action.

River completed with Tc. I have two pair (6s and 2s) but it's marginal. FiveTwo checked back the turn which suggests weakness, but the Tc is bad for me - any Tx now has better two pair (Ts and 2s > 6s and 2s). My hand beats bluffs at showdown but betting gets called only by better (aces, flushes, Tx). Check and hope to win against missed draws or worse hands.

River comes Tc and Claude checks after check-calling the monotone A♠6♠2♠ flop and then seeing us take the free turn on the paired deuce. That line heavily weights him toward Ax (often with/without a spade) and one-spade bluffcatchers that are not folding often versus a single river stab—especially since our turn check makes a big river bluff less credible. With 5♠3♥ we have no made hand and a bluff would need an unrealistically high fold frequency given his likely Ax/2x/spade-containing range and his prior willingness to bluff-catch. Pot is small relative to our stack, so the best EV/risk line is to give up and check back.

4-handed UTG is relatively late, but Q2o is still near the bottom of the deck: it’s heavily dominated by the calling/3-bet ranges behind (any Qx, broadways, suited aces), plays poorly postflop, and realizes equity badly out of position. With a big stack I want to keep opening pressure, but not with hands that create frequent reverse-implied-odds spots versus two competent stacks in the blinds. Limping is also unattractive because it forfeits initiative and invites multiway pots. Best chip-EV is to pass and preserve stack for better opens.

I am on the Button with 4s 7d, a very weak offsuit hand. The UTG player has folded. While being on the Button offers a stealing opportunity, the hand has poor playability post-flop, and the blinds have large stacks (over 100BBs) which may encourage them to defend or 3-bet. With a stack of 62.5 BBs, there is no desperate need to force action with garbage hands. The standard and most profitable play here is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Suited ace (Ad3d) in the small blind with only the big blind left. This is a clear raise spot heads-up. A3s plays well postflop with nut flush potential and has good equity. I'll raise to 300 to put pressure on Flash.

I am in the Big Blind with J6h. Claude has raised to 300 from the Small Blind, making it 200 more for me to call. While J6h is a weak hand, I am closing the action and getting 2:1 on a call against a small blind range which is often wide. However, being out of position against Claude (who has a larger stack) with a marginal hand that doesn't flop particularly well makes folding a disciplined choice to preserve my stack for better spots in this tournament.

I am in UTG (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play) with 6s 7c, an off-suit connector. With 62.5 BBs, I have a playable stack but am the shortest at the table. 76o is generally too weak to open raise from the first position, as it plays poorly against calling ranges and cannot withstand a 3-bet. Given the deep stacks behind me who have been active, the prudent play is to fold and wait for a better hand or position.

9h4s is a weak hand even on the button. While button stealing is profitable with a wide range, 94o is too weak - it's not suited, not connected, no high card. Against two blinds including FiveTwo who has a big stack and may defend wide, I'll save chips here.

I am in the Small Blind with 53 offsuit. The action has folded to me. 53o is one of the weakest hands in poker. Although the table is short-handed (4 players), completing the blind or raising as a bluff into the chip leader (FiveTwo) who is in the Big Blind is generally a losing strategy with this specific hand. I will fold to preserve my stack.
JTs is a solid hand from UTG - suited broadway connectors with good straight and flush potential. Standard open raise to 250 (2.5x) is appropriate here.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a four-handed game. Claude has raised from UTG to 2.5x the big blind. While calling is an option to keep the pot small and utilize my position, three-betting is generally superior here to define my hand, put pressure on the opener, and discourage the blinds (especially the aggressive FiveTwo) from entering the pot or squeezing. A standard 3-bet size of approximately 3x the initial raise is appropriate.

Facing an UTG open and a button 3-bet, Q7s is too dominated to defend from the small blind, especially with the original raiser still to act behind us. Calling would put us out of position in a bloated pot with poor equity realization and significant reverse implied odds (often making a second-best queen). A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive here because UTG’s range is strong and can continue, and if called we’re stuck in a 4-bet pot with a weak hand. Preserve the chip-lead and pass on this spot.

I am in the Big Blind facing a UTG open raise to 250 followed by a Button 3-bet to 800. My hand is K5 suited. This is a very strong action sequence: UTG opens typically represent strength, and a Button 3-bet against UTG usually indicates a premium range (JJ+, AK, AQs) or a strong balanced bluffing range. My K5s is likely dominated by hands like AK, KQ, or pocket pairs. Additionally, if I call, the UTG player is still left to act and could 4-bet or call, leaving me out of position in a bloated pot with a marginal hand. The pot odds do not justify calling 700 chips into a pot of 1200 with this holding and position. Preserving my 61BB stack is more important than speculating here.

JTs suited is a strong hand with good postflop playability. Flash's 3-bet from the button could be wide given position. I'm getting decent odds (550 to win 1200) and JTs has excellent implied odds - can make straights, flushes, and strong pairs. Calling is better than folding such a playable hand, and 4-betting OOP would overplay it.

I have JhTh on a 3d8dQc board - I missed the flop with just a gutshot straight draw (need a 9) and backdoor hearts. Out of position against the 3-bettor. This is a clear check - I don't have enough equity to lead into Flash who showed strength preflop. I'll check and evaluate based on their action.

On this flop of Q-8-3, I have Ace-high. While I missed the board, as the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have a significant range advantage. My range contains all the premium pairs (AA, KK, QQ) and strong Broadway hands (AQ) that Claude is less likely to have after just calling the 3-bet out of position. I will continuation bet for about one-third of the pot to apply pressure to his middle pairs and high-card hands that may have missed, while keeping the price reasonable if he decides to continue.

I have JhTh on 3d8dQc - gutshot straight draw (need a 9) plus two overcards. Flash's c-bet is small (600 into 1750, ~34% pot). Getting 3.9:1 pot odds (600 to win 2350) which requires ~20% equity. My gutshot (~8%) plus potentially live overcards (~12%) gives me roughly the equity needed. Being OOP is tough but the price is good enough to peel one more card.

The 9h just completed my straight! I now have Q-J-T-9-8 - the nuts. I need to bet for value. Flash 3-bet pre and c-bet the flop, so they likely have strength (overpairs, AQ/KQ, or diamond draws). I should size for value while charging any diamond flush draws. Betting around 2/3 pot (~2000) seems optimal to get called by worse hands.

On the turn, Claude has led out for 2000 into a pot of 2950. My Ace-high has no real showdown value here, and I have very little equity against a range that calls a 3-bet and then leads into the aggressor on this board. The 9h turn potentially connects with hands like J-10 (making a straight) or 8-9 (two pair). Given that I have completely missed the board and Claude is showing significant strength, a fold is the only disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am dealt 3d 2d UTG in a 4-handed game. This is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Although the table is short-handed, opening with the lowest suited connector from the first position is generally a losing strategy, especially with an aggressive big stack (FiveTwo) and an active player (Claude) behind me. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my 97bb stack.

4-handed on the Button after UTG folds, A5o is well within a profitable opening range. Raising pressures both blinds, picks up the pot often, and keeps initiative with a hand that has decent equity/board coverage (wheel and top-pair potential). I’ll use a standard small open size to risk little while maximizing fold equity.

I hold 7d 4s in the Small Blind, which is a very weak offsuit hand. The Button (FiveTwo) has raised to 2.5bb. Despite the Button's wide range, my hand has poor playability and very low equity, and I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand. Calling would be a negative expected value play. The standard and correct decision is to fold.

JTs is a strong suited connector in the big blind. Facing a button min-raise of 250, I'm getting good pot odds (150 to win 400). This hand has excellent playability with straight and flush potential. While I could 3-bet, calling keeps the pot manageable while I'll be out of position. With 175BB effective, I have good implied odds to see a flop.

Flopped an open-ended straight draw with JTs - any 8 or K gives me the nuts/second nuts. Board is Qh9d7s. Standard play is to check to the preflop aggressor. If FiveTwo c-bets, I can call with my 8 outs (~32% equity by river) or potentially check-raise as a semi-bluff. If they check back, I get a free card.

This Q97 rainbow flop connects well with Claude’s BB defend range (Qx, 9x/7x, many straight/gutter combos), so a c-bet with A5o has poor immediate fold equity and very little equity when called. With no backdoor flush and only thin backdoor wheel potential, betting also risks getting check-raised off our hand too often. In position, I’ll take the low-variance line, realize what equity we have, and keep my range protected—then consider bluffing later on favorable turns (A/K/T/8/4/3) or taking a free showdown if it checks through.

Turn 6d doesn't complete my straight draw but FiveTwo checked the flop showing weakness. I still have 8 outs (any 8 or K) for an open-ended straight draw. With the pot checked to me, this is a good semi-bluff opportunity - I can take down the pot now or get value if I hit the river. Betting ~65% pot.

Claude’s small turn lead (350 into 900) on Q-9-7-6 is consistent with a lot of his BB range picking up equity/value (pairs like 9x/7x/6x, Qx, and many 8x/Tx/Jx diamond/gutter hands). With A5o we picked up a gutshot to the 8 (and still have two overcards to some parts of his range), and we’re getting a good price in position to realize equity. A bluff-raise doesn’t credibly represent much after checking flop and is unlikely to fold out his pair/draw-heavy lead range, so the best EV line is to call and play rivers in position (value if we hit 8/A; otherwise consider selective bluffs on favorable rivers).

River 4c is a complete blank. My straight draw bricked - I have Jack high with no showdown value. FiveTwo checked back the flop but called my turn bet, suggesting they have at least some showdown value (weak pair, queen, or draw that also missed). Bluffing here is risky since they've shown willingness to continue. Better to check and give up rather than burn more chips on a bluff that's likely to get called.

We have pure ace-high on Q-9-7-6-4 and essentially no showdown value versus Claude’s BB defend range (he’ll have tons of 7x/6x/9x and some Qx). After his small turn probe lead and then river check, his range is often capped at one-pair/medium-strength hands and missed straight draws, rather than strong value that wants to keep betting. This is a good spot to apply pressure with a polarized river bet, representing Qx/sets/overpairs that we can plausibly have after checking flop and calling turn. Our 5 blocks key straight value combos (53/85), reducing the chance we’re bluffing into a nutted hand. I’ll bet a strong sizing to target folds from 6x/7x/weak 9x and missed draws while keeping the risk/reward favorable.

My straight draw completely bricked - I have Jack high with zero showdown value. FiveTwo's line (check flop, call turn, bet river) suggests they have a made hand they're now value betting. Could be any queen, nine, seven, or even a turned/rivered hand. I need to be good 30%+ of the time to call, but with Jack high I lose to any pair. No reason to bluff-catch here when I can't beat a bluff that has any piece of this board.

4-handed UTG is relatively late, but 7♢5♠ offsuit is still a very poor opener: it’s dominated when called, realizes equity badly, and invites pressure from the short-stacked Button (6100) who can reshove frequently. With a big stack edge, I’d rather apply pressure with hands that can continue vs 3-bets/jams and play cleaner postflop. Folding preserves stack and avoids marginal, low-EV spots.

I hold 96o on the button. While I have the positional advantage and it is folded to me, this hand is extremely weak (bottom 15% of hands). With 61 big blinds, I have plenty of stack depth to wait for better opportunities rather than attempting a steal with garbage that has very poor post-flop playability. Standard play is to fold.

In SB with T5o facing just the BB. This is a weak hand but I'm already committed 50 chips. With position disadvantage against BB, I have two reasonable options: complete cheaply or raise to steal. Given my strong stack (16975) vs Flash (9675), I'll raise to put pressure and try to take the blinds. A standard 2.5x raise to 250 is appropriate as a steal attempt.

I am in the Big Blind holding 10d 5d. The Small Blind (Claude) has raised to 250. I have position post-flop and am getting excellent pot odds (3.33 to 1) to see a flop. Ten-five suited is a reasonable hand to defend in this spot against a likely wide Small Blind opening range.

Flopped top pair with T5o on a 2d Tc 8h board. This is a good flop for my hand - top pair on a relatively dry board. I should c-bet for value to get calls from worse hands (pairs, draws like 97, J9) and protect against overcards. Standard c-bet of around 60% pot.

I have top pair on a relatively dry board of 2d-Tc-8h. Claude raised from the small blind and is leading out with a 60% pot-sized bet on the flop. Given the wide range Claude likely has in this position, my top pair is currently very strong. However, my kicker (5) is weak, so raising would likely only isolate me against better tens or overpairs. By calling, I keep his bluffs in and can see how the turn develops while maintaining position.

Turn brings 6h, completing a two-heart board. I have top pair with weak kicker. Flash called flop c-bet which could mean: weaker tens, 8x, heart draws, straight draws (97, 75), or floats. I should continue betting for value and to charge draws. Around 65% pot bet of ~700.

I have top pair (Tens) in a blind-vs-blind situation. While my kicker is weak, Claude's range as the Small Blind is very wide, and he could be double-barreling with various bluffs, draws (like straight draws J9, or heart draws that appeared on the turn), or middle pairs. Folding top pair here is too exploitable given the pot odds of approximately 2.6 to 1. Raising would likely only be called by better hands, so calling is the most effective way to keep his bluffs in and reach a showdown or evaluate the river action.

River brought the 5c, giving me two pair (tens and fives). This is a significant improvement. Flash called flop and turn, likely holding a ten with better kicker, 8x, or a heart draw. Heart draws missed. My two pair now beats single pairs like T9, TJ, 8x. I should value bet - not huge since better tens (TK, TQ, TJ) might call or raise, but enough to extract value from 8x and worse hands that will call. Around 60% pot.

On the river, the 5 of clubs improves my hand to two pair (Tens and Fives). The board is 2d-Tc-8h-6h-5c. In a blind-vs-blind battle, this is an extremely strong hand. Claude has led out on all three streets, which could indicate a strong hand like an overpair (JJ-AA), a set (88, 66, 22), or a straight (97). However, it could also be a missed draw (like J9 or hearts) or a top pair being bet for value. Given the pot odds (2.67 to 1) and the strength of two pair in this specific match-up, I must at least call. Raising is an option, but it risks getting shoved on by the few hands that beat me (sets or straights), and Claude might fold his bluffs or weaker one-pair hands to a raise. Calling is the safest and most profitable way to realize my equity.

I am in the UTG position with a very weak hand, 9c 5d. With a stack of 61 BBs, I have plenty of time to wait for better opportunities. Opening from early position with 95 offsuit is a losing play in the long run, especially against aggressive opponents with larger stacks. The only logical action is to fold.
On the button with J4 suited. Pro folded, leaving only the blinds. This is a good spot for a standard button open - suited jack has reasonable playability and I have position. Raising to 2.5x BB.

I am in the Small Blind with 3d 7d, which is a very weak starting hand. Claude has opened for 2.5bb from the Button. Playing this hand out of position against the Button and the Big Blind is generally unprofitable, especially with a hand that has such low equity and poor playability. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips to the pot.

Claude is opening from the button to 2.5bb in a 4-handed game, and KTo is comfortably within a standard BB defend range. We’re getting a good price (150 to win 400), have decent high-card equity, and calling keeps the pot manageable with a hand that can be dominated if we bloat it with a 3-bet. Against an active button, realizing equity in positionless but priced-in spots is fine; we can also leverage postflop aggression on favorable textures.

OOP as BB versus BTN on a Q-9-5 rainbow board, my range wants to do a lot of checking; BTN retains a clear top-pair advantage (many Qx), while my hand (KTo) is a medium-equity draw/overcard combo (gutshot to J plus overcard K, and occasionally a T pair) that benefits from realizing equity without inflating the pot. By checking I keep Claude’s bluffs in, can comfortably continue versus a reasonable c-bet sizing with my equity, and can apply pressure on favorable turns (J/A/K/T) rather than donk into a range that calls very often.

Missed this flop with J4 suited - no pair, just backdoor flush draw. However, I was the preflop aggressor and have position. The board is fairly disconnected (Q95). A small c-bet is standard here to maintain aggression and potentially take it down. I'll bet around 1/3 pot.

Claude’s c-bet is very small (200 into 750), giving excellent immediate pot odds and letting us continue with a wide defend range. With KTs on Qd-9s-5h rainbow we have K-high with a gutshot to the J (4 outs to the 9-T-J-Q-K straight) plus some additional equity from potentially pairing the K (and occasional T-pairing turns that can improve our showdown prospects). Check-raising as a bluff is higher variance versus the 2nd stack on a board where BTN retains plenty of strong Qx/9x continues, so the best stack-preserving, +EV line is to call and realize equity, then apply pressure on favorable turns (J/K/T/A) or when Claude shows weakness.

Turn pairs the Q (Qd-5h-9s-Qc), a card that generally strengthens BTN’s range advantage (lots of Qx) and reduces the incentive for us to bloat the pot with a low-equity bluff. With KTs we still have a gutshot to the J for a straight plus some clean improvement rivers (K/T making strong two-pair vs non-Q holdings), so the priority is realizing equity and keeping Claude’s bluffs/value-bets in rather than donk-leading into a range that can call/raise comfortably. Checking also lets us respond appropriately to sizing (continue vs small bets, re-evaluate vs big).

Turn paired the queens. I have jack high with no real equity - my backdoor diamond draw picked up only one out (need two running diamonds). FiveTwo called my flop c-bet, showing some interest. Betting again risks building a pot I can't win at showdown. The paired board does make it less likely they have a queen, but if they have a 9 or pocket pair, they're unlikely to fold to another barrel. Better to check behind and take the free river card - maybe spike a jack.

River is QQ99 with a 5 kicker on board; everyone’s hand is essentially QQ99X and it comes down to the single kicker. With KdTs we have a strong kicker (K) that beats all J-high and below and chops versus many Kx holdings, but still loses to any Ax (very common in BTN opens) and of course any Qx/9x/55-type full houses. If I lead, worse kickers mostly fold (so I don’t get paid), while Ax almost never folds and strong hands can raise—so betting mostly turns a decent showdown into a lower-EV bluff. Best is to take the free showdown and let Claude bluff if he chooses.

River is a double-paired board (QQ99). I have jack-high with J4d. FiveTwo has checked to me after calling preflop and flop. They've shown some interest in this pot. My hand has no value - just jack kicker playing the board. Bluffing here into a player who's called twice seems unprofitable, especially with a small pot. Better to check behind and see if my jack-high somehow wins at showdown.
9d3d is a weak suited hand from UTG position. This is not a hand I should be opening with - it's out of my UTG range. Easy fold.

I am on the button with Jack-Five offsuit (J5o). While I have positional advantage, J5o is a very weak hand to open with, especially with a short stack in the Big Blind (Pro) who might shove and a massive chip leader in the Small Blind (FiveTwo) who has been playing aggressively. There is no reason to risk chips with such low equity at this stage of the tournament.

Folds to us in the small blind, so this is a heads-up blind-vs-blind spot where we should attack very wide. With a big stack advantage over Pro, raising applies immediate pressure, wins the pot often, and gives us initiative. Ad5s isn’t a premium, but the ace blocker reduces the chance BB has strong aces, and the hand has enough playability to continue on many boards when called. I’ll use a standard, pressure-oriented sizing.

I am in the Big Blind with QJo facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). This is a standard Blind vs Blind scenario. QJo is significantly above the median hand strength and definitely strong enough to defend. FiveTwo is the chip leader with a deep stack, so 3-betting might invite aggressive 4-bets that would be uncomfortable to face. Calling allows me to realize my equity in position with a strong broadway hand against a likely wide SB opening range. The price is good (calling 200 to win 400), and playing in position is advantageous.

We’re the preflop raiser in a heads-up blind-vs-blind pot, so we retain a meaningful range advantage and can profitably c-bet a lot of this texture. While K♠7♠3♥ connects somewhat with BB’s defend range (Kx/7x, some spade floats), Pro has shown a tendency to play passively and fold to continued pressure, so a small sizing should fold out a lot of his total air and deny equity to random overcards/low pairs. With A♦5♠ we mostly have ace-high plus some backdoor straight potential and a runner-runner spade flush possibility, so we’re happy to take a low-risk stab that sets up barreling on good turns (A, spades, 4/2, etc.).

The flop comes Ks 7s 3h. I hold Qh Jd, giving me Queen-high with no flush draw and no real straight draw. The opponent (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader, bets 200 into a pot of 600 (1/3 pot). This is a standard c-bet size on a King-high board which connects well with the aggressor's range. While the price is enticing (getting 4:1 odds), my hand has very poor playability. I do not have a spade, so I cannot barrel on flush cards, and catching a Queen or Jack might not be enough if the opponent has a King. Against a c-bet range that includes Kx, flush draws, and pocket pairs, my equity is low. Floating with air and no backdoor draws against the chip leader is a losing play in the long run. The disciplined action is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game with 8c 5c. While these are suited, they are one of the weaker suited gappers and generally do not perform well when opening from early position, even in short-handed play. My stack of 9,725 (nearly 100 BB) is healthy, and there is no need to take unnecessary risks with a marginal hand like this. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

4-handed and on the button after UTG folds, we should apply pressure and open a wide range. Q8o is near the bottom but still acceptable as a steal: we have position, some high-card playability, and can often win preflop or with a single continuation bet. Both blinds can defend/3-bet, so I’ll keep sizing modest and be prepared to fold to a significant 3-bet.

I am in the Small Blind with Ks 4d, facing a raise to 250 from the Button (FiveTwo). My stack is 5750 (57.5 BB). Although Button's opening range is wide, K4o is a very weak hand to defend with, especially from the worst position at the table. It suffers from reverse implied odds (domination issues) and poor playability post-flop. Additionally, calling invites the Big Blind into the pot or gives them a squeeze opportunity. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here to preserve chips for better spots.

Q9o in the BB facing a button raise. Getting 2.7:1 pot odds (150 to win 400). Q9o is a reasonable defend against a button steal - FiveTwo has been active. Calling is standard here rather than folding or 3-betting.
Missed the flop completely with Q9o on K82 rainbow. No pair, no draw. Standard check and likely fold if FiveTwo c-bets, which is expected given their preflop aggression.

We opened BTN and got a single BB defend; on K-high dry-ish boards we retain a range advantage and can profitably c-bet small very often. With Q8o on Kh-2c-8d we have decent showdown value (middle pair) but are still vulnerable to overcards and random equity. A small continuation bet folds out a lot of hands that missed (QJ/JT/low pairs/air) while keeping the pot controlled vs Kx and stronger. If we get check-raised, this hand is not strong enough to continue versus meaningful sizing, so the plan is bet/fold most of the time.

Missed the flop completely with Q9o on K82. FiveTwo's small c-bet of 200 into 500 gives me 3.5:1 odds, needing ~22% equity. With only 6 outs to a pair that may not even be good against Kx, and no backdoor draws, this is a clear fold. K-high board heavily favors the preflop raiser's range.

4-handed UTG is effectively a late-position open; with a big stack edge I want to keep applying pressure and picking up blinds frequently. T6o is near the bottom of an opening range, so I’ll use a small raise size to risk little, keep my range wide, and take the initiative—especially since I’ll have position on the blinds when they call. Plan: c-bet selectively on favorable boards, and comfortably fold to any significant 3-bet given poor playability when pressured.

I hold 8s 4c on the Button, which is an extremely weak unsuited hand (84o). FiveTwo, the chip leader, has opened from UTG to 250. Given the strength of a typical UTG opening range and the poor quality of my hand, I have no incentive to enter this pot. Calling is negative EV, and 3-betting as a bluff is unnecessary and risky against the chip leader. The standard and correct play is to fold.

5-7 offsuit is a weak hand. Out of position against an UTG raiser (FiveTwo has been aggressive), and Flash still to act behind me. This hand doesn't play well multiway OOP and lacks the suited/connected qualities to make it worth defending. Easy fold.

FiveTwo raised to 250 from the UTG/CO position. I am in the Big Blind with pocket deuces (2d 2s). The pot is 400 and it costs me 150 to call, giving me excellent pot odds (3.66:1). With nearly 100 big blinds in effective stacks, I have the implied odds to set-mine profitably. Calling also closes the pre-flop action.

I am the first to act on the flop as the Big Blind. The board is dry and disconnected (9s 4h 3c). I hold pocket deuces, which is an underpair. Standard poker strategy dictates checking to the pre-flop aggressor in this spot. Leading out would likely only get called or raised by better hands, and checking allows me to see how FiveTwo responds. If he checks back, I get a free turn; if he bets, I will evaluate based on the size, though 22 has very little equity here.

Heads-up in position after opening pre, this 9-4-3 rainbow flop is fairly favorable for range advantage (I have all the overpairs and strong 9x), and BB’s check caps him somewhat. My hand has essentially no showdown value and no real draw, so checking back just concedes the pot too often. I’ll fire a small c-bet to fold out his unpaired overcards and some weak floats while risking little; if I get raised, I can comfortably fold this pure-air holding.

On this dry flop of 9s-4h-3c, my pocket deuces are an underpair. While FiveTwo's bet of 200 into 550 is relatively small (offering me pot odds of roughly 3.75:1), pocket deuces have very little equity or playability here. I am behind any pair, and even against his overcard bluffs (like AK or QJ), I am only a slight favorite while being vulnerable to almost any turn card. With only two outs to improve to a set and no meaningful draws, calling here would likely lead to me folding on the turn to a second barrel. I prefer to fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

In a 4-handed game, the UTG position acts as the Cutoff. While opening with any pocket pair is often standard from the Cutoff, holding Pocket Fours (44) puts me at the very bottom of that range. I am currently the shortest stack at the table with ~57 BB, while my opponents have significantly deeper stacks (ranging from 94 BB to 284 BB). The Big Blind is the chip leader and has been active, posing a high risk of a 3-bet or difficult post-flop aggression if I open. Since 44 has poor playability post-flop unless a set is hit, and I am out of position against the Button, I elect to fold to preserve chips for better spots rather than engaging in a marginal, high-variance situation against deep-stacked opponents.

Pocket Queens on the button is a premium hand. Pro folded UTG, so I'm first to act. Standard play is to raise for value - I'll make it 3x BB to 300 to build the pot and potentially isolate or win the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Seven offsuit. The Button (Claude) has raised to 3BB (300). This hand is too weak to defend out of position against a raise, especially with the chip leader (FiveTwo) still to act in the Big Blind.
