Q7o does not play well post-flop and the risk-to-reward ratio for a 3-bet bluff or a call is unfavorable here. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Claude is opening Button to 3x and we’re getting a very good price closing the action (200 to win 450). J8o is comfortably within a BB defend vs BTN range: it has decent high-card + straight potential and plays fine in a single-raised pot. A 3-bet bluff here is less attractive because J8o has poor blocker properties (doesn’t remove strong continuing hands like AJ/KJ/QQ+), and we’re out of position deep, so I’d rather keep the pot smaller and realize equity. Plan postflop: check a lot, continue on favorable boards (J-high/8-high, strong draws), and be cautious on A/K/Q-heavy textures where our pair potential is dominated.

We’re heads-up OOP in a single-raised pot on A♦T♠9♥. My hand (J♥8♦) has a strong open-ended straight draw (7/Q outs) plus a backdoor heart draw, so it performs well versus a BTN c-bet range. However, this flop also connects well with Claude’s opening range (many Ax/Tx), so leading isn’t necessary and a pure check-raise strategy would be too aggressive on a board where he can continue often. Best is to check my range, allow him to c-bet frequently, and then decide between call and occasional check-raise based on sizing/reads; if he checks back, I happily realize equity with a free turn.

I have QQ on A9T rainbow. The Ace is concerning but FiveTwo just flat-called preflop from BB, so their range includes many hands that missed. I have an overpair to 9 and T. Need to bet for value and protection against draws (KJ, QJ gutshots) and weaker pairs. Standard c-bet around 50-60% pot.

Claude’s 375 c-bet into ~650 (about 58% pot) gives me excellent immediate odds: I’m calling 375 to win 1400, needing ~26.8% equity. With J♥8♦ on A♦T♠9♥ I have an open-ended straight draw (4 sevens + 4 queens = 8 clean outs), which is ~31–32% to get there by the river, plus some additional equity when we turn a J (often best vs his air). A check-raise semi-bluff is an option, but this is a board that connects well with BTN’s range (Ax/Tx/two-pair/strong draws), so he’ll continue frequently and we don’t gain much fold equity while bloating the pot OOP. Best line is to call, realize equity, and apply pressure selectively on favorable turns (7/Q, or cards that reduce his range advantage when he slows down).

Turn goes check to us (to call 0) after Claude c-bet flop. On A♦T♠9♥K♠ our hand J♥8♦ is still a clean open-ender (7/Q for 8 outs) with essentially no showdown value, so taking a free card is fine, but stabbing is higher EV because Claude’s check caps him toward lots of medium/weak hands (Tx/9x, some missed broadways, some pot-control Ax) that can fold now—especially with the K♠ adding pressure. I’ll use a medium sizing to generate folds without risking too much vs a possible check-raise; if raised, we can comfortably fold (not getting the right price). If called, we barrel big for value on 7/Q rivers and mostly shut down on bricks.

The Ks is a terrible card for my QQ. The board now has A-K-T-9, and any J completes a straight. FiveTwo's donk bet of 600 into ~1350 pot is suspicious - could be Kx that just hit, Ax gaining confidence, two pair, a set, or QJ for the made straight/draw. My QQ is now an underpair to two overcards. However, this sizing (44% pot) could also be a probe/semi-bluff. Folding seems too tight given pot odds (600 to win 2000), and raising is terrible as I only get called by better. Calling to see a river and re-evaluate.

River is Kc, completing the board A♦ T♠ 9♥ K♠ K♣. With J♥8♦ we missed the open-ender and have essentially zero showdown value, so checking is mostly just giving up and folding to any bet.

This river is an excellent bluffing card for our line: after check/calling flop and leading turn on the K♠, our perceived range contains a lot of Kx (now trips) and some boats (AK, K9, KT, 99/TT), while Claude’s turn-call range includes many Ax/Tx hands that are now bluff-catchers facing a polarized river bet on a paired top card. We want a sizing that credibly represents strong Kx/boats and pressures Ax/Tx into folding without requiring an unrealistic fold frequency.

I’ll fire a near-pot, polarized bluff and fold to any raise.

Board is 9h-Ad-Ts-Ks-Kc. My QQ is now just a pair of Queens with A-K-K on board. FiveTwo donk-bet turn when K hit, now bets 2500 into 2600 pot on river when K paired. This line screams strength - Kx now has trips, Ax has two pair, QJ has broadway straight. My QQ loses to all of these. FiveTwo's flat pre then donk-betting when K appears is classic "I hit my hand" play. Even getting 3:1 pot odds, I'm not good 25%+ of the time here against their value-heavy line. Fold.
A7o from UTG in a 4-handed game. This is a reasonable opening hand at this stack depth. Standard raise to 2.5x to build the pot with an ace-high hand while maintaining fold equity.

Holding Ace-Five offsuit on the button in a four-handed game provides a good opportunity to 3-bet as a semi-bluff. Claude has been opening frequently, and the Ace in my hand serves as a strong blocker to premium hands like AA, AK, or AQ. By raising here, I can take control of the pot and potentially win it immediately, or play it in position if Claude calls. Given the stack sizes, a 3-bet to 750 is a standard sizing to put pressure on the opener and the blinds.

We’re in the small blind facing an UTG open and a Button 3-bet, with the big blind still to act. 9♣7♥ is a weak, offsuit hand with poor playability out of position and no relevant blockers for a 4-bet bluff (doesn’t block AA/KK/QQ/AK). Calling 700 creates a bloated pot OOP versus strong ranges and invites the BB to come along or jam, putting us in tough spots. Despite our chip lead, this is not a good spot to apply pressure with this combo; we can find much better squeeze candidates and preserve stack edge.

I hold Ace-King suited (AcKc) in the Big Blind, a premium hand. The action involves an UTG raise to 250 from Claude and a Button 3-bet to 750 from Flash. With a stack of 5650 (56.5 BB), I am deep enough that an immediate all-in shove (over 50BB) would be a significant overbet and might only get called by better hands or flips, folding out dominated ranges I want to keep in or induce shoves from. However, calling is passive and plays poorly out of position in a likely multi-way pot. A cold 4-bet is the optimal line to assert strength, deny equity to the UTG opener, and isolate the Button or induce a 5-bet jam from worse hands like QQ-99 or AQ. I will raise to 2000 (roughly 2.7x the 3-bet), which commits a significant portion of my stack (leaving ~3650 behind with a pot of ~4000+ if called), setting up an easy shove on any flop or a snap-call if they jam pre-flop.

I opened A7o from UTG, Flash 3-bet to 750, and now Pro has 4-bet to 2000 with only 3750 total stack (already committed over 50%). Pro's 4-bet here represents significant strength - he's nearly pot committed. Flash is still behind me and could wake up with a hand too. A7o plays terribly against a 4-bet range and I'm potentially squeezed between two players. Easy fold - I've only invested 250 chips and A7o is dominated by virtually any 4-betting range.

After my initial 3-bet with Ace-Five offsuit, Pro has cold 4-bet from the Big Blind. While Pro is the short stack with 37.5 big blinds, a cold 4-bet into both the UTG opener and the 3-bettor represents a very strong range, typically JJ+ or AK. Although my Ace acts as a blocker, A5o is heavily dominated by Pro's calling and raising range (like AQ, AK, or larger pairs). To see the hand to the end, I would eventually have to commit 3000 more chips (to cover his total 3750 stack) to win a total pot of approximately 7800. This requires about 38% equity. Against a range of [TT+, AQs+, AKo], A5o only has about 29-30% equity. Preserving my stack of nearly 80 big blinds is more important than taking a high-variance, mathematically disadvantageous gamble here.

AQo is a premium hand in a 4-handed game. I am first to act (UTG) and should definitely open for a raise. A standard 3x big blind open is appropriate here to build the pot and potentially isolate or take the blinds down.

Facing a 3x open from Flash in 4-handed play, 4♠2♠ is simply too low in raw equity and, more importantly, suffers from heavy reverse implied odds (dominated flushes/straights when we make marginal holdings). While we have position and a big stack, this is not a strong candidate for a profitable flat (poor realization) or a 3-bet bluff (too weak when called and doesn’t block strong continues). Preserve stack and wait for better steal/pressure spots.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Three offsuit (Jc 3h), facing a 3bb open raise from Flash in UTG position. J3o is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position against an early position opener. There are no pot odds or strategic reasons to continue with this hand. The correct play is to fold.

Ah6h in the BB facing a 300 UTG raise. Suited ace is a solid defending hand. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 450). This is a standard call - suited aces have good postflop playability with nut flush potential. No need to 3-bet here, just defend and see a flop.

Flopped top pair with Ah6h on 2d Ac Tc. My kicker is weak but I have the best ace. Board has club draw potential. Standard play here is to check to the preflop raiser (Flash) and let them c-bet, then call. I'll keep the pot controlled with my marginal top pair.

I have top pair with a strong kicker (AQo) on a board of A-T-2. This board is very favorable for my pre-flop raising range. Since the Big Blind checked to me, I will place a continuation bet for value. There are several draws available (flush and straight draws) that I want to charge, and I can also get called by weaker Aces or Tens.

I flopped top pair with A6 on A-T-2 board with two clubs. Flash c-bet 400 into 650. I have top pair but weak kicker. Pot odds are 1450:400 = 3.6:1. Standard play is to call - raising risks getting 3-bet by better aces or sets, and I want to keep the pot controlled with a marginal hand. Club draw is out there but I'm not folding top pair to a standard c-bet.

Turn 8d is a brick. I have top pair with weak kicker (A6). Flash c-bet flop and I called. Now it's checked to me. With marginal top pair, I should check to control pot size. If Flash bets, I can call river. If they check, I might thin value bet a safe river. No need to bloat the pot OOP with a hand that's ahead of bluffs but behind value.

I have top pair with a strong kicker (AQ). The 8d on the turn is a relatively safe card that doesn't change the board texture significantly. Claude has checked to me again after calling my flop bet, which suggests he likely holds a weaker Ace, a Ten, or a draw (especially clubs or a straight draw). I will bet for value to get called by those weaker hands and to charge the draws. A bet of 850 (roughly 60% of the pot) is a standard sizing to maximize value while keeping the pot manageable.

Turn 8d is a brick. I have top pair with A6 - weak kicker but still top pair. Flash bet 850 into 1450, about 60% pot. Pot odds are 3150:850 = 3.7:1. Flash could have better aces (AK, AQ, AJ, AT), sets, or semi-bluffing club draws. Folding top pair here is too weak given pot odds and Flash's range includes plenty of worse hands and draws. I should call and evaluate the river.

River Jd is a bad card for me. It completes KQ straight and gives AJ two pair (Flash's range includes AJ given the preflop raise). I have top pair weak kicker (A6). Betting for value is thin - I get called mostly by better hands (AJ, AT, AK, AQ, sets). Checking keeps pot controlled and gives me showdown value. If Flash bets, I'll face a tough decision but likely call given pot odds. Best play is check and see what happens.

On the river, the board has become quite coordinated with the Jack of Diamonds. This card completes potential straights (KQ), two-pair combinations (AJ, JT, T8), and a back-door diamond flush. While I still have top pair with a strong kicker (AQ), Claude has called two streets of betting and now checks for the third time. Given the stack depths and the board texture, betting again risks a check-raise from a hand that improved on the river, and it is unlikely a hand significantly worse than mine will call a third barrel. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value and win against weaker Aces or missed club draws without further risk.

4-handed, UTG is effectively a cutoff spot and Q9o is a reasonable open: it has decent high-card value, can make top pair with good kicker strength versus blinds’ wide ranges, and benefits from our big stack applying pressure. With short/medium stacks behind, raising is higher EV than limping, and we can often win the blinds or take favorable flops in position vs the blinds’ defended ranges. I’ll use a standard small open to keep our risk low while still leveraging fold equity.

I am on the Button with AdTd (Ace-Ten suited). The UTG player (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader and quite active, has opened to 250 (2.5BB). In a 4-handed game, the UTG position acts as the Cutoff, so their range is reasonably wide. ATs is a strong hand that plays very well in position. While a 3-bet is a viable option to apply pressure, flat-calling allows me to keep the pot manageable, keep the opener's range wide (including hands I dominate like KTs, QTs, weaker Ax), and leverage my position post-flop without bloating the pot against a potentially dominating hand (like AK, AQ, or high pairs). Given my stack depth of ~67BB, I prefer the lower variance line of calling and playing post-flop.

8h3d is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Even with decent pot odds (200 to win 650), this hand rarely flops well and will be difficult to play profitably against two opponents post-flop. Easy fold.

I am in the big blind holding 6d 3s, which is a very weak starting hand. FiveTwo opened for a raise from the UTG/Cutoff position and Pro called from the button. Even though it's only 150 more to call into a pot of 650, my hand has extremely poor playability and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand against two opponents, one of whom (FiveTwo) has been very active and holds a massive chip lead. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser on K-K-2, this is a very static, high-card paired board where a small c-bet has strong fold equity versus Pro’s large whiffed portion (Ax/Qx/Jx, small pairs that don’t want to continue) while keeping our risk low when we’re called (mostly by any Kx and some stubborn pocket pairs). With Q9o we have essentially no showdown value, so betting small is better than checking and surrendering our equity share. If raised we can comfortably fold; if called, we’ll usually slow down and look for selective barrels on favorable turns (A, club, or cards that improve our perceived range).

The opponent (FiveTwo) c-bets 200 into a pot of 650 on a paired board of [2c, Kc, Ks]. This is a very standard small c-bet (approx 30% pot) that a wide opener will make with their entire range, including air, pocket pairs, and Kings. I hold [Ad, Td], giving me Ace-high which is currently ahead of all their bluffs (like QJ, JTs, suited connectors). Folding Ace-high to a single small bet on this texture in position against an active opener would be over-folding. Raising doesn't accomplish much as I isolate myself against better hands. Calling allows me to keep their bluffing range wide and re-evaluate on the turn, utilizing my position. The price is excellent (calling 200 to win a total pot of 1050), requiring very little equity to continue.

We’re heads-up as the preflop aggressor on a very range-advantage board (K-K-x). After Pro calls the small flop c-bet, the Jh is a good barrel card: it’s an overcard to a lot of his “float once” hands (Ax highs, small/mid pocket pairs) and it credibly improves my value range (KJ/JJ/AK/KQ). With Q9 I have essentially no showdown value but I did pick up a small gutshot to the T, so continuing with a controlled second barrel is higher EV than checking and letting him realize equity or stab. I’ll use a small-to-medium sizing to maximize fold equity versus his marginal continues while keeping the bluff cheap and folding comfortably if raised.

On the Turn, the board is [2c, Kc, Ks, Jh]. The pot is 1050, and the opponent bets 350 (1/3 pot). While I hold Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (requiring a Queen), the Jack is a card that interacts very well with an active UTG opening range (connecting with KJ, QJ, AJ, JT). Even pocket pairs like QQ-88 remain ahead of me. While the price is enticing (getting 4:1 odds), my outs are not guaranteed to be clean (a Queen could give the opponent a Full House if they hold KJ, KQ, or JJ), and my Ace-high is unlikely to be good against a double-barrel on this texture. Preserving my stack in this spot is prudent given I am likely behind a significant portion of their value range and only ahead of pure bluffs.

I am in UTG (effectively the Cutoff in a 4-handed game) with 5h 9d, which is a very weak unsuited hand. With 63 big blinds, there is no pressure to make desperate moves, and this hand is far below the opening range for any position, especially first to act. The correct play is a standard fold.

K9o on the button is a solid stealing hand. Pro folded UTG, leaving only the blinds. This is a standard spot to open raise and try to take down the blinds. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x BB) - a standard button open size.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit in the small blind, which is the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Claude has raised from the button to 250. There is no strategic reason to call or raise with this hand, especially out of position against a button raise and with the chip leader yet to act in the big blind. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Claude opens the button to 2.5bb and we’re getting a decent price, but Th2o is too far down the range: dominated when we make a ten, poor playability out of position, and minimal profitable runouts. With deep stacks there’s no need to defend this weakly, and it’s not a good candidate for a 3-bet bluff either. Preserve chips and fold.
QTo from UTG in a 4-handed game is a reasonable open. It's a connected broadway hand with good high card strength. Standard opening raise to 250 (2.5x BB).

I am on the button with Jack of Spades and Six of Diamonds (J6o). Claude has raised to 2.5 big blinds from UTG. J6o is a very weak hand to defend against an early position open, even with the benefit of being on the button. With a healthy stack of over 100 big blinds, there is no reason to get involved in a pot with a marginal hand like this. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

4-handed, Claude’s UTG open is likely fairly wide. KTo is strong enough to defend the small blind getting a good price (200 more to win 400) and plays reasonably as a bluff-catcher/top-pair type hand. I prefer calling over 3-betting here because KTo is often dominated when called, and a 3-bet risks inducing a shove from the shorter-stacked big blind, which we’d have to fold to and burn chips. Call and play postflop, keeping ranges wide and pot size controlled out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 3s, facing a raise from UTG and a call from the Small Blind. The pot odds are attractive (calling 150 to win a total pot of 750, or 5:1), but my hand is extremely weak (bottom 5% of hands). Playing 83 offsuit out of position against two opponents, one of whom showed strength from early position, will lead to significant under-realization of equity. It is difficult to hit a flop hard enough to continue profitably. Saving the 150 chips is the better long-term play.

Flop comes Q♠8♣7♥, and with K♠T♦ we have no pair and no real straight draw—just two overcards plus a weak backdoor spade possibility. Out of position versus the preflop raiser, this board connects reasonably well with Claude’s opening range (many Qx, 88/77, broadways), so leading doesn’t accomplish much and risks getting raised or called by better. Best line is to check, keep the pot controlled, and evaluate versus a c-bet size (likely folding to anything but a very small stab).

I flopped top pair with a ten kicker on 7h8c Qs. FiveTwo checked to me. This is a clear value bet spot - I should bet to get value from worse queens, middle pairs, and charge straight draws (9T, 56, 69). Betting around 60% pot.

Claude’s 375 c-bet into 600 is a fairly standard sizing on a board that hits his range, but we’re getting ~27.8% pot odds (call 375 to win 1350). With K♠T♦ we have 6 immediate overcard outs plus some backdoor equity (spades/straight runouts) and, importantly as the big stack, we can apply pressure on favorable turns (A/K/T/J/9/spade) if he slows down. A check-raise here will often get continued against by his Qx/sets and risks creating an inflated pot OOP with a weak hand, so the best line is to continue as a float and re-evaluate on the turn.

Claude’s flop c-bet followed by a check on a total blank turn (2h) caps his range toward a lot of give-ups and some marginal one-pair/pocket-pair hands looking for pot control. With K♠T♦ we have little showdown value but still some equity (K or T improving), and this is a good spot to apply pressure and deny his overcard-equity hands from realizing for free. A medium stab should fold out his air and some underpairs/weak 8x at a good frequency, while keeping our risk controlled if he springs a check-raise (we can comfortably fold).

FiveTwo check-called flop then donk-leads 650 on a blank 2h turn. This sizing (48% pot) is suspicious - could be a weaker queen trying to block, two pair/set building the pot, or a draw semi-bluffing. With top pair decent kicker, I'm ahead of most of their value range (weaker queens like Q9, QJ) and draws. Raising risks folding out worse hands and only getting action from better. Calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me evaluate the river.

River is Q♠8♣7♥2♥9♥ and we have K♠T♦: pure air with essentially zero showdown value after we take this line (check/call flop, lead turn, get called). Claude’s turn check then call caps him heavily to one-pair hands (a lot of Qx, some 8x/underpairs) plus some slowplays; the 9♥ is a great scare card because it completes the backdoor heart flush and also brings in JT for a straight (and improves hands like 98). Our turn lead is very consistent with picking up a backdoor-heart draw with two hearts and now barreling for value, so we can credibly represent a strong made hand. Checking just concedes the pot almost always, so the best play is to apply pressure with a sizable bluff that targets folds from his Qx/no-heart and medium pairs. If he raises, we snap-fold.

The 9h river is terrible for my hand. It completes: JT for a straight (J-T-9-8-7), 56 for a straight (5-6-7-8-9), T6 straight, and any heart flush draw. FiveTwo's line of check-call flop, donk turn, then size up on river screams strength - either a completed draw or slow-played two pair/set. With just top pair ten kicker, I'm losing to all straights, flushes, sets, and two pairs (Q9, Q8, Q7, 78, 97, 98 all got there or were already ahead). I'm only beating weaker queens without draws and pure bluffs. Given the coordinated board and FiveTwo's aggressive tendencies with made hands, this is a fold.

I am in the UTG position with Jc 6c. This is a weak, speculative hand in a 4-handed game. While the suitedness adds some value, J6 suited is generally a fold from under the gun as it's easily dominated and difficult to play post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my 10,125 chip stack.

4-handed and folded to us on the Button, we should be opening very wide to pick up the dead blinds and leverage position. 75o is near the bottom of a reasonable BTN opening range, but still profitable as a small raise given our big stack and positional advantage, especially versus a shorter SB stack that is incentivized to play more fit-or-fold. I’ll keep the sizing modest to risk little while denying the blinds a cheap look; if we face a 3-bet (especially from the SB), this hand will usually just fold.

I am holding 3s 6d in the Small Blind, which is an extremely weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. Facing a raise from the Button (FiveTwo, the chip leader), I am out of position and unlikely to realize any equity even if I call. The pot odds do not justify playing such a poor hand from the worst position. Folding is the only standard and correct play here to preserve my stack of 61.5 BB for better opportunities.

3c4c suited is a marginal hand to defend from the BB. While suited connectors have some playability, this is at the bottom of the range. FiveTwo has been aggressive and has a 3:1 stack advantage. The pot odds of 150 to win 550 (27%) aren't quite good enough for this weak holding that will be difficult to play postflop out of position. Folding is the disciplined play.

4-handed UTG is effectively a cutoff open, and AQo is well within a strong, profitable opening range. Raising builds the pot when we have a hand that dominates many calls (worse Ax/Qx, broadways), applies pressure to the blinds, and keeps initiative. With a shorter stack on the Button who may reshove, this open also sets up a clear response (AQo can often continue vs jams depending on sizing/ranges).

I am on the Button with Qh 7d. The player Under The Gun (FiveTwo) has raised to 250. Q7 offsuit is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop and is often dominated by a UTG opening range (e.g., KQ, AQ, QJs). Even against a loose opener, calling is negative EV, and 3-betting is not suitable with this holding. Folding is the correct standard play.

8d9h in the SB facing a UTG open from FiveTwo who has been very active. Getting 2:1 pot odds to call 200 more. This is a speculative connected hand that plays okay multiway. Will call to see a flop cheaply, hoping Flash comes along or I flop well.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of Sevens (7h 7s). FiveTwo raised to 250 from UTG, and Claude called from the Small Blind. The action returns to me needing to call 150 more into a pot that will be 600. Pocket sevens are a strong hand but vulnerable in a multi-way pot, especially out of position. However, with the current odds, folding is out of the question. I could raise to thin the field, but given FiveTwo's large stack and active play style, and Claude's tendency to defend, a 3-bet might just bloat the pot when I am out of position for the rest of the hand. I will choose to call to see a flop, looking to set-mine or navigate safe boards, keeping the pot manageable.

I have 8d9h on a Jd Kh 4s board - complete whiff. No pair, no meaningful draw. I have only backdoor straight possibilities needing both a T and Q. With no equity and position, I should check and see a free turn. If anyone bets, I'm folding.

I am holding pocket sevens on a board with two overcards (King and Jack). This flop likely favors the UTG raiser's (FiveTwo) range significantly. Since Claude has checked to me, my best course of action is to check as well. Leading into the pre-flop raiser here would be risky, and I want to see how FiveTwo acts before committing more chips. My hand is currently a marginal holding that is easily beaten by any King or Jack, so I will play cautiously.

We have no made hand (Ace-high) but do hold a gutshot to Broadway (T completes). As the preflop raiser on a fairly dry K-J-4 rainbow board, we retain a meaningful range advantage (strong Kx, AA/KK/JJ, AK/KQ) and can profitably use a small multiway c-bet to fold out a lot of missed hands (small pairs without a K/J, random suited connectors, weak Qx/Tx) while denying equity. Keeping the sizing small also limits the cost when we get called and makes folding to a check-raise straightforward.

I have 8d9h on Jd Kh 4s - complete miss. Only a gutshot to a straight (need a T for 8-9-T-J). Getting 4:1 pot odds but a gutshot is only ~8% to hit. Even if I hit, higher straights (AQ) are possible. No reason to continue with this weak holding.

I am holding pocket sevens on a board of K-J-4. This is a very poor flop for my hand as there are two overcards that hit a typical UTG opening range very hard. FiveTwo raised from UTG pre-flop and is now continuation-betting into two players. While the bet size is small (1/3 pot), my hand has very little potential to improve and I am out of position. Claude has already folded, and continuing with third pair against the chip leader's UTG range is likely to lead to losing more chips on later streets. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in UTG with AJs, a premium hand in a 4-handed game. With a stack of over 60 big blinds, I should open raise to build the pot and seize the initiative. The standard open size at this table has been around 2.5bb (250), which balances value generation with pot control.
34o is a very weak hand. Facing an UTG raise of 250, this is a clear fold even from the button. No reason to call with such poor holdings against what is typically a strong opening range.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c 7d. Pro (UTG) raised to 250 (2.5x BB). My stack is healthy at nearly 100 BBs, but 97o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a raise from the under-the-gun position. In a 4-handed game, ranges are wider, but this hand still lacks the connectivity or high-card strength required to profitably defend against a raise. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here.

We’re in the BB facing a 2.5x UTG open (4-handed) from a ~59bb stack. 8♣4♦ offsuit is too weak to profitably defend out of position versus an UTG range, and it’s also a poor candidate to 3-bet bluff because we have very limited playability and can’t comfortably continue versus a 4-bet or postflop pressure. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.
K6o from UTG is too weak to open, even 4-handed. It's a dominated hand that will often face difficult postflop decisions. Folding is standard here.

I am on the button with Ten-Six offsuit. While I have the opportunity to steal the blinds, this hand is significantly below the standard opening range for the button. FiveTwo in the small blind has a very large stack and has shown a high level of aggression, while Pro in the big blind has a shorter stack and might be looking to defend or shove. There is no reason to risk chips with a weak hand at this stage of the tournament.

Folds to me in the small blind heads-up vs the big blind with a large stack advantage. JTo is strong enough to open and benefits from taking the initiative; Pro’s stack is short enough that they’re under pressure and will fold a meaningful portion of hands. I’ll use a small, standard SB open to risk little while still generating fold equity. If Pro jams, I can comfortably raise/fold given the required equity versus a typical shove range and my tournament leverage.

I am in the Big Blind with AJo facing a 2.5x raise from the Small Blind (FiveTwo), who is the massive chip leader and aggressive. AJo is a very strong hand in a Blind vs Blind configuration, well ahead of the opener's likely wide range. While a 3-bet is a viable option for value, calling is superior here for several reasons: 1) It keeps the opponent's range wide, including many dominated hands (weak Ax, Kx, Qx) that might fold to a 3-bet. 2) I have position post-flop, allowing me to control the pot size and bluff-catch effectively against an aggressive player. 3) 3-betting risks bloating the pot or facing a 4-bet shove, which would be a high-variance spot for my 62bb stack. Therefore, I will flat call to play a pot in position with a strong hand.

This K-T-7 two-tone flop hits the BB’s defending range reasonably well (lots of Kx, Tx, suited spades), while my JTo is a medium-strength made hand (second pair) that doesn’t want to face a raise/shove and inflate the pot out of position. Checking keeps my range protected, controls pot size, and lets Pro stab with worse hands/draws that I can often call at sensible sizes. If Pro checks back, I realize equity and can value/protect on many turns.

I am in the Big Blind with AdJd (Wait, hand history says Jd Ah, which is offsuit). Pre-flop, I defended against a Small Blind open. The flop comes Ts 7d Ks. This board connects well with both ranges. The Small Blind (FiveTwo) checks to me. My hand (Ace-high + gutshot straight draw to the nuts) has decent showdown value and draw equity. Betting here likely only folds out hands I am already beating (lower air), while hands that beat me (Kx, Tx, 7x) will call or raise. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially hit a Queen for the straight or an Ace for top pair on the turn, and keeps the pot manageable. There is no need to turn this hand into a bluff yet or inflate the pot with marginal value.

Turn 3c is a brick that doesn’t improve Pro’s range much, while after the flop checked through Pro is weighted toward medium-strength hands (Tx/7x/pocket pairs) and floats/spade draws that will often fold to a delayed c-bet or call once. With middle pair (T) and no spade, I want to (1) deny equity to spade/overcard hands, (2) get thin value from worse pairs, and (3) keep the pot small enough that I can comfortably fold if Pro springs a check-raise jam with a king/two pair+. A small, pressure-applied sizing accomplishes all three.

In this Blind vs Blind spot, I am facing a half-pot bet (250 into 500) on the Turn after the action went check-check on the Flop (K-T-7). The Turn card is a 3c, which is a total blank. My hand (AJo) holds Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needing a Queen). The opponent's line of checking the flop and leading small on the turn is often indicative of a marginal hand (like a weak Ten, a Seven, or a small pocket pair) or a semi-bluff. I am getting good pot odds (3:1) to call. I have 4 outs to the nut straight and 3 outs to top pair, plus my Ace-high occasionally has showdown value against pure bluffs. I have position, which gives me the option to bluff the river if checked to, or check back for showdown. Folding is too tight given the price and my equity.

River brings the 3rd spade, which is a meaningful range-shifter: a chunk of Pro’s turn-calling hands that were spade draws now improved, and Kx still dominates us. With JTo (one pair, no spade), a river value bet is thin at best—worse Tx/7x/pocket pairs will often be uncomfortable calling on this texture, while better hands (Kx, flushes, occasional 88/slowplays) continue. Given my big stack vs Pro’s short stack, the highest-EV/lowest-variance line is to take showdown and then decide versus a bet (comfortably calling small, folding to large pressure).

I am in the Big Blind with AJo. The board is Ts 7d Ks 3c 8s. The action went check-check on the Flop, then I called a small 250 bet on the Turn. On the River, the 8s completes a flush draw (spades) and puts a potential straight (J9) on the board. The opponent checks. My Ace-high has some showdown value against missed straight draws like QJ or Q9, or random air. Turning this into a bluff is risky against the massive chip leader who might look me up with any pair (Tx, 8x, 7x). Since I have position and a hand that can win against bluffs, checking back to realize my equity is the lowest variance and most standard play.

In a 4-handed game, Queen-Jack suited (Qc Jc) is a very strong hand to open from under-the-gun (effectively the cutoff). I have a healthy stack of nearly 100 big blinds. I will open with a standard raise to 300 chips to build the pot and take control of the hand, which has been consistent with my opening sizes in previous hands.

Flash opened UTG to 3bb; with 4♣3♦ offsuit we have very poor equity, no useful blockers, and terrible playability. Even on the button, calling invites a squeeze from the blinds and leaves us navigating dominated/low-connectivity spots with little ability to realize equity. A bluff 3-bet also isn’t attractive with this hand (no high-card blockers, weak postflop when called). Preserve chips and wait for better steal/3-bet candidates.

I am in the Small Blind with J3o, a very weak hand. The UTG player (Flash) has raised to 300. Calling would require investing 250 chips into a pot that will be difficult to win out of position with such a weak holding against a likely strong UTG range. The pot odds do not justify a call given the poor equity and playability of J3o. Folding is the only standard option.

KJo in the BB facing a 3x open from UTG (Flash). This is a decent hand - strong enough to defend but marginal for 3-betting against an UTG range which is typically tighter. I'm getting good pot odds (200 to win 450) and I close the action. Calling to see a flop is the standard play here.

Completely whiffed this flop with KJo on 6d-2s-5s. I have two overcards but no pair, no draw. This low, connected board with a spade draw could easily hit Flash's UTG range (pocket pairs, suited connectors). No reason to bet as a bluff here - I'll check and potentially catch a K or J on later streets, or give up if faced with aggression.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with two overcards and a backdoor club draw on a low, connected flop (6d 2s 5s). Claude checked from the Big Blind, which is a standard play for a caller. While this board texture can favor a Big Blind's defending range, I will put out a standard continuation bet of around 40% of the pot to test his hand and potentially take the pot down immediately. If called, I still have two overcards that could improve my hand on the turn.

Missed flop completely with KJo on 6d-2s-5s. Flash c-bet 250 into 650 (small ~38% pot sizing). I have two overcards but no pair, no draws, and no backdoor spades. Pot odds are about 3.6:1 needing ~22% equity - I have roughly 12% to hit on turn with 6 outs. Against UTG opener's range on this low connected board, floating with just overcards is marginal. No backdoor flush draw makes this even weaker. Disciplined fold.

4-handed UTG with AsKh is a clear value open. We also have a big stack advantage over three shorter stacks, so raising applies pressure and denies them cheap realization. A slightly larger open helps reduce multiway pots and sets up comfortable call-offs versus reshoves from the short stacks (especially Button).

I am on the Button with 7c 6h, an off-suit connector. The player in UTG (FiveTwo) has raised to 300. Given that an UTG opening range is typically strong and 76o is a weak speculative hand that plays poorly against strong ranges, I cannot profitably call or 3-bet. My stack is 57.5 BB, which allows me to wait for better spots. Folding is the standard play here.

Q6o is a weak offsuit hand facing a UTG raise. FiveTwo opened from UTG which typically indicates a stronger range. I'm out of position in the SB and would need to call 250 into a 450 pot. Q6o doesn't play well enough OOP to justify calling here - easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Qc 8h. FiveTwo, the table chip leader, has raised to 300 from the UTG position. While FiveTwo is an active player, an UTG open typically represents a stronger range than late-position opens. My hand, Queen-Eight offsuit, has poor playability post-flop and is often dominated by the hands an UTG player raises with (such as AQ, KQ, or QJ). Although I am getting roughly 2.25:1 odds to call, the risk of playing a marginal hand out of position against a massive stack outweighs the immediate pot odds. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in UTG position (first to act in 4-handed play) with 9c 4d, which is a very weak unconnected off-suit hand. With a stack of 57.5 BBs, I should be playing a solid range and avoiding marginal or garbage hands, especially out of position. This is a trivial fold.

7h4c is a weak offsuit hand. While I have button position, this hand plays poorly postflop and doesn't have enough value to open. FiveTwo in the BB has a massive stack and has been active. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 3s 6d, which is an extremely weak hand. The action has folded to me, leaving only the Big Blind, FiveTwo, who is the current chip leader with a massive stack and has shown significant aggression throughout the tournament (e.g., Hand 16, Hand 39). While it only costs 50 more to see a flop, 3-6 offsuit is nearly impossible to play profitably out of position, especially against a deep-stacked, aggressive opponent who is likely to put pressure on post-flop. Attempting a steal raise is also risky given FiveTwo's tendency to defend or re-raise. The best course of action is to fold and preserve my stack for a better starting hand.
KJh suited is a strong opening hand from UTG in a 4-handed game. This is a clear open raise spot. I'll raise to 2.5x to build the pot with this premium suited broadway hand.

Claude has raised to 250 from UTG (which is effectively the Cutoff in a 4-handed game). My hand, Queen-Six offsuit (Qs 6c), is quite weak and significantly under the threshold for calling or 3-betting against an open raise, even on the Button. Staying disciplined and folding here preserves my stack for more favorable spots.

Claude’s UTG open is a strong range 4-handed, and 9♠4♣ offsuit has very poor playability, reverse implied odds, and we’ll be out of position the whole hand from the small blind. Flat-calling is unprofitable and invites the BB to come along; 3-bet bluffing this combo is too low quality (no blockers, no connectivity/suitedness) and unnecessarily risks chips against the UTG range. Best is to preserve stack and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with As7d facing a 2.5x open from Claude in UTG. The Small Blind folded. I need to call 150 chips into a pot of 400, offering me direct pot odds of roughly 27%. Although UTG opening ranges are typically strong and often dominate A7o, the price is too good to fold a hand with an Ace. A7o has sufficient equity to defend, but plays poorly in inflated pots out of position, so 3-betting is risky. I will call to close the action and evaluate the flop, playing cautiously due to kicker problems if an Ace hits.

I am out of position with Ace-high (As7d) on a coordinated flop of [Td 5d 9c]. This board texture connects well with an UTG opening range, which likely includes overpairs, sets (TT, 99, 55), and strong broadway hands (AT, KTs, QJs). My hand has very little equity, lacking a flush draw or straight draw (only a backdoor straight draw). Leading out (donk betting) would be burning money. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If Claude bets, I will fold easily as I cannot float out of position with just Ace-high on this wet board.

I have KJh on Td 5d 9c. I have two overcards and a gutshot straight draw (Q gives me the nut straight). Pro checked from BB after flatting my preflop raise. This is a standard c-bet spot - I have equity with overcards and a gutshot, and Pro's range is wide from a BB defend. Betting around 60% pot to continue my aggression and potentially take it down.

I am out of position on a wet board [Td 5d 9c] with only Ace-high and no significant draws. The opponent, who opened UTG, has fired a c-bet of 350 into a pot of 550 (approx. 64% pot). This board texture connects well with the UTG range (sets, overpairs, strong draws, AT). My hand (As7d) has very low playability and equity to continue. As planned in the previous street's analysis, I will fold to this bet.

In a 4-handed game, UTG is effectively the Cutoff. Ace-Six offsuit is a marginal but playable opening hand in this position to exert pressure on the blinds and the button. I will open for a standard raise of 2.5x the big blind.

4-handed on the Button vs Flash’s likely wide UTG open, 77 is strong enough to continue and benefits from taking the initiative. A 3-bet here pressures a wide range, often wins preflop (net +400), and when called we play in position with a hand that realizes well and denies equity to two-overcard hands. With stack cover, we can apply ICM/stack pressure without risking our tournament life; plan is to play postflop in position and comfortably fold to a significant 4-bet/jam (which will be heavily weighted to bigger pairs/AK).

I am in the Small Blind with Kd Td facing a UTG open to 250 and a Button 3-bet to 850. This is a very strong action sequence. My hand, while suited and broadway, is easily dominated by the ranges of a UTG opener and a Button 3-bettor (hands like AK, KQ, AT, TT+). Additionally, I would be playing out of position in a bloated pot with a stack of roughly 54 Big Blinds, which is awkward for a cold call or a 4-bet shove. The risk of being dominated or squeezed is too high. Preserving my stack for a better spot is the optimal strategy.

QJd is a solid suited broadway hand. FiveTwo has shown aggressive 3-betting tendencies throughout the session. Getting ~1.67:1 odds to call, and QJd plays well postflop with flush and straight potential. Folding is too tight, 4-betting is too risky with this hand. Calling to see a flop is the best play.

I am currently UTG (Cutoff in a 4-handed game) with Ace-Six offsuit. I opened for 250, but FiveTwo (the chip leader) 3-bet to 850, and Claude called from the Big Blind. While the pot odds are somewhat tempting (600 to call into a 2000 pot), Ace-Six offsuit plays very poorly in 3-bet multi-way pots, especially out of position against an aggressive chip leader. My hand is likely dominated by FiveTwo's 3-betting range or Claude's calling range. Folding here preserves my stack and avoids a difficult post-flop situation where I would likely be 'fit-or-fold' with a weak kicker.

Completely whiffed this flop with QJd on 5h3d6s. I have two overcards and a backdoor diamond draw (only one diamond on board). No reason to lead into the preflop aggressor - checking to see what FiveTwo does is the correct play.

Heads-up as the preflop 3-bettor, Claude’s cold-call range contains a lot of pocket pairs (22–TT), suited connectors (76s/87s/54s), and some suited broadways. On 5-3-6 this board favors his made-hand density (sets/2p/straights) more than mine, but 77 is still a clear value/protection bet versus his many 88–TT, 6x/5x, and overcard floats with backdoors. I’ll use a small c-bet to deny equity and keep the SPR manageable; if he check-raises large it will be heavily weighted to strong value and I can play cautiously.

Completely whiffed this 5h3d6s flop with QdJd. I have two overcards and a weak backdoor diamond draw (only 3d on board). FiveTwo 3-bet preflop showing strength, likely has an overpair or strong holdings. Even with 4:1 pot odds, I'm essentially drawing to 6 outs (two overcards) and even hitting might not be good against their range. The backdoor flush draw needs runner-runner. Folding is the disciplined play here rather than chasing with minimal equity.

4-handed we can open wider, but 85o is still a very poor UTG candidate: it’s dominated when it makes a pair, has weak playability, and doesn’t realize equity well versus the blinds’ defend/3-bet ranges. As chip leader I’d rather apply pressure with hands that can continue versus resistance and play cleaner postflop. Preserve stack and pass.

Current hand is T4s on the Button. The UTG player has folded. Although being on the Button offers a positional advantage and an opportunity to steal the blinds, T4s is a very weak speculative hand with large gaps and low high-card value. My stack is the shortest at the table (54.5 BB), and the blinds (Claude and Flash) have significantly larger stacks (over 95 BBs), making them more likely to defend or 3-bet. Risking chips on a pure bluff with low equity when called is not optimal here. Preserving the stack for better spot is the correct play.

Holding 9d2c in the SB with only Flash in the BB remaining. This is a bottom-tier offsuit hand with no connectivity. Even getting 3:1 odds to complete, 92o plays terribly postflop and has minimal equity. Standard fold.

I am in UTG position (first to act in 4-handed play) with King-Three suited (Kd 3d). My stack is 54.5 BB, which is healthy but the smallest at the table. While ranges are wider in 4-handed games, K3s is still a weak hand to open from early position. It suffers from domination issues (against better Kings) and plays poorly out of position if called. Against aggressive larger stacks, I want to avoid marginal spots. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here.

92o is a weak hand even on the button. While I have position, this hand plays poorly postflop and has very little equity. FiveTwo has a massive stack and has been aggressive, so raising with this trash could easily get me into trouble. Easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit. Both the UTG player (Pro) and the Button (Claude) have folded, leaving me to face the Big Blind (FiveTwo). FiveTwo is the tournament chip leader and has been playing very aggressively. Queen-Deuce is a weak hand to play out of position, especially against a large, active stack. While it only costs 50 more to call, the risk of losing more chips post-flop out of position outweighs the potential reward of seeing a flop with such a marginal hand. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
J8o is a weak hand from UTG position, even in a 4-handed game. This is not a hand I should be opening with - it's dominated by better jacks and lacks connectivity/suitedness. Standard fold.

I am on the button with 5-4 offsuit. While the button is a prime position for stealing blinds, 5-4 offsuit is near the bottom of my range. Claude has already folded, leaving only the blinds. The small blind (FiveTwo) is the chip leader and has been quite active, and the big blind (Pro) is the short stack, making them more likely to defend or shove over a light open. Given the weakness of my hand, it is better to fold and wait for a stronger opening candidate.

Folds to me in the SB heads-up vs a shorter BB (53.5bb). K♦3♦ is a suited king with decent playability and strong blocker value; in this spot I want to open very wide to pick up the dead money and put pressure on BB’s capped range. I’ll use a standard small 2.5x sizing to keep my range wide while still generating good fold equity and setting up profitable postflop c-bets.

I am in the Big Blind with K8s and 53.5 BBs. The Small Blind (chip leader) has raised to 2.5 BBs. Given the Blind vs Blind dynamic and the opponent's likely wide range, K8s is a very strong candidate for a defense. It has good playability post-flop and sufficient equity (requiring roughly 30% pot odds, whereas I have closer to 50% equity against a wide opening range). 3-betting is an option but risks inflating the pot unnecessarily against the deep-stacked leader. Calling allows me to realize equity in position.

BB’s defend range connects relatively well with this low, disconnected board (more 8x/5x/straight-draw combos than my SB opening range), so I don’t want to auto c-bet and risk getting check-raised off a hand with real showdown value. With K♦3♦ I have bottom pair plus some backdoors (turning a diamond draw, counterfeit possibilities), but I’m not strong enough to bet for value and not cleanly a bluff either. Checking keeps pot control versus a shorter stack, lets me realize equity, and allows me to bluff-catch vs small stabs or re-evaluate on turns that improve me (K/3/♦) or favor my range.

I have top pair (Eights) with a strong King kicker on a relatively dry, unconnected rainbow board (8h 3c 5s). In a Blind vs Blind scenario, this is a very strong hand. My opponent (SB) checked to me. While betting for value is a valid option to target lower pairs or floaters, checking back has several advantages here: 1) It keeps the pot size manageable relative to my hand strength (one pair). 2) It induces bluffs from the aggressive chip leader on later streets. 3) It protects my checking range. 4) The board is dry enough that giving a free card is not very dangerous. I will check back to pot control and look to call down or bet later streets for value.

Turn brings the Td, upgrading us from weak showdown (bottom pair) into a strong semi-bluff/value candidate: we now have a pair plus a K-high diamond flush draw (good equity when called) and decent fold equity versus BB’s flop-check range (a lot of A/K-highs and gutters). Pro has shown a generally passive, fit-or-fold tendency, so I’d rather bet myself than check and hope he bluffs. A medium sizing pressures his unpaired hands, charges straight draws, and starts building a pot for when we hit a diamond on the river while keeping the bet small enough that we can comfortably fold to a rare strong check-raise.

The opponent (SB) bets small (300 into 500) on the turn [8h 3c 5s Td] after checking the flop. I hold [Ks 8s], giving me second pair with a top kicker. The Ten is an overcard to my Eight, which downgrades my hand strength, but the small bet sizing suggests either a blocking bet, a semi-bluff with a straight draw (like 97 or 76), or a weak Ten. Given the pot odds (calling 300 to win 800) and the likelihood that the aggressive chip leader is stabbing at the pot after I showed weakness by checking back the flop, folding is too tight. Raising would isolate me against better hands (Tx or better). Calling keeps his bluffing range wide and allows me to re-evaluate on the river.

River is a brick for our diamond draw and we’re left with a very weak bluff-catcher (pair of 3s) on a board where BB’s turn-calling range is heavily weighted to made hands (8x/5x/Tx/9x, plus occasional 67 straight). Given Pro’s generally passive tendencies, he’s unlikely to have many pure floats that we beat, and our hand is far too thin to value bet. Turning this into a bluff is also unattractive: we don’t have strong blockers to his main continues, and he will often just look us up with any pair after calling the turn. Best line is to check, and comfortably fold to meaningful aggression.

I hold King-Eight offsuit on a board of 8-3-5-T-9. I flopped top pair and checked back for pot control. The turn brought a Ten, downgrading my hand to second pair, and I called a small bet from the Small Blind. The river Nine puts a second overcard to my Eight and completes several straight draws (6-7, J-7). The opponent checks the river. Betting here achieves little value, as worse hands (like a 5, 3, or Ace-high) will likely fold, and better hands (Tens, Nines, two pair, straights) will call or raise. My hand has sufficient showdown value to beat missed random air or lower pairs. Checking back is the prudent play to realize my equity without inflating the pot.

I am in UTG position with Qc 3d. This is a very weak hand in a 4-handed game, even with relatively shallow stacks behind me. Usually, opening such a hand from UTG is unprofitable. Given I have a healthy stack and want to maintain a professional image while avoiding unnecessary risk with marginal-to-poor holdings, folding is the standard and correct play here.

Flash has folded, so we’re effectively on the button vs SB/BB. With position and a big stack edge, we should be opening very wide to pick up blinds/keep pressure on shorter stacks. T4o isn’t a strong hand, but it plays fine as a steal in position; if either blind 3-bets, we can comfortably fold and avoid bloating the pot. I’ll use a small, standard open size that still charges the blinds while keeping our risk low.

I am in the Small Blind with Q5o facing a standard button raise from the chip leader (FiveTwo), who has been opening frequently. While the button's range is wide, Q5o is a poor hand to defend with, especially out of position. It suffers from reverse implied odds and poor playability. My stack is around 60 big blinds, so I am not desperate. The best course of action is to fold and wait for a better spot.

J6 suited in the BB facing a button raise of 250. Getting 2.67:1 pot odds (150 to win 400). FiveTwo has been very active throughout the tournament. J6s has some playability with flush potential and the suited connector aspect. This is a standard defend from the BB given the pot odds.

Flopped nothing - no pair, no flush draw (only one diamond), no straight draw. Just overcards with J high. Best play is to check and see if FiveTwo checks behind or bets. If they bet, I'll likely fold since I have almost no equity here.

Claude’s check on 2h-4s-5d is on a board that strongly favors the BB defending range (lots of 2x/4x/5x, 63/76, and sets), so our range advantage is limited. With 4cTs we have decent showdown value (second pair) but are vulnerable to overcards; however, betting invites check-raises and forces us into awkward, higher-variance spots with a marginal hand. Checking back keeps the pot controlled, realizes our equity in position, and lets us respond intelligently to turn action (call reasonable bets on many turns, and potentially value-bet later if he stays passive).

Turn 9h doesn't help me - still have J high with no pair, no flush draw, and no meaningful straight draw. FiveTwo checked the flop behind, indicating possible weakness or a trap. I have zero showdown value. Best play is to check and potentially bluff the river if it comes a scare card, or just give up if FiveTwo bets. No reason to put money in with nothing here.

Claude has checked twice on a board that should often get a protection/value bet from a lot of his BB range (many 5x/9x/overpairs/draws). That double-check weights him toward overcards and weak hands that will fold to a stab. With 4cTs we have some showdown value but it’s thin and vulnerable to two overcards realizing equity for free; plus we can leverage our big stack and position. I’ll use a small turn bet to pick up the pot frequently and deny equity, while keeping risk low—if he check-raises, we can comfortably fold this marginal pair.

I have J high with only a gutshot straight draw (need a 3 for 2-3-4-5-6). Getting 3.4:1 on the call (225 to win 775), but gutshot is only ~8.5% to hit (~11:1). No flush draw, no showdown value. FiveTwo's small bet could be thin value or a blocker, but calling here is -EV. Time to give up.

4-handed UTG plays like a slightly earlier position, but J5o is still near the bottom of any profitable opening range: it’s frequently dominated when called (AJ/KJ/QJ/JT, 55+), has poor playability, and doesn’t realize equity well out of position versus two blinds who can defend wider. With a big stack edge, I’d rather apply pressure using hands that can barrel and realize equity better; this is an easy muck to avoid unnecessary variance.

I am on the Button with K5s, which is a standard opening hand from this position when folded to. I have a stack of roughly 60 big blinds. My goal is to steal the blinds or play a pot in position with a hand that has flush potential and a high card blocker. A standard raise size of 2.5x (250 chips) puts pressure on the blinds while not committing too many chips if I face a 3-bet.

Pocket 5s in the SB facing a button raise of 250. Getting decent pot odds (200 to win 400+) with good implied odds for set mining against Pro's likely wide button opening range. With 100+ BB effective, calling to set mine is standard. 3-betting small pairs OOP isn't ideal, and folding is too tight given the price.

I am in the Big Blind holding 2d 8c, which is a very weak starting hand. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250, and the Small Blind (Claude) has called. While I am getting 5-to-1 pot odds to call (costing 150 to see a pot that would be 750), this hand has extremely poor playability and realization of equity post-flop, especially being out of position against two opponents. Given that I have 50 hands remaining in the tournament and my stack is healthy, there is no need to commit chips with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have pocket 5s on a 3c-6h-7c board. My 55 is an underpair to both the 6 and 7, giving me only 5th pair. This is a very connected board with straight draws (45, 89, 58) and a club flush draw. Pro opened from button and could have many hands that connect with this board. My hand has marginal showdown value but is vulnerable. Checking to see what Pro does is the right play - if he checks back I can potentially win at showdown, and if he bets I can make a decision based on sizing.

I raised pre-flop with Ks5s and got called by the Small Blind. The flop comes 3c 6h 7c. This low, connected texture interacts much better with the Small Blind's calling range (pocket pairs, connectors like 67, 56, 78) than my Button opening range which contains many high cards. I have King-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4). Betting here accomplishes little; better hands like pairs will call, and worse hands might fold, but I risk getting check-raised which would force me to fold my equity. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially hitting a King, a 5, or a 4 on the turn, and keeps the pot manageable.

The turn 6d pairs the board. I have 55, giving me two pair (6s and 5s) but the 6s are from the board. Pro checked the flop behind which suggests weakness or a draw. On this paired board, if Pro has any 6, a 7, or overpair he beats me. My hand has some showdown value but betting opens me up to raises. Checking to control pot size and see a cheap showdown makes sense - if Pro bets I can evaluate, if he checks I might win at showdown.

On the turn, the board is [3c 6h 7c 6d]. The 6d pairs the board, which makes it more likely the Small Blind has trips given their calling range often includes connectors like 56s, 67s, or A6s. However, checking twice suggests they might be weak or on a draw. I hold King-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4). Betting here doesn't accomplish much: better hands like pocket pairs or a 7 will likely call, and worse hands (missed draws) I am already beating. By checking back, I take a free card to try and hit my straight or top pair on the river, while keeping the pot small with a marginal hand.

The river 6c gives me a full house (6s full of 5s). This is a strong hand. Pro has checked flop and turn, showing weakness - likely has a missed draw or weak hand. With the board showing trip 6s, anyone with a higher pair than my 5s has a better full house, but Pro's passive line suggests he doesn't have that. A small value bet could extract thin value, but Pro's weak line means he's unlikely to call with worse. Checking to realize my showdown value is the safest play.

The board has run out [3c 6h 7c 6d 6c], putting trip sixes on the board with a possible flush (three clubs) and possible straight. I hold Ks5s, giving me trip sixes with a King kicker (playing K-7-6-6-6). My opponent has checked to me on all three streets. My hand has showdown value beating Queen-high or Jack-high missed draws, but loses to any Ace, any pocket pair (which makes a full house), any club (flush), or straight. Betting achieves little as worse hands will fold and better hands (like any Ace or better) will call or raise. Checking back allows me to realize my equity against weaker high-card hands without risking chips.

I am in UTG (Cutoff in 4-handed play) with 4c 5h, which is a very weak offsuit connector. With 57 BBs, I have a healthy stack but am the shortest at the table. Given the weak nature of the hand and my position relative to the aggressive chip leader in the Big Blind, there is no justification for entering the pot. The standard play is to fold.
Pocket tens on the button is a premium hand. Pro folded, so I'm first to act among remaining players. Standard raise to build the pot and take initiative. I'll raise to 300 (3x BB).

I am in the Small Blind with King-Five offsuit. The Button (Claude) has raised to 300. While Claude opens wide from the button, K5o is a weak hand to play out of position, especially with a very aggressive chip leader (FiveTwo) still to act in the Big Blind. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and 3-betting K5o as a bluff is unnecessary at this stage of the tournament. The best play to preserve my stack for better opportunities is to fold.

Claude is opening from the button to 3bb with a stack we cover comfortably. A7o is a standard defend vs BTN, but flat-calling creates dominated-ace issues and we’ll be out of position postflop. Using our chip-lead, a small 3-bet pressures his wide opening range, wins the pot often preflop, and lets us play as the aggressor with strong ace blockers (reducing AK/AQ frequency). If he 4-bets, we can comfortably fold this combo and preserve stack.

Pocket tens facing a 3-bet from FiveTwo. They have a massive stack (32k) and have been aggressive throughout. The 3-bet to 950 is relatively small (just over 3x). With TT I have a strong hand but not strong enough to 4-bet/get it in against their range. Calling in position to see a flop is the best play - I can navigate post-flop with position and fold if overcards hit and they show aggression.

3-bet pot OOP on K97r: our range has plenty of AK/KQ/overpairs, but this specific hand (A7o) is just second pair with decent showdown value and not strong enough to happily build a pot. If we c-bet, we mostly get called by better hands (Kx, 9x, bigger pairs) and risk getting raised off our equity; we don’t gain much thin value. By checking we keep the pot manageable, protect our checking range (we can also check strong hands here), and induce bluffs from his missed broadways/backdoors that would otherwise fold to a small c-bet. Plan: check-call reasonable sizing, re-evaluate turns (improve on A/7; be cautious on T/8/6/9/K).

FiveTwo 3-bet preflop but checked this K97 board to me. My pocket tens are an overpair to the 7 and 9, only the King beats me among board cards. Their check likely indicates weakness - with a strong King or better they'd typically c-bet. I should bet for value and protection against draws (JT has a gutshot, flush draws possible). Betting around half pot to extract value and deny equity.

Claude’s small 1/3-pot stab after we check is a very standard range-bet in position in a 3-bet pot. Our A7 has a pair with an ace kicker (some showdown value) and is well ahead of his air/high-card floats (AQ/AJ/QJ/JT, etc.) while only clearly behind his value (Kx, 99/77, some 9x). Folding to this sizing would be too tight and would let him profitably stab any time we check. Raising is also unattractive: we’d mostly fold out hands we beat and get action from better hands, bloating the pot OOP with a marginal bluff-catcher. So we continue with a call and re-evaluate on the turn, being more cautious versus larger barrels on dynamic cards.

Turn pairs the 7 giving us trips (7♣7♦7♥) with an ace kicker in a 3-bet pot. Claude’s flop stab range contains a lot of Kx and some 9x/TT-QQ plus floats; if we check, he’ll often take the free card with the weaker part of that range, costing us value. Leading small-to-mid keeps his Kx and medium pairs in, looks like a range/protection bet from the 3-bettor, and cleanly sets up a pot-sized river shove with his ~9k effective stack.

FiveTwo check-called my flop bet, now leads 1600 into 5450 (about 30% pot) when the 7 pairs. Their small lead could be thin value with Kx, a probe with medium pairs, or a blocking bet. With pocket tens I have an overpair to the 7s and 9. The King is concerning - they 3-bet preflop so hands like AK, KQ, QQ+ are in their range. However, folding TT to a small bet seems too weak, and raising only gets called by better hands. Calling to control pot size and reevaluate the river is best.

River we have trips (7s) with top kicker in a 3-bet pot and Claude’s range that bet flop/called turn is heavily weighted to Kx (plus some 9x and pocket pairs). Checking risks him taking a free showdown with KQ/KJ/TT-QQ that will often check back on this paired board; jamming our full effective stack (~pot) is too polar and may fold out exactly those hands we want value from. A ~1/2-pot value bet targets his Kx and induces some thin/curious calls from 9x/medium pairs while still being strong enough that if he jams over the top, we can comfortably call given how few full-house combos are available.

FiveTwo 3-bet preflop, check-called my flop bet, led turn when the 7 paired, and now bets 3500 into 10550 on river. This line is very consistent with value - likely Kx hands (AK, KQ, KJ) that are staples of a 3-bet range. The 8d river also completes JT for a straight and gives 88 a full house. My pocket tens only beat bluffs and lower pairs, but FiveTwo's aggression profile and this value-heavy line suggest I'm behind most of the time. Getting 4:1 odds I need to be good ~25%, but their range is heavily weighted toward hands that beat me. Disciplined fold.

K8o from UTG in a 4-handed game is marginal. While 4-handed opens up ranges slightly, K8o is still too weak from early position against aggressive opponents like FiveTwo who has a massive stack and has been 3-betting frequently. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am on the button with K6o. In a 4-handed game, this is a borderline opening hand. However, the Small Blind (FiveTwo) is a massive, aggressive stack who has been active in many recent pots. The Big Blind (Pro) is relatively short-stacked and may defend or shove over a late-position raise. Given the aggression of FiveTwo and the marginal nature of my hand, I choose to fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my 96 BB stack, rather than risking a 3-bet or a difficult post-flop situation against the chip leader.

Folds to me in the SB, so this is a prime spot to attack the BB’s range and pick up the 150 dead chips. J6o is not a premium, but heads-up SB vs BB we should be raising a wide range for immediate fold equity and to avoid playing a weak hand passively OOP. I’ll use a smaller steal size to keep risk down while still applying pressure to Pro’s 56bb stack. If Pro 3-bets, this hand is an easy fold; if called, I’ll c-bet selectively on favorable textures.

Blind vs Blind scenario against the chip leader. The opponent raises to 2.5BB. I am holding 7h5d, a weak offsuit one-gapper. Pot odds require calling 150 to win a pot of 350, needing about 30% realized equity. While 75o has around 35% raw equity against a wide opening range, playing out of position significantly reduces my ability to realize that equity (likely realizing <80%). Given the 2.5x sizing (rather than a min-raise) and the disadvantage of position against an aggressive big stack, the Expected Value (EV) of calling is slightly negative. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve the 56BB stack.

I am in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. My hand is 9-5 offsuit, which is extremely weak and well outside any standard opening range, even in a short-handed format. With a comfortable stack of 9,625 chips (over 96 big blinds) and 47 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no strategic reason to play this hand. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

4-handed with the chip lead on the button, we should be opening very wide to steal blinds and apply pressure to two shorter stacks. A3o is comfortably within a BTN open range; it plays fine in position and benefits from fold equity. I’ll use a standard 2.5x size to target immediate folds while keeping the pot manageable if we face a 3-bet.

I am in the Small Blind facing a 2.5x raise from the Button (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader and likely opening wide. My hand, King-Four offsuit, is weak and plays poorly out of position. While I get reasonable pot odds to call, the reverse implied odds of being dominated (e.g., by Kx with a better kicker) and the disadvantage of playing the rest of the hand out of position make this a fold. Defending K4o is generally -EV.

QTo in BB facing a min-raise from FiveTwo on button. Getting good pot odds (150 to win 400). QTo is a standard defend against a button open - has decent playability postflop. FiveTwo has shown aggression throughout the tournament. Calling is the right play here.
Flopped nothing with QTo on 3c9s4c - just two overcards, no draws. Standard check and see what FiveTwo does. If they c-bet, I can evaluate whether to float or fold based on sizing.

We have marginal showdown value with bottom pair (A3 on 3-9-4) and are not thrilled to face a check-raise on a board where BB’s defend range connects (9x/4x/3x, pocket pairs, club draws). A small c-bet would get called often by better hands and draws, inflating the pot while we’re frequently behind. Checking back keeps the pot controlled, realizes our equity, and can induce bluffs on later streets from missed overcards while we can improve to two pair/trips on A/3 turns.

FiveTwo checked back the flop, showing weakness. Turn 6s doesn't help me - still just two overcards. I could probe bet here to try taking the pot, but checking keeps the pot small with my showdown value. If FiveTwo bets I fold; if they check again I can potentially bluff river or show down.

After checking back flop, my range is capped and Claude can take a lot of river stabs with overcards and draws. With A3 I have a small but real made hand that benefits from protection on this texture (club draws, 5x straight draws, random overcards). A small delayed c-bet should fold out a chunk of his ace-high/king-high floats and charge draws without bloating the pot versus his many better pairs (9x/6x/4x). If I get check-raised, I can comfortably fold this marginal pair.

FiveTwo checked back flop (weakness) but now bets small 200 into 550 on turn. I have QTo with zero equity - no pair, no draws on 3c9s4c6s. My two overcards are unlikely to be good against a value bet. Getting good odds (200 to win 950) but I have almost no outs. Fold is correct.

4-handed UTG I can open wider, but K2o is still at the very bottom: it’s heavily dominated by the blinds’ defending ranges, has poor playability out of position, and can’t profitably continue versus 3-bets (especially with shorter stacks who can 3-bet/jam). As chip leader, I don’t need to take thin, high-variance spots with dominated offsuit kings. Fold and preserve stack for better opens/steal spots.

I hold Ace-King suited (Ah Kh) on the Button, which is a premium starting hand. The UTG player has folded. My stack is 5,550 (approx 55 BBs). I want to open the pot for value and to assert position. A standard raise size of 2.5x the big blind (250) is appropriate to build the pot while keeping the blinds' calling ranges somewhat wide. Raising also balances my range for when I steal with weaker hands.

Q7o in the small blind facing a button raise. This is a weak offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position. Not strong enough to 3-bet for value or as a bluff, and calling OOP with this hand leads to difficult postflop spots. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with J7o. Pro has raised to 250 from the Button, which is a standard steal attempt in a 4-handed game. While the price to call is relatively cheap (150 more to win a total pot of 400), J7o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a range that likely contains many better high cards and connectors. Given the tournament stage and my healthy stack, there is no need to defend with marginal hands that perform poorly post-flop. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

Playing 4-handed, UTG acts as the Cutoff. A8o is on the borderline of the opening range. While it can be opened for a steal, the presence of the massive chip leader (FiveTwo) in the Big Blind discourages opening marginal hands that play poorly post-flop out of position. I face the risk of domination by better Aces or being floated by the deep stack. Given my healthy stack of 57BB, I can afford to wait for better opportunities or positional advantage (Button/Cutoff with better hands) rather than forcing a marginal spot here.

76o on the button with Pro folding. This is a decent spot to steal with position. 76o is playable here - I have direct position on the blinds and can represent a wide range. Standard button open to 2.5x seems right to put pressure on the blinds while not committing too much with a marginal hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Nine offsuit (J9o). Claude, the Button, has raised to 250. In a four-handed game, J9o is a marginal hand. Defending the Small Blind by calling is generally risky, especially with a very aggressive and deep-stacked chip leader (FiveTwo) in the Big Blind who is prone to squeezing. J9o does not play well out of position and doesn't have great nut potential. Given the tournament stage and the players involved, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Claude is opening from the button to 2.5x and we’re getting a very good price (150 to win 400). Even with a weak offsuit hand like Q2o, we have enough raw equity versus a wide BTN range to defend, and folding would be overly tight in a 4-handed game. I don’t love a 3-bet bluff here because Q2o has poor blockers and plays badly if called; calling keeps the pot manageable and lets us realize equity when we connect while avoiding spewing in a bloated OOP pot.

We’re out of position with Q2o on a low, disconnected 9-4-3 rainbow board and have essentially no equity beyond one overcard (the Q) and very thin backdoors. This is not a good spot to donk-lead; we don’t credibly represent much, and if we get raised or called we’ll be forced to barrel too often with little equity. Checking keeps our range protected (we have plenty of 9x/sets/stronger hands in our BB defend range) and lets Claude c-bet his wide button range; versus any reasonable bet size we can usually just fold and move on, while occasionally realizing equity for free if he checks back.

Missed the flop completely with 76s on 4-9-3 rainbow. I have a gutshot straight draw (need a 5). Standard c-bet spot as the preflop aggressor on a dry board. Small sizing around 35-40% pot should be enough to take it down sometimes while not risking too much when I'm behind. FiveTwo has been fairly sticky but this board doesn't hit many calling ranges hard.

Claude’s 200 c-bet into 750 is small, so the pot odds are tempting (we need ~21%). However, Q2o on 9-4-3r is still near the very bottom of our defend range: we have only one overcard with a bad kicker, no straight draw, and only thin backdoors. Even when we improve (Q or 2), our implied odds are poor because we’re often dominated or end up with a weak pair that can’t comfortably continue versus future barrels. Out of position, our equity realization is low, and floating just to fold later is a common leak. With better candidates available (overcards with better kickers, BDFD+BDSD, gutshots), the clean, high-EV decision is to fold and preserve chips.
J7s from UTG is too weak to open. It's a marginal suited hand that plays poorly from early position against 3 opponents. Will often be dominated when called. Standard fold.

I am on the button with K6o. In a 4-handed game, this is a standard opening hand to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. Claude has folded, and I am first to act. FiveTwo (SB) is the chip leader and quite active, while Pro (BB) is shorter with 56 BBs. I will open for 2.5x the big blind to 250.

Flash is opening fairly wide from the button and both opponents behind are much shorter stacked, so this is a good spot to apply pressure and try to win preflop with initiative. Calling 200 with 98o from the small blind is playable, but it realizes poorly OOP and invites the big blind to come along cheaply, creating a tough multiway spot. A 3-bet leverages my stack advantage, denies equity, and can fold out a lot of Flash’s weaker opens; if I face a 4-bet/jam, this hand is an easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind facing a raise from the Button (Flash) and a 3-bet from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). My hand is 8 of Clubs and 4 of Clubs, a weak suited two-gapper.
