The 3-bet is to 10bb, and calling would require committing approximately 16% of my remaining stack with a very marginal hand out of position against a massive chip leader. I am not getting the right price to set mine or speculate, and my hand has very poor equity against a 3-betting range. The only prudent play is to fold.

I opened K6o from the button, which is a standard steal attempt. FiveTwo, the chip leader in the small blind, has 3-bet to 1000. FiveTwo has shown a high frequency of aggression and 3-betting throughout the tournament. While I might be getting a decent price to call (750 to win a total pot of 2100), K6o is a weak hand that is easily dominated by FiveTwo's 3-betting range (which likely includes better Kings and Aces). Calling creates a difficult post-flop situation against the most aggressive player at the table. Given my current stack of over 90 BBs, there is no need to commit more chips with a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot to counter his aggression.

I am in the UTG position in a 4-max game with 2s 8h, which is a very weak starting hand. Given the stack sizes and the stage of the tournament, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot or attempt a bluff from this position with such a low-equity hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

4-handed and folded to us on the Button, we should be opening very wide to leverage position and our big stack. Tc8c plays well postflop (suited, connected-ish), can make strong draws, and benefits from stealing blinds versus two players who have shown a lot of fit-or-fold tendencies in prior hands. A small-but-standard open keeps our risk low while still applying pressure and setting up profitable c-bets in position.

I hold 7c 4d in the Small Blind. The Button (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader and raising frequently, has opened to 250. My hand is 7-4 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker with very little playability or equity, even against a wide opening range. Being out of position against the aggressor and having the Big Blind still to act makes this a trivial fold. I cannot profitably call or raise here.

8s3h is a very weak hand. Even though I'm getting decent pot odds (150 to win 400), 83o plays poorly post-flop and rarely makes strong hands. FiveTwo has been aggressive but I shouldn't defend this trash. Easy fold.

4-handed UTG effectively plays like the cutoff in a full ring game, so opening ranges are wide. KQo is a clear value/open-raise: it dominates many defending hands (Kx/Qx), plays well postflop, and benefits from our chip-leader leverage versus shorter stacks in the blinds/button. I’ll use a standard, slightly pressure-applying open that still keeps my risk small and range wide.

I hold Pocket Aces (As Ac) on the Button, the best starting hand. The UTG player (FiveTwo) is the chip leader and has been opening very wide and playing aggressively. I have 55 BBs. My goal is to build the pot immediately for value and isolate the loose opener. In Hand #63, I 3-bet FiveTwo's 250 open to 900 with AQs, so making a similar sized 3-bet here balances my range and disguises the strength of my hand. A 3-bet to 800 (3.2x) charges drawing hands and weaker pairs while setting up a favorable Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) for post-flop play. Flat calling is an option to trap, but given the chip leader's deep stack, I prefer building the pot now to maximize value.

8To is a weak hand facing a 3-bet. Pro 3-bet to 800 after FiveTwo's open. I'd need to call 750 into a pot of 1200. 8T offsuit plays poorly against a 3-betting range and I'm out of position. Clear fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s 8h, which is a very weak hand. FiveTwo opened from UTG and Pro 3-bet from the Button. Given the strength of my hand and being out of position against two active players, there is no reason to continue in this pot. Folding is the only sensible move.

Pro’s 3-bet from the button vs my 4-handed UTG open can be reasonably wide, and KQo is too strong to fold getting a good price. At the same time, 4-betting is awkward here: with Pro’s ~47.5bb stack, a 4-bet either (a) induces a shove that I don’t want to call off against a tighter value range, or (b) wins a relatively small pot while taking on unnecessary variance. Calling keeps in Pro’s bluffs and dominated broadways (KJ/QJ/JT-type hands), and with the resulting low SPR (~2.3) I can play straightforwardly postflop—value on K/Q-high favorable textures and exit cleanly on bad A-high/low connected boards when pressure comes.

We flopped top pair with a strong kicker in a 3-bet pot, but we’re out of position and Pro (as the preflop aggressor) is incentivized to c-bet this texture at a high frequency with both value (AK/KK/88/77, Kx) and lots of bluffs/semi-bluffs (AQ/QJ with clubs, JT/T9, pocket pairs turning into probes). In this node, the standard and highest-EV approach is to check our range and let Pro put money in with worse and with air, rather than donk-leading and potentially letting them play perfectly (folding misses / only continuing with better). With the low SPR, I’m comfortable check-calling most reasonable bets and happily stacking off versus draws + worse Kx on later streets.

I hold Pocket Aces (As Ac) on a Kc 7c 8d board. This is a very favorable flop for my range as the pre-flop 3-bettor, giving me an overpair with the Nut Flush blocker (Ac). The board is somewhat coordinated with a flush draw and straight possibilities, so checking back risks giving free cards to draws. FiveTwo is a loose, deep-stacked player who likely connects with this King-high board (holding Kx) or has a draw/pocket pair. Given the SPR of ~2.7, I want to build the pot to get stacks in by the river. A continuation bet of roughly 1/3 pot serves to extract value from weaker Kings and draws while denying equity to hands that missed. I choose a bet size of 600 to keep their calling range wide.

We flop top pair (K) with a strong kicker in a 3-bet pot versus the preflop aggressor. Pro’s small c-bet (600 into 2350) is consistent with a very wide range here: value (AK/KQ/KJ/overpairs/sets) plus a lot of continuation bluffs and semi-bluffs (AQ/QJ/JT/T9, some club draws). With Qc in hand we block a chunk of the natural club-bluff region, which slightly reduces the urgency to “protect” with a check-raise, and a raise would also fold out many worse hands/air that we want to keep betting. Given the still-manageable SPR and our hand being clearly strong but not invulnerable, the highest-EV line is to check-call and let Pro keep firing with dominated Kx and bluffs, then make a straightforward decision on the turn based on sizing/runout.

Turn is a brick (3h) and we still have TPTK-ish (top pair, strong kicker) in a 3-bet pot versus a very short effective stack (~1.4 SPR). If we check, Pro can often check back hands we dominate (QQ-JJ/TT, some 8x, weaker Kx) and we miss a key street of value. Leading for a medium-small size targets exactly that calling region, charges club/straight draws, and sets up an easy river shove/call sequence given stacks. The sizing also makes it hard for Pro to profitably float/fold later while keeping worse made hands in.

I hold Pocket Aces (As Ac) in a 3-bet pot on a Kc 7c 8d 3h board. The opponent, a loose-aggressive chip leader, has donk-bet led out for 1100 on the turn, a line often indicative of a made hand like Top Pair (Kx) wanting to prevent a check-back, or a draw setting a price. The 3h is a brick that changes very little. With 4150 chips remaining and facing a bet of 1100, the effective SPR if I call would be less than 1 on the river. I am currently ahead of his value range (Kx) and semi-bluff range. Calling allows scare cards (clubs, straight completers) to roll off on the river which could kill the action or allow draws to get there. Given the opponent's loose profile, he is likely to call an all-in with Top Pair or strong draws. I unblock King combos and block the Nut Flush (Ac), increasing the likelihood he calls with worse made hands rather than folding a naked nut flush draw. Shoving now maximizes value and denies equity while I have the clear advantage.

We’re facing what looks like Pro’s turn shove over our 1,100 lead. With 8,200 in the pot and 3,050 more to call, we need only ~27% equity.

On Kc-8d-7c-3h we have top pair with a strong kicker (KQ). Pro’s value region certainly contains AK/AA and occasionally sets, but sets/two-pair are relatively scarce given the preflop 3-bet node. Importantly, this jam line can also include hands we beat (some KJ/KT that shove for protection at low SPR) plus a meaningful chunk of semi-bluffs (club draws and OESDs like T9s). Even though Qc blocks some club-bluff combos, we should still realize enough equity versus that combined range to clear the 27% threshold.

Given the favorable price, our hand being near the top of our range here, and the relatively small additional risk to our stack, calling is the highest-EV decision.

I am in UTG (Cutoff in 4-handed play) with 7s5c, a weak off-suit one-gapper. Despite having a deep stack of over 110 big blinds, this hand has very poor playability and equity. It is well outside standard opening ranges, even for loose aggressive strategies. Given the stack depths and the fact that I just won a large pot in the previous hand, there is no incentive to get involved with a trash hand from early position. Folding is the only reasonable option.
J8o on the button is a reasonable stealing hand with only the blinds left to act. Pro folded, giving me position. Standard button raise to try to take down the blinds. I'll make it 250.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d 5h. Claude, on the button, has raised to 250. This is a standard button steal attempt. While 65o is a speculative hand that can occasionally play well post-flop, playing it out of position against Claude with a 'to call' amount of 200 is not ideal, especially when there is still a very active and deep-stacked player in the Big Blind (FiveTwo) who might 3-bet. Given the tournament context and my current stack size, folding is the most disciplined and safe play here to preserve my chips for better spots.

Button open is likely wide 4-handed, and AJs suited is comfortably ahead of that range while playing very well postflop. With SB folded, I want to isolate and take initiative, also leveraging my big stack vs Claude’s shorter stack by applying pressure and denying him an easy realization. A 3-bet to ~9bb is a solid size OOP versus a 2.5x open that builds a pot for value but doesn’t overcommit against a possible 4-bet range.

FiveTwo 3-bet to 900 after my button raise. J8o is too weak to defend against a 3-bet, even with position. FiveTwo has been aggressive throughout, but calling 650 more with an offsuit J8 is burning chips. This hand doesn't play well enough post-flop to justify the investment. Folding is the disciplined play.
7d5d is a weak suited connector that's not strong enough to open from UTG, even 4-handed. Being out of position with this marginal hand against deep-stacked opponents is a losing play. Easy fold.

I am on the button with King-Six suited, which is a strong hand for an open-raise in a four-handed game. Claude has already folded, so I only have the blinds to contend with. FiveTwo in the Small Blind is the chip leader and has shown significant aggression, but I have position on both remaining players. I will open with a standard raise to 250 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately, or play the hand in position if called.

J3o is too weak to defend from the small blind versus a button 2.5x open: it’s dominated when we make a jack, plays poorly out of position, and doesn’t have the suited/connected equity to realize well. A light 3-bet bluff also isn’t attractive here because we’d be risking a lot OOP without strong blockers and can get flatted by both Flash and the big blind. Best is to cut losses and wait for a better spot to apply pressure with our chip lead.

I am in the Big Blind with K4o facing a 2.5x raise from the Button (Flash). While the pot odds (calling 150 to win 400, requiring ~27% equity) are mathematically tempting against a wide button opening range, K4o is a very weak hand that suffers significantly from poor playability out of position. It has poor equity realization, meaning I will often have to fold the best hand post-flop or fail to extract value when I am ahead. With a healthy stack of over 110 BBs after a recent big win (Hand #108), there is no need to defend with the very bottom of my range and bleed chips in a marginal spot. Suited K4 might be a call, but offsuit is a standard fold.

I am holding QJo under the gun in a 4-handed game. This is a standard opening hand in this position. My stack of 9225 chips (92 BB) is healthy, though I am currently the second-shortest stack. FiveTwo, the chip leader, has been active in 3-betting my opens in previous hands (Hands 106 and 95), so I need to be prepared to fold if they show significant strength. However, I must maintain an aggressive opening range to prevent being blinded down as we approach the end of the tournament. I will open for a standard 2.5x raise.

Facing Flash’s UTG open (4-handed), 9h6s offsuit is a weak, dominated holding with poor playability and limited equity realization, even in position. It has no blockers for a profitable 3-bet bluff, and flatting invites tough multiway scenarios versus the blinds and reverse implied odds when we make marginal pairs. With plenty of better hands to defend/attack with on the button, this is a disciplined fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Js 7c facing a raise to 250 from Flash in the UTG position. J7 offsuit is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against an UTG opening range which is typically strong. Even though the pot odds are roughly 2:1, my equity realization will be low due to the positional disadvantage and the likelihood of being dominated. There is no strategic merit in calling or 3-betting this hand in this spot.

K2o is a weak hand facing a UTG raise. Even with decent pot odds (150 to win 400), K2o plays poorly post-flop - when I hit a king I'm often dominated by better kickers. UTG raises typically indicate stronger ranges. Standard fold here.

4-handed UTG plays like the cutoff, so opening ranges are fairly wide. J4s is weak but has decent playability (flush/straight potential) and benefits from position and my big-stack leverage versus three shorter stacks. Raising also denies the blinds a cheap realization and can win the pot pre-flop often. Plan: open and comfortably fold to meaningful 3-bets from the blinds/button.

I am on the Button with Qd6d. The UTG player (FiveTwo), who is the massive chip leader, has opened to 250 (2.5bb). Although 4-handed play encourages wider ranges, Q6s is a marginal hand that is easily dominated by stronger Queens or Ace-high hands in the opener's range. Calling invites squeezes from the blinds and puts me in a spot with a weak flush draw or weak top pair potential against an aggressive big stack. 3-betting is a bit too loose here given the hand strength. Folding is the most standard and disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I have 5c6c, a suited connector in the small blind. FiveTwo raised to 250 from UTG. I need 200 to call into a 400 pot - decent pot odds. This is a speculative hand with straight and flush potential that plays well with implied odds. Against a deep-stacked opponent (32k), I can potentially win a big pot if I connect. Calling to see a flop multiway (Flash may come along) is reasonable. The hand doesn't play well enough to 3-bet, and folding is too tight given the price.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ten-Eight offsuit. The UTG player (FiveTwo) has raised to 2.5x, and the Small Blind (Claude) has called. I only need to call 150 more into a total pot of 600, giving me 4:1 pot odds. While T8o is a weak hand, the price is attractive enough to close the pre-flop action and see a flop in a three-way pot. Raising would be ill-advised against an UTG open, and folding is too tight given the favorable pot odds.

Complete whiff on this flop. I have 5c6c with no pair, no flush draw (board is spade-heavy, I have clubs), and only a gutshot to 4 for a wheel. The board of As 3h 3s heavily favors the preflop raiser's range with Ax hands and pairs. No reason to bluff here - just check and give up if there's action.

On a board of Ace-Trey-Trey, I have absolutely nothing with Ten-high. This board favors the pre-flop raiser's range significantly, as they are the most likely to hold an Ace. Since the Small Blind has already checked, I will check as well and see how the original aggressor (FiveTwo) reacts. Betting here would be a pure bluff into two opponents on a board where one of them is very likely to have at least a pair of Aces.

We have a clear range advantage as the preflop raiser on A33 (I have far more strong Ax and overpairs than the blinds). Both opponents checked, so a small c-bet will often take it down immediately from pocket pairs/broadways, while keeping my risk low multiway. My hand also has equity: a one-card spade makes a flush (though not always the best), giving me good turns to barrel. If I get check-raised, it’s heavily weighted to trips/Ax in this multiway spot, so I can just let it go.

Complete miss on this board. I have 5c6c with no pair, no flush draw (clubs don't help on this spade board), and only a gutshot to a wheel (need a 4). FiveTwo's bet of 250 into 750 represents strength on an A-high paired board. I need ~20% equity to call but only have about 8% with my gutshot. Easy fold.

On a board of As-3h-3s, I have absolutely no equity with Ten-high. FiveTwo, as the UTG raiser, has a range that heavily favors holding an Ace. Even if FiveTwo is betting with air or a lower pocket pair, I am currently behind and have no draws to improve. Calling a 1/4 pot bet with zero connectivity or high-card value would be a waste of chips. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

In a 4-handed game, the UTG position acts as the Cutoff. While suited connectors like 5h4h have playability, they are at the very bottom of an opening range. Considering the presence of the massive chip leader (FiveTwo) in the Big Blind who has been playing aggressively, opening this hand risks facing a 3-bet or playing a bloated pot with a marginal holding. Having recently doubled up to a comfortable second-place stack, preserving chips and waiting for a stronger spot is the prudent play.

On the button with 65o - weak hand but premium position. Pro folded, so only blinds remain. FiveTwo (BB) has been aggressive with 3-bets throughout the tournament. 65o is marginal for a steal, but button position gives good fold equity. I'll make a standard raise to try to take down the blinds. If I get 3-bet, I'll fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 6s. Claude has raised to 250 from the Button. While the pot odds are somewhat tempting (200 to call into a pot of 400), 8-6 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against a button raise, especially with the chip leader (FiveTwo) still to act in the Big Blind. Folding preserves my stack for more favorable situations later in the tournament.

Claude is opening button very wide 4-handed and I’m getting an excellent price: 150 to win a pot that will be 550, needing only ~27% equity. T6o is comfortably within a BB defend versus a BTN open at these odds. A 3-bet would leverage my big stack, but T6o is a poor 3-bet candidate (dominated when called, low playability) and I’d rather realize equity and keep the pot manageable against a shorter stack.

On K♦ K♥ 9♦ I have essentially no made hand and only weak backdoor potential (can pair a T/6, or pick up a backdoor straight on 7/8). This board strongly favors the preflop raiser’s range, and leading doesn’t make sense with my hand. Best is to check my entire range here, keep the pot small, and then proceed by folding to most bets with this particular combo (continuing only versus an exceptionally small stab or if I pick up equity on the turn).

Flop is Kd Kh 9d - a paired king board. I have complete air with 65o, no pair and no diamond draw. However, this is a good board to c-bet on. FiveTwo's calling range from the BB likely consists of medium hands that missed this board. A paired king flop rarely connects with BB defending ranges, and they'd likely 3-bet preflop with KQ+. A small c-bet around 200 should get folds often enough to be profitable.

Claude’s 200 c-bet into 550 is a small, range-y stab on a board that heavily favors his perceived range, but the price is good: calling 200 wins a 950 pot (≈21% required). Even though T6o has no pair and no diamond, I still have some equity via pairing T/6 (often good when he’s c-betting air) plus a small backdoor straight (7/8 runout). With a big stack edge and against a wide BTN open, I can defend once here and play turns straightforwardly: continue mainly when I improve or when he gives up; fold versus meaningful turn barrels.

Turn Tc gives me K K T T 9 (two pair: kings and tens). That’s a decent showdown hand versus Claude’s wide BTN stab range, but it’s still clearly behind any Kx (trips) and can get put in tough spots if I start building the pot OOP. Checking keeps his bluffs/semi-bluffs (diamonds, QJ, random floats) betting, while also controlling pot size versus the part of his range that has a king. Plan: check-call reasonable sizing, and be cautious versus a big polar barrel/jam from his shorter stack.

Turn is Tc on a Kd Kh 9d board. I have complete air with 65o - no pair, no draws. FiveTwo called my flop c-bet, indicating some interest (likely a 9, diamond draw, or floating). The Tc could have improved their hand if they have T9, QJ, JT type holdings. With no equity and already getting called once, continuing to barrel is risky. I should check back here to control pot size, see a free river, and potentially bluff if a scare card comes or just give up.

River is K♦ K♥ 9♦ T♣ Q♠ and after he checked back turn, Claude’s range is heavily weighted toward bluff-catchers (A-high, small pairs, some 9x/Tx) and missed diamonds, with fewer strong Kx hands. Our hand (T♥6♣) makes KKTTQ, which is comfortably ahead of that capped range but loses to Jx straights and any Kx/full house. A small lead functions as a thin value bet and a blocker: it gets called by worse (Qx/Tx/9x), denies him the chance to realize with air that might check back, and lets us bet/fold efficiently versus an all-in raise from his strong hands.

River brought Qs, completing possible straights with a J. FiveTwo makes a small 350 bet into 950 pot (~37%). I have complete air with 6-high. My options: 1) Fold - give up on a failed bluff attempt, 2) Call - makes no sense with 6-high, 3) Raise as bluff representing J for straight or a king. The problem is I checked turn showing weakness, making it hard to credibly represent strength now. FiveTwo has a massive 32k stack and has shown willingness to call down throughout. A bluff raise is too risky against this opponent profile. Folding and saving chips is the correct play.
A5o in UTG at a 4-handed table. While A5o is marginal from UTG in a full ring, with only 4 players this becomes a reasonable open. With 61 BBs effective, I'll make a standard 2.5x open raise.

I am on the button with Jack-Ten offsuit (Jh Tc). Claude, the table's shortest stack (approximately 59 big blinds), has opened for 2.5x from under-the-gun. In a 4-handed game, UTG is essentially the Hijack position. While JTo is a playable hand in some contexts, it is generally dominated by a standard UTG opening range (which includes hands like AJ, KJ, QJ, and pocket pairs). Calling here also leaves me vulnerable to a squeeze from FiveTwo or Pro in the blinds. Given that I have a healthy stack and there are 36 hands remaining in the tournament, I prefer to find a more advantageous spot than calling a raise with a marginal offsuit connector.

Claude’s 2.5x open in 4-handed play is likely fairly wide, and I’m getting a good price (200 to win 400). 7-6 offsuit isn’t strong enough to 3-bet as a bluff versus a shorter stack that can respond by jamming, but it has reasonable equity and connectivity to realize postflop. Calling keeps variance down while still defending my small blind appropriately as chip leader; I’ll proceed mostly via check/call on favorable, dynamic boards and play cautiously on high-card/dry textures.

I am in the Big Blind holding 5c 2s, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. The action started with an UTG raise from Claude to 250, followed by a call from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). Although I am getting pot odds of 4:1 (calling 150 to win a pot of 600), my hand has extremely poor playability and very low equity against reasonable ranges for an UTG raiser and a SB caller. Furthermore, I would be playing out of position in a multi-way pot. The risk of domination or simply missing the flop entirely is too high to justify the call. Conserving chips for a better spot is the optimal strategy here.

Heads-up versus Claude with a short effective stack (~5.9k). On 6c-5h-Jd I have a marginal made hand (pair of sixes) with some backdoor improvement, but it’s not strong enough to build a pot OOP by donk-betting into the preflop raiser. Checking keeps my range protected (I can still have strong hands like sets/2p), lets Claude continuation-bet his air, and allows me to realize equity by check/calling reasonable sizing while avoiding getting forced into a commitment line versus a polarized response.

Flopped middle pair with ace kicker (A5 on 6-5-J board). FiveTwo checked to me. This is a good spot to c-bet for value - my hand is likely ahead of villain's checking range. Dry board favors the preflop raiser. Betting around 50% pot for thin value and protection.

Claude’s 1/3-pot c-bet is consistent with a very wide range (lots of overcards/air that will often fold). With 7h6d on Jd-6c-5h I have a strong “middle-strength” hand: pair of sixes plus an open-ended straight draw (4/8) and some two-pair/trips equity. Versus his likely c-bet range I’m ahead of many hands (Ax/Kx/Qx), and raising denies equity while putting maximum pressure on his short ~5k stack. The sizing also sets up a straightforward stack-off: if he jams, my pair+OESD has enough equity versus his calling/jamming range (Jx/overpairs/strong draws) to continue, and when he folds I win a meaningful pot immediately.

FiveTwo check-raised my c-bet from 300 to 1000. I have middle pair with ace kicker. Getting 2.7:1 on a call (700 to win 1900). FiveTwo has been aggressive throughout - could have Jx, sets, two pair, but also draws (87, 74, 43) or bluffs. Given pot odds and villain's aggression tendencies, folding middle pair top kicker seems too tight. Raising commits too much with this marginal hand. Calling to see turn and reevaluate.

After my flop check-raise gets called, Claude’s range is still fairly wide but strengthened: lots of Jx, some 6x/5x, pocket pairs, and a few floats/draws that continued because he was getting a good price. The turn (2h) is essentially a blank for my hand, but it *does* increase pressure on his marginal continues (77–TT, weak 6x/5x, overcards that peeled) while my hand has solid equity when called: pair of 6s + OESD (4/8) plus additional improvement to two-pair/trips (7 or 6) gives me plenty of outs versus one-pair hands. With the pot already 2600 and Claude effectively short (~3.6k behind after his flop call), the cleanest and highest-EV line is to jam now: maximize fold equity, deny river realization, and simplify decisions OOP. If he calls with Jx/overpairs/strong draws, I still have meaningful equity; if he folds his weaker continues, I win a big pot immediately.

FiveTwo check-raised flop, now fires 3625 into ~2600 pot on turn - massive overbet. My middle pair with ace kicker was okay for calling the flop check-raise, but this sizing on a brick turn represents extreme strength. FiveTwo's line screams value: sets (JJ, 66), two pair (J6, J5, 65), or strong Jx. While FiveTwo has been aggressive, this specific line (check-raise then overbet turn) is rarely a bluff. If I call 3625, I'm left with only 1250 chips - basically pot committed for river. Middle pair isn't strong enough to commit my tournament life here. Disciplined fold.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game with 5c 8s. This is a very weak starting hand and does not warrant an opening raise or a call, especially with an aggressive chip leader like FiveTwo on the button and two capable stacks in the blinds. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

4-handed and on the button after UTG folds, this is a prime spot to attack the blinds with a very wide opening range. 9♠2♣ is near the bottom and plays poorly postflop, so I want the cheapest possible steal attempt: a min-raise. With my big stack covering both blinds, I can apply pressure; if either blind 3-bets (especially vs Claude’s shorter stack), I simply fold and lose the minimum. If called, I’ll lean on position and selectively c-bet favorable boards rather than building a big pot with a weak holding.

I am in the Small Blind with K3o facing a min-raise from the Button (FiveTwo). While the pot odds are tempting (calling 150 to win 350), playing King-Three offsuit out of position is generally a losing play due to poor playability and reverse implied odds (domination issues with the King). The Big Blind is also short-stacked and could squeeze. It is better to preserve chips than to bleed them in marginal spots out of position against the chip leader.

T4o is a weak offsuit hand. While I'm getting 3.5:1 pot odds to call 100, this hand plays poorly postflop out of position against FiveTwo who has been aggressive and has a massive chip lead. Folding saves chips for better spots.

4-handed from UTG we can open wider than full-ring, but 95o is still near the bottom of any profitable opening range: it’s offsuit, easily dominated, and performs poorly when we get defended (likely vs both blinds) or 3-bet (Flash has shown willingness to fight back). With a large chip lead, there’s no need to take thin, high-variance spots to win a small pot; we can apply pressure with better candidates and preserve our edge.

Current hand is 116/150. I am on the Button with Qh 3s (Q3 offsuit). The UTG player (FiveTwo) has folded. Although being on the Button offers a positional advantage to steal the blinds, Q3o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop and is easily dominated. With comfortable stack depth (109 BB) and being second in chips among active players, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with such marginal holdings. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here to preserve the stack for better opportunities.

K5o in the SB heads up against the BB. This is a decent spot to raise and try to take it down preflop. Flash has been somewhat active. With a king-high hand, I'll make a standard raise to 250 to put pressure on the big blind rather than limping and playing out of position postflop.

I am in the Big Blind with Kh7s. Claude, the Small Blind, has raised to 250. This is a standard blind-vs-blind situation. I need to call 150 more into a pot of 350, meaning I only need about 30% equity to make the call profitable. K7o performs well against a typical Small Blind opening range, and I will have the advantage of being in position for the remainder of the hand. Claude's stack is relatively short (around 45 BBs), so I don't want to bloat the pot with a 3-bet unless I have a premium hand. Calling allows me to see a flop and utilize my position to outplay the opponent or hit a pair.

I completely missed this flop with K5o. The board is T-8-9 with two diamonds - very coordinated. I have no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw. Flash called from BB and this board hits a lot of their calling range. Betting here with K-high no draw is just burning chips. I'll check and fold to any bet.

On a highly coordinated flop of Tc-8d-9d, I have an open-ended straight draw (any 6 or Jack gives me a straight). Claude, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked to me. This board texture heavily favors my range as the Big Blind caller, but it also contains many hands that could have hit Claude's range (sets, two pairs, or straights like QJ or 76). By checking behind, I exercise pot control and ensure that I see a turn card for free to realize the equity of my straight draw. Additionally, checking allows me to keep the pot small in case Claude is planning a check-raise on this wet board.

The turn 5d improved my hand significantly. I now have bottom pair (5s) AND the nut flush draw with my Kd. The board is T-8-9-5 with three diamonds. I have strong equity here - any diamond gives me the nut flush, and my pair might even be good sometimes. With pot at 500 and position acting first, I should bet for value and protection. If Flash has a made hand or draw, they'll likely call or raise. Betting around 300-350 to build the pot with my strong draw.

On the turn, the board has become increasingly dangerous with three diamonds now present (8d, 9d, 5d) and a highly connected texture (Tc-8d-9d). I currently hold King-high with an open-ended straight draw (any 6 or Jack gives me a straight). Claude, who checked the flop, has now led out for 325 into a 500 pot. My equity is strictly tied to hitting my straight, but several factors make this a difficult call: 1) The pot odds (325 to win 1150) require approximately 28% equity, but my 8 outs to a straight give me only about 17% to hit on the river. 2) Even if I hit my straight, the 6d or Jd would put four diamonds on the board, potentially giving Claude a flush. 3) If I hit a Jack, I could be losing to a higher straight if Claude holds a Queen. Given that I have no diamond in my hand to provide a 'backup' flush draw or a blocker, and the board is very wet, chasing this draw against Claude's lead is mathematically incorrect. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am in UTG (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play) with [8h 6s]. This is a weak off-suit one-gapper. With three players yet to act, including a massive chip leader in the Big Blind, this hand does not have enough playability or equity to justify an open raise. The standard play is to fold.

5d4h on the button is a weak hand. While position is good, 54 offsuit doesn't play well postflop and isn't strong enough to open-raise profitably against two blinds, especially with FiveTwo's large stack in the BB who has been active. Better to wait for a stronger spot.

I have Ace-Queen Offsuit in the Small Blind, which is a premium hand in a 4-handed game. Since it has been folded to me, I am facing only the Big Blind (FiveTwo), who has a significant chip lead. I should raise to build the pot and take the initiative in the hand, rather than just calling. A standard raise size from the Small Blind is around 3x to 4x the big blind to account for being out of position post-flop.

Flash is opening from the small blind and can be very wide in this blind-vs-blind spot. Even though T3s is near the bottom of a defend, we’re getting a reasonable price (250 more to win a pot that will be 700) and we have a big stack edge, so we can realize equity with suitedness and some postflop maneuverability. I don’t love 3-betting this specific hand because it lacks good blockers and plays poorly when called, so I’ll take the lower-variance defend and play fit-or-fold with occasional aggression on favorable textures.

As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage on this paired board (4-3-4). Ace-Queen is currently the best hand against most of the Big Blind's calling range. I will place a standard continuation bet to take down the pot immediately or protect my hand against backdoor draws and overcards. A sizing of 300 into the 700 pot is efficient for this purpose.

Flash is c-betting very small (300 into 1000) in a blind-vs-blind spot where his preflop range is extremely wide and this paired, low board is not a slam-dunk for his range. With Ts3s we have a pair (some showdown value) plus backdoor spades and some turn improvement (T/3 give us stronger hands). Raising would turn a marginal made hand into a bluff and can bloat the pot unnecessarily as the big stack with ICM pressure already working for us. Best is to continue cheaply, keep his bluffs in, and reassess versus bigger turn barrels.

The Jack of Clubs on the turn is an overcard to the board but does not improve my Ace-Queen high. FiveTwo called my flop continuation bet, which suggests they likely have a piece of the board, a pocket pair, or a draw (like diamonds or a straight draw). Since the Jack hits some of the Big Blind's calling range and I am out of position against a very active chip leader, I will check to control the size of the pot. This allows me to see how FiveTwo reacts and potentially catch bluffs from missed draws on the river, or see a free river card to improve my hand.

Flash’s turn check after a very small flop c-bet in a blind-vs-blind spot is often a give-up with two overcards / random air. With T3s we have a marginal made hand (pair of 3s) that benefits a lot from denying equity—many of his hands have 6–12 outs to beat us on the river. I’ll bet a controlled size to fold out his A-high/K-high type hands and realize value/protection without bloating the pot. If he check-raises, I’m comfortably folding this hand given our capped strength and big-stack tournament leverage.

I am facing a bet of 600 from FiveTwo on a Jack-turn after I checked. The board is paired (4-4-3-J), and while the Jack is an overcard to my hand, FiveTwo is an extremely aggressive chip leader who frequently bets when checked to. My Ace-Queen still holds significant showdown value against his wide range of bluffs, floating cards, or diamond draws. The pot odds are favorable (600 to call into a total pot of 1,900, requiring about 24% equity). Raising here would likely only be called by better hands (trips, a Jack, or a high pocket pair), so calling is the most prudent way to see a river and potentially catch a bluff or improve to the best hand.

The river Queen of Spades is an excellent card for me, as it gives me Top Pair, Top Kicker (TPTK). On the turn, I checked and FiveTwo bet 600, which I called. The board is now 4-3-4-J-Q. While it's possible FiveTwo has a 4 for trips, his aggressive nature suggests he could be betting a Jack, a draw, or a pure bluff. By checking the river, I allow him to continue his aggression and potentially bluff with missed diamond or straight draws, or value bet a weaker hand like a Jack. If I bet, he might fold his bluffs. Given his high activity level, checking is the best play to either induce a bet or get to showdown against his range.

River goes check to us after we bet turn and got called. With Ts3s on 4d 3d 4c Jc Qs we have a weak made hand (two pair, 4s and 3s, but with a bad kicker situation versus any higher pair/most 3x). A river bet is unlikely to get called by worse (his A-high/whiffed diamonds fold) and is very likely to get called by better (any pocket pair 5+ that plays as two pair with the board 4, any Jx/Qx, any 3x with a better kicker, any 4x). So betting mostly folds out hands we already beat and isolates ourselves versus hands that beat us. Taking the free showdown maximizes our EV and keeps variance low as the big stack.
I have QQ in UTG - a premium hand. With ~50BB effective stack, I should raise for value. Standard open to 2.5-3x BB is appropriate. I'll raise to 250 to build the pot while not scaring everyone away.

In a 4-max game, Claude's raise from UTG (the first position to act) represents a relatively strong range. While Ace-Seven offsuit is a decent hand, it is frequently dominated by an UTG opening range. Additionally, with the chip leader (FiveTwo) and another similar stack (Pro) still to act behind me in the blinds, calling risks being squeezed. Given Claude's relatively short stack (under 50 BBs), a 3-bet bluff is also less effective as he may feel more committed to the pot. I will choose the conservative path and fold this marginal hand to wait for a better spot to utilize my position.

4-handed, Claude is opening 2.5x from UTG with a shorter ~48bb stack, which is likely fairly wide. 98o is a reasonable defend but plays awkwardly OOP in a single-raised pot and invites the BB to squeeze. I prefer a light isolation 3-bet to take initiative, pressure the short stack, and set up a low SPR where I can win with a lot of c-bets when he misses. If Claude 4-bet jams, I can comfortably fold and move on with my chip lead.

I am in the Big Blind with Kc 5c. Claude opened from UTG to 250, and the chip leader FiveTwo 3-bet to 850 from the Small Blind. This is a strong line. I have a marginal suited King, but cold-calling a 3-bet here is dangerous, especially with the original raiser (Claude) still to act behind me. Claude could 4-bet shove given his stack size, or just call, leading to a difficult multi-way pot where my hand is easily dominated. Given the stack depths and the strength shown by the SB 3-bet against an UTG open, folding is the disciplined and correct play to preserve my 108bb stack.

QQ is a premium hand and FiveTwo has been aggressive throughout the tournament. With ~48BB effective, facing a 3-bet to 850, I should 4-bet for value. FiveTwo's wide 3-betting range makes this a clear value spot. 4-betting to ~2200 puts pressure on villain while building the pot with my strong holding. If shoved on, I'm committed to call with QQ.

Claude’s 4-bet to 2200 with only 625 behind is effectively a shove/commitment. If I continue, I’m basically locked into calling the remaining 625 on any flop, so this becomes an all-in preflop decision.

From this point, calling costs me 1975 more to play for a total pot of ~5800, so I need about 34% equity. With 8c9s offsuit, I’m not getting that reliably versus a short-stack 4-bet/commit range that is typically pair-heavy and ace/broadway-heavy (where I’m often dominated and can’t realize anything postflop due to the tiny SPR). With a big stack and plenty of better spots to leverage pressure, folding avoids a marginal-to-negative gamble.

I’ll take the disciplined fold and keep my chip-lead strategy intact.

I am holding Jack-Trey offsuit in the UTG position (4-max). This is an extremely weak starting hand with poor connectivity and post-flop potential. Given that I am first to act with three players behind me, including the chip leader FiveTwo, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with this hand. Folding is the standard professional play here to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

4-handed on the button with a big stack, our primary edge is applying pressure and stealing blinds. Even though 7c2c is very weak, opening a very wide button range can be +EV given how often the blinds must fold, and we have position to realize equity when called. I’ll use the minimum raise to risk less with the bottom of range while still leveraging fold equity; plan is to fold to any substantial 3-bet and play cautiously postflop if called.

I am in the Small Blind with 2s 5h, which is one of the worst starting hands in poker. The Button (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader and playing aggressively, has min-raised to 200. Although the price to call is cheap (150 chips to win a pot that will be 500), playing 5-2 offsuit out of position against an aggressive chip leader is not profitable due to poor playability and reverse implied odds. I will fold.

Holding 4d5s in the BB facing a min-raise from FiveTwo. Getting 3.5:1 odds to call 100 chips. While the pot odds are decent, 45o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position postflop. FiveTwo has been aggressive and will likely c-bet, putting me in difficult spots. With 5875 chips and 31 hands left, no need to defend marginal hands. Fold.

4-handed UTG plays more like a cutoff; Ts7s is a reasonable open with good playability and a suited component. With a large chip-lead, I want to apply steady pressure and pick up blinds often while keeping the open size efficient and not bloating pots unnecessarily. If faced with a sizable 3-bet from the tougher stacks, this hand can comfortably fold.

I am on the Button with QJo. The UTG player (FiveTwo), who is the massive chip leader, has opened to 250 (2.5bb). While FiveTwo is aggressive, an UTG opening range typically includes hands that dominate QJo (AQ, KQ, AJ, KJ) or strong pairs. QJo has poor playability post-flop against a strong range and suffers from reverse implied odds. Additionally, flat-calling invites squeezes from the blinds. Folding is the most prudent long-term play here to preserve chips for better opportunities.
3d9s is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Facing an UTG raise from a player with a massive stack (33k), I'm out of position with no real equity or implied odds. Easy fold.

I am in the big blind with Jc 2d, a very weak hand. FiveTwo, who has a significant chip lead and has been playing aggressively, has raised to 250 from under-the-gun. While it only costs 150 more to call, J2o is easily dominated and plays very poorly post-flop, especially out of position against the chip leader. I will preserve my stack of nearly 10,000 chips for a better spot.

I am in UTG position with J8o (Jack of Diamonds, Eight of Clubs). In a 4-handed game, UTG is the first to act. J8o is a weak hand that is not suitable for opening the pot from early position, even with 4 players. My stack is healthy (108 BB), so there is no pressure to make loose plays. Folding is the standard and correct play here.
As Tc on the button is a strong hand. Pro folded UTG. I should raise to steal blinds or isolate. Standard button open to 250 (2.5x) with this premium suited broadway hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Qs 7c, a weak off-suit hand. Claude, the Button, has raised to 250. While I only need to commit 200 more to call, playing Q7o out of position against the Button and the chip-leading Big Blind is not profitable. I have a healthy stack of nearly 100 big blinds and can wait for better opportunities.

Button is opening to 2.5x with a relatively wide range and we’re getting a great price in the BB (150 to win 400 ≈ 27% needed). A8o has enough raw equity to defend and can make top pair/straight draws. I don’t want to 3-bet this offsuit ace and create a low-SPR pot where we’re often dominated when we flop an ace and can get put in tough spots versus his continuing range. Best is to realize equity with a defend and play postflop.

This flop (Q-J-7 rainbow) heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range (lots of Qx/Jx, strong broadways, and overpairs), while our hand is just ace-high with no meaningful straight/flush backdoors. Donk-betting here would be low-EV and easy to punish. Best is to check our range, keep the pot controlled, and respond to sizing (mostly folding versus a c-bet, continuing only if we pick up equity on later streets).

I have ATs with a gutshot straight draw (need K for Broadway) on a Q-J-7 board. FiveTwo checked to me. I was the preflop aggressor with position. This is a good spot for a c-bet - I have two overcards, a gutshot, and can represent the Q or J. Betting around 60% pot to continue aggression and potentially take it down.

Facing a flop c-bet of 350 into 550 (pot now 900), we’re getting ~28% pot odds, but Ad8s on Qc-Jh-7s has very poor equity versus Button’s c-betting range (lots of Qx/Jx, KQ/AQ, AJ/KJ, overpairs, plus strong draws like T9). We also lack meaningful backdoors (no flush draw, no real straight backdoor), so continuing would be mostly hoping to spike an Ace (often dominated by AQ/AJ) or an 8 (rarely good). With a big stack edge, there’s no need to force a low-equity bluff line against a competent, shorter-stacked opponent—best is to preserve chips and move on.
8h5c is a very weak hand - unsuited, disconnected, no high card value. From UTG position against 3 opponents, this is a clear fold. No equity to justify playing.

I am on the button with 8d 7s. In a 4-handed game, this is a marginal hand. FiveTwo (SB) has been very aggressive as the chip leader, and Pro (BB) is capable of defending. Given the stack sizes and the tendency of FiveTwo to put pressure on opponents, I will fold this hand to avoid a difficult post-flop situation with a weak holding.

Folded to us in the SB vs Pro in the BB. 7♣3♦ offsuit is near-bottom of range and plays very poorly out of position in a raised pot. Completing keeps the pot small, lets us realize some equity cheaply, and also allows us to fold comfortably if Pro attacks with a raise (we only risk the extra 50). I’ll reserve the open-raise frequency for hands with better playability/blockers.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc Th facing a limp from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). The pot is 200. JTo is a playable hand, especially in position post-flop against a wide SB limping range. Raising is an option to attack the dead money, but against the chip leader who can call widely, bloating the pot with a marginal offsuit hand increases variance unnecessarily. Checking allows me to see a free flop in position, realize my equity, and keep the pot manageable. I will check and play post-flop.

Limped SB vs BB, we arrive on Qc-9d-4c with 7c3d: no pair, no gutshot, and only a thin backdoor club draw. In a small limped pot, leading as a bluff here burns chips because Pro will continue with any Qx/9x/pairs and a chunk of club holdings, and we have poor equity when called. Best is to keep the pot small, realize what little equity we have, and comfortably check-fold to meaningful pressure.

I am in the Big Blind with JcTh in a limped pot against the Small Blind (FiveTwo). The flop is 9dQc4c. I have an open-ended straight draw (needs an 8 or King to complete). The Small Blind has checked to me. While betting is a viable option to deny equity and take down the pot immediately, checking back is a strong play here for several reasons. First, it guarantees I see the turn card for free, realizing my equity without risk of being check-raised by the aggressive chip leader. Second, J-high has very little showdown value, but the draw is strong; keeping the pot small maximizes my implied odds if I hit. Third, in a limped pot, the opponent's range contains many random hands that might connect with this board (Qx, 9x, 4x, flush draws), so folding equity might be lower than expected. I will take the free card.

Limped SB vs BB and flop checked through, both ranges are wide and capped. The turn pairing the 4 is a good stab spot: it reduces the relative strength of many one-pair hands, and we can credibly represent a delayed value hand (Qx that pot-controls flop, or 4x). With 7c3d we have essentially no showdown value, so checking just forfeits the pot too often. I’ll bet a non-min small sizing to pick up the 200 chip pot at high frequency and keep my risk low; if Pro raises, we comfortably fold.

I hold JcTh on a board of 9d Qc 4c 4d. I have an open-ended straight draw (needs an 8 or a King), giving me 8 outs. The pot is currently 325 chips (200 from previous streets + 125 bet by opponent). I need to call 125 to win a total pot of 450, offering me pot odds of approximately 28%. My equity with 8 outs and one card to come is roughly 16-17%. This is insufficient to call based on direct odds. Furthermore, the board is paired and has two flush draws (diamonds and clubs), meaning some of my outs (clubs/diamonds) are 'dirty' and could give an opponent a flush, or the board pairing could give them a full house. The reverse implied odds and the lack of direct odds make this a fold.

I am dealt Queen-Six offsuit (Q6o) in the under-the-gun position in a 4-handed game. While the ranges widen as the table gets shorter, Q6o is still significantly below the standard opening range for the first player to act. Given the stack sizes and the presence of an aggressive chip leader (FiveTwo) on the button, there is no reason to risk chips with a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to utilize my stack.

Button vs blinds after UTG folds is a prime steal spot. A9o is comfortably ahead of the blinds’ folding ranges and plays well in position with strong top-pair potential and some blocker value to strong Ax. With our big stack edge, applying pressure with a standard open size should generate a lot of immediate folds while keeping the pot manageable if we get defended.

I hold 4s 2d in the Small Blind, which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Hold'em. FiveTwo, the chip leader on the Button, has raised to 250. Despite the active nature of the opponent, playing 4-high out of position is a significantly negative EV play. The pot odds of calling 200 to win a pot of 400 (2:1) are insufficient to justify the equity disadvantage and the lack of playability post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

5d7h is a weak offsuit hand facing a button raise from FiveTwo who has been aggressive throughout. While I'm getting ~2.67:1 pot odds, this hand plays poorly postflop out of position. With only 6025 chips left vs FiveTwo's massive 33k stack, I should preserve my stack for better spots rather than defend with a marginal hand.

4-handed we can open wider, but 9♦4♠ offsuit (9h4d) is still too weak from UTG: poor playability, dominated when we make a pair, and doesn’t realize equity well out of position versus the blinds’ defend ranges. With a big chip lead, there’s no need to take thin, negative-EV spots—fold and preserve stack for better opens/3-bet spots.

I am on the Button with 8c 3h after the UTG player folded. This is an extremely weak hand (bottom 10% of hands). While position is favorable, the hand has very poor playability and equity. Attempting to steal the blinds with such a weak holding is generally negative EV, especially with reasonable stack depths in the blinds (Claude 60bb, Flash 98bb). The standard and prudent play is to fold.
Ah6h in the SB with only Flash in the BB left. Suited ace is a strong hand heads-up. Standard play is to raise for value/isolation. I'll raise to 250.

I am in the Big Blind with 2-3 offsuit, the weakest starting hand. Claude in the Small Blind raised to 2.5x. While the pot odds are decent and I would have position post-flop, this hand has zero playability and very little equity against even a wide SB stealing range. Conserving chips for a better opportunity is more prudent.

I am in UTG (first to act) in a 4-handed game with [5d 8d]. Although 4-handed play allows for wider ranges, 8-5 suited is a 'three-gapper' and generally too weak to open profitably from the first position, especially with active players and a massive chip leader behind me. My stack is healthy (over 100 BB), so there is no need to take unnecessary risks with speculative hands out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

J6o on the button - marginal hand but good position. Pro folded so I'm first to act with only blinds behind. With 61BB stack, I should be opening wide from the button to steal blinds. FiveTwo has been aggressive with 3-bets but my position gives me fold equity. Standard 2.5x open to steal.

I am holding 3h Jc in the small blind. The button (Claude) has raised to 250. This is a very weak hand to play out of position, especially against a button open which often contains stronger cards or has positional advantage. Given the stack sizes and the stage of the tournament, there is no reason to defend with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Claude is opening the Button wide and I’m getting a very good price in the BB (150 more to win 400, and we realize well with suited hands). 7h4h is weak but has decent equity vs a Button range and some implied odds when we make straights/flushes. A light 3-bet would bloat a pot OOP with a dominated, low-connectivity hand and invites him to continue in position, so I prefer defending via call and playing postflop from a controlled pot.

We’re OOP as the BB defender on a very connected 9-8-7 board where BTN’s range has lots of strong continues (pairs + draws) and he’ll c-bet frequently. With 7h4h we have a marginal made hand (middle pair, weak kicker) plus only a backdoor flush, so betting risks getting raised/put in tough spots and bloating the pot against a range that can continue aggressively. Checking keeps ranges protected, lets Claude bluff his overcards, and allows us to play a controlled check/call or check/fold line based on sizing and runout.

I have J-high on a super connected 9-8-7 board. FiveTwo checked to me. This board smashes calling ranges - any T or 6 makes a straight, and tons of pair+draw combos exist. With zero equity (no pair, no draw), c-betting into this texture is burning money. Check back and take a free card, maybe hit a J or get a favorable turn to bluff.

Flop went check-check on a very dynamic 9-8-7 board, which caps Claude’s range somewhat toward overcards, some medium pairs, and occasional slowplays. The turn J♠ increases the number of scary rivers and gives his range more equity, while our 7♠4♥ is a thin, vulnerable showdown hand. I prefer a small probe/blocking bet to (1) deny equity to A/K/Q-high hands that checked back, (2) pick up the pot immediately a decent amount, and (3) set the price so I’m less exposed to a large delayed c-bet. If he raises, I’m comfortably folding this hand on this texture.

The Js on the turn gives me top pair. Board is now 9-7-8-J - very connected. FiveTwo's small 250 bet (31% pot) could be a blocking bet, weak value, or probing. Anyone with T-x has a straight (JT987). However, folding top pair to this tiny bet is too weak. Raising risks getting blown off my hand if they have a straight. Calling is best - I have showdown value and can reassess on the river.

River completes more straights (notably 6T/6x) and the board is very connected; after Claude checks flop and calls my turn probe, his range contains plenty of hands that either beat a weak 7 (any 8x/9x/Jx, some better 7x, and occasional straights) or have pure overcards that I already beat. Betting now mostly folds out the hands I’m ahead of (A/K/Q-high) and gets called by better, so it’s not a good value/protection bet on the river. I’ll check and aim to realize showdown; if he bets, I can decide based on sizing and his line.

River: 9h-7d-8c-Js-5s. I have top pair Jacks with weak 6 kicker. FiveTwo checked to me. This board is extremely connected - any T makes a straight. Betting for value risks a check-raise from straights. My hand has showdown value but isn't strong enough to value bet thin against FiveTwo's range which could include many straights and better Jx. Check back and show down - I beat busted draws and weaker pairs.
4c 2s is one of the worst starting hands in poker. From UTG position with 3 players to act behind me, this is a clear fold.

I am on the button with Ten-Six offsuit in a four-handed game. While the button is a prime stealing position, Ten-Six is a relatively weak hand to open with, especially with a chip leader like FiveTwo in the small blind who has shown a tendency to be active. Given my healthy stack and the number of hands remaining, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with a marginal hand.

Folded to me in the small blind versus Pro’s big blind. With a big stack edge and position disadvantage postflop, I still want to apply preflop pressure and play a wide raising strategy here. K6o is comfortably inside a standard SB open-raise range heads-up, blocks some strong Kx continues, and benefits from fold equity. I’ll use a modest 2.5x sizing to pick up the dead money often while keeping the pot manageable out of position and allowing me to fold cleanly versus a 3-bet.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 8h, a strong suited connector. The Small Blind (FiveTwo) has raised to 250. I need to call 150 more into a pot of 350, getting good pot odds (approx 2.3:1) and will have position on the aggressor post-flop. Suited connectors play very well in position deep-stacked. Defending here is standard and profitable.

Heads-up SB vs BB on A♦5♠3♦, I have K♣6♦ with no made hand but a useful backdoor diamond plus some overcard equity versus small pairs/5x/3x. As the preflop raiser, this Ace-high board is a strong range advantage spot (I have far more Ax), so I want to c-bet at high frequency for a small sizing to realize fold equity against his broad preflop defend range and to set up profitable turn barrels on diamonds, broadways, and some low cards. If raised, I can comfortably fold this hand.

I hold 9h 8h on a flop of 5s 3d Ad. This board completely misses my hand; I have no pair, no flush draw, and no straight draw. The Ace heavily favors the Small Blind's raising range. Although the bet is small (175 into 500), I have essentially zero equity and no reasonable path to winning this pot without significant bluffing, which is ill-advised on this texture against the chip leader. Folding is the standard and correct play.

In a 4-handed game, 9d 7d is a decent speculative hand to open from the first position (UTG/Hijack). Opening helps maintain an aggressive image and allows me to take down the blinds or play a pot with a hand that has good post-flop playability. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the blinds and potentially the button.

4-handed, Flash’s UTG opening range should be fairly wide, and on the button I can apply a lot of pressure with position and a big stack advantage over the table. Ah7c is not a great flatting hand (domination issues, and it plays worse multiway if SB/BB come along), but it makes a reasonable light 3-bet because the ace blocks many strong continue/4-bet hands (AK/AQ/AJ/AA) and I’ll win the pot preflop at a high frequency. If Flash continues, I still have position and can play cautiously on ace-high boards. I’m raising to a size that discourages the blinds from coming along while not overcommitting versus a 4-bet.

I hold 5c 6d in the Small Blind, a very weak hand. Facing a UTG open to 300 and a Button 3-bet to 950, I am up against significant strength and would be out of position in a bloated pot. My hand has very low equity against their ranges, and calling would be -EV. A cold 4-bet bluff is ill-advised given the action. I will fold and preserve my stack.

T3o is a very weak hand. Facing a 3-bet to 950 from FiveTwo who has been aggressive but also has position on me, I have no business calling 850 with this holding. Even with the pot at 1400, the pot odds don't justify playing such a marginal hand out of position. Easy fold.

I opened 9d 7d from the first position in a 4-handed game, and FiveTwo 3-bet me from the button. While 9d 7d is a decent speculative hand with good implied odds, I am out of position against the chip leader. FiveTwo has been very aggressive throughout the tournament, and calling here puts me in a difficult spot post-flop where I will likely have to check-fold many boards or face heavy pressure. Given that I have a healthy stack of 95 BBs and we are approaching the final hands of the tournament, I prefer to fold and preserve my chips for a more advantageous situation where I have either a better hand or position.

4-handed, ranges are wide and A4o is comfortably in an UTG (4-max) opening range. With a large chip lead, raising pressures the blinds’ medium/short stacks and wins the pot often enough preflop. I’ll use a modest size to avoid bloating the pot with a marginal ace and to keep my open/fold vs 3-bets clean while still denying the blinds a cheap realization.

I am on the Button with Qh 9h facing a UTG raise to 250 from the chip leader, FiveTwo. While FiveTwo has been active, a UTG opening range is typically stronger, often dominating Q9s (e.g., AQ, KQ, QJ, A9s). Calling invites squeeze plays from the blinds and exposes me to reverse implied odds post-flop. Given the tournament stage (Hand 128/150) and my healthy stack, folding is the prudent, low-variance play to preserve chips for better opportunities.

J4o is a weak offsuit hand. Facing a raise from FiveTwo (who has been active), I'm out of position with poor card quality. J4o doesn't play well multiway or heads-up OOP. The 200 to call isn't worth it with this holding. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Deuce suited (Ad 2d). FiveTwo, the chip leader, raised from the Cutoff/UTG position in this 4-handed game. My hand is strong enough to defend the blind, especially since I am getting attractive pot odds, needing to call 150 to win a total pot of 550. While 3-betting is an option to put pressure on the opener, FiveTwo's large stack and active playstyle make calling a more prudent way to realize my equity and keep the pot manageable. I will call to see the flop.

I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser, FiveTwo, who has a significant chip lead. The flop of 9-K-T is very favorable for his opening range, which includes many broadway cards and sets. My Ace-Deuce of diamonds provides a backdoor nut flush draw and an overcard, but it is currently just Ace-high on a coordinated board. Leading out would likely be met with a raise or a call by a better hand. Checking is the standard play here to see how the raiser reacts and to keep the pot controlled.

Heads-up vs the BB on K♦T♥9♠, this is a dynamic board that connects with a lot of BB continues (Kx/Tx/9x, QJ, J8, etc.), but as the preflop raiser I still retain enough range/nut advantage (AK/KQ, AA/KK/TT/99, QJ) to apply pressure. With A♣4♠ specifically I have little showdown value and only thin backdoors, so checking would often just concede the pot. I’ll use a small c-bet to fold out unpaired junk and some weak 9x/underpairs while keeping my risk low; if I face a raise I can comfortably fold.

The flop of 9-K-T is very coordinated and favors the pre-flop raiser's range. However, FiveTwo has made a relatively small continuation bet of 200 into a 550 pot. With Ace-Deuce of diamonds, I have the nut backdoor flush draw and one overcard. The pot odds are favorable, as I only need to call 200 to win a total pot of 950. While my hand is currently just Ace-high, the small bet size and my backdoor equity justify a call to see a turn card. If the turn is a diamond, I'll pick up a strong draw; if it's a blank and FiveTwo continues with significant aggression, I can fold then.

On the turn, I have picked up the nut flush draw with the Trey of Diamonds. The board is quite coordinated (9-K-T-3) and likely connects well with FiveTwo's pre-flop raising range (Broadway cards, sets). My hand is currently Ace-high, but I have significant equity with the draw to the nut flush. In this position, I will check to the aggressor. If FiveTwo bets, I have a very comfortable call to try and realize my equity on the river. Leading out into the chip leader on a board that favors him is unnecessarily risky, as it could invite a raise that forces me to pay more than I want to see the final card.

Turn 3♦ is essentially a brick for my actual hand (A♣4♠) and, after BB check-calls the flop on K♦T♥9♠, their range is heavily weighted toward made hands and strong draws (Kx/Tx/9x, QJ/J8, etc.). A second barrel here would need a lot of folds to be profitable, but those hands generally continue versus reasonable sizing, meaning I’d be lighting chips on fire with very little equity. By checking back, I keep the pot controlled, realize my A-high showdown value versus missed draws/floats, and can still consider bluffing/value-betting on select rivers depending on runout and villain action.

The river 6h is a complete blank and does not improve my Ace-high. My hand has very little showdown value, but it might occasionally beat a missed straight draw or a lower Ace-high. FiveTwo's check on the turn after a small flop continuation bet suggests a marginal made hand (like a 9 or a 10) or a draw that he is happy to take to showdown cheaply. Since I missed my flush draw and am out of position against the massive chip leader, I will check. If he checks back, I get to see the showdown for free; if he bets, I will likely fold as Ace-high is rarely good here if he decides to put more money in.

River is checked to me in a small pot (950) after BB check-calls flop then check-checks turn/river. With A♣4♠ on K♦T♥9♠3♦6♥ I have only A-high, but I do retain some real showdown value versus the portion of Flash’s range that took one stab at realizing equity (missed AQ/AJ/J8/Q8 type floats). 

A bluff here would mainly target one-pair bluff-catchers (Kx/Tx/9x, small pairs), but Flash’s line is consistent with exactly those hands checking to induce, and at this stack depth he can look me up fairly often. Since betting doesn’t get called by worse and risks getting snapped by better (or by made straights like 87 that got there on the 6), checking back maximizes EV by taking the free showdown and avoiding lighting chips on fire in a spot with unclear fold equity.

I am in UTG position with a very weak hand, Queen-Three offsuit (Q3o). With three players left to act behind me, including the big stack in the Big Blind, the probability of dominating an opponent or winning a showdown is very low. This hand falls well outside standard opening ranges from early position. Preserving chips is preferable to engaging in a marginal spot with poor position and card strength.

8c2h is a junk hand - one of the worst starting hands in poker. While I'm on the button with good position, FiveTwo has been aggressive with 3-bets and has a massive stack. Not worth opening this trash and getting 3-bet. Fold and wait for better spots in the remaining 21 hands.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit. The action has been folded to me, making this a standard situation to attempt a blind steal or build a pot with a superior hand. A9o is significantly stronger than the average range the Big Blind (FiveTwo) will be defending with. Given FiveTwo's large stack and active play, I want to establish control of the hand. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to take the initiative.

We’re heads-up versus the small blind open, and JJ is well ahead of Flash’s likely wide SB opening range. Calling keeps in all dominated broadways/low pairs but also invites awkward postflop OOP and lets them realize equity cheaply. A value 3-bet takes initiative, denies equity, and can win preflop often while still getting action from worse (Tx/Qx/Kx/Jx, suited connectors, smaller pairs). With stacks ~92bb effective, I’ll size to about 3.5–4x the raise from OOP.

I opened to 300 with A9o from the Small Blind, and FiveTwo (the chip leader) 3-bet to 1100 from the Big Blind. While A9o is ahead of a wide range in a blind-vs-blind battle, FiveTwo has been very active and aggressive. Calling this 3-bet would put me in a large pot out of position for the rest of the hand with a hand that is easily dominated by his value range (AJ+) and difficult to realize equity with when I miss the flop. Given my stack of 92 BBs and the fact that there are only 21 hands remaining in the tournament, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to engage rather than risking a significant portion of my stack in a marginal spot against the chip leader.

Pocket 4s from UTG with ~65BB effective stack. Small pairs play well for set mining. I'll open raise to 250 (2.5x) - standard open size. If I get 3-bet significantly, I can fold. If called, I'm looking to hit a set on the flop. Folding is too tight here, and limping invites more players.

I am on the button with Ten of Spades and Deuce of Hearts. Claude has opened from the UTG position for 250 chips. My hand is extremely weak (T2o) and does not play well post-flop, especially against an under-the-gun range. With 9200 chips and 20 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no strategic reason to defend or bluff with such low equity. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

Facing an UTG 2.5x open from a competent opponent, 5♣3♠ is near-bottom of range: no high-card value, weak blockers, and very poor playability out of position (especially if BB comes along). Calling invites reverse implied odds, and 3-bet bluffing is better done with hands that block strong continues (Ax/Kx) or have better equity realization. With a big stack and small pot, this is an easy discipline fold.

I hold Pocket Queens (Qh Qs) in the Big Blind. This is a premium hand. Facing a standard open from Claude in the UTG (Cutoff in 4-handed) position, my standard play is to 3-bet for value. I am out of position, so I should use a larger sizing (roughly 3.5x to 4x the open) to discourage calls with speculative hands and charge dominated pairs (99-JJ) or high cards (AQ, AJ, KQs). Calling is an option to trap, but it allows the opponent to realize equity cheaply and leaves me guessing on Ace/King high boards. I will raise to 900 to take the initiative and build the pot.

Pocket 4s facing a 3-bet from Pro. I opened 250, Pro raised to 900. I need 650 to call into a 1200 pot (~35% equity needed). Set mining math: hit set ~12% of the time, need ~5.4k implied odds to break even. Pro has ~8.5k behind after this bet, so implied odds are there. Easy post-flop play - no set, no bet. Calling to set mine.

I hold Pocket Queens on a dry, paired board of 7-2-2. As the pre-flop 3-bettor out of position, I have a significant range advantage. The board texture interacts poorly with the opponent's calling range, which likely consists of mid-pocket pairs (88-JJ) and broadway cards (AK, AQ). Checking is a valid option to induce bluffs or protect a checking range, but it risks the opponent checking back mid-pairs for pot control, causing me to miss a street of value. A small continuation bet of roughly 1/3 pot serves to extract value from pairs like 88-JJ that will likely call, while denying free equity to overcards. Given the Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) of roughly 3, a small bet allows me to construct a geometric pot-building line to potentially get stacks in by the river if the opponent holds a strong second-best hand.

Missed my set on 7-2-2 board. Pro 3-bet preflop and c-bet 600 into ~1850. My pocket 4s are only beating AK/AQ type hands but crushed by any overpair (55+), which makes up a significant portion of Pro's 3-bet range. Calling here starts a guessing game where I'll face bigger bets on turn/river without position. Set mining failed - disciplined fold to preserve chips for better spots with 20 hands left.

I am UTG (under the gun) with 9-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. In a 4-handed game, standard strategy dictates opening a much stronger range than this from the first position. With a stack of 92BB, there is no reason to attempt a loose bluff here, especially since I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand against any callers. Folding is the only disciplined play.

With Flash folded, this is a prime Button steal spot. KQo is well within a strong BTN opening range 4-handed: it plays well in position, dominates many blind-defending Kx/Qx holdings, and we can apply pressure to shorter stacks (especially Claude in the BB). I’ll use a standard, slightly larger than min open to deny cheap realization and keep initiative.

I hold 5c 3d in the Small Blind, facing a raise to 250 from the Button (FiveTwo). This is a trash hand (53 offsuit) with very poor playability and equity. Even though the Button's range might be wide, I am out of position and the hand is too weak to call or 3-bet bluff. The pot odds of 2:1 are insufficient for such a weak holding. Folding is the correct decision.

I have 6c3c in the BB facing a button open to 250. I need 150 to call into a 400 pot, getting about 2.7:1 odds. While 63s is a weak hand, it's suited and I'm getting a decent price. I can defend here and fold to aggression if I miss the flop. With 55BB effective, I can afford this speculative call.

I flopped bottom pair (6s) with a very weak kicker on an A-Q-6 board with two hearts. FiveTwo's button opening range connects well with this board - they'll have plenty of Ax, Qx, and overpairs. My hand is too weak to bet for value and also too weak to bluff with. Checking to see what FiveTwo does is the optimal play - I can potentially showdown cheaply if checked through or fold to significant aggression.

Claude’s BB defend range is wide, and on A♥Q♥6♦ we retain a clear range advantage as the preflop raiser. With K♥Q♠ we have a strong bluff-catcher/marginal value hand: we’re behind Ax but ahead of many floats, worse Qx, and pocket pairs, and we want to deny equity to hands with two overs or heart draws. A small c-bet also keeps the pot controlled and leverages position. If Claude check-raises large, this hand is strong enough to bet but not to stack off—plan is to fold to big pressure and continue versus calls.

I’ll c-bet ~1/3 pot to get value/protection efficiently and keep my range wide.

I have bottom pair (6s) with a weak kicker on Ah-Qh-6d board. FiveTwo's small c-bet of 200 into 500 gives me 3.75:1 pot odds. Against a wide button c-betting range, my bottom pair has enough equity to call this sizing once. The small bet suggests either a thin value bet or a probe with air. I can call and reevaluate on the turn - if they fire big again, I'll fold. The price is right to float one street.

Turn brought the Kc, making the board AhQhKc6d - extremely coordinated with Broadway cards. My bottom pair (6s) with 3 kicker is essentially worthless now. FiveTwo's button range connects heavily with A-K-Q boards. I should check and fold to any bet - there's no value in betting and no reason to call further aggression with this weak holding.

We turned top two (KQ) on a very dynamic board where Claude’s BB defend/call range contains lots of Ax (A2–AJ), Qx, Kx, and heart/straight-draw combos. After his turn check, we should value bet and deny equity rather than give a free card to hearts or a T/J river that can create nasty spots. A medium sizing keeps worse one-pair hands and draws in while still charging them, and also lets us play cleanly versus a rare check-raise (which is heavily weighted to AK/AQ/sets given this line and his short stack).

Board is AhQhKc6d - three Broadway cards connected. My bottom pair with 3 kicker is essentially worthless against FiveTwo's button range that connects heavily with this board. They have tons of Ax, Kx, Qx, JT for straights, and two pair combos. Getting 2.5:1 but my equity is terrible here. Continuing would be burning chips. Easy fold.

4-handed UTG, A9s is comfortably within an opening range. We cover all stacks and can apply pressure to the shorter blinds while still having a hand with good playability (nut-flush potential, strong top-pair potential, and blocker to AA/AK). I’ll use a standard, slightly larger-than-min open to reduce blind overcalls and keep initiative, while still being able to react appropriately versus a 3-bet from the button.

I am holding Qs 2d on the Button. The player in the UTG/CO position (FiveTwo) has raised to 250 (2.5BB). My hand is statistically very weak (bottom 15-20% of hands) and plays poorly post-flop, especially against a raising range. Even though I have position, calling is not profitable due to reverse implied odds and the risk of being squeezed by the blinds. 3-betting is also not optimal with this specific holding. The disciplined play is to fold.

I have pocket 5s in the small blind facing a UTG raise to 250. Pocket pairs are good set-mining hands. The pot is 400 and I only need 200 to call. With a stack of 5125, I have decent implied odds if I hit a set against FiveTwo's deep stack (34k). Calling to see a flop is the standard play here.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Ten offsuit (Ks Th). The UTG player (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader and plays quite aggressively, raised to 250. The Small Blind (Claude) called.
