The pot is currently 600, and it costs me only 150 more to call. I am getting 4-to-1 odds on a call, which is excellent for a hand like KTo in a short-handed game. While KTo can be dominated by an UTG opening range in a full-ring game, in a 4-handed tournament, it is well within the defending range against a wide opener. I will call to see the flop and play post-flop out of position, rather than bloating the pot with a 3-bet against a deep-stacked, aggressive opponent.

I have pocket 5s on A-6-6 board. I missed my set and now have a small underpair. With an ace on board and paired sixes, my hand is very weak against two opponents. Either could have an ace, a 6, or an overpair. Checking is the clear play here - no value in betting and I'm unlikely to fold out better hands.

I have King-high on an Ace-high paired board (A-6-6). This texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's (FiveTwo) range, as it contains many strong Aces and overpairs. Since I am out of position and have no pair or draw, there is no reason to lead out. I will check and see how the original aggressor acts. My goal is to get to a cheap showdown or fold if the pressure becomes too great, given my lack of equity on this board.

Paired A66 is a strong range-advantage board for the UTG raiser, and both blinds checking looks capped/weak a lot of the time (random broadways, small pairs, weak floats). With A9 we have a medium-strength top pair that benefits from protection/value versus worse aces and pocket pairs, while also being happy to just win the pot now. Multiway I’ll keep sizing small to target folds and avoid bloating the pot versus the few 6x/trap hands; if I face a big check-raise/shove from a blind (especially with the other player behind), I can comfortably fold this kicker.

I have pocket 5s on A-6-6 board facing a 250 bet. My hand is an underpair with almost no equity. FiveTwo likely has an ace, a 6, or an overpair. Calling just to see more streets is -EV when I'm beat most of the time and have no real draws. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Ten offsuit on a flop of Ace-Six-Six. The pre-flop aggressor, FiveTwo, has placed a continuation bet of 250 into a pot of 750 after the Small Blind checked. While I am getting 4-to-1 pot odds to call, this board texture is very unfavorable for my hand. I have no pair, no draw, and very little chance of having the best hand. FiveTwo's range as the UTG raiser heavily includes Aces and pocket pairs that have me crushed. Furthermore, being out of position makes it difficult to realize any equity on future streets. Rather than floating a bet with only King-high and no real plan to win the pot, I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am in UTG (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play) with 4h 6s, which is a very weak unsuited one-gapper. My stack is healthy at 112.5 BBs. Given the poor playability of the hand and my position being first to act, there is no strategic incentive to enter the pot. The chip leader (FiveTwo) is in the Big Blind and has been aggressive, making stealing attempts with trash hands risky. The standard and correct play is to fold.

On the button with Q5o after UTG folds. This is a marginal hand but button is the best stealing position. With ~49BB stack, I should be somewhat selective, but Q5o has some playability and I can fold to a 3-bet. Standard button open to 250.

I am in the Small Blind with Ah6h, a strong suited Ace in 4-handed play. The Button (Claude) has raised to 250. Claude has a relatively short stack compared to the table leader. A 3-bet here allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop, or isolate Claude if the Big Blind (FiveTwo) folds. A6s plays well against a wide Button opening range and has good post-flop potential with nut-flush possibilities. I will raise to 750 to put pressure on Claude and discourage FiveTwo from entering with a marginal hand.

We’re facing a button open (250) and a small blind 3-bet to 750 with Q9s in the BB. Folding is fine, but the price is strong (650 to win 1100) and Q9s has decent playability/implied odds when we flop pair+draws or strong draws. A 4-bet bluff is unnecessary here (poor blockers, and Flash/Claude stack sizes make a shove/call-back plausible), so I’ll take the lower-variance defend and realize equity postflop cautiously OOP, ready to fold to heavy pressure on bad textures or if Claude back-jams and we don’t get the right price.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash (750) and a cold-call from FiveTwo. I need to call 500 into 1750 pot (3.5:1 odds). However, Q5o is a very weak hand that plays poorly multiway. Both opponents have shown strength - Flash 3-betting and FiveTwo cold-calling. My hand has poor equity against their ranges and will be difficult to play profitably postflop OOP vs Flash. Even with decent pot odds, folding is correct here.

I am the pre-flop aggressor having 3-bet from the Small Blind. The board of K-8-4 rainbow is very favorable for a 3-betting range, which includes strong Kings (AK, KQs) and premium pairs (AA, KK, QQ) that the Big Blind (FiveTwo) is less likely to have after cold-calling. Although I only have Ace-high, I have the range advantage on this dry board. A continuation bet of roughly 1/3 the pot is standard here to put pressure on FiveTwo's mid-pairs and suited connectors that missed. If I am called, I have one overcard and a backdoor nut-flush draw (one heart on board) to potentially improve on the turn.

We defended pre because of price/playability, but this flop is very unfavorable: K84r heavily favors the SB 3-bettor’s range, and with Q9s we have no pair and no meaningful draw (just thin backdoors). Flash’s small c-bet is designed to fold out exactly this type of hand and we’re OOP with limited ability to realize equity. Continuing risks getting barreled off while often being dominated when we improve. Best is to give up and preserve stack.

Q9 suited in UTG at a 4-handed table. This is a playable hand - suited broadway with decent postflop potential. With 47 BBs and 17 hands left, I should be looking for spots to accumulate chips. Standard open to 2.5x.

I am holding 7s 5c on the button. Claude, who is the shortest stack at the table, has raised to 2.5x the big blind from UTG. 75o is a very weak starting hand and does not play well against an opening range, particularly with the aggressive FiveTwo in the small blind and Pro in the big blind still to act. Given we are at hand 134 of 150, I should look for higher-equity spots to increase my stack rather than defending or bluffing with a low-value hand like this.

Facing Claude’s UTG 2.5bb open from the small blind, 76o is a low-realization hand out of position. Flatting invites the BB in at a good price and creates a tough multiway spot with poor playability; 3-betting as a bluff here also isn’t great since we lack blockers and Claude has shown willingness to fight back (4-bet/shove potential). With a large chip lead, we don’t need to take thin, OOP marginal spots—fold and preserve EV for better steal/pressure situations.

I am in the Big Blind with AJo facing a UTG raise from Claude to 250. With only 4 players at the table, AJo is a very strong hand, often ahead of a standard opening range. The pot odds are favorable (calling 150 into a pot of 400), offering roughly 27% required equity, which I easily exceed. While a 3-bet is a viable option to isolate, calling keeps Claude's range wide and manages the pot size while playing out of position. I will call to realize my equity.

I am in the Big Blind with AhJh on a dry K-3-3 rainbow flop. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor (Claude, UTG), who has all the strong Kings and pocket pairs in his range. While I have Ace-high with a decent kicker, leading out (donking) is unlikely to be profitable as it folds out worse hands and gets called by better. The standard play is to check to the aggressor to see if they continue. If Claude bets, I will evaluate based on sizing, but for now, checking allows me to realize equity or potentially catch a bluff later.

I opened pre and the flop is 3d Ks 3h - a very dry, paired board that favors the preflop raiser. I have Q-high with a backdoor heart flush draw. This is a standard c-bet spot - the K-high board hits my perceived range well and Pro will have a hard time continuing without a King or better. A small bet around 1/3 pot should get folds often enough to be profitable.

The opponent c-bets small (200 into 550) on a very dry, paired board (K-3-3). This texture connects well with a UTG opening range (lots of Kx), but also encourages c-betting with the entire range, including air (AQ, QJ, smaller pairs). I hold Ace-Jack high, which is ahead of all his bluffs. The price to call is excellent (roughly 21% equity required), and I have 6 outs to potentially improve to top pair, plus showdown value against unpaired hands. Folding is too tight given the price and range interaction. Raising turns my hand into a bluff unnecessarily. Calling to re-evaluate on the turn is the best line.

On the turn, the board is [3d Ks 3h 9d]. I hold [Jh Ad], giving me the nut flush draw and two overcards to the nine (though the King is the top pair on board). The 9d doesn't change the texture significantly for the pre-flop raiser's range, which likely contains many Kx hands. Checking is the standard play out of position. It allows me to realize my equity cheaply. If my opponent checks back, I get a free look at the river. If he bets, I have very strong equity (nut flush draw + overcards) to consider calling or even check-raising semi-bluff depending on the sizing, but leading out (donking) is generally suboptimal here.

I turned a pair of 9s on this board (3d Ks 3h 9d). With Q9hh, I now have 9s and 3s two pair with Q kicker. Pro called my flop c-bet - likely has a King, a pocket pair, or was floating. My hand improved significantly. I should bet for value - if Pro has Kx they'll likely call, and I'm ahead of most floats. Betting ~55% pot to extract value while keeping pressure.

The turn is the 9d, making the board [3d Ks 3h 9d]. I hold [Jh Ad], giving me the nut flush draw in addition to my Ace-high overcard. The pot is 1475 (including opponent's bet), and I need to call 525, getting roughly 2.8:1 odds (requiring ~26% equity). My flush draw alone gives me ~18% equity, and my Ace outs add another ~6%, putting me right around the required equity on direct outs alone. Furthermore, against a short-stacked opponent (Claude has ~2700 left), I have excellent implied odds if I hit the flush on the river to stack him. A check-raise is risky as it might isolate me against his value range (Trips/Strong Kx) and he is short enough that he might shove over a raise. Calling maximizes my option to realize equity and win a large pot if I improve, while keeping my range wide.

On a river board of [3d Ks 3h 9d 3s], the board contains three Threes. My hand is [Jh Ad], giving me Trip Threes with an Ace-King kicker (playing the board's King). This means any pair in my opponent's hand (making a Full House), any King (Threes full of Kings), or any Nine (Threes full of Nines) beats me. I only beat pure bluffs (missed draws) and I chop with other Ace-high hands. Leading out (donking) has no value as better hands will call/raise and worse hands will fold. My best option is to check, hoping for a showdown where I might win against air or chop with Ax. If I face a significant bet, I will likely have to fold given how much of a UTG range constitutes a Full House here.

River brought the third 3, making the board 3-3-3-K-9. My hand plays as trip 3s with K-Q kickers (3-3-3-K-Q). Pro has shown call-call-call line which is consistent with Kx. The problem is: any Ace in Pro's hand beats me (they'd play 3-3-3-A-K), and if they have K with a better kicker than Q, they beat me too. A value bet here likely only gets called by hands that beat me (Ax, KA, quads) while worse hands fold. Best to check back for showdown value and pot control.

In a 4-handed environment, QTo is strong enough to open from the first position (UTG/CO). Raising to 3BB (300) is a standard opening size that allows me to take the initiative. I am mindful of FiveTwo's large stack on the button and their history of aggressive 3-betting, but I must maintain a reasonable opening range to stay competitive for the 2nd place spot.

Facing a 3x UTG open 4-handed, 2♠3♦ is at the very bottom of the deck with poor playability and no meaningful blockers to the opener’s continuing range. Calling is -EV (dominated, hard to realize equity), and turning it into a 3-bet bluff is unnecessary variance given our large chip lead and the presence of short stacks who can shove over. Preserve stack and wait for higher-leverage steal/3-bet spots with better equity/blockers.

I hold 73o in the Small Blind, which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise to 300 from Flash in the UTG position, I am out of position with a trash hand. Even in a 4-handed game where ranges are wider, 73o has very poor equity and playability. The pot odds do not justify a call, and the risk of being squeezed by the Big Blind or playing a bloated pot out of position with trash is too high. This is a trivial fold.

AKs is a premium hand - one of the best in poker. Flash opened from UTG to 300 and I'm in the BB with effective stack of 5600. This is an easy 3-bet spot for value. Standard sizing is around 3x the open, so I'll raise to 900. This builds the pot with a strong hand and puts pressure on Flash while keeping my stack-to-pot ratio manageable.

In this 4-handed situation, I opened QTo from the UTG position (which acts as the Cutoff here). Claude, in the Big Blind, has 3-bet to 900. While I have position on Claude for the rest of the hand, QTo is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by Claude's 3-betting range (which likely includes hands like AQ, KQ, and AJ, as well as medium-to-large pairs). Claude has shown aggression in previous hands, and with only 15 hands remaining in the tournament, I need to protect my stack to ensure a top-two finish. Calling 600 more into a pot of 1250 (requiring roughly 32% equity) is potentially break-even if Claude's range is wide, but QTo's poor playability in 3-bet pots and the risk of being dominated make folding the most prudent long-term strategy here.

4-handed from UTG our opening range should be fairly wide, and 66 is strong enough to raise for both value and initiative. As the big stack, a standard open pressures the medium stacks and can pick up the blinds often, while still giving us good playability when called (set potential and can navigate many flops with position on blinds). I’ll use a simple 2.5x size to build the pot and discourage the blinds from realizing equity too cheaply, without committing too much against potential 3-bets.

I am holding 8s 7c (8-7 offsuit) on the Button. The UTG player (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader, has raised to 2.5BB. While being on the Button provides a positional advantage, 8-7 offsuit is a statistically weak hand that performs poorly against a standard UTG opening range. It is easily dominated and lacks the flush potential of a suited connector. Given that I have a healthy stack of over 100BB and we are deep in the tournament (Hand 136/150), there is no incentive to get involved with marginal holdings in this spot. The standard and most prudent play is to fold.

2c4s is a very weak offsuit hand. Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo (who has a massive 33k stack), I'm out of position with no playability. This is a clear fold - calling would just bleed chips with a hand that rarely connects well enough to win.

I am in the Big Blind with Q2o. FiveTwo, the table chip leader, has raised to 250. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (150 to win a total pot of 550, requiring roughly 27% equity), Q2o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against an aggressive big stack. With only 15 hands remaining in the tournament and a healthy stack of 95.5 BBs, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand and risk being put in difficult spots on later streets. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit in the UTG position with 4 players remaining. This is a premium hand, well above the average strength required to open from this position. With a stack of over 100 big blinds, I should raise to build the pot and define my hand strength, rather than limping. A standard opening raise size of 2.5x the big blind (250) is appropriate to balance my range and put pressure on the blinds and the button.

76o is a weak hand facing an UTG raise. Even on the button with position, this hand doesn't play well against a UTG opening range. With two players behind who could squeeze and only 14 hands left, I should preserve my 6000 stack for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with J6o. Pro has opened for a standard raise from UTG. This hand is significantly below the threshold for defending the small blind, especially out of position against a stack that covers me. Calling or raising would put me in a vulnerable spot post-flop or potentially invite a squeeze from the chip leader, FiveTwo, in the Big Blind. The best play is a disciplined fold.

4-handed, BB vs a 2.5x UTG open, K6 suited is a defend and also a reasonable light 3-bet candidate when we cover and can apply pressure. Pro has shown willingness to open/fold to 3-bets in prior hands, and with his ~100bb stack he’s incentivized to avoid marginal continue spots. By 3-betting we pick up the dead money often, take initiative when called, and still have decent playability from the suited K (backdoor/nutty flush potential). Sizing to ~9bb pressures his range without overcommitting us.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit in position (UTG vs BB) against the active chip leader, FiveTwo, who has 3-bet to 900. Given the stack depth (~100 BB) and my positional advantage, calling is the most prudent play. A 4-bet would isolate me against a premium range and potentially force a fold from the bluffs in their wide 3-betting range, whereas calling allows me to keep their range wide and play poker post-flop. I need to call 650 more chips to match their 900 raise.

This is a 3-bet pot where Pro’s preflop calling range contains a lot of Ax (plus some pairs like TT–QQ), so a flop c-bet with Kh6h doesn’t have great equity when called and won’t generate enough folds to justify building the pot OOP. As the bottom of my 3-bet range with no diamond, no gutshot, and only weak backdoors, I prefer to check and either realize equity when he checks back (allowing me to potentially spike a K) or comfortably fold versus meaningful pressure.

I hold Top Pair with a strong Kicker (AsQc) on an Ace-high board (3d Ac 5d) in a 3-bet pot against the chip leader. The opponent, the pre-flop 3-bettor, has checked to me. This board texture hits my calling range very hard, so the opponent's check likely indicates pot control with an underpair (KK-99), a give-up, or a trap with a very strong hand. By checking back, I control the pot size, disguise the strength of my hand, and induce bluffs on later streets. Betting risks isolating myself against better hands (AK, sets, two pair) or facing a difficult check-raise from flush draws. I aim to realize my equity and potentially get value on safer turn/river cards.

After checking back the flop, Pro’s range is still heavy on Ax and some pocket pairs/broadways. The turn 4c gives us a useful double-gutshot (2 or 7 makes a straight), so we now have equity to go with a bluff. Rather than check again and let Pro take a large stab that can deny our equity, I prefer a small delayed c-bet that (1) targets folds from his KQ/QJ-type floats and some mid pairs, (2) keeps our risk controlled against Ax (we’re not trying to blow up the pot), and (3) sets up profitable barrels on some rivers when we improve or when scary cards hit. Sizing ~1/3 pot is appropriate in this 3-bet pot on a dynamic low board.

I hold Ace-Queen (Top Pair, strong kicker) on a board of 3-A-5-4. The turn card (4c) introduces a potential wheel straight for any Deuce (A-2-3-4-5). FiveTwo, the chip leader and pre-flop 3-bettor, checked the flop and has now led out for a small size (600 into 1850, ~1/3 pot) on the turn. This small sizing could represent a blocker bet with pairs like KK/QQ, a thin value bet with a weak Ace, or a cheap bluff. While I lose to AK, sets, two pair, or the unlikely A2/22 straight, I am well ahead of bluffs and pocket pairs below Aces. Raising would likely only get called by better hands or fold out worse ones. Calling keeps their range wide and offers excellent pot odds. I will call to see a river.

River is a great card to apply maximum pressure: it completes the front-door diamonds (3d/5d/4d) and pairs the 4, which is very bad for Pro’s turn-calling range of mostly one-pair Ax/overpairs. With Kh6h we have essentially zero showdown value, so checking just concedes. Our line (check flop, small stab turn) credibly represents diamond draws that now got there, plus occasional boats (AA/A4s/55/33), and Pro is relatively capped because he checked back flop and only called turn (many strong hands/flushes can still exist, but a lot of his range is bluff-catchers that hate this river). A near-pot polarized bet should fold out a meaningful portion of Ax without diamonds and almost all non-A hands, making this a profitable bluff.

On the river, the board is 3d-Ac-5d-4c-4d. The 4d completes the diamond flush draw and pairs the board, making full houses possible. FiveTwo leads out with a pot-sized bet of 3000. My hand (AsQc) is merely top pair with a good kicker. Given the action (check-check flop, small bet turn, bomb river on a flush card), FiveTwo's range is extremely polarized towards nutted hands (flushes, straights with a 2, or full houses) or pure bluffs. I lose to any flush, any straight (A2/22), full houses (33/55/44), and better Aces (AK, A5 which makes two pair). While FiveTwo is aggressive, calling a pot-sized bet in a 3-bet pot when the most obvious draws get there is losing strategy. I preserve my remaining ~57bb stack for a better spot.

4c6c is a weak suited connector that plays poorly from UTG. With 13 hands left in the tournament and a 6000 chip stack (60bb), I should maintain disciplined play and fold marginal hands from early position. This hand lacks the equity and playability needed to profitably open from UTG.

Ten-Five offsuit is a very weak hand, even from the button. While opening for a steal is a common strategy in late position, T5o sits well outside a standard opening range. Given that FiveTwo (the chip leader) is in the Small Blind and has been playing aggressively, and Pro is in the Big Blind with a stack size comparable to mine, the risk of being called or three-bet outweighs the reward of a 1.5 BB steal. With only 12 hands remaining in the tournament, I prefer to conserve my stack for better spots rather than play a low-equity hand out of position post-flop against active opponents.

Folded to us in the small blind, this is a great spot to pressure the big blind with our big stack advantage. 5♠2♠ isn’t strong, but it has playable suited/wheel potential and benefits from taking initiative; Pro has shown willingness to fold to aggression, and a small raise keeps our risk controlled while still generating fold equity and setting up profitable c-bets on favorable textures.

The opponent in the Small Blind (FiveTwo) has a massive chip lead and raises to 250 (2.5 BB). I am in the Big Blind with T6o. While I have position post-flop and get decent pot odds (calling 150 to win 350, requiring ~30% equity), T6o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop due to reverse implied odds (often dominated if I hit a Ten). Standard defending ranges against a 2.5x raise typically include T7o+ or T8o+ but fold T6o. Given the stack depth and the opponent's aggressive capability as the chip leader, folding is the prudent play to preserve chips for better opportunities.

I am dealt 2c 8s under the gun in a four-handed game. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given my position and the fact that there are three players behind me, including the chip leader FiveTwo, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot with such a poor hand. I will preserve my stack of 9500 chips for a better opportunity.

With Flash folding, this is a prime steal spot on the Button versus two shorter stacks in the blinds. A2s plays well in position (nut-flush potential, wheel equity) and benefits from initiative. Raising also pressures the blinds’ calling/3-bet ranges and can win the pot uncontested frequently. I’ll use a standard small sizing to keep my range wide and avoid over-risking versus potential 3-bets while still applying pressure.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Deuce offsuit, a very weak hand. The Button (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader and playing aggressively, has raised to 250. Playing J2o out of position against an aggressive big stack is not profitable. I should fold and preserve my 85bb stack for better spots.

7d5s is a weak offsuit hand. FiveTwo has been very active and has a massive stack (35k+). I'm out of position with a marginal hand that doesn't play well postflop. While pot odds are decent (150 to win 400), 75o is just too weak to defend here profitably against an aggressive opponent. Folding to preserve my short stack.

4-handed ranges are wider, and K9o is near the bottom but still a profitable open given the dead money and our big stack. Raising (rather than limping) lets us leverage fold equity versus the blinds and keep initiative; the K blocks some strong Kx continues. I’ll use a small open to reduce the cost of folding to a 3-bet while still pressuring the blinds. Plan: comfortably fold to most 3-bets/large jams, and c-bet selectively on favorable flops when called.

I am holding J4s on the Button. The UTG player (FiveTwo), who is the massive chip leader, has opened to 225. Although FiveTwo is opening frequently, J4s is a weak hand that does not play well against an UTG range, even a loose one. Calling would likely invite squeezes from the blinds or lead to a difficult post-flop spot with a marginal hand. 3-betting is too risky given the chip leader's stack and ability to apply pressure. With 10 hands remaining and a healthy stack of over 85 big blinds, there is no need to force action with sub-optimal holdings. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Pocket tens is a premium hand. FiveTwo has been very active throughout this tournament, opening frequently from all positions. With TT in the small blind facing a UTG open, I should 3-bet for value. This isolates the opener, builds a pot with my strong hand, and puts pressure on both FiveTwo and Flash in the BB. A 3-bet to around 750 is appropriate sizing - roughly 3.3x the open.

I am in the Big Blind with 92o. There was an initial raise from FiveTwo (UTG) and a 3-bet from Claude in the Small Blind. 92o is one of the weakest possible hands and does not play well in a 3-bet pot, especially out of position. Given that we are nearing the end of the tournament (Hand 140 of 150), preserving my stack to maintain my second-place position is more important than calling with trash equity. I will fold.

K9o was a fine small open 4-handed, but versus a SB 3-bet from a shorter stack (5.1k) the SPR if we call will be low and we’ll be put in many awkward spots where we’re forced to fold to jams, and when we do connect (Kx/9x) we’re often dominated by Claude’s value-heavy continues (KQ/KJ/KT, pairs). The price is tempting, but the reverse implied odds and commit dynamics make this a clear fold. A 4-bet jam would rely on very high fold equity against a player who is likely 3-betting with a calling range already, so it’s not attractive with this blocker profile.

I hold Pocket Jacks (Jc Jd) in the UTG position (which is effectively Cutoff in a 4-handed game). This is a premium hand, ranked highly in strength, especially short-handed. My stack is healthy at ~86 BB. The Big Blind (FiveTwo) is the chip leader with a massive stack, so I need to be prepared for potential aggression, but JJ is strong enough to play for stacks or value. Limping is weak and invites multi-way pots where Jacks lose value. A standard open raise is the best play to build the pot and extract value from worse hands. I will raise to 250 (2.5x BB), consistent with standard sizing at this table.

K3s on the button facing UTG raise. UTG ranges are typically tight, and K3s doesn't play well against that range. While I have position, this hand is too weak to profitably call or 3-bet against an early position raiser. With only 10 hands left and a medium stack, I should preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 4s, a very weak hand. Pro raised to 250 from UTG (Under The Gun), which typically represents a strong range. Given my hand's poor playability and the fact that I would be out of position for the rest of the hand, there is no reason to call or raise. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack for the remaining hands.

4-handed, UTG’s open range should be fairly wide, and as the big stack I can leverage fold equity rather than defend passively OOP with a marginal offsuit hand. T7o isn’t a great flat (poor playability and domination issues), but it works fine as a low-cost 3-bet bluff: I win the dead money often, deny equity when he folds, and if called I retain initiative and can c-bet many textures. If UTG 4-bets, I simply fold and preserve my chip-lead edge.

I hold Pocket Jacks (Jc Jd) and am facing a 3-bet to 900 from the chip leader, FiveTwo, in the Big Blind. FiveTwo has demonstrated frequent aggression and wide 3-betting ranges (e.g., Hand #137 with A5s). My hand is strong, but 4-betting might fold out the bluffs I dominate or isolate me against premium holdings. I have position on the Big Blind, which provides a significant advantage post-flop. Calling keeps their range wide and sets up a favorable SPR of ~4 for post-flop play. I call.

3-bet pot, OOP with T7o and we flop top pair on a dynamic 7c-4c-5s texture. This board connects reasonably well with Pro’s flatting range (small pairs, suited connectors, club draws), so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a big bet and get forced into tough spots versus a check-raise. That said, checking gives him a very comfortable stab with overcards and/or clubs, and our hand benefits a lot from denying equity (two overcards, A-high with a club, etc.) while also getting thin value from 66–99 and some 5x/4x floats. Best is a small c-bet that keeps his range wide, protects, and lets me comfortably fold to a big raise.

I hold Pocket Jacks on a low, connected board of 7c-4c-5s. I am in position against the aggressive chip leader who 3-bet pre-flop and led out for a small size (approx 30% pot). My overpair is likely the best hand currently, beating hands like TT-88, AK, AQ, and various draws. Raising would likely isolate me against superior overpairs (QQ-AA) or strong combo draws, while folding out the bluffs I dominate. Calling allows me to keep the opponent's range wide, control the size of the pot, and leverage my position on future streets. The small bet size makes a call very attractive to see a turn card safely.

The 6c is a very unfavorable turn for my exact hand: it completes a lot of Pro’s flop-calling range (any 3x or 8x now has a straight) and brings in the third club, giving him many strong semi-bluffs/flushes. With T7 (top pair, weak kicker) I don’t want to barrel for “protection” into a card that improves his range and invites raises, especially in a 3-bet pot where stacks can get awkward quickly. Checking keeps the pot controlled, lets him bluff his missed overcards/one-club hands, and allows me to play a clearer bluff-catch/fold game based on his sizing and river runout.

The turn card is the 6c, making the board 7c-4c-5s-6c. This is a very coordinated board: there is now a four-card straight (4-5-6-7) on the board, meaning any 3 or 8 makes a straight, and there are three clubs, making a flush possible. I hold Jc-Jd. I have an overpair and the Jack-high flush draw. My opponent, the aggressive chip leader, checked the turn. 

Given the texture, my hand has showdown value against unmatched high cards (like AK/AQ without a club) but is behind overpairs (QQ-AA), straights, flushes, and sets. Betting here is thin for value because worse hands likely fold, and better hands will call or check-raise, putting me in a difficult spot. By checking back, I control the pot size, realize my equity with the Jc flush draw, and get to see a river card to potentially improve or re-evaluate. There is no need to turn my hand into a bluff or bet for protection in a spot where I could easily be drawing dead or thin.

River completes a very connected 7-6-5-4-2 runout with three clubs, where Pro’s flop-calling range contains many hands that beat T7 (3x/8x straights, 65 two-pair, sets, occasional flushes). After he checked back the turn, his range is somewhat capped toward one-pair/showdown hands and some give-ups, so betting mostly gets called by better and folds out the hands we beat. Checking keeps the pot controlled, realizes our showdown value with top pair, and gives him room to bluff missed/high-card holdings; we can bluff-catch versus smaller bets and comfortably fold versus big polar sizing.

The board is 7c-4c-5s-6c-2s. There is a four-card straight on the board (4-5-6-7), meaning any 3 or 8 makes a straight. There are also three clubs, making a flush possible. I hold pocket Jacks (Jc-Jd), giving me an overpair. Since I only hold one club (Jc), I do not have a flush. FiveTwo, the aggressive chip leader who 3-bet pre-flop, has checked the turn and now checked the river. This passive line suggests they likely do not have a strong hand like a straight, flush, or high overpair (QQ+), which would typically bet for value or protection on this coordinated board. They likely hold missed high cards (AK, AQ) or a mid-pair (99, TT) they are pot-controlling. Betting is thin for value as worse hands (AK) likely fold and better hands (traps) call or raise. Checking back guarantees I realize my showdown value against their missed range without opening myself up to a check-raise bluff or value raise.

K3o is a weak holding from UTG position. With 8 hands remaining and a medium stack of 62bb, I should be playing solid poker and not open weak offsuit kings that will often be dominated. Folding is the standard play here.

Hand 72 suited is mathematically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Although I am on the button and it is a potential stealing opportunity, the risk-to-reward ratio is unfavorable given the stack sizes of the blinds. FiveTwo (SB) has a massive chip lead and can afford to defend or 3-bet light, and Pro (BB) has a similar stack size to mine. With only 9 hands remaining in the tournament, I will preserve my stack and wait for a more profitable spot rather than attempting a high-risk steal with the worst hand in the deck.

Blind-vs-blind with folds to me, we should attack Pro’s big blind very wide. 9♦6♥ is too weak to limp-call profitably OOP, but it plays fine as part of a high-frequency SB raising range because we win immediately a lot and can leverage initiative postflop. I’ll use a standard small size to keep my range wide and apply pressure without bloating the pot unnecessarily.

I am in the Big Blind with 94o, a very weak hand. The Small Blind (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader, has raised to 2.5bb (250). The pot odds require me to call 150 into a pot of 350, offering 30% odds. While I would have position post-flop, 94o performs poorly even against a wide blind-vs-blind range, often having around 30-33% equity but suffering from poor playability (difficulty realizing that equity). Given the raise size is larger than a min-raise and my hand is in the bottom of my range, the expected value of calling is negative. I will fold and preserve my stack.

With only 8 hands remaining in the tournament, I am currently sitting in 3rd place out of 4 players. My hand, Jack-Five offsuit (J5o), is extremely weak, especially from the Under the Gun (UTG) position in a 4-handed game. There is no strategic advantage to playing this hand, as it is likely dominated by my opponents' ranges. I should preserve my stack and look for better opportunities to move up in rank or challenge the other medium stack (Pro) or the short stack (Claude) in more favorable spots.

4-handed and on the button after UTG folds, this is a clear steal spot. Js6s has decent playability (suited, can make strong draws) and benefits a lot from position. With my large stack versus two shorter blinds, a raise applies maximum pressure and wins the blinds often; if I face a big 3-bet/shove from these stacks I can comfortably fold and preserve my lead.

I am in the Small Blind with 4s 2s facing a button raise to 250 from FiveTwo. My hand is extremely weak (low suited one-gapper). Although I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 400, so 3:1), I will be out of position against a deep-stacked aggressor for the rest of the hand. Additionally, the Big Blind is still to act and could squeeze. At this late stage of the tournament (Hand 143/150), preserving my stack (9925) is important, and defending with such poor equity out of position is a losing play. Folding is the clear best option.

K7 suited in the BB facing a min-raise from FiveTwo. Getting 400:150 pot odds (~2.67:1). K7s is a marginal hand but has decent playability with the king and flush potential. FiveTwo has been very active throughout the tournament. This is a standard defend from the BB given the price.

I flopped a gutshot straight draw (need a J for the nut straight) with backdoor diamond flush draw and an overcard. Board is Q-T-9, fairly connected. With no bet to call, I should check and see what FiveTwo does. If he checks, I get a free card. If he bets, I'll evaluate based on pot odds for my ~4-5 out draw.

Heads-up in position after stealing pre, this is a very good flop to continuation-bet. I have an open-ended straight draw (8 or K) plus backdoor spades, so I can profit both by folding out Claude’s air/underpairs and by building a pot when I improve. A small-to-mid c-bet leverages my range advantage from the button, denies equity to hands like A-high/small pairs, and keeps my risk controlled versus a check-raise from a shorter stack.

Pot is 775, need to call 225 (3.44:1 odds, need ~22% equity). I have a gutshot straight draw (4 outs for J = nut straight) plus backdoor diamond flush and K overcard. Pure gutshot equity is ~8-9% but with backdoor flush and implied odds against FiveTwo's large stack, this small bet is worth calling. If I brick the turn, I can easily fold to further aggression.

Turn brought Tc, pairing the board. My gutshot draw remains (need J for K-high straight). Board is now 9d-Qs-Th-Tc - very connected and paired. I have 4 outs (~8% equity). With action checked to me and no bet to call, I should check. Betting would be a pure bluff into a paired board against FiveTwo who has shown aggression - not advisable with my marginal holdings. Check and hope to see a free river, fold to significant aggression.

Claude’s flop check-call range is a lot of Qx/9x/pocket pairs plus some straight draws. The turn pairing the T is a good card for my perceived range (I have more Tx as the preflop raiser), and he’s still capped after checking again. With Js6s I retain an open-ender (8 or K) so I can profitably semi-bluff: win immediately versus his weak floats/9x, and build the pot for when I hit. I’ll use a medium size that applies pressure without bloating risk versus a check-raise shove from a shorter stack.

Pot is 1550, need to call 550 for odds of ~3.8:1 (need ~21% equity). I only have a gutshot (4 outs = ~8% on river). Board paired with tens, FiveTwo is continuing to bet into me showing strength. My backdoor flush draw is dead. With only 8% equity needing 21%, this is a clear fold despite the pot odds being somewhat decent - I'm just too far behind mathematically.

4-handed UTG ranges do widen, and as the big stack I can apply pressure with low risk. Td5s is too weak to open large, but a min-raise gives a strong risk/reward (risk 200 to win 150) and keeps pots small when called. Opponents have shown plenty of preflop folding to my opens and relatively low 3-bet frequency, so taking the steal is higher EV than flat-calling or folding in this lineup.

I am holding Ten of Clubs and Trey of Spades (T3o) on the Button. The player Under the Gun (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader and playing aggressively, has min-raised to 200. T3o is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity, even against a wide opening range. Despite having position and a healthy stack of roughly 100 BBs, there is no strategic incentive to get involved in this pot with garbage cards, especially with only 6 hands left in the tournament. The standard and correct play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

J8o in the SB facing an UTG open from FiveTwo. This is a marginal hand out of position against an UTG range, which should be tighter even from an aggressive player. With only 6 hands left and a decent stack, no need to defend with weak hands OOP. Fold and wait for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Jh 3c. FiveTwo, the massive chip leader, has raised to 200 from UTG. While the price to call is small (100 more into a pot of 350), J3o is a very weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive chip leader. Given that there are only a few hands left in the tournament and my goal is to maintain my stack, I will choose the disciplined path and fold this weak hand.

I am holding 9s7d (97 offsuit) in the Under the Gun (UTG) position with 4 players remaining. This is a statistically weak hand that plays poorly out of position and has very low equity against calling ranges. With 99 BBs, I have a healthy stack and no pressure to make loose moves, especially with only 5 hands left in the session. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here.

8d4c on the button is too weak to open, even in position. FiveTwo in BB has a massive 35k stack and has been aggressive - high risk of facing a 3-bet. With only 6 hands left in the tournament, I should preserve my 57BB stack for better spots rather than open this trash hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 5-4 offsuit, a very weak starting hand. Pro and Claude have already folded. Although it would only cost 50 more chips to see the flop, I would be playing out of position against FiveTwo, the significant chip leader. Given that we are at hand 145 of 150 and I am currently in a close race for second place in stack size, I prefer to fold this weak hand and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips in the remaining five hands.

Pocket 9s in UTG is a solid hand. With 57 BBs effective, I should open raise for value. Standard sizing of 2.5x to 250-300. This hand plays well against calls and I can comfortably 4-bet or call a 3-bet depending on stack depths.

I am on the button with Q3 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Claude, who is sitting with a relatively short stack, has raised from the under-the-gun position. In a 4-handed game, his opening range from UTG is likely quite strong or at least stronger than Q3o. Given that we are near the end of the tournament (Hand 146 of 150) and I have a decent stack of 92 BB, there is no reason to risk chips with a marginal-to-poor hand against an early position raise. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

4-handed, UTG opening range is wider than full-ring and AJo is strong enough to 3-bet from the SB. Calling invites the BB in and leaves us out of position without initiative; 3-betting lets us win the pot immediately a lot, isolate Claude, and deny equity. With our big stack we can apply pressure, but we can still comfortably fold if Claude 4-bet jams (his shove range is likely strong enough that AJo performs poorly). Sizing to 900 (about 3.6x) is standard OOP and prices out the BB’s speculative calls.

I am in the Big Blind with Kd 5c. The action started with a UTG raise from Claude and a 3-bet from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). K5 offsuit is a very weak hand to defend against a 3-bet, especially when the 3-bet comes from the Small Blind against a UTG opener, which typically indicates a strong range (JJ+, AK, AQs). My pot odds are roughly 2.5:1, requiring about 28% equity against the combined ranges, but realizability is very low out of position and sandwiched if the UTG calls. Given the tournament stage (Hand 146/150) and my stack size, preserving chips is far better than gambling with a dominated hand in a bloated pot. The only standard play here is to fold.

Facing a 3-bet from FiveTwo with pocket 9s. FiveTwo has been aggressive throughout the tournament with a massive stack. My options: fold is too tight with 99, 4-betting to ~2200 essentially commits me with my stack. Calling 650 into 1250 gives me decent pot odds to see a flop. With 5 hands left and a medium stack, I'll call and play post-flop carefully - can fold to heavy action on bad boards or continue on favorable flops.

We’re the 3-bettor with range advantage on T♦8♠3♦, and A♦J♠ is a good low-risk c-bet candidate: two overcards plus the A♦ blocker reduces Claude’s strongest diamond-combo continues while still allowing us to win the pot immediately versus his underpairs and unpaired broadways. With Claude’s short effective stack (SPR ~2.5), any bet can face a jam; a small sizing maximizes folds without committing us. If he shoves, we comfortably fold A-high. If he calls, we can continue on favorable turns (A/J/diamond, some high cards) and otherwise slow down.

I have 99 on 3d-Td-8s. My nines are an overpair to the 3 and 8, but underpair to the T. FiveTwo's c-bet of 500 into 1900 is a small sizing (~26% pot). Getting almost 5:1 on a call with a solid middle pair that beats many of their bluffs and weaker holdings. Folding is too tight here. Raising commits too much of my remaining 4300 stack. Calling keeps my options open - I can fold to heavy turn/river pressure on bad runouts or continue extracting value on clean cards.

Turn 9♣ is a dynamic card that connects hard with Claude’s flop-calling range (QJ is now a made straight, 76/J7 improve, T9 appears, and many pair+draw combos pick up extra equity). With A♦J♠ we’re still just A-high with a thin gutshot to a 7 and some overcard outs that aren’t clean versus his Tx/straights. Given his short stack, any turn bet effectively invites a shove and we can’t profitably continue, so betting mainly burns chips. Checking keeps us from spewing, realizes our equity when he checks back, and lets us consider bluffing selectively on favorable rivers (especially a diamond where A♦ blocks the nut flush).

I just spiked a set of 9s on the turn! The board is 3d-Td-8s-9c. This is an excellent turn card for me - I went from a vulnerable underpair to the nuts essentially. FiveTwo checked, which is interesting given their aggression. The board has some draws (diamond flush draw, J-7 straight possible), so I need to bet for value and protection. With pot at 2900 and my stack at 4300, I'll bet around 65% pot (~1900) to build the pot while keeping room to bet river.

Turn 9♣ gives us a real semi-bluff candidate: A♦J♠ has an open-ended straight draw (Q/7) and key blockers (J blocks some QJ/JT/JJ continues). Claude’s 1900 into 4800 is a strong, polar sizing, but *calling* is unattractive: we’re getting ~28% pot odds and have only ~17% raw equity to hit the straight on the river, with limited implied value versus a short stack and the risk our 7-out is dirty vs QJ. Folding preserves chips but gives up a spot where we can apply maximum pressure.

Best play is to leverage our stack and credible value range (sets/straights in a 3-bet pot after we check turn) by check-raising to put Claude all-in. This maximizes fold equity versus his one-pair/protection bets and semi-bluffs, and when called we still have outs to improve (and sometimes chop on Q vs QJ). Raising also avoids awkward river decisions if we just call and face a shove.

Sizing: raise to 4300 total, which effectively puts his remaining stack in.

I flopped bottom pair and turned a set of 9s - this is a monster! FiveTwo raised my value bet to 4300, and I only have 2400 remaining. The pot is 9100 and I'm getting nearly 4:1 pot odds. My set of nines is incredibly strong here - only TT or J7/76 for straights beat me. FiveTwo could easily have overpairs like JJ/QQ/KK/AA, two pair, draws (diamond flush, straight draws), or even be semi-bluffing. With my stack size and hand strength, this is a trivial all-in call.

With only four hands remaining in the tournament, preserving stack size is crucial. I am currently UTG in a 4-handed game holding 4d 5s, which is a very weak hand. Given the proximity to the end of the tournament and my position, there is no strategic value in playing this hand against opponents who have been active and aggressive. Folding is the most prudent move to avoid unnecessary losses.

4-handed after UTG folds, we’re in a prime steal spot on the Button with a suited, connected hand that plays well in position. With a big stack edge, I want to keep opening pressure on the blinds and build pots where I realize equity well. A small open also keeps my risk low while forcing SB/BB to defend wider and make tougher postflop decisions out of position.

It is Hand 147 of 150. FiveTwo (Button) has a massive chip lead and has been opening frequently. While 7h 8c is a playable connector, playing it out of position against the aggression of the chip leader is risky at this stage. I am currently in a close race for 2nd/3rd place with Flash and Claude, so preserving my stack is more valuable than marginally positive chip EV spots that carry high variance and positional disadvantage. The raise size is 2.5x, offering decent odds, but the strategic downside of playing a bloated pot OOP with a weak hand is too high.

4-2 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. Even getting 2.7:1 pot odds, calling 150 out of position against FiveTwo (who has been aggressive) is -EV. This hand has virtually no playability post-flop. Easy fold.

4-handed UTG plays like a late position; A5s is comfortably in my opening range. It has good playability (suited, wheel/straight potential) and an Ace blocker that reduces opponents’ strongest 3-bet holdings. With a big stack edge over all three opponents, I want to apply steady pressure and take initiative rather than limp. A small open also keeps my risk controlled while still forcing blinds to defend wider or fold too much.

I hold pocket threes (3c 3s) on the Button. The UTG player, FiveTwo, who is the chip leader and opening a very high frequency of hands, has opened to 250. Given the depth of our stacks (I have ~97 BBs) and my position, this is a suitable spot to 'set mine'. A call allows me to see a flop cheaply in position. If I hit a set, I have great implied odds against the loose chip leader. If I miss, I can easily fold to aggression. 3-betting is risky as it inflates the pot with a hand that plays poorly post-flop without improvement, and folding is too tight against such a wide range.

I have pocket 8s in the SB facing an UTG open and a button call. Pocket 8s is a solid hand - I can either 3-bet for value/isolation or call to set mine. With two players already in and being out of position post-flop, calling to see a flop and potentially hit a set is reasonable. Getting good implied odds with deep stacks.

With the hand being #148 of 150 and my current stack being slightly behind Pro's, I need to look for opportunities to gain chips. FiveTwo opened from UTG and received two callers, creating a 4-way pot. I am in the Big Blind and only need to call 150 more to see a flop in a pot of 850 (offering 1:6.6 odds). Although J4o is a very weak hand, the pot odds are mathematically favorable to see a flop. I will call and play extremely cautiously, looking to fold to any aggression unless I flop two pair or better.

I have pocket 8s on a 4h-4s-Ts board. My 8s are an overpair to the 4s but under the Ten. With 3 opponents and a paired board with flush draw potential, I should bet for value and protection. A bet around 40-50% pot will charge draws and get value from underpairs/Ace-high while defining where I stand. If I get raised significantly, I can reevaluate.

I have flopped trips (three of a kind) with a Jack kicker on a 4-4-T board. This is an extremely strong hand. Claude has led into the field for 450 chips. This 'donk' bet often signifies a Ten or a flush draw. If I raise now, I risk folding out weaker hands or draws that might otherwise continue, and I'd likely isolate myself against only the strongest parts of the opponents' ranges (like a better 4 or pocket tens). By just calling, I disguise the strength of my hand, maintain a favorable pot-to-stack ratio, and keep both FiveTwo and Pro in the pot, potentially allowing them to commit chips with overcards or smaller pairs. Given it's hand 148 of 150 and I need to close the gap on Pro, I want to maximize the potential to win a large pot without blowing everyone out too early.

Multiway on T44 with a donk bet and a call, a bluff-raise has low fold equity (Tx/4x/pairs continue a lot), and getting 3-bet/continued into by Pro behind would put us in a tough spot with just ace-high. Calling 450 into a pot that will be ~2350 gives a good price and keeps ranges wide; we can improve on A/5/diamond turns and we retain the ability to apply pressure on many turn cards (especially broadway/A) if the blinds slow down. If Pro raises big behind, we can comfortably fold and preserve our chip-lead edge.

I hold Pocket Threes (3c 3s) on a board of 4h-4s-Ts. This gives me a weak two pair, counterfeit by the board pair of 4s, meaning any pair >3, any 4, or any Ten beats me. Facing a lead from the SB and two calls in a 4-way pot, it is virtually certain I am behind. Although the pot odds are favorable (getting roughly 6:1), I likely have very few clean outs (drawing to a 3 might still lose to a 4 or better full house) and am drawing nearly dead against the ranges of three opponents who have shown interest in this texture. Preserving my stack in hand 148/150 is prioritized over making a loose call with an underpair.

Turn brought the 5s, putting three spades on board. My pocket 8s are now vulnerable to flushes. Two opponents called my flop bet - they could have flush draws that just completed, tens, or even a 4. With a medium-strength hand on a wet board, checking is prudent to control pot size and get to showdown cheaply. If someone bets, I can evaluate based on sizing.

I have trips (three of a kind) with a Jack kicker on a board of 4-4-T-5. On the flop, Claude led into the field and I called along with FiveTwo. Now on the turn, the 5 of spades completes a potential flush. Claude has checked, which often indicates he is either pot-controlling a Ten or was on a draw that he is now playing cautiously. FiveTwo is still behind me. Since I am at Hand 148 of 150 and need to accumulate chips to surpass Pro and Claude, I will place a value bet. This bet targets hands like a Ten (Tx) or a single high spade (like the Ace or King of spades) that might call to see a river. If I am raised significantly, particularly by the chip leader FiveTwo, I will have to re-evaluate the strength of my hand against a potential flush.

Turn brings the third spade, completing a lot of the flop’s spade-draw continues. Flash’s small 950 lead into 3300 can be value/blocking with a made flush, but also can be thin with Tx/5x trying to set a price. With Ad5d we have two pair (5s and 4s on board) with an Ace kicker—strong enough to continue, but not strong enough to bloat the pot into a range that’s now heavily weighted toward flushes/4x.

Calling gives us good pot odds (950 to win 4250 ≈ 22%), keeps dominated Tx/5x hands in, and avoids getting jammed on by flushes (and potentially induced check-raises from Claude) where we’d be forced into a high-variance spot late in the match. We also retain real improvement: river 5 or 4 gives us a full house/quads on board, and we can re-evaluate river sizing on blanks versus big pressure.

Three spades on board. Flash bet 950, FiveTwo called. My pocket 8s are very vulnerable here. I have the 8s (spade) giving me a weak flush draw, but if either opponent has a made flush, I'm in terrible shape. Even if I spike a spade, any higher spade beats me. With two players showing strength on this wet board, my 88 is almost certainly behind - likely facing a made flush, trips, or strong draws. Pot odds are ~4.5:1 (~18% needed), but my equity is low against their ranges. Folding is correct.

On the river, the Ace of Hearts appears. I currently hold three of a kind (fours) with a Jack kicker. The board has three spades, making a flush possible. However, on the turn, I bet 950 and FiveTwo simply called. This suggests he might have a Ten, an Ace with a high spade, or a flush draw that he didn't want to raise yet. Since there are only three hands left in the tournament and I need to accumulate chips to overtake Pro and Claude, I will place a value bet of 1800. This amount is likely to be called by a Ten or a newly improved Ace, while not being so large that it only gets called by hands that beat me (like a flush or a full house). If FiveTwo raises significantly, I will have to consider the high probability of a flush.

We have A5 for top two pair on a paired board (Aces and Fives). Flash’s river sizing (1800 into 6050) is a ~42% pot bet giving us excellent pot odds: we need to be good only ~23% of the time.

While his turn lead on the 3rd spade and river follow-through can certainly be a made flush or 4x/boat, this line also contains plenty of semi-bluffs from the turn (picked up a flush draw) that now value-bet thin after improving to an Ace on the river, plus occasional Tx/5x “block/value” bets and missed bluffs. Raising is unattractive because worse hands won’t call often and better hands (flushes/4x/AT) will stack us, so we take the high-EV, low-mistake option and call.

It is Hand 149 of 150. I am playing 4-handed from UTG (effectively the Cutoff). My hand is JTo. While JTo is sometimes included in a Cutoff opening range, it is near the bottom of that range. With the tournament nearly over, the Big Blind (chip leader) has a significant stack advantage and may defend widely or apply pressure, and there are two other active players behind me. The potential gain of stealing the blinds (1.5BB) does not outweigh the risk of playing a dominated hand in a potentially inflated pot, especially when my goal is to maximize my final stack and I am currently in a close race for position. A fold is the prudent, zero-variance play here.
KTo on the button is a solid opening hand. With only the blinds left to act, this is a standard raise spot. I'll open to 2.5x to take down the blinds or play in position postflop.

With hand 149 of 150, I'm holding Ace-Seven offsuit in the Small Blind. Claude has raised from the Button, which is a wide opening position. A7o is strong enough to continue, but since we are very close to the end of the tournament and I want to minimize unnecessary variance while maintaining my second-place position, I will choose to call rather than 3-bet. This allows me to see a flop and potentially win a medium-sized pot without risking a significant portion of my stack to a 4-bet shove from Claude or the chip leader in the Big Blind.

We’re getting a very good price closing the action: 150 more to win a 600 pot (~4:1), so we only need ~20% equity. J4 suited is weak and can be dominated, but the suited component plus multiway pot odds make it a profitable defend versus a BTN open and SB flat. Squeezing here would bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand against opponents deep enough to continue, so the best EV line is to call and proceed with a mostly check-and-realize strategy (continue on strong draws/top-pair+ boards, otherwise keep pots small).

On a board of 7s-Ah-Ad, I have flopped a monster: a full house (Aces full of Sevens). The board is incredibly dry, and since I hold one of the remaining two Aces, it's unlikely my opponents have much to connect with. Leading out into the pre-flop aggressor (Claude) would likely result in folds from their entire range except for a very lucky pocket pair or the case Ace. By checking, I allow Claude to continue their aggression with a continuation bet or allow FiveTwo to bluff. With only two hands left in the tournament, I need to maximize value from my big hands to potentially close the gap on the chip leader.

On A-A-7 we have pure air (J4) with only a very weak backdoor club possibility. This board strongly favors the preflop raiser (Claude) and is also a board where multiway bluffs perform poorly because someone often has an ace or will continue with pocket pairs. Donk-betting or check-raising would bloat the pot out of position with a hand that can’t withstand pressure. Best EV is to check and let Claude decide whether to c-bet; if it checks through we can consider selective delayed bluffs on favorable turns.

Flop is 7s Ah Ad - a paired ace board. Both opponents checked to me. This is a great c-bet spot as the preflop raiser. The paired aces reduce the likelihood they hold an ace, and I can credibly represent an ace here. A small bet (~1/3 pot) should take this down often, and if called I still have overcards with backdoor equity.

On a board of 7s-Ah-Ad, I have flopped the absolute nuts with Aces full of Sevens (As-Ah-Ad-7s-7h). Since I hold one of the remaining two Aces and one of the remaining two Sevens, it is extremely unlikely that my opponents have connected with this board in any significant way. Claude's bet of 275 is likely a standard continuation bet with a wide range of hands. Raising now would likely force a fold from almost everything Claude is betting, including bluffs and weak pairs. By calling, I keep FiveTwo in the pot, allow Claude to continue their story on the turn, and maximize the potential to extract more chips in the final two hands of the tournament.

We’re facing a small multiway c-bet (275 into 750; pot is 1300 after Flash calls). Calling 275 to win 1575 gives us ~4.7:1, so we only need ~17% equity. On A-A-7 our hand isn’t pure air: we effectively have trip aces with a J kicker, which can already be best versus a large portion of both ranges that missed the ace (many suited connectors/pairs/weak broadways), and we also retain some backdoor club/runout improvement. Raising is bad (multiway, hard to fold out better kickers and never folds an ace), and folding is too tight given the price and our kicker equity. Best EV is to call and proceed carefully—fold to meaningful turn pressure, and only continue on favorable turns/check-through scenarios.

I have the absolute nuts with Aces full of Sevens. The board is incredibly dry (7s-Ah-Ad-4d). I hold an Ace and a Seven, making it very unlikely that my opponents have a strong hand to call a lead bet. On the flop, Claude (the pre-flop aggressor) bet and was called by both me and FiveTwo. This suggests there is some interest in the pot. By checking again on the turn, I am continuing to play my hand deceptively. I want to induce Claude to fire a second barrel with his air or medium-strength hands, or allow FiveTwo to take a stab at the pot. Given there is only one hand left after this, I need to extract as much value as possible, and checking is the best way to keep my opponents' bluffs in their ranges.

Turn improves us massively: with Jc4c on A-A-7-4 we now have a full house (AAA44). This is an extremely strong hand multiway; only hands containing a 7 (making AAA77) or the very rare AsAc (quads) beat us, while both opponents can easily have Ax, pocket pairs, or floats that will continue versus a reasonable sizing. Checking risks it checking through and also lets bad rivers (especially a 7) reduce our edge to a chop/lose to quads 7s, so we should start building the pot now with a value lead that’s small enough to get called by trips. If raised, we can comfortably continue/stack off given our near-nut strength.

I have K-high on a paired ace board. My flop c-bet was a semi-bluff that got called by two players. Now FiveTwo donk-bets the turn - this line (check-call flop, donk-bet turn) often indicates strength or at minimum a made hand. I have zero showdown value with K-high, Flash is still behind me, and continuing as a bluff here would be expensive with two opponents. Easy fold.

I have the absolute nuts with Aces full of Sevens (As-Ah-Ad-7s-7h). The board is extremely dry and I hold an Ace and a Seven, making it highly improbable that my opponents have connected in any significant way. FiveTwo has taken the lead on the turn with a bet of 650. Since I am playing Hand 149 of 150 and want to maximize my stack, I will choose to call here. Raising now would likely signal extreme strength and force a fold from bluffs or medium-strength hands. By calling, I maintain the deception of a weak hand or a draw, encouraging FiveTwo to fire a second barrel on the river where I can then look to extract maximum value.

I have the absolute nuts with Aces full of Sevens (As-Ah-Ad-7s-7h). There is no hand that can beat me since Quads are impossible (I hold one Ace and two are on the board). Throughout the hand, I have played deceptively by checking and calling to hide the strength of my full house. FiveTwo, the chip leader, showed aggression on the turn by betting 650 after I checked. In this second-to-last hand of the tournament, I want to maximize the chips I can take from him to potentially challenge for first place. Since FiveTwo is an aggressive player and likely perceives my line as weak or a drawing hand, checking the river is the best move to induce a final bluff or a value bet from an Ace he might be holding. If I bet out, I risk him folding his bluffs; by checking, I keep his entire range in play.

River is checked to us heads-up vs Flash with pot 2875. We hold AAA44 (full house) on A-A-7-4-3, which is an extremely strong value hand; Flash’s line (flop call, turn call, river check) is heavily weighted to Ax and some 7x/pocket pairs that will often check-call. We should bet for value rather than check back, using a sizing that’s big enough to extract from any ace but not so huge that it folds out the weaker parts too often. ~70% pot targets Ax while still giving 7x/PPs some incentive to look us up. If Flash check-raises large, we can continue (call/jam) given how few combos beat us (mainly A7, 77, AA).

I hold the absolute nuts with Aces full of Sevens (As-Ah-Ad-7s-7h). On this extremely dry board (7s-Ah-Ad-4d-3s), with three Aces accounted for between my hand and the board, no higher full house or quads are mathematically possible. FiveTwo, the current chip leader, has shown significant strength by leading the turn and firing a substantial bet of 2000 on the river. Given that this is the penultimate hand of the tournament, my objective is to maximize my stack to challenge for first place. FiveTwo is likely to call a raise with any Ace-X hand, a smaller full house like 44 or 33, or perhaps even a bluff if he feels committed to the pot. I will move all-in to extract the maximum possible value from his range.

We have an extremely strong hand: AAA44 (aces full of fours). On this board we only lose to very few holdings—mainly A7 (AAA77) and the one combo of AsAc (quads). We also occasionally chop vs A4 (AAA44). 

Flash’s river check-raise jam after check/calling flop and turn is polarized, but it can very reasonably contain many worse value hands that will play it this way: any Ax that decides to “go with trips,” and especially hands like 77 (777AA), 44 (444AA), 33 (333AA), or A3 (AAA33), all of which we beat. Given the line and player pool tendencies, it’s hard to justify folding a near-nut full house.

Pot odds: we’re calling 10,425 more to win 27,725 total, needing only ~37.6% equity. Against any range that includes even a modest amount of worse Ax / lower full houses (and/or bluffs), we clear that comfortably. Calling also has big upside in chips and can eliminate Flash without risking our tournament life.

Last hand of the tournament, UTG with Q5o. This is a weak offsuit hand that doesn't play well from early position. With 10175 chips (about 100 BB), I have a healthy stack and no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal holdings. Standard fold from UTG.

This is the final hand of the tournament, and I have a commanding chip lead. My current hand, Queen-Five offsuit (Qh 5d), is a very weak starting hand. While I am on the button and could attempt a steal, there is no strategic reason to risk any chips with a marginal hand at this stage. Folding allows me to preserve my stack and conclude the tournament safely in the lead.

Blind-vs-blind with two folds, ranges are very wide and initiative matters a lot. T8o is well within a profitable small blind opening range: it has decent high-card value, can make strong top pairs/straights, and benefits from fold equity preflop. Completing gives the big blind a free look and lets them realize equity easily; raising pressures their weakest hands and defines the pot. I’ll use a standard ~2.5x sizing to keep my range wide while still generating folds and maintaining maneuverability postflop.

This is the final hand of the tournament (150/150). I am currently in 4th place with 9425 chips, trailing Claude in 3rd place (10175) by 750 chips. Folding guarantees I finish last. To have any chance of improving my standing, I must accumulate chips this hand. Simply stealing the blinds with a 3-bet would net me roughly 150-250 chips, which is insufficient to overtake Claude. Therefore, I need to see a flop and hope to hit a strong hand (like two pair, trips, or a flush) to win a larger pot against the Small Blind. My hand, T3s, has some post-flop playability and I have position. The price to call is cheap (150 chips for a pot of 500, offering decent odds). I will call to see a flop and look for an opportunity to win a significant pot.

Blind-vs-blind ranges are wide and I retain the initiative from the preflop raise. On A97 with two spades I have a range advantage (I have more strong Ax), and a small c-bet should fold out a lot of BB’s whiffs (broadways, low pairs without a spade, random trash). My hand has little showdown value but does have a gutshot to the 6 plus backdoor diamond equity, so betting is better than giving a free card. I’ll use a small sizing to keep risk low and keep my range flexible versus raises.

I am in 4th place (9,275 chips) on the final hand and must overtake Claude (10,175 chips) to avoid finishing last. The gap is 900 chips, meaning I need to increase my starting stack of 9,425 by at least 751 chips. Currently, the opponent (FiveTwo) has contributed 450 chips to the pot (250 pre-flop + 200 flop). If I raise now and he folds, I profit only 450 chips, ending with 9,875, which is still 4th place. Folding also guarantees 4th. The only way to surpass Claude is to win a pot where FiveTwo commits at least ~300 more chips (total >750). Therefore, I must Call the flop bet to keep him in the hand, hoping he bets the Turn, providing an opportunity for me to bluff-raise or hit a lucky card to win a sufficiently large pot.

Turn pairs the 9, which is a good card for my perceived range as the preflop raiser (I can credibly have more strong 9x/boats like A9/99). Pro’s flop-calling range contains a lot of one-and-done hands (small pairs, random floats, some 7x without a spade) that will hate facing a second barrel on a paired board. My actual hand has no showdown value but retains decent equity via a double-gutshot (6 or J) if called. I’ll apply pressure with a small, efficient barrel that risks little while keeping my range wide; if raised I can comfortably fold.

It is the final hand of the tournament (150/150). I am currently in 4th place with a starting stack of 9,525 chips. Claude is in 3rd with 10,175. To avoid finishing last, I must overtake Claude. The chip difference is 650. I have already invested 450 chips. The current pot is 1,200 (including opponent's turn bet). If I win this pot immediately, my stack becomes 10,275 (9525 - 450 + 1200), which is sufficient to secure 3rd place. Folding guarantees 4th place. My hand (Th 3h) has very little showdown value on a board of [9s Ad 7s 9c], effectively just Ten-high. However, the opponent's turn bet of 300 into a pot of 900 is small and signals weakness or a blocking bet. The turn card (9) pairs the board, which is an excellent card for me to represent, as a 9 would likely just call the flop. By raising, I credibly represent trips (9x) and put maximum pressure on weak Ax or draw holdings. Since maintaining my current stack still results in last place, I must take the high-variance line to steal the pot. A raise to 1200 is large enough to fold out marginal hands while offering a good risk/reward ratio to gain the necessary rank jump.

Pro’s turn raise is concerning, but the sizing is relatively small and his most natural value hands here are a lot of 9x that just turned trips (plus some Ax for thin value/protection and some spade-draw/semi-bluffs). With Td8h on A97-9 I have a double-gutshot (6 or J) to a straight (8 clean outs), and when I get there it’s well-disguised and can win his remaining stack frequently versus trips/Ax. Calling 900 to win a pot that will be 3300 gives me workable immediate odds, and the implied odds versus a shorter stack make up the rest. Plan: call now; on many bricks/spades I can check-fold to heavy river pressure, but on 6/J I’m very often continuing (often check-calling a shove).

River is a total brick for my hand (T8 high) and I’m almost never winning at showdown. After Pro check-raises the turn on A97-9, his range is polarized: lots of 9x/value, but also a meaningful chunk of spade-draw/semi-bluff raises that missed on the 2c river. Since I called the turn raise, I can credibly represent slowplayed trips/boats (A9/97/99) and apply pressure to his missed draws and some marginal Ax that may just want to get to showdown. I’ll lead a medium bluff sizing that doesn’t risk too much but needs only modest fold equity; if he raises, I’m done with the hand.

This is the final hand of the tournament (150/150). I am currently in 4th place with 7,875 chips remaining (started hand with 9,525). Claude is in 3rd place with 10,175. To avoid finishing last, I must end this hand with more chips than Claude. Folding leaves me with 7,875, guaranteeing last place. Calling 1,300 is also a losing play, as my hand (Pair of Nines, Ten kicker playing the board) loses to the opponent's likely range (Ax or better) given his line of calling a turn raise and leading the river. Since I have zero showdown value to win and folding is a guaranteed loss of rank, my only option with non-zero equity is to turn my hand into a bluff. I represented trip 9s on the turn. By shoving all-in for 7,875, I put maximum pressure on the opponent's marginal made hands (like weak Ax) that might have led as a blocking bet. If he folds, I win the pot of 4,600, bringing my stack to 12,475, securing 3rd place. This is a mandatory 'stop-and-go' style bluff as a hail mary to avoid last place.

We’re facing an all-in raise after I led the river as a bluff on A♢ 9♠ 7♠ 9♣ 2♣. With T♦8♥ my actual showdown hand is just one pair (9s) with A-T-8 kickers—no trips, no two pair, no missed draw to fall back on.

Pot odds: calling 6,575 to win 19,050 means I need ~34.5% equity. After Pro check-raised the turn and now jams over my river lead, his range is heavily value-dense: any 9x (trips), any Ax (two pair Aces+9s), any pocket pair (makes two pair with the board 9s), plus some 7x/2x. The only hands I beat are a narrow slice of missed spade-draw bluffs that also have very low kickers (and many missed spade draws are broadways that still beat my T kicker at showdown). That’s nowhere near enough to reach the required equity.

Since my river lead was a bluff-catcher setup and I got jammed on, the correct maximally +EV decision is to give up and preserve the remaining stack.
